素问.title_en: Discourse on the True Qi of High Antiquity
paragraphs_en: In former times there was Huangdi (the Yellow Emperor). At birth he was already imbued with spiritual power; while still an infant he could speak; in his childhood he was quick and discerning; grown to youth he was sincere and diligent; and having reached maturity he ascended to the throne. He then asked his Celestial Teacher, saying: 'I have heard that the people of high antiquity all lived past a hundred years, yet their movements did not decline. The people of the present age, however, decline in their capacities by the age of fifty. Is this because the times have changed, or because people themselves have lost the Way?' Qibo replied: 'Among the people of high antiquity, those who understood the Way patterned themselves on yin and yang and lived in harmony with the arts and numbers of self-cultivation. They were moderate in eating and drinking, regular in their rising and resting, and did not labor recklessly. Hence their bodies and spirits remained together, and they were able to live out their allotted heavenly years, departing only after passing a hundred years of age. The people of today are not like this. They take wine as if it were a beverage and treat recklessness as their norm. They enter the bedchamber while intoxicated, letting their desires exhaust their essence and dissipate their true qi. They do not know how to maintain fullness, do not govern their spirit at the proper times, but strive only to please their hearts, going against the joys of life, and are irregular in rising and resting. Hence they decline by the age of fifty.'

Now when the sages of high antiquity taught those below them, they always spoke of vacuous evils and thieving winds, which must be avoided at the proper times. By being tranquil, placid, empty, and desireless, the true qi followed accordingly, and the essence and spirit were guarded within — from where, then, could illness come? Thus their wills were at ease and their desires few; their hearts were calm and without fear; their bodies labored yet did not become weary; their qi followed smoothly, and each person's desires were satisfied, each obtaining what they wished. They found their food delicious, were content with their clothing, delighted in their customs, and did not envy one another whether of high or low station — hence these people were called simple and unadorned. Thus cravings and desires could not weary their eyes, and excessive and deviant influences could not confuse their hearts. Whether foolish or wise, worthy or unworthy, none feared external things, and so they accorded with the Way. The reason they were all able to live beyond a hundred years without their movements declining is that their virtue was whole and undisturbed.

Huangdi said: 'When a person grows old and is without children, is it because their physical capacity is exhausted, or is it simply the natural course of the heavenly numbers?' Qibo said: 'In a girl, at seven years of age, the kidney qi becomes abundant; her teeth change and her hair grows long. At two sevens (fourteen), tiangui — the reproductive essence that matures at puberty and governs fertility — arrives, the Conception Vessel opens, the Penetrating Vessel becomes abundant, and her monthly courses come at their proper time; hence she is able to bear children. At three sevens (twenty-one), the kidney qi is level and balanced, so her wisdom teeth emerge and her growth reaches its fullest extent. At four sevens (twenty-eight), her sinews and bones are firm, her hair reaches its fullest growth, and her body is flourishing and strong. At five sevens (thirty-five), the Yangming vessel declines, her face begins to wither, and her hair begins to fall. At six sevens (forty-two), the three yang vessels decline in the upper body, her face becomes entirely withered, and her hair begins to turn white. At seven sevens (forty-nine), the Conception Vessel becomes deficient, the Penetrating Vessel declines and diminishes, tiangui is exhausted, and the pathway of the earth is no longer open; hence her body deteriorates and she can no longer bear children. In a boy, at eight years of age, the kidney qi becomes replete, his hair grows and his teeth change. At two eights (sixteen), the kidney qi becomes abundant, tiangui arrives, his essence qi overflows and is released, yin and yang become harmonious, and so he is able to father children. At three eights (twenty-four), the kidney qi is level and balanced, his sinews and bones become strong, so his wisdom teeth emerge and his growth reaches its fullest extent. At four eights (thirty-two), his sinews and bones are flourishing and abundant, his flesh and muscles full and strong. At five eights (forty), the kidney qi declines, his hair falls and his teeth wither. At six eights (forty-eight), the yang qi is exhausted and declines in the upper body, his face withers, and his hair and temples turn gray and white. At seven eights (fifty-six), the liver qi declines, his sinews can no longer move freely, tiangui is exhausted, his essence grows scant, his kidney viscus declines, and his body as a whole reaches its limit. At eight eights (sixty-four), his teeth and hair are gone. The kidney governs water; it receives the essence of the five viscera and six bowels and stores it. Hence, when the five viscera are flourishing, the kidney is able to discharge essence in reproduction. But now, when the five viscera have all declined, the sinews and bones become slack and feeble, and tiangui is exhausted. Hence the hair and temples turn white, the body becomes heavy, one's walk becomes unsteady, and one is no longer able to have children.' Huangdi said: 'But there are those who, though already old in years, are still able to have children — why is this?' Qibo said: 'This is because their heavenly allotted lifespan exceeds the ordinary measure, and their qi and vessels remain constantly open and unobstructed, so that their kidney qi still has a surplus. Although such a person may still have children, in a man this will not go beyond eight eights (sixty-four), and in a woman not beyond seven sevens (forty-nine), for by then the essential qi of Heaven and Earth within them will have become entirely exhausted.' Huangdi said: 'Now, those who follow the Way — can they live to a hundred years and still be able to have children?' Qibo said: 'Those who follow the Way are able to hold back old age and keep their bodies whole. Even though they may be advanced in years, they remain able to father children.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that in high antiquity there were Realized Persons (zhenren) who grasped and held Heaven and Earth, who mastered yin and yang, who breathed the essential qi, who stood alone and guarded their spirit, so that their muscles and flesh were as one — hence they could live as long as Heaven and Earth, without any limit or end. This was because they lived according to the Way. In middle antiquity, there were Consummate Persons (zhiren) whose virtue was pure and whose grasp of the Way was complete. They harmonized themselves with yin and yang, attuned themselves to the four seasons, withdrew from the ordinary world and departed from vulgar customs, accumulated their essence and kept their spirit whole, and roamed freely between Heaven and Earth, their sight and hearing extending beyond the eight directions. This is how they increased their years and strengthened themselves, and they too returned to the state of the Realized Persons. Next there were Sages who dwelt in harmony with Heaven and Earth, followed the principles of the eight winds, and adapted their cravings and desires to the ordinary world. They harbored no feelings of resentment or anger; in their conduct they did not wish to separate themselves from the world, yet in their dress and bearing they did not wish to conform to vulgar fashion. Outwardly they did not wear out their bodies with worldly affairs, and inwardly they were free from the burden of anxious thoughts. They made tranquility and contentment their constant task, and self-fulfillment their achievement; their bodies did not become worn out, nor did their essence and spirit scatter, and so they too could live to a hundred years. Next there were Worthies who took Heaven and Earth as their model, patterned themselves on the sun and moon, discerned and arrayed themselves according to the stars and constellations, followed yin and yang in their proper accord and opposition, and distinguished the four seasons. They sought to follow high antiquity and unite themselves with the Way, and so they too were able to increase their lifespan, though theirs had its limit.'

素问.title_en: Great Discourse on Regulating the Spirit in Accord with the Four Seasons' Qi
paragraphs_en: The three months of spring are called the period of unfolding and renewal. Heaven and Earth together bring forth life, and the myriad things flourish. One should retire late and rise early, walk broadly in the courtyard, let the hair hang loose and the body relax, so as to allow the will to be generated. One should give life and not kill, give and not take away, reward and not punish — this is the way of nurturing life in response to the qi of spring. To go against this injures the liver, and in summer this will bring about cold-transmutation disorders, so that one has little with which to support growth. The three months of summer are called the period of luxuriant flourishing. The qi of Heaven and Earth intermingle, and the myriad things bloom and bear fruit. One should retire late and rise early, without growing weary of the long days. One should not let the will become angered, but allow the flower-like brilliance of the body to reach its fullness, allow the qi to be released, as though what one loved were outside oneself — this is the way of nurturing growth in response to the qi of summer. To go against this injures the heart, and in autumn this will bring about recurrent fevers and chills, so that one has little with which to support the gathering-in of autumn, and by the winter solstice one will fall gravely ill. The three months of autumn are called the period of calm equanimity. The qi of Heaven becomes brisk and the qi of Earth becomes clear. One should retire early and rise early, rising together with the crowing of the cock. One should keep the will at peace and tranquil, so as to soften the severity of autumn, gather in the spirit and qi, and cause the autumn qi to be level; one should not let the will extend outward, so as to keep the lung qi clear — this is the way of nurturing the gathering-in in response to the qi of autumn. To go against this injures the lung, and in winter this will bring about diarrhea with undigested food, so that one has little with which to support the storing-up of winter. The three months of winter are called the period of closing and storing. The waters freeze and the earth cracks open; one should not disturb one's yang. One should retire early and rise late, always waiting for the sunlight to appear. One should keep the will as though hidden and concealed, as though harboring a private intention, as though one had already attained something. One should avoid the cold and seek warmth, and not allow the skin to leak and let the qi be repeatedly stolen away — this is the way of nurturing the storing-up in response to the qi of winter. To go against this injures the kidney, and in spring this will bring about wilting and reversal of the limbs, so that one has little with which to support the generation of spring.

The qi of Heaven is pure, tranquil, and luminous. It stores its virtue ceaselessly, and therefore does not descend. If the brightness of Heaven were obscured, then even the sun and moon would lose their brightness, and evil influences would harm the body's openings; the yang qi would become blocked and closed, and the qi of Earth would cloud over what should be bright — the clouds and mist would lose their clarity, so that in response, the white dew above would fail to descend. When the qi of Heaven and Earth no longer communicate and intermingle openly, the life of the myriad things would fail to be bestowed, and if not bestowed, the great trees would largely die. Malign qi would fail to disperse, wind and rain would lose their regulation, the white dew would fail to descend, and dead and withered plants would fail to flourish. Thieving winds would arrive repeatedly, violent rains would arise repeatedly, Heaven and Earth and the four seasons would fail to preserve one another, and, having lost accord with the Way, life would be cut off and extinguished before its time. Only the sage follows the Way of Heaven and Earth; hence his body suffers no strange illness, the myriad things do not lose their due, and the qi of life is never exhausted. To go against the qi of spring causes the lesser yang not to be generated, and the liver qi becomes altered within. To go against the qi of summer causes the greater yang not to grow, and the heart qi becomes hollowed out within. To go against the qi of autumn causes the greater yin not to be gathered in, and the lung qi becomes scorched and full. To go against the qi of winter causes the lesser yin not to be stored, and the kidney qi alone sinks down. Now, yin and yang of the four seasons are the root and foundation of the myriad things. This is why the sage nourishes yang in spring and summer, and nourishes yin in autumn and winter, so as to follow this root; thus he rises and falls together with the myriad things at the gate of growth and development. To go against this root is to hew down this foundation and to ruin one's own true nature.

Thus yin and yang and the four seasons are the beginning and end of the myriad things, the root of death and life. To go against them gives rise to calamity and harm; to follow them prevents severe illness from arising — this is called attaining the Way. The Way is something the sage puts into practice, while the fool merely wears it as an ornament. Following yin and yang brings life; going against them brings death. Following them brings order; going against them brings chaos. To reverse what should be followed is to go against it, and this is called internal obstruction. This is why the sage does not treat illness that has already arisen, but treats illness before it arises; does not bring order to disorder that has already occurred, but brings order before disorder occurs — this is precisely what is meant. For if one only administers medicine after the illness has already formed, or only governs after disorder has already formed, this is like digging a well after one has become thirsty, or casting weapons after the battle has already begun — is this not too late?

素问.title_en: Discourse on How the Generative Qi Communicates with Heaven
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'Since ancient times, communication with Heaven has been the root of life; this root lies in yin and yang. Within Heaven and Earth, within the six directions, the qi of the nine provinces, the nine orifices, the five viscera, and the twelve joints of the limbs — all communicate with the qi of Heaven. It gives birth to the five phases, and its own qi is threefold, the three yin and three yang. Those who repeatedly violate these principles will be injured by evil qi — this is the very root of long life. When the qi of the blue Heaven is pure and tranquil, the will and mind are well ordered; if one accords with this, the yang qi becomes firm, and even though thieving evils may be present, they cannot cause harm — this comes from following the sequence of the seasons. Therefore the sage transmits his essence and spirit, takes in the qi of Heaven, and thereby communicates with spiritual brilliance. If he loses this, then within, the nine orifices become blocked, and without, the flesh and muscles become congested; the defensive qi scatters and dissolves — this is called self-injury, a whittling away of one's qi.'

The yang qi of the body is like Heaven and the sun: if it loses its proper place, one's life is shortened and one's vitality fails to shine forth. Just as the motion of Heaven depends on the brightness of the sun, so does the body's vitality depend on its yang qi. Thus yang qi, by its nature, rises upward and guards the exterior. When affected by cold, the yang qi ought to move like a turning door-pivot; if instead one's rising and resting are as agitated as if startled, the spirit qi will float upward and scatter. When affected by summer-heat, excessive sweating brings panting and gasping; if the person is still, there is much talking; the body feels as if scorched like burning charcoal, and only when sweat is released does the heat disperse. When affected by dampness, the head feels as if wrapped in a cloth; if the damp-heat is not dispelled, the large sinews contract and shorten while the small sinews become slack and lengthen — contraction and shortening cause spasm, while slackness and lengthening cause wilting weakness. When affected by pathogenic qi, swelling occurs; when the four limbs are afflicted in turn, the yang qi becomes exhausted.

When yang qi is overtaxed through vexation and overwork, it becomes overextended; the essence becomes exhausted and accumulates repeatedly, and in summer this causes a person to suffer 'scorching reversal.' The eyes become blind and unable to see, the ears become blocked and unable to hear — the condition collapses like a ruined dam, gushing forth uncontrollably, impossible to stop. When yang qi is affected by great rage, the body's qi becomes cut off, and blood congests in the upper body, causing a person to suffer 'assaulting reversal.' If the sinews are injured and become slack, as though they can no longer contain the body properly, and sweating occurs unevenly on one side, this causes a person to become paralyzed on one side. If sweat is exposed to dampness, boils and heat-rash are produced. Excessive consumption of rich food and fine grains gives rise to large carbuncles in the extremities, striking a person as easily as emptying a vessel. Sweating from exertion, when exposed to wind, and chilled and constricted, produces acne-like eruptions, which when stagnant become boils.

When yang qi is properly refined, it nourishes the spirit; when it is properly soft and pliant, it nourishes the sinews. If the pores' opening and closing lose their proper regulation, cold qi follows and enters, giving rise to severe stooping of the back. When cold sinks into the vessels, it becomes fistula, lingering and lodging in the flesh and interstices. When the qi at the transport points is transformed and becomes attenuated, it is transmitted and becomes excessive fearfulness, and further becomes fright and panic. When the nutritive qi fails to follow its proper course and rebels within the texture of the flesh, this gives rise to welling-abscess and swelling. If the sweat governed by the lung's corporeal soul is not fully discharged, the body becomes weakened and the qi is scorched; the points and transport-holes then close up, and this brings about wind-malaria. Thus wind is the beginning of the hundred diseases. When the body is pure and tranquil, the flesh and interstices remain closed against invasion, so that even in the presence of great winds and severe poisons, one cannot be harmed — this comes from following the sequence of the seasons. Thus when illness lingers long, it is transmitted and transformed into other conditions, and when the upper and lower parts of the body no longer communicate with each other, even a good physician can do nothing. Hence when yang qi accumulates without release, illness leads to death, for the yang qi becomes obstructed; where there is obstruction, one ought to drain it. If proper treatment is not applied swiftly, an unskilled practitioner will only ruin the patient.

Thus, over the course of a single day, the yang qi governs the exterior of the body. At dawn, the human qi is generated; at midday, the yang qi reaches its height; as the sun sets in the west, the yang qi has already become deficient, and the qi gates then close. Therefore, in the evening one should gather in and guard the yang qi, and should not disturb the sinews and bones, nor expose oneself to mist and dew. To act contrary to these three periods of the day will cause the body to become distressed and depleted.

Qibo said: 'Yin stores the essence and rouses it forth repeatedly to respond to yang; yang guards the exterior and secures it firmly. If yin cannot overcome yang, then the pulse flows rapidly and urgently, and when yang qi converges, this results in mania. If yang cannot overcome yin, then the qi of the five viscera contend with one another, and the nine orifices fail to communicate. This is why the sage arranges yin and yang in proper order, so that the sinews and vessels are harmonious, the bones and marrow are firm and solid, and the qi and blood all follow their proper course. When this is so, the interior and exterior are regulated and harmonious, evil qi cannot cause harm, the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, and the qi stands firm as before.'

When wind lodges in the body and overwhelms the qi, the essence is thereby lost, and this is evil injuring the liver. If, in addition, one eats to excess, the sinews and vessels become slack and disordered, and intestinal flux gives rise to hemorrhoids. If one drinks to excess, the qi rebels upward. If one exerts great physical force, the kidney qi is thereby injured, and the great bone of the lower back is thereby damaged. Now, the essential point of yin and yang is this: when yang is dense and secure, it becomes firm. If the two are not in harmony, it is like having spring without autumn, or winter without summer. To bring about their harmony is called the sage's proper measure. Thus if yang is too strong and cannot be kept dense and secure, the yin qi is thereby cut off. When yin is level and balanced and yang is dense and secure, the essence and spirit are well ordered; when yin and yang become separated and severed from each other, the essence qi is thereby exhausted. When the body is affected by exposure to dew and wind, this gives rise to cold and heat disorders.

Thus, if one is injured by wind in spring, the evil qi lingers and lodges within, and this becomes diarrhea with undigested food. If one is injured by summer-heat in summer, in autumn this becomes recurrent malarial fevers. If one is injured by dampness in autumn, qi rebels upward causing cough, and this develops into wilting weakness and reversal. If one is injured by cold in winter, in spring one will certainly develop warm-disease. Thus the qi of the four seasons, in turn, injures the five viscera.

That from which yin essence is generated is rooted in the five flavors; yet the five palaces of yin, the five viscera, can also be injured by the five flavors. Thus, if the flavor sour is consumed to excess, the liver qi becomes overflowing, and the spleen qi is thereby cut off. If the flavor salty is consumed to excess, the qi of the great bones becomes taxed, the muscles are shortened, and the heart qi is repressed. If the flavor sweet is consumed to excess, the heart qi becomes labored and full, the complexion turns black, and the kidney qi loses its balance. If the flavor bitter is consumed to excess, the spleen qi fails to be moistened, and the stomach qi becomes thickened and stagnant. If the flavor pungent is consumed to excess, the sinews and vessels become slack and dissolved, and the essence and spirit are thereby exhausted. Therefore, if one carefully harmonizes the five flavors, the bones will be straight and the sinews supple, the qi and blood will flow freely, and the interstices of the skin will be firm and dense. When this is so, the bone qi will be replete with essence. If one carefully follows the Way according to its proper norms, one will long enjoy the years allotted by Heaven.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the True Words Stored in the Golden Casket
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: 'Heaven has eight winds, and the channels have five winds — what does this mean?' Qibo replied: 'The eight winds give rise to evil qi, which becomes wind within the channels, striking the five viscera, and the evil qi thereby brings forth disease. What is called the overcoming of the four seasons is this: spring overcomes late summer, late summer overcomes winter, winter overcomes summer, summer overcomes autumn, and autumn overcomes spring — this is what is meant by the overcoming relationships among the four seasons. The east wind arises in spring, and its disease is located in the liver, with its transport point at the neck. The south wind arises in summer, and its disease is located in the heart, with its transport point at the chest and rib-side. The west wind arises in autumn, and its disease is located in the lung, with its transport point at the shoulders and back. The north wind arises in winter, and its disease is located in the kidney, with its transport point at the lower back and thighs. The center corresponds to earth, and its disease is located in the spleen, with its transport point at the spine. Thus the qi of spring causes disease located in the head; the qi of summer causes disease located in the viscera; the qi of autumn causes disease located in the shoulders and back; the qi of winter causes disease located in the four limbs. Thus in spring one is prone to nosebleeds and disorders of the nose; in midsummer one is prone to disorders of the chest and rib-sides; in late summer one is prone to diarrhea with undigested food and cold in the middle; in autumn one is prone to wind-malaria; in winter one is prone to impediment and reversal. Thus if in winter one does not disturb the yang qi through excessive exertion, then in spring one will not suffer nosebleeds, nor illness of the neck; in midsummer one will not suffer disorders of the chest and rib-sides; in late summer one will not suffer diarrhea with undigested food and cold in the middle; in autumn one will not suffer wind-malaria; and in winter one will not suffer impediment, reversal, or diarrhea with undigested food, nor abnormal sweating. Now essence is the root and foundation of the body. Hence one who stores the essence well will not suffer warm-disease in spring. If in summer one sweats but the heat is not released, in autumn this will develop into wind-malaria. This is the method of pulse diagnosis for the ordinary healthy person.'

Hence it is said: within yin there is yin, and within yang there is yang. From dawn to midday is the yang of Heaven — the yang within yang. From midday to dusk is also the yang of Heaven — but the yin within yang. From nightfall to the cock's crow is the yin of Heaven — the yin within yin. From the cock's crow to dawn is also the yin of Heaven — but the yang within yin. Thus the human being corresponds to this as well. Now, speaking of yin and yang in the human being: the exterior is yang and the interior is yin. Speaking of yin and yang within the human body: the back is yang and the abdomen is yin. Speaking of yin and yang among the viscera and bowels of the human body: the viscera are yin and the bowels are yang. The five viscera — liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney — are all yin. The six bowels — gallbladder, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, bladder, and triple burner — are all yang. The reason one wishes to know the yin within yin and the yang within yang is this: winter diseases are located in yin, summer diseases are located in yang, spring diseases are located in yin, and autumn diseases are located in yang — in each case one must observe where the disease is located, in order to apply needling or stone therapy accordingly. Thus: the back is yang; the yang within yang is the heart. The back is yang; the yin within yang is the lung. The abdomen is yin; the yin within yin is the kidney. The abdomen is yin; the yang within yin is the liver. The abdomen is yin; the utmost yin within yin is the spleen. All of these are the mutual correspondences of yin and yang, exterior and interior, inner and outer, male and female counterparts, and thus they correspond to the yin and yang of Heaven.

Huangdi said: 'The five viscera correspond to the four seasons — does each have its own particular correspondences?' Qibo said: 'They do. In the east, the color is green-blue; it enters and communicates with the liver, opens into the eyes, and stores its essence in the liver. Its diseases manifest as fright and panic. Its flavor is sour, its category is grass and trees, its domestic animal is the fowl, its grain is wheat. Among the four seasons it corresponds to spring, and above, to the planet Jupiter, the Year Star. This is why the qi of spring is felt in the head. Its note is jue, its number is eight, and thus one knows that its diseases are located in the sinews. Its odor is rank. In the south, the color is red; it enters and communicates with the heart, opens into the ears, and stores its essence in the heart. Hence its diseases are in the five viscera. Its flavor is bitter, its category is fire, its domestic animal is the sheep, its grain is glutinous millet. Among the four seasons it corresponds to summer, and above, to the planet Mars, the Sparkling Deluder. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the vessels. Its note is zhi, its number is seven, and its odor is scorched. In the center, the color is yellow; it enters and communicates with the spleen, opens into the mouth, and stores its essence in the spleen. Hence its diseases are at the root of the tongue. Its flavor is sweet, its category is earth, its domestic animal is the ox, its grain is panicled millet. Among the four seasons it corresponds to late summer, and above, to the planet Saturn, the Quelling Star. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the flesh. Its note is gong, its number is five, and its odor is fragrant. In the west, the color is white; it enters and communicates with the lung, opens into the nose, and stores its essence in the lung. Hence its diseases are in the back. Its flavor is pungent, its category is metal, its domestic animal is the horse, its grain is rice. Among the four seasons it corresponds to autumn, and above, to the planet Venus, the Great White. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the skin and body hair. Its note is shang, its number is nine, and its odor is fishy. In the north, the color is black; it enters and communicates with the kidney, opens into the two lower orifices, and stores its essence in the kidney. Hence its diseases are at the valley junctures of the flesh. Its flavor is salty, its category is water, its domestic animal is the pig, its grain is beans. Among the four seasons it corresponds to winter, and above, to the planet Mercury, the Star of Chen. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the bones. Its note is yu, its number is six, and its odor is putrid. Thus, one who is skilled in reading the pulse carefully examines the five viscera and six bowels, their mutual accordance and opposition, the yin and yang, exterior and interior, and the ordered correspondences of male and female counterparts, and holds this in the mind, uniting the mind with the essence of the teaching. One should not teach this to a person unfit for it, nor transmit it to one who is not genuine — this is what is meant by attaining the Way.'

素问.title_en: Great Treatise on the Correspondences of Yin and Yang to Phenomena
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: Yin and yang are the way of heaven and earth, the fundamental principle of the myriad things, the parents of change and transformation, the root and origin of life and death, the abode of spirit and brightness. In treating disease, one must always seek the root. Thus accumulated yang forms heaven, and accumulated yin forms earth. Yin is tranquil and yang is restless; yang generates, and yin matures; yang kills, and yin stores away. Yang transforms into qi, and yin forms into physical shape. When cold reaches its extreme, it generates heat; when heat reaches its extreme, it generates cold. Cold qi generates turbidity; heat qi generates clarity. When clear qi lies below, it causes undigested diarrhea; when turbid qi lies above, it causes distension and bloating. This is what happens when yin and yang act contrary to their proper places — the reversal and compliance that underlie disease.

Thus clear yang forms heaven, and turbid yin forms earth; the qi of earth rises to become clouds, and the qi of heaven descends to become rain. Rain issues from the qi of earth, and clouds issue from the qi of heaven.

Thus clear yang emerges from the upper orifices, and turbid yin emerges from the lower orifices; clear yang issues forth at the interstitial spaces of the skin, and turbid yin moves through the five viscera; clear yang fills and strengthens the four limbs, and turbid yin returns to the six bowels.

Water is yin, and fire is yang. Yang is qi, and yin is flavor. Flavor nourishes physical form, physical form is sustained by qi, qi is generated from essence, and essence arises through transformation. Essence is nourished by qi, and physical form is nourished by flavor; transformation generates essence, and qi generates physical form. Flavor, if excessive, injures physical form; qi, if excessive, injures essence. Essence transforms into qi, and qi may in turn be injured by flavor. Yin flavors exit through the lower orifices, and yang qi exits through the upper orifices. Flavors that are strong belong to yin; those that are mild belong to the yang aspect of yin. Qi that is strong belongs to yang; that which is mild belongs to the yin aspect of yang. Strong flavor causes purging; mild flavor allows free passage. Mild qi causes venting and dispersal; strong qi generates heat. The qi of vigorous fire weakens, while the qi of mild fire flourishes. Vigorous fire consumes qi, and qi is nourished by mild fire. Vigorous fire disperses qi, while mild fire generates qi. As for qi and flavor: the acrid and the sweet, which disperse outward, belong to yang; the sour and the bitter, which induce vomiting and purging, belong to yin.

When yin prevails, yang becomes diseased; when yang prevails, yin becomes diseased. When yang prevails, there is heat; when yin prevails, there is cold. Extreme cold generates heat; extreme heat generates cold. Cold injures physical form; heat injures qi. Injury to qi causes pain; injury to physical form causes swelling. Thus, when pain precedes swelling, it means qi has injured the physical form; when swelling precedes pain, it means the physical form has injured qi. When wind prevails, there is movement and tremor; when heat prevails, there is swelling; when dryness prevails, there is desiccation; when cold prevails, there is puffiness and edema; when dampness prevails, there is diarrhea with loose, watery stool.

Heaven has the four seasons and the five phases, by which it generates, grows, gathers in, and stores away, and by which it produces cold, summer heat, dryness, dampness, and wind. Man has the five viscera, which transform into five qi, giving rise to joy, anger, sorrow, worry, and fear. Thus joy and anger injure qi, while cold and summer heat injure the physical form. Sudden rage injures yin; sudden joy injures yang. When rebellious qi surges upward, it fills the vessels and departs from the physical form. If joy and anger are not moderated, and cold and heat exceed their proper bounds, then life cannot be secured. Thus extreme yin will inevitably turn into yang, and extreme yang will inevitably turn into yin. Hence it is said: if one is injured by cold in winter, one will certainly suffer a warm disease in spring; if injured by wind in spring, one will develop undigested diarrhea in summer; if injured by summer heat in summer, one will certainly suffer intermittent fevers in autumn; if injured by dampness in autumn, one will in time develop cough.

Huangdi said: I have heard that the sages of high antiquity, in discussing and ordering the human body, distinguished and classified the viscera and bowels, traced and connected the channels and vessels, joined and linked the six confluences, each following its own channel; the qi-points that arise each have their own location and name; the valleys and ravines belonging to the bones each have their own point of origin; the divisions and regions, whether contrary or compliant, each have their own order and pattern; the yin and yang of the four seasons all have their governing sequence; and the correspondences between outer and inner all have their exterior and interior aspects. Is this truly so? Qibo replied: The east generates wind; wind generates wood; wood generates the sour flavor; the sour flavor generates the liver; the liver generates the sinews; the sinews generate the heart; the liver governs the eyes. In heaven this is the mysterious and profound; in man it is the Way; on earth it is transformation. Transformation generates the five flavors; the Way generates wisdom; the mysterious and profound generates spirit. Spirit in heaven is wind, on earth is wood, in the body is the sinews, in the viscera is the liver, in color is verdant green, in musical note is jue, in the vocal sound is shouting, in movement is grasping, in the orifice is the eyes, in flavor is sour, in emotion is anger. Anger injures the liver; sorrow overcomes anger. Wind injures the sinews; dryness overcomes wind. Sour flavor injures the sinews; the acrid flavor overcomes the sour. The south generates heat; heat generates fire; fire generates the bitter flavor; the bitter flavor generates the heart; the heart generates blood; blood generates the spleen; the heart governs the tongue. In heaven this is heat, on earth is fire, in the body is the vessels, in the viscera is the heart, in color is red, in musical note is zhi, in the vocal sound is laughter, in movement is anxious agitation, in the orifice is the tongue, in flavor is bitter, in emotion is joy. Joy injures the heart; fear overcomes joy. Heat injures qi; cold overcomes heat. Bitter flavor injures qi; the salty flavor overcomes the bitter. The center generates dampness; dampness generates earth; earth generates the sweet flavor; the sweet flavor generates the spleen; the spleen generates flesh; flesh generates the lung; the spleen governs the mouth. In heaven this is dampness, on earth is earth, in the body is the flesh, in the viscera is the spleen, in color is yellow, in musical note is gong, in the vocal sound is singing, in movement is retching, in the orifice is the mouth, in flavor is sweet, in emotion is pensiveness. Pensiveness injures the spleen; anger overcomes pensiveness. Dampness injures the flesh; wind overcomes dampness. Sweet flavor injures the flesh; the sour flavor overcomes the sweet. The west generates dryness; dryness generates metal; metal generates the acrid flavor; the acrid flavor generates the lung; the lung generates the skin and body hair; the skin and body hair generate the kidney; the lung governs the nose. In heaven this is dryness, on earth is metal, in the body is the skin and body hair, in the viscera is the lung, in color is white, in musical note is shang, in the vocal sound is weeping, in movement is coughing, in the orifice is the nose, in flavor is acrid, in emotion is worry. Worry injures the lung; joy overcomes worry. Heat injures the skin and body hair; cold overcomes heat. Acrid flavor injures the skin and body hair; the bitter flavor overcomes the acrid. The north generates cold; cold generates water; water generates the salty flavor; the salty flavor generates the kidney; the kidney generates the bone marrow; the marrow generates the liver; the kidney governs the ears. In heaven this is cold, on earth is water, in the body is the bones, in the viscera is the kidney, in color is black, in musical note is yu, in the vocal sound is groaning, in movement is shivering, in the orifice is the ears, in flavor is salty, in emotion is fear. Fear injures the kidney; pensiveness overcomes fear. Cold injures the blood; dryness overcomes cold. Salty flavor injures the blood; the sweet flavor overcomes the salty. Hence it is said: heaven and earth are the above and below of the myriad things; yin and yang are the male and female of blood and qi; left and right are the pathways of yin and yang; water and fire are the manifest signs of yin and yang; yin and yang are the capacity and origin of the myriad things. Hence it is said: yin, dwelling within, is that which yang guards and preserves; yang, dwelling without, is that which yin puts to use and directs.

Huangdi said: How does one take yin and yang as the model? Qibo said: When yang prevails, the body is hot, the interstitial spaces of the skin are closed, breathing is coarse and labored, causing one to bend forward and back; sweat does not come out, and heat persists; the teeth become dry, and there is vexation, oppression, and abdominal fullness — this is fatal. Such a person can endure winter but not summer. When yin prevails, the body is cold and sweats, the body is habitually cool, one shivers repeatedly and feels cold; when cold reaches an extreme it becomes reversal, and reversal causes abdominal fullness — this is fatal. Such a person can endure summer but not winter. These are the transformations that occur when yin and yang alternately prevail — the manifest forms and states of disease.

Huangdi said: How does one harmonize these two? Qibo said: If one is able to understand the seven depletions and eight benefits, then the two can be harmonized; if one does not know how to apply this, then the season of early decline sets in. At forty years of age, one's yin qi is already reduced by half, and daily activity begins to decline. At fifty, the body feels heavy, and the ears and eyes are no longer sharp and clear. At sixty, yin withers, qi greatly declines, the nine orifices no longer function smoothly, there is deficiency below and excess above, and tears and mucus flow together. Hence it is said: those who understand this remain strong, and those who do not grow old — though they issue from the same source, they come to differ in name. The wise observe what is common; the foolish observe what is different. The foolish are insufficient; the wise have surplus. When there is surplus, the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, the body is light and strong, the old are restored to vigor, and the vigorous become even more sound. This is why the sage attends to the affairs of non-action, delights in the capacity for calm and contentment, follows his desires and pleases his intent within the guarding of emptiness and nothingness — and thus his years and life are without limit, ending only together with heaven and earth. This is how the sage cultivates the body.

Heaven is insufficient in the northwest, hence the northwest is yin, and in man the right ear and eye are not as sharp as the left. Earth is not full in the southeast, hence the southeast is yang, and in man the left hand and foot are not as strong as the right. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: The east is yang; in yang, its essence gathers above — gathering above, the upper part is bright and clear while the lower part is deficient, and so the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, but the hands and feet are less agile. The west is yin; in yin, its essence gathers below — gathering below, the lower part is abundant while the upper part is deficient, and so the ears and eyes are not sharp and clear, but the hands and feet are agile. Thus when both are affected by pathogenic influence, if it is above the right side is more severely affected, if it is below the left side is more severely affected — this is because heaven and earth, and yin and yang, cannot themselves be entirely complete, and so pathogenic qi lodges in the incomplete places. Thus heaven possesses essence, and earth possesses physical form; heaven has its eight guiding sequences, and earth has its five patterns, and so they are able to serve as the parents of the myriad things. Clear yang ascends to heaven, and turbid yin returns to earth; thus the movement and stillness of heaven and earth are governed by spirit and brightness as their guiding principle, and so they are able to generate, grow, gather in, and store, ending only to begin again. Only the worthy person, above, aligns with heaven to nourish the head; below, models earth to nourish the feet; and in the middle, draws on human affairs to nourish the five viscera. The qi of heaven communicates with the lung; the qi of earth communicates with the throat; the qi of wind communicates with the liver; the qi of thunder communicates with the heart; the qi of grain communicates with the spleen; the qi of rain communicates with the kidney. The six channels are like rivers; the intestines and stomach are like the sea; the nine orifices are the qi through which water pours forth. Taking heaven and earth as yin and yang: the sweat of yang is named after the rain of heaven and earth; the qi of yang is named after the swift wind of heaven and earth. Sudden, violent qi resembles thunder; rebellious qi resembles yang. Thus, if in treatment one does not model the guiding sequences of heaven and does not apply the principles of earth, calamity and harm will arrive.

Thus when pathogenic wind arrives, it is as swift as wind and rain. Therefore the skilled physician treats the skin and body hair; the next best treats the muscles and flesh; the next treats the sinews and vessels; the next treats the six bowels; and the next treats the five viscera. One who must treat the five viscera has an even chance of life and death. Thus the pathogenic qi of heaven, if it invades, harms the person's five viscera; the cold and heat of food and drink, if they invade, harm the six bowels; the damp qi of earth, if it invades, harms the skin, flesh, sinews, and vessels. Therefore the physician skilled in the use of needles draws yang from yin and draws yin from yang, treats the left to address the right and treats the right to address the left, uses himself to understand the other, uses the exterior to know the interior, and observes the principle of excess and insufficiency; by perceiving the subtle he grasps the fault, and so his use of the needle is never dangerous. The physician skilled in diagnosis examines the complexion and palpates the pulse, first distinguishing yin and yang; he discerns clarity and turbidity, and thereby knows the region of disease; he observes the breathing and listens to the voice, and thereby knows the source of suffering; he observes the seasonal pulse standards, and thereby knows what governs the disease. He presses the cubit and inch pulse positions, observes whether the pulse is floating, sunken, slippery, or rough, and thereby knows where the disease arises; in treating, he makes no error, and in diagnosing, he loses nothing. Hence it is said: when a disease first arises, it can be resolved by needling alone; when it is at its height, one must wait for it to wane before it can be resolved. Thus, according to its lightness, disperse it upward and outward; according to its severity, diminish it; according to its decline, restore and make it manifest again. When the physical form is insufficient, warm it with qi; when essence is insufficient, supplement it with flavor. When the disease is high in the body, induce vomiting to expel it; when it is low, draw it out and drain it away; when there is fullness in the middle, purge it from within; when there is pathogenic qi present, soak the body to induce sweating; when it is in the skin, sweat it out and disperse it; when it is fierce and swift, press and restrain it; when it is excess, disperse and drain it. Examine the yin and yang to distinguish the soft from the hard: treat yang disease through yin, and yin disease through yang; fix the blood and qi so that each keeps to its own place. When blood is in excess, one should drain it off; when qi is deficient, one should guide and lead it.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Separation and Union of Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that heaven is yang and earth is yin, the sun is yang and the moon is yin; that the greater and lesser months together make three hundred sixty days forming one year, and that man corresponds to this as well. Now, the three yin and three yang do not seem to correspond simply to yin and yang — what is the reason for this? Qibo replied: Yin and yang can be counted to ten, extended to a hundred, counted to a thousand, extended to ten thousand; and beyond ten thousand they become too great to be counted in full — yet their essential principle is one. Heaven covers and earth supports, and the myriad things are thereby born. That which has not yet emerged from the earth is called dwelling in yin, and is termed yin within yin; that which has emerged from the earth is termed yang within yin. Yang bestows uprightness, and yin serves as its master. Thus birth depends on spring, growth depends on summer, gathering-in depends on autumn, and storage depends on winter; when this normal order is lost, the four directions of heaven and earth become obstructed. The transformations of yin and yang, as they occur in man, can likewise be counted and enumerated.

Huangdi said: I would like to hear about the separation and union of the three yin and three yang. Qibo said: When the sage stands facing south, the front is called Vast Brightness, and the rear is called the Great Thoroughfare. The region of the Great Thoroughfare is called Shaoyin (Lesser Yin); above Shaoyin is called Taiyang (Greater Yang). Taiyang is rooted at the point Zhiyin and knots at the Gate of Life; it is termed yang within yin. Above the midpoint of the body is called Vast Brightness; below Vast Brightness is called Taiyin (Greater Yin); in front of Taiyin is called Yangming (Yang Brightness). Yangming is rooted at the point Lidui, and is termed yang within yin. The exterior aspect of Jueyin (Reverting Yin) is called Shaoyang (Lesser Yang); Shaoyang is rooted at the point Qiaoyin, and is termed the lesser yang within yin. Thus, in the separation and union of the three yang: Taiyang is the opening, Yangming is the closing, and Shaoyang is the pivot. These three channels must not lose their proper relation to one another; when they beat together without floating apart, this is called the unity of yang.

Huangdi said: I would like to hear about the three yin. Qibo said: The exterior is yang, and the interior is yin; thus the interior belongs to yin, and its thoroughfare lies below, called Taiyin (Greater Yin). Taiyin is rooted at the point Yinbai, and is termed yin within yin. Behind Taiyin is called Shaoyin (Lesser Yin); Shaoyin is rooted at the point Yongquan, and is termed the lesser yin within yin. In front of Shaoyin is called Jueyin (Reverting Yin); Jueyin is rooted at the point Dadun — where yin is cut off from yang — and is termed the utmost yin within yin. Thus, in the separation and union of the three yin: Taiyin is the opening, Jueyin is the closing, and Shaoyin is the pivot. These three channels must not lose their proper relation to one another; when they beat together without sinking away, this is called the unity of yin. Yin and yang flow on unceasingly, accumulating and transmitting to complete one full cycle; qi governs the interior, and physical form governs the exterior, and together they bring each other to completion.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Differentiation of Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Man has four channels and twelve followers — what does this mean? Qibo replied: The four channels correspond to the four seasons, and the twelve followers correspond to the twelve months; the twelve months correspond to the twelve vessels. The pulse has yin and yang; one who knows yang knows yin, and one who knows yin knows yang. In yang alone there are five types, and five times five makes twenty-five yang manifestations. What is called the yin pulse is the true visceral pulse; when it appears nakedly, it signals defeat, and defeat inevitably means death. What is called the yang pulse is the yang of the stomach cavity, a pulse still carrying stomach qi. By distinguishing yang, one knows the location of the disease; by distinguishing yin, one knows the appointed time of death or life. The three yang are manifest at the head, and the three yin are manifest at the wrist — these are, in fact, one and the same principle. By distinguishing yang, one knows the times to be wary of in disease; by distinguishing yin, one knows the appointed time of death or life. One must carefully master yin and yang, and need not consult with the multitude. As for yin and yang in the pulse: that which recedes is yin, and that which arrives is yang; that which is still is yin, and that which moves is yang; that which is slow is yin, and that which is rapid is yang. Whenever one grasps the true visceral pulse, devoid of stomach qi: if the liver pulse arrives suspended, cut off, and tense, death comes in eighteen days; if the heart pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in nine days; if the lung pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in twelve days; if the kidney pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in seven days; if the spleen pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in four days.

It is said: when disease arises in the two yang, it manifests in the heart and spleen; there may be difficulty with private, hidden matters, and in women, absence of menstruation. If it progresses, it becomes wind-wasting; if it progresses further, it becomes panting-rushing — this is fatal and cannot be treated. It is said: when disease arises in the three yang, it manifests as chills and fever, and below as carbuncles and swellings, as well as wasting, reversal, and calf pain and numbness. If it progresses, it becomes a withering and loss of luster of the skin; if it progresses further, it becomes hernial swelling. It is said: when disease arises in the one yang, there is shortness of breath, frequent coughing, and frequent diarrhea. If it progresses, it becomes a clutching sensation in the heart; if it progresses further, it becomes obstruction. When disease arises in the two yang and one yin together, it chiefly causes fright, alarm, and back pain, frequent belching and yawning — this is called wind reversal. When disease arises in the two yin and one yang together, there is frequent distension, fullness in the heart region, and frequent belching. When disease arises in the three yang and three yin together, it causes hemiplegia, wasting and flaccidity, and inability to raise the four limbs.

When the pulse beats as one yang, it is called hook-like; when it beats as one yin, it is called downy; when the yang pulse beats forcefully and tense, it is called string-like; when the yang pulse arrives and then abruptly cuts off, it is called stone-like; when yin and yang pulses pass into one another smoothly, it is called flowing. When yin contends within and yang is disturbed without, the ethereal-soul sweat is not contained, the four limbs become cold and reversal arises; when this reversal arises it smokes and steams the lung, causing the person to pant and wheeze. That which yin generates, when harmonized with its root, is called harmony. Thus, when hardness meets hardness, the yang qi is shattered and dispersed, and the yin qi thereupon perishes. When it becomes turbid and disordered, the hard and the soft fail to harmonize, and the qi of the channels is thereupon cut off. Those of the category of dying yin die within no more than three days; those of the category of living yang die within no more than four days. As for what is called living yang and dying yin: transmission from the liver to the heart is called living yang; from the heart to the lung is called dying yin; from the lung to the kidney is called double yin; from the kidney to the spleen is called overturning yin, which is fatal and cannot be treated. Binding in the yang causes swelling of the four limbs. Binding in the yin causes bloody stool of one sheng; a second binding, two sheng; a third binding, three sheng. When yin and yang bind and become skewed, with more yin and less yang, this is called stone water, with swelling of the lower abdomen. Binding of the two yang is called wasting-thirst. Binding of the three yang is called diaphragm obstruction. Binding of the three yin is called water. Binding of one yin and one yang together is called throat blockage. When the yin pulse beats and the yang pulse is distinct and separate, this indicates pregnancy. When yin and yang are both deficient, and there is diarrhea with prolapse, it is fatal. When yang is added onto yin, this is called sweating. When yin is deficient and yang beats forcefully, this is called flooding. When the three yin all beat together, death occurs at midnight on the twentieth day. When the two yin both beat together, death occurs in the evening of the thirteenth day. When the one yin beats alone, death occurs on the tenth day. When the three yang all beat together and drum forcefully, death occurs on the third day. When the three yin and three yang all beat together, there is fullness of the heart and abdomen; if the eruption is complete and private, hidden functions cannot be attained, death occurs on the fifth day. When the two yang both beat together, and the disease is a warm disease, it is fatal and cannot be treated — death occurs within no more than ten days.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Secret Canon of the Numinous Orchid Chamber
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I would like to hear about the mutual offices and relative rank of the twelve viscera — which are noble and which are lowly? Qibo replied: How thorough a question this is! Allow me to explain it fully. The heart is the office of the sovereign ruler; spirit and brightness issue from it. The lung is the office of the minister and mentor; regulation and moderation issue from it. The liver is the office of the general; planning and deliberation issue from it. The gallbladder is the office of impartial judgment; decision and decisiveness issue from it. The pericardium is the office of the minister-messenger; joy and delight issue from it. The spleen and stomach are the offices of the granary and storehouse; the five flavors issue from them. The large intestine is the office of transmission and conduction; transformation and change issue from it. The small intestine is the office of reception and abundance; the transformation of things issues from it. The kidney is the office of vigor and strength; skill and ingenuity issue from it. The triple burner is the office of the drainage channels; the waterways issue from it. The bladder is the office of the regional capital; the body fluids are stored there, and only through the transformation of qi can they be released.

In all, these twelve offices must not lose their proper relation to one another. Thus, when the sovereign is enlightened, those below are at peace; if one nurtures life on this basis, one will live long, free from danger to the end of one's days, and if one governs the empire on this basis, it will greatly flourish. When the sovereign is not enlightened, the twelve offices fall into danger, the pathways of communication become blocked and impassable, and the physical form is thereby gravely injured; if one nurtures life on this basis, calamity follows, and if one governs the empire on this basis, its very foundation is gravely imperiled. Be warned of this, be warned of this!

The utmost Way lies in the subtle and minute, and its transformations are without end — who can know its origin? How constrained and pressing it is! One who seeks to grasp it must be watchful and vigilant — who can know its essentials? Amid such deep and diligent concern, who is truly skilled? The reckoning of the vague and indistinct arises from the minutest hair's breadth; the reckoning of the hair's breadth arises from measurement; multiplied a thousand-fold, ten-thousand-fold, it may be extended ever greater, and by extending and enlarging it, its concrete form comes to be established. Huangdi said: Excellent indeed! I have heard that the Way of refined brightness and the enterprise of the great sages, in proclaiming and illuminating the great Way, is not to be received save through fasting, purification, and the choosing of an auspicious day. Huangdi thereupon chose an auspicious day and favorable omen, and stored this teaching in the Chamber of the Numinous Orchid, to be handed down and preserved.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Six Sections and Visceral Manifestations
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that heaven forms a year through six periods of six, while man corresponds to this through the framework of nine-nine, and that man likewise has three hundred sixty-five joints, matching heaven and earth from ancient times — yet I do not understand what this means. Qibo replied: What an illuminating question! Let me explain it fully. The six-six periods and the nine-nine correspondence are the means by which the measures of heaven and the numbers of qi are made correct. The measures of heaven govern the movements of the sun and moon; the numbers of qi record the workings of transformation and generation.

Heaven is yang and earth is yin; the sun is yang and the moon is yin. Their movements have divisions and order, their cycles have paths and patterns. The sun travels one degree each day, the moon travels thirteen degrees and a fraction more, so that through the alternation of long and short months, three hundred sixty-five days make up a year, and the accumulated surplus of qi fills out into an intercalary month. One establishes the starting point at the beginning, corrects the markers at the midpoint, and calculates the remainder at the end, and thus the measures of heaven are complete.

Huangdi said: I have now heard of the measures of heaven; I wish to hear how the numbers of qi correspond to them. Qibo said: Heaven takes six-six as its period, earth takes nine-nine as its framework of correspondence. Heaven has ten stems; when the stems have cycled six times, a sexagenary cycle is complete; when the sexagenary cycle repeats six times, the year is complete — this is the rule of three hundred sixty days. From ancient times, that which connects with heaven is the root of life, and this root lies in yin and yang. The qi of the nine provinces and the nine orifices of the body all connect with the qi of heaven. Thus it generates five, and its qi is three; three completes heaven, three completes earth, three completes man; three multiplied by three makes nine; nine divides into nine fields, and the nine fields correspond to the nine viscera. Hence there are four visceral forms and five spirit viscera, together constituting the nine viscera that correspond to heaven and earth.

Huangdi said: I have now heard of the correspondence of six-six and nine-nine. You have spoken of accumulated qi filling out into intercalation; I wish to hear what is meant by qi. Please, Master, dispel my ignorance and resolve my doubts. Qibo said: This was kept secret by the high sovereigns of antiquity and handed down by former teachers. Huangdi said: Please let me hear it fully. Qibo said: Five days are called a watch; three watches are called a qi-period; six qi-periods are called a season; four seasons are called a year, and each governs its own rule. The five movements succeed one another and each in turn governs; when the cycle of the year is complete, it turns and begins again — the seasons are established and the qi is distributed like a ring without end, and the watches follow the same pattern. Hence it is said: one who does not know what is added to the year, the flourishing and decline of qi, and where deficiency and excess arise, cannot be considered a skilled physician.

Huangdi said: The beginnings of the five movements are like a ring without end — what of their excess and insufficiency? Qibo said: The five qi arise in succession, each having what it overcomes; the changes of flourishing and deficiency are their constant pattern. Huangdi said: What is a balanced qi? Qibo said: It is one without excess. Huangdi said: What of excess and insufficiency? Qibo said: These are recorded in the classics. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'what it overcomes'? Qibo said: Spring overcomes late summer, late summer overcomes winter, winter overcomes summer, summer overcomes autumn, autumn overcomes spring — this is called the mutual overcoming of the seasons according to the five phases, each naming its viscus by its qi. Huangdi said: How does one know which overcomes? Qibo said: One seeks its arrival, always returning to the start of spring. If it arrives before its time, this is called excess; it then oppresses what it cannot overcome and rides roughshod over what it overcomes — this is called excessive qi. One cannot then distinguish whether evil and deviance arise internally, and the physician cannot prevent it. If it has not arrived by its time, this is called insufficiency; then what it normally overcomes acts recklessly, and that which it generates suffers illness, while that which it cannot overcome oppresses it — this is called qi under duress. What is meant by 'seeking its arrival' is the time when the qi arrives. If one carefully watches for that time, the qi's arrival can be anticipated; but if one misses the time and fails to observe correctly, the five modes of treatment cannot be distinguished, and evil and deviance arise internally, which the physician cannot prevent. Huangdi said: Can it ever fail to follow this succession? Qibo said: The qi of the blue heaven cannot be without constancy; when the qi fails to follow its succession, this is called abnormal, and when it is abnormal, change occurs. Huangdi said: When it is abnormal and changes, what then? Qibo said: When change arrives, sickness occupies its place; if it is what it overcomes, the illness is mild; if it is what it cannot overcome, the illness is severe; and if compounded further by being struck again by evil qi, death results. Hence, if it is not the proper time, the illness is mild; if it is the proper time, the illness is severe.

Huangdi said: Good. I have heard that when qi combines it gives rise to form, and that through its transformations one establishes its proper name. Regarding the movements of heaven and earth and the transformations of yin and yang, in relation to the myriad things, which are few and which are many — may I hear of this? Qibo said: What a thorough question! Heaven is so vast it cannot be measured, earth is so great it cannot be gauged. Since Your Majesty's spirit-like inquiry reaches so far, let me set forth its general pattern. Plants produce the five colors, and the transformations of the five colors cannot all be seen; plants produce the five flavors, and the excellence of the five flavors cannot be exhausted. People's cravings and desires differ, each connecting with what suits it. Heaven feeds man with the five qi, earth feeds man with the five flavors. The five qi enter through the nose and are stored in the heart and lungs, causing the five colors above to become clear and lustrous and the voice to become resonant. The five flavors enter through the mouth and are stored in the intestines and stomach; the flavors are stored so as to nourish the five qi. When the qi is harmonious, fluid and humor are thereby generated, and the spirit then arises of itself.

Huangdi said: What of the manifestations of the viscera? Qibo said: The heart is the root of life, the seat of the transformations of spirit; its bloom is in the face, its fullness is in the blood vessels; it is the greater yang within yang, and it connects with the qi of summer. The lung is the root of qi, the dwelling place of the corporeal soul; its bloom is in the body hair, its fullness is in the skin; it is the lesser yin within yang, and it connects with the qi of autumn. The kidney governs hibernation and storage, and is the root of sealing and storing, the dwelling place of essence; its bloom is in the hair of the head, its fullness is in the bones; it is the lesser yin within yin, and it connects with the qi of winter. The liver is the root of endurance against exhaustion, the residence of the ethereal soul; its bloom is in the nails, its fullness is in the sinews; it generates blood and qi; its flavor is sour, its color is blue-green; it is the lesser yang within yang, and it connects with the qi of spring. The spleen, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, triple burner, and bladder are the root of the granary and storehouse, the residence of the nutritive qi; they are called vessels, able to transform waste matter, turning over flavors so that they pass in and out; their bloom is in the four whites around the lips, their fullness is in the flesh; their flavor is sweet, their color is yellow; these belong to the category of utmost yin, connecting with the qi of earth. In all, the function of the eleven viscera depends upon the gallbladder.

Therefore, when the Renying pulse at the neck is one degree stronger than normal, the disease is in the lesser yang; when two degrees stronger, the disease is in the greater yang; when three degrees stronger, the disease is in the yang brightness; when four degrees or more, this is called blocked yang. When the Cunkou pulse at the wrist is one degree stronger, the disease is in the reverting yin; when two degrees stronger, the disease is in the lesser yin; when three degrees stronger, the disease is in the greater yin; when four degrees or more, this is called barred yin. When both the Renying and Cunkou pulses are four times stronger than normal, this is called barred-and-blocked; when the pulse of barred-and-blocked becomes excessive, it can no longer reach the utmost essential qi of heaven and earth, and death follows.

素问.title_en: On the Generation and Formation of the Five Viscera
paragraphs_en: The heart pairs with the blood vessels; its bloom shows in the complexion; it is governed by the kidney. The lung pairs with the skin; its bloom shows in the body hair; it is governed by the heart. The liver pairs with the sinews; its bloom shows in the nails; it is governed by the lung. The spleen pairs with the flesh; its bloom shows in the lips; it is governed by the liver. The kidney pairs with the bones; its bloom shows in the hair of the head; it is governed by the spleen.

Therefore, eating too much salty food causes the blood vessels to congeal and stagnate, changing the complexion; eating too much bitter food causes the skin to wither and the body hair to fall out; eating too much pungent food causes the sinews to tighten and the nails to wither; eating too much sour food causes the flesh to become calloused and thick and the lips to turn outward and crack; eating too much sweet food causes the bones to ache and the hair to fall out — these are the injuries caused by the five flavors. Thus the heart desires bitter, the lung desires pungent, the liver desires sour, the spleen desires sweet, and the kidney desires salty — these are the correspondences of the five flavors to the five viscera. As for the qi of the five viscera: if the complexion appears as green as dead grass, death follows; as yellow as the fruit of the trifoliate orange, death follows; as black as soot, death follows; as red as clotted blood, death follows; as white as dried bone, death follows — these are the five colors that indicate death. If the complexion is as green as a kingfisher's feathers, life is indicated; as red as a cockscomb, life is indicated; as yellow as a crab's belly, life is indicated; as white as pig fat, life is indicated; as black as a crow's feathers, life is indicated — these are the five colors that indicate life. A healthy complexion generated from the heart is like vermilion wrapped in white silk; generated from the lung is like crimson wrapped in white silk; generated from the liver is like dark blue-green wrapped in white silk; generated from the spleen is like the fruit of the trichosanthes wrapped in white silk; generated from the kidney is like purple wrapped in white silk — these are the outward blooms produced by the five viscera. Colors and flavors correspond to the five viscera: white corresponds to the lung and to pungent flavor; red corresponds to the heart and to bitter flavor; green corresponds to the liver and to sour flavor; yellow corresponds to the spleen and to sweet flavor; black corresponds to the kidney and to salty flavor. Likewise, white corresponds to the skin, red to the blood vessels, green to the sinews, yellow to the flesh, and black to the bones.

All the blood vessels belong to the eyes; all the marrow belongs to the brain; all the sinews belong to the joints; all the blood belongs to the heart; all the qi belongs to the lung — these govern the rising and settling of qi and blood through the four limbs and the eight great junctures. Thus when a person lies down, the blood returns to the liver; when the liver receives blood, the eyes are able to see; when the feet receive blood, they are able to walk; when the palms receive blood, they are able to grasp; when the fingers receive blood, they are able to hold. If, upon rising from lying down, one is struck by wind, blood that congeals in the skin causes numbness; blood that congeals in the vessels causes stagnation; blood that congeals in the feet causes cold reversal. In these three cases, the blood moves but cannot return to its proper cavity, and thus numbness and cold reversal occur. The human body has twelve great valleys and three hundred fifty-four small ravines, minus twelve transport points — these are all places where defensive qi lodges and where evil qi may take up residence; needles and stone lancets should be applied along these to remove it.

In diagnosing the onset of disease, the five determinations serve as the guiding principle. To know the origin of a disease, one must first establish its root — what is called the five determinations are the five pulses. Thus, in headache and disorders of the vertex, with deficiency below and excess above, the fault lies in the foot lesser yin; if severe, it enters the greater yang. In dizziness, spinning, staggering, blurred vision, and deafness, with excess below and deficiency above, the fault lies in the foot lesser yang; if severe, it enters the reverting yin. In abdominal fullness and distension, with support failing at the diaphragm and flanks, with reversal below and clouding above, the fault lies in the foot greater yin and yang brightness. In coughing and rising qi, with reversal in the chest, the fault lies in the hand yang brightness and greater yin. In vexation of the heart and headache, with the disease located within the diaphragm, the fault lies in the hand greater yang and lesser yin.

As for the pulse's smallness or largeness, smoothness or roughness, floating or sinking quality — these can be distinguished by the fingers; the manifestations of the five viscera can be inferred by category; the correspondence of the five viscera to the five tones can be understood by intuition; and subtle diagnosis by the five colors can be observed with the eyes. One who can combine examination of the pulse and the complexion can achieve complete accuracy. When the red pulse arrives panting and hard, the diagnosis is: there is accumulated qi within, which at times interferes with eating — this is called heart-numbness, acquired from external illness, from worry and overthinking that empties the heart, so that evil qi follows and enters. When the white pulse arrives panting and floating, with deficiency above and excess below, there is fright and accumulated qi in the chest, panting with deficiency — this is called lung-numbness with cold and heat, acquired from intoxication followed by sexual intercourse. When the blue-green pulse arrives long, twanging left and right like a plucked string, there is accumulated qi below the heart supporting the flanks — this is called liver-numbness, acquired from cold and dampness, following the same pattern as hernia, with lower back pain, cold feet, and headache. When the yellow pulse arrives large and empty, there is accumulated qi in the abdomen and reversal qi called reversal-hernia — women follow the same pattern — acquired from exerting the four limbs to the point of sweating and then being struck by wind. When the black pulse arrives hard above and large, there is accumulated qi in the lower abdomen and genital region — this is called kidney-numbness, acquired from bathing in cold water and then lying down.

In general, when observing the five colors together with an unusual pulse: a yellow face with green eyes, a yellow face with red eyes, a yellow face with white eyes, or a yellow face with black eyes — in all these cases the patient will not die. A green face with red eyes, a red face with white eyes, a green face with black eyes, a black face with white eyes, or a red face with green eyes — in all these cases the patient will die.

素问.title_en: Further Discourse on the Five Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that some practitioners of the arts consider the brain and marrow to be viscera, while others consider the intestines and stomach to be viscera, and still others consider them to be bowels. Daring to ask — these views contradict one another, yet each claims to be correct, and I do not understand the reasoning behind them. I wish to hear your explanation. Qibo replied: The brain, the marrow, the bones, the blood vessels, the gallbladder, and the uterus — these six are generated by the qi of earth; they all store yin and take earth as their model, and so they store without discharging; they are called the extraordinary bowels. As for the stomach, large intestine, small intestine, triple burner, and bladder — these five are generated by the qi of heaven; their qi resembles heaven, and so they discharge without storing. These receive the turbid qi of the five viscera and are called the bowels of conveyance and transformation; they cannot retain their contents for long, but convey and discharge them. The anus also serves the five viscera, ensuring that water and grain are not retained for long. What are called the five viscera store essential qi without discharging it, and so they may be full but cannot be replete. The six bowels convey and transform substances without storing them, and so they may be replete but cannot be full. The reason for this is: when water and grain enter the mouth, the stomach becomes replete while the intestines remain empty; when the food descends, the intestines become replete while the stomach becomes empty. Hence it is said: replete but not full, full but not replete.

Huangdi said: Why is the qi opening at the wrist alone able to serve as the governor of the five viscera? Qibo said: The stomach is the sea of water and grain, the great source of the six bowels. The five flavors enter the mouth and are stored in the stomach, which nourishes the qi of the five viscera. The qi opening is also connected to the greater yin. Thus the qi and flavor of the five viscera and six bowels all issue from the stomach and manifest as changes at the qi opening. Therefore, the five qi enter through the nose and are stored in the heart and lung; when the heart and lung are diseased, the nose in turn becomes obstructed.
