When yang joins with yin, yin becomes excess and yang becomes deficient; when yang brightness is deficient, there is shivering cold and chattering of the jaw; when greater yang is deficient, there is pain in the lower back, upper back, head, and neck; when all three yang are deficient, yin qi prevails, and when yin qi prevails the bones grow cold and ache. Because the cold arises internally, both the interior and exterior are cold. When yang is exuberant, the exterior grows hot; when yin is deficient, the interior grows hot; when both interior and exterior are hot, the patient pants and is thirsty, and therefore craves cold drink. All this comes from having been injured by summer heat: the heat qi, being exuberant, lodges within the skin and outside the intestines and stomach — this is the dwelling place of the nutritive qi. This causes the sweat pores to become loose and the striae and interstices to open; then, on encountering the qi of autumn, the patient sweats and meets with wind, or bathes in water, so that water qi lodges within the skin and dwells together with the defensive qi. Now the defensive qi circulates through the yang by day and through the yin by night; this qi, on reaching yang, moves outward, and on reaching yin, presses inward; when the inner and outer clash, the attack occurs daily.

Huangdi asked: Why do some attacks occur every other day? Qibo replied: When the qi lodges deep and presses inward upon yin, the yang qi alone breaks forth while the yin evil remains lodged within; yin and yang contend but the evil cannot get out, and so the attack comes every other day. Huangdi said: Good. What qi causes the attack to come later on some days and earlier on others? Qibo said: The evil qi lodges in the wind mansion (fengfu) and travels downward along the spine. The defensive qi gathers greatly at the wind mansion once each day and night; on the next day it descends one vertebral segment, and so the attack comes later — this is because the evil first lodged in the spine and back. Each time the defensive qi reaches the wind mansion, the striae and interstices open; when they open the evil qi enters, and when it enters the disease erupts. This is why, day by day, the attack comes a little later. As the qi issues from the wind mansion and descends one segment each day, by the twenty-fifth day it reaches down to the sacrum, and on the twenty-sixth day it enters the spine and pours into the fu-lü vessel that runs along the spine; its qi then travels upward, and after nine days emerges at the supraclavicular fossa. As its qi rises higher day by day, the attack accordingly comes earlier and earlier. As for attacks occurring every other day, this is because the evil qi presses inward upon the five viscera and connects laterally with the membrane source; its path is long, its qi lies deep, and its movement is slow, so that it cannot travel together with the defensive qi nor emerge all at once — hence the attack comes only every other day.

Huangdi asked: You, Master, say that whenever the defensive qi reaches the wind mansion, the striae and interstices open, and when they open the evil qi enters, and when it enters the disease erupts. But now if the defensive qi descends one segment each day, its emergence does not always coincide with the wind mansion — how then can the attack still occur daily? Qibo replied: This is because the evil qi lodges in the head and neck and travels downward along the spine; deficiency and excess differ from case to case, and the evil strikes different places, so it need not coincide exactly with the wind mansion. Thus when the evil strikes the head and neck, the disease appears when the qi reaches the head and neck; when it strikes the back, the disease appears when the qi reaches the back; when it strikes the lower back and spine, the disease appears when the qi reaches the lower back and spine; when it strikes the hands and feet, the disease appears when the qi reaches the hands and feet. Wherever the defensive qi happens to be, if it meets there with the evil qi, the disease erupts. Hence wind has no fixed lodging place; wherever the defensive qi issues forth it must open the striae and interstices, and wherever the evil qi unites with it, that becomes its lodging place. Huangdi said: Good.

Now wind and malaria resemble one another and belong to the same category, yet wind alone persists constantly, while malaria has fixed times when it subsides — why is this? Qibo said: Wind qi lingers in the place it occupies, and so it persists constantly; the qi of malaria follows the channels and vessels, sinking inward and pressing against the interior, and only when the defensive qi responds to it does the attack occur. Huangdi asked: Why does malaria sometimes bring cold first and heat afterward? Qibo said: If one is injured by the great heat of summer, sweating profusely so that the striae and interstices open wide, and then encounters the chill and dampness of summer water-cold, which lodges within the striae and skin, and in autumn is further injured by wind, the disease is thereby formed. Now cold is a yin qi and wind is a yang qi; since the patient was first injured by cold and afterward injured by wind, cold comes first and heat comes afterward. The disease erupts at fixed times, and this is called cold malaria. Huangdi asked: Why does heat sometimes come first and cold afterward? Qibo said: This is because the patient was first injured by wind and afterward injured by cold, so heat comes first and cold comes afterward; this too erupts at fixed times and is called warm malaria. When there is only heat without cold, the yin qi is exhausted first and the yang qi alone breaks forth, so that the patient suffers shortness of breath and vexation, with hot hands and feet and a desire to vomit; this is called dan malaria (heat malaria).

Huangdi said: The classics say that what is in excess should be drained, and what is deficient should be supplemented. Now heat represents excess and cold represents deficiency. Yet in malaria, when the patient is cold, hot soup and fire cannot warm him, and when he is hot, ice water cannot cool him — these too belong to the categories of excess and deficiency. At such times, even a skilled physician cannot halt the attack; one must wait until it declines of itself before needling. What is the reason for this? I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: The classics say: do not needle blazing heat, do not needle a chaotic pulse, do not needle profuse sweating — for in these the disease runs counter to treatment and cannot yet be cured. When malaria first breaks out, the yang qi joins with the yin; at this time yang is deficient and yin is exuberant, and there is no qi in the exterior, so shivering cold comes first. When the yin qi's counterflow reaches its extreme, it turns and re-emerges as yang; yang then rejoins with yin in the exterior, so that yin becomes deficient and yang becomes replete, and heat and thirst follow. Thus in the qi of malaria, when it joins with yang, yang prevails, and when it joins with yin, yin prevails; when yin prevails there is cold, and when yang prevails there is heat. Malaria arises from the irregular qi of wind and cold; when the disease reaches its extreme it reverses. When the disease breaks forth it is as fierce as fire, as unstoppable as wind and rain. Hence the classics say: "At the height of its exuberance it must be avoided; once it has declined, treatment will surely succeed greatly" — this is what is meant. Before malaria has broken out, yin has not yet joined with yang, nor yang with yin; if one regulates the patient at this time, the true qi can be settled and the evil qi will perish. This is why the skilled physician cannot treat the disease once it has already erupted, for its qi is then in counterflow. Huangdi said: Good. How should one attack it — early or late? Qibo said: Just as malaria is about to erupt, yin and yang are about to shift; this always begins at the four extremities. Once yang has been injured, yin follows it. Therefore, before the appointed time, one should bind the limbs firmly so that the evil qi cannot enter and the yin qi cannot escape. Carefully observe and, wherever the grandchild vessels are found engorged, hard, and full of blood, one should drain them all — for the true qi has not yet gone forth to join with the evil. Huangdi asked: When malaria is not erupting, what is its condition then? Qibo said: The qi of malaria must alternate between exuberance and deficiency, according to wherever the qi currently resides. When the disease is in the yang, there is heat and the pulse is agitated; when it is in the yin, there is cold and the pulse is quiet. At the extreme, both yin and yang decline together and the defensive qi separates from the evil, so the disease finds respite; when the defensive qi gathers again, the disease returns.

Huangdi asked: Sometimes an attack skips two days, or even comes only after several days, and sometimes there is thirst and sometimes not — why is this? Qibo replied: When the attack skips days, it is because the evil qi and the defensive qi lodge in the six bowels and at times miss one another, unable to meet, so the patient rests for several days before the attack recurs. In malaria, yin and yang alternately prevail, sometimes severely and sometimes mildly, and so there is sometimes thirst and sometimes none. Huangdi asked: The treatises say that injury by summer heat must result in malaria by autumn, yet malaria does not always follow this pattern — why is that? Qibo said: This describes the disease as it corresponds to the four seasons; cases whose manifestations differ run contrary to the seasons. Malaria contracted in autumn brings severe cold; contracted in winter, the cold is mild; contracted in spring, the patient dislikes wind; contracted in summer, there is profuse sweating.

Huangdi asked: In warm malaria and cold malaria, where does the disease lodge — in which viscus does it reside? Qibo said: Warm malaria is contracted when one is struck by wind in winter; the cold qi is then stored within the bone marrow. When spring arrives, the yang qi bursts forth greatly, yet the evil qi cannot emerge of itself. Then, on encountering the great heat of summer, the brain and marrow are scorched, the flesh wastes away, and the striae and interstices open and discharge; or perhaps through some exertion, the evil qi and sweat both issue forth together. This disease is stored in the kidney, and its qi first moves from within outward. In such a case, yin is deficient and yang is exuberant; when yang is exuberant there is heat, and when it declines the qi turns back inward again — once it re-enters, yang becomes deficient, and when yang is deficient there is cold. Hence heat comes first and cold comes afterward; this is called warm malaria. Huangdi asked: What of dan malaria (heat malaria)? Qibo said: In dan malaria, the lung has habitually held heat, its qi exuberant throughout the body; there is counterflow surging upward, while the qi within is replete and does not discharge outward. Then, through some exertion, the striae and interstices open, and wind-cold lodges within the skin and between the flesh, and the attack erupts. When it erupts, the yang qi is exuberant, and being exuberant without declining, the disease results. Since its qi does not reach the yin, there is only heat without cold; the qi is stored internally in the heart and lodges externally between the flesh, causing the patient to waste away and lose flesh. Hence it is called dan malaria. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Needling for Malaria
paragraphs_en: In malaria of the foot greater yang, the patient suffers lower back pain and heaviness of the head; cold arises from the back, cold comes first and heat follows, blazing and searing; when the heat subsides, sweat pours out, and the disease is difficult to resolve. Needle the Weizhong (BL-40) point to let blood. In malaria of the foot lesser yang, the patient's body feels sluggish and weary; the cold is not severe and the heat is not severe, the patient dislikes seeing people, and on seeing people the heart is fearful and uneasy; heat predominates and sweating is profuse. Needle the foot lesser yang channel. In malaria of the foot yang brightness, cold comes first, with shivering and shuddering, severe cold lasting long before heat arises; when the heat departs, sweat pours out and the patient delights in seeing the light of sun and moon or the warmth of fire, feeling relieved thereby. Needle the foot yang brightness channel on the instep. In malaria of the foot greater yin, the patient is despondent, given to deep sighing, has no appetite, suffers much alternating cold and heat with sweating; when the attack comes the patient tends to vomit, and once the vomiting ceases the attack subsides. Treat accordingly. In malaria of the foot lesser yin, the patient vomits severely, with much alternating cold and heat, heat predominating over cold; the patient wishes to shut the doors and windows and stay withdrawn, and the disease is difficult to resolve. In malaria of the foot reverting yin, the patient suffers lower back pain and fullness of the lower abdomen, difficulty urinating resembling dribbling retention though it is not true dribbling retention, frequent urination, a mind troubled by fear, and qi insufficiency, with oppressive distress in the abdomen. Needle the foot reverting yin channel.

In lung malaria, the patient's heart feels cold; when the cold is extreme, heat follows, and in the interval between attacks the patient is easily startled, as though seeing something. Needle the hand greater yin and yang brightness channels. In heart malaria, the patient's heart is greatly vexed and craves cold water, yet on the contrary suffers more cold than heat. Needle the hand lesser yin channel. In liver malaria, the patient's complexion turns a pale bluish-green, and there is deep sighing; the patient's condition resembles that of one near death. Needle the foot reverting yin channel to let blood. In spleen malaria, the patient feels cold with abdominal pain; when heat comes there is rumbling in the intestines, and once the rumbling subsides sweat breaks out. Needle the foot greater yin channel. In kidney malaria, the patient shivers, with lower back and spinal pain that makes turning difficult, and difficulty with bowel movements; the eyes twitch and blur, and the hands and feet are cold. Needle the foot greater yang and lesser yin channels. In stomach malaria, the patient is troubled even as the disease develops, feeling hungry easily yet unable to eat; if he does eat, the abdomen becomes distended and enlarged. Needle the crossing vessels of the foot yang brightness and greater yin channels to let blood.

When malaria erupts and the body is just growing hot, needle the pulsing vessel on the instep, opening its aperture to let blood, and the heat will subside at once. When malaria is just about to turn cold, needle the hand yang brightness and greater yin, and the foot yang brightness and greater yin channels.

When the malaria pulse is full and large, promptly needle the back transport points, using a medium needle at each of the five flank-transport points beside the spine, adjusting for the patient's constitution — stout or thin — to let blood. When the malaria pulse is small and replete, promptly moxa the lesser yin channel at the shin and needle the well points of the toes. When the malaria pulse is full and large, promptly needle the back transport points, using each of the five flank-transport and back-transport points, proceeding until blood is reached. When the malaria pulse is moderate, large, and deficient, it is proper to use medicinals and not proper to use needling. In treating malaria generally, one should begin treatment about the time it takes to eat a meal before the attack; if this time is missed, the opportunity is lost. In all cases of malaria where the pulse is not apparent, needle between the ten fingers to let blood; once the blood is released, the disease will surely resolve. First examine the body for spots red as small beans and remove them all.

In malaria, the timing of each attack differs; one should examine the form of the disease in order to know which channel's disease it is. Needle about the time it takes to eat a meal before the onset: one needling brings decline, a second brings improvement, a third brings resolution. If it does not resolve, needle the two vessels beneath the tongue to let blood; if it still does not resolve, needle the exuberant channel at Weizhong to let blood; and if one further needles the points below the nape flanking the spine, the disease will surely resolve. The two vessels beneath the tongue are the Lianquan points.

In needling malaria, one must first ask where the disease first erupts, and needle that place first. If headache and heaviness come first, first needle the top of the head and the region between the two temples and eyebrows to let blood. If pain in the nape and back comes first, needle that first. If pain in the lower back and spine comes first, first needle Weizhong to let blood. If pain in the hand and arm comes first, first needle the hand lesser yin and yang brightness channels between the fingers. If aching pain in the foot and shin comes first, first needle the foot yang brightness channel between the toes to let blood. In wind malaria, when the attack comes the patient sweats and dislikes wind; needle the back transport points of the three yang channels where blood is found. When aching pain of the shin bone is so severe that it cannot be named on pressing, this is called bone-marrow disease; use the chisel needle to needle Juegu (GB-39) and let blood, and it will resolve at once. For slight pain throughout the body, needle Zhiyin (BL-67). At the well points of all the yin channels, do not let blood; needle every other day. When malaria brings no thirst and the attacks come every other day, needle the foot greater yang channel. When there is thirst and the attacks come every other day, needle the foot lesser yang channel. For warm malaria with no sweating, apply the fifty-nine-point needling method.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Qi Reversal
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When cold and heat transfer among the five viscera and six bowels, what results? Qibo said: When the kidney transmits cold to the liver, there is welling abscess-swelling and shortness of qi. When the spleen transmits cold to the liver, there is welling abscess-swelling and sinew spasm. When the liver transmits cold to the heart, there is manic derangement and blockage in the diaphragm region. When the heart transmits cold to the lung, there is lung wasting; in lung wasting, the patient drinks one measure and urinates two, and this is fatal and cannot be cured. When the lung transmits cold to the kidney, it becomes gushing water; in gushing water, pressing the abdomen finds it not hard, for the water qi has lodged in the large intestine — when the patient walks briskly there is a gurgling sound as of water sloshing in a pouch. This is a disease of water.

When the spleen transmits heat to the liver, there is fright and nosebleed. When the liver transmits heat to the heart, death follows. When the heart transmits heat to the lung, it is transmitted as diaphragm-wasting. When the lung transmits heat to the kidney, it is transmitted as flaccid rigidity (soft tetany). When the kidney transmits heat to the spleen, it is transmitted as deficiency and dysenteric diarrhea, which is fatal and cannot be cured.

When the uterus transmits heat to the bladder, there is dribbling urinary block and blood in the urine. When the bladder transmits heat to the small intestine, there is obstruction of the intestines and difficult bowel movement, manifesting above as ulceration of the mouth. When the small intestine transmits heat to the large intestine, there results hidden conglomeration and sinking stagnation. When the large intestine transmits heat to the stomach, the patient eats readily yet grows thin — this is called "eating and wasting." When the stomach transmits heat to the gallbladder, this too is called "eating and wasting." When the gallbladder transmits heat to the brain, there is aching pain at the bridge of the nose and deep-source nasal congestion; in deep-source nasal congestion, turbid discharge flows down without cease, which may progress to nosebleed and blurred, dim vision. All these conditions are obtained through the reversal of qi.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Coughing
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: How is it that the lung causes coughing? Qibo replied: All the five viscera and six bowels can cause coughing, not the lung alone. Huangdi said: I wish to hear their manifestations. Qibo said: The skin and body hair correspond to the lung; the skin and body hair receive the evil qi first, and the evil qi thereby follows into its corresponding organ. If cold food or drink enters the stomach, it travels along the lung vessel upward to the lung, causing the lung to become cold; when the lung is cold, the exterior and interior evils unite, and the evil qi accordingly lodges there, producing lung cough. Each of the five viscera receives disease according to its own season; when it is not that viscus's season, the disease is transmitted from another viscus to it. Since man corresponds to heaven and earth, each of the five viscera governs its own season, and when affected by cold at that season it contracts disease — if mild, this becomes cough; if severe, it becomes diarrhea or pain. When cold is encountered in autumn, the lung is affected first; when encountered in spring, the liver is affected first; when encountered in summer, the heart is affected first; when encountered at the height of yin (late summer), the spleen is affected first; when encountered in winter, the kidney is affected first.

Huangdi asked: How are these distinguished from one another? Qibo said: In lung cough, the patient coughs with wheezing breath that makes a sound; in severe cases there is spitting of blood. In heart cough, coughing brings pain in the heart, with a sensation in the throat as though something were stuck there; in severe cases the throat swells and there is throat obstruction. In liver cough, coughing brings pain beneath both flanks; in severe cases the patient cannot turn the body, and turning causes fullness beneath both sides. In spleen cough, coughing brings a dull, dragging pain beneath the right flank that pulls toward the shoulder and back; in severe cases the patient cannot move, and moving worsens the cough. In kidney cough, coughing brings pain that pulls between the lower back and the back; in severe cases the patient coughs up phlegm-saliva.

Huangdi asked: What of coughing in the six bowels, and from where do they receive the disease? Qibo said: When cough in the five viscera persists long, it is transmitted to the six bowels. When spleen cough does not resolve, the stomach receives it; in stomach cough, coughing brings vomiting, and in severe cases long roundworms are vomited up. When liver cough does not resolve, the gallbladder receives it; in gallbladder cough, coughing brings vomiting of bile. When lung cough does not resolve, the large intestine receives it; in large intestine cough, coughing brings involuntary loss of stool. When heart cough does not resolve, the small intestine receives it; in small intestine cough, coughing brings passing of gas, the gas escaping together with the cough. When kidney cough does not resolve, the bladder receives it; in bladder cough, coughing brings involuntary loss of urine. When cough persists long without resolving, the triple burner receives it; in triple burner cough, coughing brings abdominal fullness and lack of desire for food or drink. All these conditions gather in the stomach and connect with the lung, causing the patient to produce much phlegm and saliva, with facial swelling and counterflow of qi.

Huangdi asked: How should this be treated? Qibo said: To treat the viscera, treat their transport (shu) points; to treat the bowels, treat their uniting (he) points; for facial swelling, treat the channel points. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Pain
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that one who speaks well of heaven must find confirmation in man; one who speaks well of antiquity must find correspondence in the present; one who speaks well of man must find application in himself. Only thus does the Way remain free of confusion and its essential principles reach their utmost — this is called illumination. Now I ask you, Master, for something that can be known through words, seen through observation, and grasped through palpation, so that it may be verified in myself, dispelling ignorance and resolving doubt. May I hear of it? Qibo bowed twice, touching his head to the ground, and replied: What is it you wish to ask concerning the Way?

Huangdi said: I wish to hear what qi brings about sudden pain in the five viscera of man. Qibo replied: The channels and vessels flow without cessation, circulating in an unending round. When cold qi enters a channel, it causes delay and stagnation; the blood congeals and does not move. When the cold lodges outside the vessel, blood becomes scant there; when it lodges within the vessel, the qi cannot pass through — hence sudden pain arises.

Huangdi said: In some cases the pain suddenly stops; in some it is severe and unceasing; in some it is so severe that pressure cannot be borne; in some pressure brings the pain to a halt; in some pressure brings no relief; in some there is a throbbing that responds to the touch of the hand; in some the pain pulls between the heart and the back; in some it pulls between the flanks and the lower abdomen; in some abdominal pain pulls toward the inner thigh; in some the pain persists for days and forms an accumulation; in some the patient suddenly collapses in pain, losing consciousness, and revives again after a short while; in some there is pain with vomiting; in some abdominal pain is followed by diarrhea; in some there is pain with blockage and no passage. All these various pains differ in form from one another — how are they to be distinguished?

Qibo said: When cold qi lodges outside the vessel, the vessel becomes cold; when the vessel is cold it contracts and shrinks, and when it contracts and shrinks it becomes taut and tight, pulling on the small collateral vessels outside — hence sudden pain arises. If warmth is obtained, the pain stops at once; but if the patient is struck by cold again, the pain persists long. When cold qi lodges within the channel vessel and clashes with warm qi, the vessel becomes full, and when full there is pain that cannot bear pressure. When cold qi lingers and warm qi rises from below, the vessel becomes engorged and enlarged and the blood and qi fall into disorder, causing severe pain that cannot bear pressure. When cold qi lodges between the intestines and stomach, beneath the membrane source, the blood cannot disperse and the small collaterals are pulled taut, causing pain; pressing on it disperses the blood and qi, so pressure brings the pain to a stop. When cold qi lodges in the vessel flanking the spine, deep pressure cannot reach it, so pressure brings no relief. When cold qi lodges in the thoroughfare vessel (chongmai), which arises at Guanyuan (CV-4) and ascends straight up along the abdomen, the cold causes the vessel to become blocked; when the vessel is blocked the qi is likewise obstructed, producing a throbbing that responds to the touch of the hand. When cold qi lodges in the vessel of the back transport points, the vessel becomes congealed; when the vessel is congealed the blood becomes deficient, and deficient blood causes pain. Since these transport points connect to the heart, the pain pulls between the two; pressing on it brings warm qi to the area, and once warm qi arrives, the pain stops. When cold qi lodges in the vessel of reverting yin — the vessel of reverting yin encircles the genitals and connects with the liver — the cold qi lodging within the vessel causes the blood to congeal and the vessel to tighten, so pain pulls between the flanks and the lower abdomen. When reversing qi lodges in the inner thigh, the cold qi rises to reach the lower abdomen, and the congealed blood below pulls upward, so abdominal pain pulls toward the inner thigh. When cold qi lodges between the small intestine and the membrane source, within the collateral blood vessels, the congealed blood cannot pour into the great channels, and blood and qi linger and cannot move — hence over time an accumulation forms. When cold qi lodges in the five viscera, causing reversal and counterflow surging upward and discharging, the yin qi becomes exhausted and the yang qi has not yet entered — hence the patient suddenly collapses in pain and loses consciousness; when the qi returns, life is restored. When cold qi lodges in the intestines and stomach, causing reversal and counterflow surging upward and out, there is pain together with vomiting. When cold qi lodges in the small intestine, the small intestine cannot form and hold its contents, so there is diarrhea following the pain. When heat qi lingers in the small intestine, there is pain within the intestines; with dan-heat and parching thirst, the stool becomes hard and dry and cannot pass, so there is pain together with blockage and no passage. Huangdi said: This is what is meant by something that can be known through words.

What of something that can be seen through observation? Qibo said: The five viscera and six bowels each indeed have their own region; observing the five colors — yellow and red indicate heat, white indicates cold, and green-blue and black indicate pain. This is what is meant by something that can be seen through observation. Huangdi asked: What of something that can be grasped through palpation? Qibo said: Observing the vessel governing the disease — if it is hard and full of blood, or if it is sunken and depressed — all these can be grasped through palpation.

Huangdi said: Good. I know that the hundred diseases arise from qi: anger makes qi rise, joy makes qi relax, grief makes qi dissipate, fear makes qi descend, cold makes qi contract, heat makes qi leak out, fright makes qi fall into disorder, overexertion makes qi become depleted, and brooding makes qi become knotted. These nine qi differ from one another — what diseases does each produce? Qibo said: Anger causes qi to counterflow, and in severe cases the patient vomits blood or suffers undigested-food diarrhea; hence qi is said to rise. Joy harmonizes the qi and allows the will to reach its aims, so that the nutritive and defensive qi flow freely; hence qi is said to relax. Grief tightens the heart's connecting vessel, causes the lung lobes to spread and lift, and blocks the upper burner so that it fails to communicate; the nutritive and defensive qi fail to disperse, and heat qi collects within; hence qi is said to dissipate. Fear causes the essence to retreat; as it retreats, the upper burner closes, and when it closes the qi turns back; as it turns back the lower burner becomes distended; hence qi is said to fail to move. Cold causes the striae and interstices to close, so the qi cannot move; hence qi is said to contract. Heat causes the striae and interstices to open, so that the nutritive and defensive qi flow freely and sweat pours out profusely; hence qi is said to leak out. Fright leaves the heart with nothing to rely upon, the spirit with nowhere to return, and thought with nothing settled; hence qi is said to fall into disorder. Overexertion causes panting breath and sweating, so that both the exterior and interior are exhausted; hence qi is said to become depleted. Brooding causes the heart to fix upon something, so that the spirit has somewhere to return, and the upright qi lingers there and does not move; hence qi is said to become knotted.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Abdomen
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: There is a disease in which the heart and abdomen are full and distended, such that if one eats in the morning one cannot eat in the evening. What disease is this? Qibo replied: It is called drum distension. Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: Treat it with chicken-dung wine (ji shi li) — one dose brings improvement, two doses cure it. Huangdi said: Why does it sometimes recur? Qibo said: This is because of immoderate eating and drinking, so the disease recurs from time to time. Even though the disease may have been cured, when the immoderation returns, the disease should return as well, because qi gathers and collects in the abdomen.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the chest and rib-sides are propped up and full, hindering food intake. When the disease arrives, the patient first smells a fishy, rank odor, discharges a clear fluid, first spits blood, has cold limbs, dizziness, and from time to time bleeds from the front or back passage. What is this disease called, and how is it contracted? Qibo said: This disease is called blood exhaustion (xue ku). It is contracted in youth, from a great loss of blood, or from entering the bedchamber while intoxicated, whereby the qi is exhausted and the liver is injured, so that the menses diminish and cease to come. Huangdi said: How is it treated, and by what method? Qibo said: Use four parts cuttlefish bone and one part madder root, combined together and formed into pills with sparrow eggs, the size of small beans; take five pills before meals, washed down with a broth of salted fish, to benefit the intestines and the injured liver.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the lower abdomen is swollen and full, with roots fixed above and below, left and right. What disease is this, and can it be treated? Qibo said: The disease is called fu liang (hidden beam). Huangdi said: How is fu liang contracted? Qibo said: It wraps around a great mass of pus and blood, lodged outside the intestines and stomach; it cannot be treated — if one treats it, pressing and palpating it will bring death. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: Below, it presses upon the yin region and pus and blood must descend; above, it presses upon the stomach duct and gives rise to a diaphragm obstruction, forming an abscess beside the stomach duct. This is a chronic disease and difficult to treat. If it is located above the navel, it is contrary and dangerous; if located below the navel, it is compliant. Do not disturb it or forcibly attack it — this is discussed in the treatise on needling methods. Huangdi said: There are people whose body, thighs, and shanks are all swollen, with pain encircling the navel. What disease is this? Qibo said: This disease is also called fu liang; it is rooted in wind. Its qi overflows into the large intestine and lodges in the huang, the membrane source, whose origin lies below the navel; hence the pain encircles the navel. It must not be disturbed — to disturb it brings on a disease of water and difficult urination.

Huangdi said: Master, you have repeatedly said that in cases of heat in the center and wasting in the center, one should not take rich, fatty foods, fragrant herbs, or mineral drugs — mineral drugs bring on mania, and fragrant herbs bring on manic frenzy. Yet those who suffer heat-in-the-center and wasting-in-the-center disorders are all wealthy and eminent people; now if I forbid them rich food, this goes against their inclinations, and if I forbid fragrant herbs and mineral drugs, the disease will not be cured. I wish to hear your explanation. Qibo said: The qi of fragrant herbs is rich and fine, and the qi of mineral drugs is fierce; the qi of both is urgent, swift, hard, and forceful. Therefore, unless a person is of calm heart and harmonious temperament, these two should not be taken. Huangdi said: Why is it that these two should not be taken? Qibo said: The heat qi of the disease is swift and fierce, and the qi of these drugs is likewise so; when the two meet, they are liable to injure the spleen internally. The spleen belongs to earth and is averse to wood; for one who takes these drugs, the crisis should be reassessed on the days jia and yi.

Huangdi said: Good. There is a disease in which the chest is swollen, the neck aches, the chest is full, and the abdomen is distended. What disease is this, and how is it contracted? Qibo said: It is called jue ni, reversal counterflow. Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: If moxibustion is applied, it causes loss of voice; if the stone lancet is applied, it causes mania; one must wait until the qi is balanced, and only then can it be cured. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: The yang qi is doubled above, being in excess above; if moxibustion is applied, the yang qi is driven into the yin, and entering the yin it causes loss of voice; if the stone is applied, the yang qi becomes deficient, and being deficient it causes mania. One must wait until the qi is united and balanced before treating, so that the patient may be made whole.
Huangdi said: Good. How does one know that a woman is pregnant? Qibo said: The body shows signs of illness, yet the pulse shows no pathogenic sign.

Huangdi said: When there is heat disease accompanied by pain, what is the cause? Qibo said: Heat disease pertains to the yang pulses, arising from the movement of the three yang. At the ren ying pulse point, if it is once as large as normal, it is shaoyang; if twice as large, it is taiyang; if three times as large, it is yangming, entering into the yin. Now when yang enters into yin, the disease is located in the head and abdomen, causing distension and fullness together with headache. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Needling for Lumbar Pain
paragraphs_en: The foot taiyang channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain that pulls at the nape, spine, sacrum, and back, as though carrying a heavy weight; needle the xi-cleft point of the taiyang channel proper to let blood; in spring, do not let blood. Shaoyang, when disordered, causes lower back pain as if the skin were being pricked with a needle, gradually worsening, so that one cannot bend forward or backward, nor turn to look back; needle the tip of the cheng gu bone of the shaoyang channel to let blood — the cheng gu is the bone that rises alone on the outer edge of the knee; in summer, do not let blood. Yangming, when disordered, causes lower back pain such that one cannot turn to look back, and turning as if seeing something strange, being prone to sorrow; needle the yangming channel at the front of the shin, three punctures, harmonizing above and below to let blood; in autumn, do not let blood. Foot shaoyin, when disordered, causes lower back pain that pulls into the inner border of the spine; needle the shaoyin channel above the inner ankle, two punctures; in spring do not let blood — if too much blood is let, it cannot be restored. The jueyin channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain as if the lower back were a strung bow or crossbow-string, taut; needle the jueyin channel at the outer side of the calf's fish-belly near the heel — palpate along it until a nodule like a string of beads is felt, and then needle it; this disease may cause the patient to talk excessively, or conversely to fall silent and dull-witted; needle it three times. The jie mai, when disordered, causes lower back pain that pulls into the shoulder, with blurred vision, and occasional involuntary urination; needle the jie mai at the transverse vessel on the outer border of the cleft, between the sinew and flesh of the knee, to let blood, stopping once the blood changes color. The jie mai may also, when disordered, cause lower back pain as if a sash were pulling at it, constantly as though the back were about to break, with a tendency to fear; needle the jie mai where, in the cleft, a knotted vessel like a millet grain can be felt; needling it, the blood will spurt out black; stop once red blood appears. The tongyin channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain as if a small hammer were lodged within, with sudden swelling; needle the tongyin channel above the outer ankle at the tip of the jue gu, three punctures. The yangwei channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain with sudden swelling above the pain; needle the yangwei channel where it joins the taiyang channel below the calf, about one chi above the ground. The transverse network vessel, when disordered, causes lower back pain such that one can neither bend forward nor backward; bending backward risks falling, this disorder being caused by lifting heavy objects and injuring the back, so that the network is severed and bad blood collects there; needle it between the cleft and the yang sinew, several cun above the cleft, transversely, two punctures, to let blood. The huiyin channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain with profuse sweating above the pain; once the sweat dries, the patient wishes to drink, and after drinking wishes to run about; needle the zhiyang channel, three punctures above, located transversely five cun below the cleft on the qiao channel; observe where it is most replete and let blood there. The feiyang channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain with a fluttering sensation above the pain; in severe cases, sorrow and fear; needle the feiyang channel, five cun above the inner ankle, in front of the shaoyin channel, at its meeting point with the yinwei channel. The changyang channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain that pulls into the chest, with blurred vision; in severe cases, the body arches backward and the tongue curls so that speech is impossible; needle the inner sinew, two punctures, located above the inner ankle in front of the large sinew, behind the taiyin channel, about two cun above the ankle. The dispersing vessel, when disordered, causes lower back pain with heat; when the heat is severe, vexation arises; below the lower back it feels as if a beam of wood lay crosswise within it; in severe cases there is involuntary urination; needle the dispersing vessel, located between the bone and flesh in front of the knee, at the bundled vessel on the outer border of the network, three punctures. The rouli channel, when disordered, causes lower back pain such that one cannot cough; coughing causes the sinews to contract and tighten; needle the rouli channel, two punctures, located outside the taiyang channel, behind the jue gu of the shaoyang channel.

When lower back pain flanks the spine and extends up to the head, stiff and rigid, with blurred vision as though about to collapse, needle the foot taiyang channel at the cleft to let blood. When lower back pain is accompanied by cold above, needle the foot taiyang and yangming channels; when accompanied by heat above, needle the foot jueyin channel. When one cannot bend forward or backward, needle the foot shaoyang channel. When there is heat within accompanied by panting, needle the foot shaoyin channel, letting blood at the cleft. When lower back pain is accompanied by cold above and one cannot turn to look back, needle the foot yangming channel; when accompanied by heat above, needle the foot taiyin channel; when there is heat within accompanied by panting, needle the foot shaoyin channel. When defecation is difficult, needle the foot shaoyin channel. When the lower abdomen is full, needle the foot jueyin channel. When the pain is as if the back were broken, so that one can neither bend nor lift it, needle the foot taiyang channel. When the pain pulls at the inner border of the spine, needle the foot shaoyin channel. When lower back pain pulls into the lower abdomen and draws at the flanks, so that one cannot lean back, needle the point where the lower back and sacrum meet, above the two protruding hip bones. Determine the number of punctures according to the waxing and waning of the moon; once the needle is withdrawn, the disease is cured at once. For pain on the left, needle the right; for pain on the right, needle the left.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Wind
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When wind injures a person, it may become cold-heat disease, or heat in the center, or cold in the center, or li feng, leprous wind, or hemiplegia, or remain merely wind itself. The diseases differ from one another, and their names are not the same; sometimes the wind penetrates internally to the five viscera and six bowels. I do not understand the explanation for this and wish to hear it. Qibo replied: Wind qi lodges between the skin and its texture, unable to pass through internally or to be released externally. Wind is skilled at moving and frequently changes; when the interstices of the skin are open, one feels a sudden chill; when they are closed, there is heat and oppression. When there is cold, the appetite for food and drink declines; when there is heat, the flesh wastes away. Thus the person shivers uncontrollably and cannot eat — this is called cold-heat disease. When wind qi enters the stomach together with the yangming channel and follows the vessel upward to the inner canthus of the eye, if the person is fat, the wind qi cannot be released externally, and it becomes heat in the center, with yellowing of the eyes; if the person is thin, the wind qi is released externally and becomes cold, becoming cold in the center, with tearing of the eyes. When wind qi enters together with the taiyang channel and travels through the shu points of the various vessels, dispersing between the partitions of the flesh, it interferes with the defensive qi, so that its pathway is obstructed; this causes the flesh to swell up angrily and develop sores. Where the defensive qi becomes congealed and fails to move, the flesh becomes numb and insensate. In li, the nutritive qi becomes heated and putrid, its qi impure, causing the bridge of the nose to decay and the complexion to become ruined, with the skin ulcerating and festering. When wind-cold lodges in the vessels and does not depart, it is called li feng, leprous wind, or also called cold-heat.

One injured by wind in spring, on the days jia and yi, develops liver wind; one injured by wind in summer, on the days bing and ding, develops heart wind; one struck by pathogenic qi in late summer, on the days wu and ji, develops spleen wind; one struck by pathogenic qi in autumn, on the days geng and xin, develops lung wind; one struck by pathogenic qi in winter, on the days ren and gui, develops kidney wind. When wind strikes the shu points of the five viscera and six bowels, this too becomes wind of the corresponding viscus or bowel; entering by whichever gate it strikes, it becomes one-sided wind. When wind qi follows the fengfu point upward, it becomes brain wind; when wind enters the connections of the head, it becomes eye wind with cold eyes; when one drinks wine and is struck by wind, it becomes leaking wind; when one enters the bedchamber, sweats, and is struck by wind, it becomes internal wind; when one has just washed the hair and is struck by wind, it becomes head wind; when wind persists long and enters the interior, it becomes intestinal wind with undigested-food diarrhea; when it remains outside in the interstices of the skin, it becomes discharging wind. Thus wind is the chief cause of the hundred diseases; as it transforms and changes, it gives rise to other diseases, without a fixed pattern — yet in every case it is brought about by wind qi.

Huangdi said: Why do the forms and appearances of wind in the five viscera differ? I wish to hear of their diagnostic signs and clinical manifestations. Qibo said: The signs of lung wind are: profuse sweating, aversion to wind, a pale whitish complexion, occasional coughing and shortness of breath, improving during the day and worsening in the evening; the diagnostic sign is above the eyebrows, where the color is white. The signs of heart wind are: profuse sweating, aversion to wind, a parched and depleted look, irritability and a tendency to startle others in anger, a red complexion; when the disease is severe, speech becomes impaired; the diagnostic sign is at the mouth, where the color is red. The signs of liver wind are: profuse sweating, aversion to wind, a tendency to sorrow, a faintly greenish complexion, dryness of the throat, irritability, and at times an aversion to women; the diagnostic sign is below the eyes, where the color is green. The signs of spleen wind are: profuse sweating, aversion to wind, bodily lassitude, disinclination to move the limbs, a pale, faintly yellowish complexion, and lack of appetite; the diagnostic sign is on the nose, where the color is yellow. The signs of kidney wind are: profuse sweating, aversion to wind, a puffy, swollen face, aching of the spine such that one cannot stand upright, a sooty-black complexion, and difficulty with the private functions; the diagnostic sign is on the flesh over the kidney region, where the color is black. The signs of stomach wind are: profuse sweating at the neck, aversion to wind, inability to swallow food and drink, obstruction of the diaphragm, frequent abdominal fullness, distension when insufficiently clothed, and diarrhea upon eating cold food; the diagnostic sign is a thin body with a large abdomen. The signs of head wind are: profuse sweating of the head and face, aversion to wind; a day before the wind arrives, the disease grows severe with unbearable headache, so that one cannot go outdoors; when the day of the wind itself arrives, the disease eases somewhat. The signs of leaking wind are: profuse sweating at times, such that one can never wear thin clothing; sweating upon eating, and in severe cases sweating over the whole body, panting and aversion to wind, clothes constantly damp, dryness of the mouth with frequent thirst, and inability to perform labor. The signs of discharging wind are: profuse sweating, with sweat soaking through the clothing, dryness of the mouth with moisture above the lip, inability to perform labor because of this wind, and, when the whole body aches, a sensation of cold. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Bi (Blockage) Syndrome
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: How does bi arise? Qibo replied: The three qi of wind, cold, and dampness arrive together and combine to form bi. Where wind qi predominates, it becomes wandering bi; where cold qi predominates, it becomes painful bi; where dampness qi predominates, it becomes fixed bi. Huangdi said: Why are there five kinds? Qibo said: One who encounters this in winter develops bone bi; one who encounters it in spring develops sinew bi; one who encounters it in summer develops vessel bi; one who encounters it in late summer develops muscle bi; one who encounters it in autumn develops skin bi. Huangdi said: What qi causes bi to lodge internally in the five viscera and six bowels? Qibo said: Each of the five viscera has a corresponding tissue that it governs; when the disease persists long without resolving, it lodges internally in that corresponding viscus. Thus, if bone bi does not resolve and the person is struck again by pathogenic qi, it lodges internally in the kidney; if sinew bi does not resolve and is struck again, it lodges in the liver; if vessel bi does not resolve and is struck again, it lodges in the heart; if muscle bi does not resolve and is struck again, it lodges in the spleen; if skin bi does not resolve and is struck again, it lodges in the lung. What is called bi arises, in each case according to its season, from repeated exposure to the qi of wind, cold, and dampness.

In general, when bi lodges in the five viscera: in lung bi, there is vexation and fullness, panting and vomiting; in heart bi, the vessels are obstructed, and when vexed there is throbbing below the heart, sudden upward surging of qi with panting, dryness of the throat with frequent belching, and when counterflow qi rises, there is fear; in liver bi, there is startling upon lying down at night, excessive drinking with frequent urination, and a sensation above as if pregnant; in kidney bi, there is frequent distension, the buttocks substituting for the heels, and the spine bent so that it substitutes for the head; in spleen bi, the four limbs are languid and weak, coughing arises with vomiting of fluid, and above there is great obstruction; in intestinal bi, there is frequent drinking yet inability to void, with qi in the middle panting and struggling, and periodic undigested-food diarrhea; in bladder bi, the lower abdomen and bladder are painful within when pressed, as though scalded with hot water, urination is difficult and unsmooth, and above there is clear nasal discharge.

As for yin qi: when it is calm, the spirit is stored; when it is agitated, it is dissipated and lost. Overeating and overdrinking injure the intestines and stomach. When excessive qi causes panting, bi collects in the lung; when excessive qi causes worry and brooding, bi collects in the heart; when excessive qi causes involuntary urination, bi collects in the kidney; when excessive qi causes exhaustion and depletion, bi collects in the liver; when excessive qi causes the flesh to waste away, bi collects in the spleen. When the various forms of bi persist without resolving, they also penetrate further inward; where wind qi predominates, the person is more easily cured. Huangdi said: In bi, sometimes there is death, sometimes prolonged pain, and sometimes easy cure — what is the reason for this? Qibo said: When it enters the viscera, it causes death; when it lingers between the sinews and bones, the pain is prolonged; when it remains between the skin, it is easily cured.

Huangdi said: What of bi lodging in the six bowels? Qibo said: This too has its root in diet and dwelling. The six bowels each also have their shu points; when wind, cold, and damp qi strike these shu points, and diet corresponds with and aggravates them, the pathogenic qi follows the shu points inward and lodges, in each case, in its corresponding bowel. Huangdi said: How is this treated with needling? Qibo said: The five viscera have their shu points, and the six bowels have their he, uniting, points; following the divisions of the vessels, each has its point of onset; by following and needling wherever the disorder has passed, the disease is cured.

Huangdi said: Can the nutritive and defensive qi also cause bi in a person? Qibo said: The nutritive qi is the essential qi of water and grain; it harmonizes and regulates the five viscera and spreads throughout the six bowels, and thus it is able to enter the vessels; hence it travels up and down along the vessels, penetrating the five viscera and connecting with the six bowels. The defensive qi is the fierce qi of water and grain; its qi is swift, rapid, and slippery, and it cannot enter the vessels; hence it travels within the skin and between the partitions of the flesh, steaming the huang membranes and dispersing through the chest and abdomen. When its qi is countered, disease results; when its qi is followed, the disease is cured. Since the defensive qi does not combine with the qi of wind, cold, and dampness, it does not become bi. Huangdi said: Good. In bi, there is sometimes pain, sometimes no pain but numbness, sometimes cold, sometimes heat, sometimes dryness, sometimes dampness — what is the reason for this? Qibo said: Where there is pain, cold qi predominates; because of the cold, there is pain. Where there is neither pain nor sensation, the disease has persisted long and penetrated deeply, so that the movement of nutritive and defensive qi becomes rough and impeded, the channels and network vessels become at times sparse and thus obstructed, and the skin is not nourished, hence numbness results. Where there is cold, yang qi is scant and yin qi abundant, reinforcing the disease, hence cold results. Where there is heat, yang qi is abundant and yin qi scant; the pathogenic qi overcomes the yang and encounters the yin, hence bi-heat results. Where there is profuse sweating with dampness, this is because dampness has been encountered severely; yang qi is scant and yin qi abundant, and when the two qi interact, sweat is emitted and dampness results. Huangdi said: Now, as bi is a disease, why is there sometimes no pain? Qibo said: When bi is in the bones, there is heaviness; when it is in the vessels, the blood congeals and does not flow; when it is in the sinews, the limb is bent and cannot be extended; when it is in the flesh, there is numbness; when it is in the skin, there is cold. Where all five of these are present together, there is no pain. In general, among the various kinds of bi, when cold is encountered the sinews contract, and when heat is encountered they become lax. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Wei (Wilting) Syndrome
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: How is it that the five viscera cause a person to develop wei, wilting or atrophy syndrome? Qibo replied: The lung governs the skin and body hair; the heart governs the blood vessels; the liver governs the sinews and membranes; the spleen governs the flesh and muscles; the kidney governs the bones and marrow. Thus, when the lung is heated and its lobes are scorched, the skin and body hair become weak and feeble; when this becomes acute and thin, it gives rise to wei with limpness of the limbs. When the heart qi is heated, the lower vessels reverse and rise upward; once they rise, the lower vessels become deficient, and this deficiency gives rise to vessel wei, in which the joints become as though snapped and unable to lift, and the shins become limp and cannot bear the body's weight on the ground. When the liver qi is heated, the gallbladder leaks its qi and the mouth becomes bitter, the sinews and membranes dry out; when the sinews and membranes dry out, the sinews become tense and contracted, giving rise to sinew wei. When the spleen qi is heated, the stomach dries out and there is thirst, the flesh becomes numb, giving rise to flesh wei. When the kidney qi is heated, the lower back and spine cannot be raised, the bones wither and the marrow diminishes, giving rise to bone wei.

Huangdi said: How is this contracted? Qibo said: The lung is the chief of the viscera and the canopy of the heart; when one experiences loss or bereavement, or when one's desires are not fulfilled, the lung is stirred to wheeze; wheezing then leads to heat in the lung and scorching of its lobes. Hence it is said: the five viscera, because of heat in the lung and scorching of its lobes, give rise to wei with limpness of the limbs — this is what is meant. When grief and sorrow are excessive, the connecting vessel of the uterus becomes severed; once severed, yang qi stirs internally, and when this erupts there is collapse below the heart and frequent bloody urination. Hence the lost text Ben Bing says: when the great channels are empty and hollow, this gives rise to muscle bi, which is transmitted and becomes vessel wei. When thoughts and longings are endless, and one's wishes are not fulfilled, so that the mind wanders licentiously outward, and one enters the bedchamber to excess, the ancestral sinew becomes slack and lax, giving rise to sinew wei, as well as to white seminal discharge. Hence the lost text Xia Jing says: sinew wei arises from the liver, brought on by indulgence within the bedchamber. When one is gradually steeped in dampness, engaged in work involving water, or dwells for a long time in a damp place, the flesh becomes soaked and macerated, becoming blocked and numb, giving rise to flesh wei. Hence the Xia Jing says: flesh wei is contracted from damp places. When one travels far and becomes fatigued, and encounters great heat and thirst, the thirst causes the yang qi to attack internally; this internal attack causes heat to lodge in the kidney. The kidney is the viscus of water; now when water cannot overcome fire, the bones wither and the marrow becomes deficient, so that the feet cannot bear the weight of the body, giving rise to bone wei. Hence the Xia Jing says: bone wei arises from great heat. Huangdi said: How does one distinguish among these? Qibo said: In lung heat, the complexion is white and the body hair is ruined; in heart heat, the complexion is red and the network vessels overflow; in liver heat, the complexion is greenish and the nails are withered; in spleen heat, the complexion is yellow and the flesh twitches; in kidney heat, the complexion is black and the teeth become dry and lusterless.

Huangdi said: What Master you have said is acceptable; but the treatises state that in treating wei, one should select the yangming channel alone — why is this? Qibo said: Yangming is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; it governs the moistening of the ancestral sinew, and the ancestral sinew governs the binding of the bones and the smooth functioning of the joints. The chong channel is the sea of the channels and vessels; it governs the irrigation of the muscle interstices, and joins with the yangming at the ancestral sinew. The meeting of all the yin and yang channels at the ancestral sinew occurs at the qi thoroughfare, and yangming is the chief among them; all these channels belong to the girdling vessel and connect with the governing vessel. Therefore, when yangming is deficient, the ancestral sinew becomes slack, the girdling vessel fails to draw it taut, and thus the feet become wei and unusable. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: In each case, supplement the ying, spring, point and free the shu, stream, point, regulate deficiency and excess, and harmonize counterflow and compliance; treating the sinews, vessels, bones, and flesh each according to the month in which they receive qi, the disease will be cured. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Jue (Reversal) Syndrome
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is meant by cold and heat in jue, reversal? Qibo replied: When yang qi declines below, it becomes cold reversal; when yin qi declines below, it becomes heat reversal. Huangdi said: Why does the heat of heat reversal necessarily arise from beneath the feet? Qibo said: Yang qi arises from the outer aspect of the five toes; the yin vessels gather beneath the foot and collect in the sole of the foot; hence when yang qi predominates, the sole of the foot becomes hot. Huangdi said: Why does the cold of cold reversal necessarily proceed from the five toes upward to the knee? Qibo said: Yin qi arises from the inner aspect of the five toes, gathers below the knee, and collects above the knee; hence when yin qi predominates, cold extends from the five toes up to above the knee. This cold does not come from outside — it comes entirely from within. Huangdi said: What imbalance causes cold reversal to arise? Qibo said: The anterior yin is where the ancestral sinew gathers, and where the taiyin and yangming channels converge. In spring and summer, yang qi is abundant and yin qi is scant; in autumn and winter, yin qi is abundant and yang qi declines. When a person of robust constitution overexerts himself sexually during autumn and winter, the lower qi struggles to rise upward and cannot recover; essence qi overflows and drains downward, and pathogenic qi thereupon follows it and rises. Because the qi is drained from within, the yang qi declines and cannot seep through and nourish the channels and network vessels; the yang qi diminishes day by day, while the yin qi alone remains, and so the hands and feet become cold because of this. Huangdi said: How does heat reversal come about? Qibo said: When wine enters the stomach, the network vessels become full while the channel vessels become empty. The spleen governs the circulation of the fluids of the stomach; when yin qi is deficient, yang qi enters; when yang qi enters, the stomach becomes disharmonious; when the stomach is disharmonious, the essence qi is exhausted; when the essence qi is exhausted, it fails to nourish the four limbs. Such a person must frequently become intoxicated or enter the bedchamber after eating to excess; the qi collects in the spleen and cannot disperse, the qi of wine combines with the qi of grain, and heat becomes intense within; hence heat spreads throughout the body, with internal heat and reddish urine. Now the qi of wine is intense and fierce; the kidney qi weakens, and the yang qi alone prevails, so the hands and feet become hot because of this. Huangdi said: In jue, sometimes there is abdominal fullness, sometimes a sudden loss of consciousness, and sometimes the person does not regain consciousness for half a day or, at the longest, a full day — why is this? Qibo said: When yin qi is abundant above, there is deficiency below, and this deficiency below causes abdominal distension and fullness. When yang qi is abundant above, the lower qi doubles up and rises, and pathogenic qi moves in counterflow; this counterflow throws the yang qi into disorder, and when the yang qi is in disorder, the person loses consciousness.

Huangdi said: Good. I wish to hear of the forms and clinical manifestations of jue in the six channel vessels. Qibo said: In jue of the taiyang, the head swells and feels heavy, the feet cannot walk, and there is dizziness with collapse. In jue of the yangming, there is manic disease with a desire to run about and shout, abdominal fullness with inability to lie down, a red and feverish face, and seeing and speaking of delusions. In jue of the shaoyang, there is sudden deafness, swollen and hot cheeks, rib-side pain, and inability to move the shin. In jue of the taiyin, there is abdominal fullness and distension, difficulty with bowel movements, lack of appetite, vomiting upon eating, and inability to lie down. In jue of the shaoyin, there is dryness of the mouth, reddish urine, abdominal fullness, and heart pain. In jue of the jueyin, there is swelling and pain of the lower abdomen, abdominal distension, difficulty urinating and defecating, a preference for lying with the knees bent, retraction and swelling of the genitals, and internal heat of the shin. Where there is excess, drain it; where there is deficiency, supplement it; where there is neither excess nor deficiency, treat according to the affected channel. In taiyin jue reversal, the shin is tense and cramped, and heart pain pulls into the abdomen; treat the channel governing the disease. In shaoyin jue reversal, there is deficient fullness, vomiting and disturbance, and clear diarrhea below; treat the channel governing the disease. In jueyin jue reversal, there is cramping and lower back pain, deficient fullness, blockage of the front passage, and delirious speech; treat the channel governing the disease. When all three yin channels are in reversal together, there is inability to void urine or stool, the hands and feet become cold, and death occurs within three days. In taiyang jue reversal, there is stiffness and collapse, vomiting of blood, and frequent nosebleeds; treat the channel governing the disease. In shaoyang jue reversal, the joints do not move freely; when the joints do not move freely, the lower back cannot walk and the neck cannot turn to look back; if intestinal abscess develops, it cannot be treated, and if there is fright, the patient dies. In yangming jue reversal, there is panting and coughing, fever, frequent fright, nosebleed, and vomiting of blood. In hand taiyin jue reversal, there is deficient fullness with coughing and frequent vomiting of foam; treat the channel governing the disease. In hand jueyin, the heart master, and hand shaoyin jue reversal, there is heart pain pulling into the throat, with fever; this is fatal and cannot be treated. In hand taiyang jue reversal, there is deafness with tearing, the neck cannot turn to look back, and the lower back cannot bend forward or backward; treat the channel governing the disease. In hand yangming and hand shaoyang jue reversal, there is throat bi, obstruction, with swelling of the throat and stiffness; treat the channel governing the disease.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Disease Patterns
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When a person suffers from an abscess of the stomach duct, how should one diagnose it? Qibo replied: To diagnose this, one should examine the stomach pulse. Its pulse should be deep and fine. When it is deep and fine, the qi is rebellious; when the qi is rebellious, the Renying pulse is exceedingly full. When it is exceedingly full, there is heat. The Renying pulse is the stomach pulse. When it is rebellious and full, heat gathers at the stomach opening and cannot move on, and thus an abscess forms in the stomach duct.

Huangdi said: Good. Why is it that a person may lie down yet be unable to rest peacefully? Qibo said: It is because one of the viscera has sustained some injury. If the essence has somewhere to settle and lodge, one is at ease; if not, the person's affliction cannot be held in abeyance, and so he cannot rest.

Huangdi said: Why can some people not lie flat on their backs? Qibo said: The lung is the canopy covering the viscera. When the lung qi is exuberant, its pulse becomes large; when the pulse is large, one cannot lie flat on the back. This is discussed in the chapter on the Yin and Yang of the Unusual and the Constant.

Huangdi said: There is a case of reversal disorder in which, on examination, the right pulse is deep and tight while the left pulse is floating and slow. If this is not simply a seasonal pattern, where does the disease actually reside? Qibo said: In winter, when examined, the right pulse should indeed be deep and tight — this corresponds to the season. But a left pulse that is floating and slow runs contrary to the season. When the disorder is on the left, the disease should be governed by the kidney, though it is somewhat related to the lung as well, and there should be lower back pain. Huangdi said: How can this be explained? Qibo said: The Shaoyin channel passes through the kidney and connects with the lung. Now that a lung-type pulse is found where the kidney should govern, the kidney has become diseased, and therefore the kidney gives rise to lower back pain.

Huangdi said: Good. There is a case of neck abscess: some treat it with the stone lancet, others with needling and moxibustion, and in both cases the patient recovers. Where does the truth of the matter actually lie? Qibo said: These are cases of the same name but different categories. When the abscess arises from qi that has merely stagnated, it is fitting to open and remove it with the needle; when the qi is exuberant and blood has gathered, it is fitting to use the stone to drain it. This is what is meant by 'treating the same disease by different methods.'

Huangdi said: There is a disease of anger and mania — where does this disease arise? Qibo said: It arises from yang. Huangdi said: How does yang cause a person to become manic? Qibo said: When the yang qi is suddenly broken off and cannot resolve itself, the person becomes prone to anger; this disease is called 'yang reversal.' Huangdi said: How can one recognize it? Qibo said: The Yangming pulse normally moves, while the Taiyang and Shaoyang pulses normally do not move; when the pulses that normally do not move begin to move, and move greatly and rapidly, this is the sign. Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: Depriving the patient of food will cure it at once. Food, once it enters the yin, nourishes and increases the qi of the yang; therefore depriving the patient of food cures it at once. One may also have the patient drink a decoction made from raw iron filings, for raw iron filings are swift at bringing qi downward.

Huangdi said: Good. There is a disease in which the body is feverish and the limbs feel weak and languid, sweat pours out as though from bathing, the patient is averse to wind, and the qi is scant — what disease is this? Qibo said: This disease is called 'wine wind.' Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: Take Alisma and Atractylodes, ten parts each, and Herba Lysimachiae, five parts; combine and take a three-finger pinch of the mixture before meals.

As for what is meant by 'deep and fine' pulses, it is that beneath the hand the pulse feels as fine as a needle; when one presses and palpates it, if there is a gathering, it feels hard, and if it is spread out, it feels large. The 'Upper Canon' speaks of how qi connects with heaven; the 'Lower Canon' speaks of the transformations of disease. The 'Golden Chest' resolves questions of life and death; 'estimation and assessment' means to press and gauge the pulse; and 'the unusual and the constant' refers to unusual diseases. What is called 'unusual' means that an unusual disease does not necessarily bring death in accordance with the season; what is called 'constant' means that a disease will bring death in accordance with the season. What is called 'estimating' means to press and search for the pattern — that is, to palpate and seek out the principle of the pulse; 'assessing' means to determine the location of the disease and gauge it according to the four seasons.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Unusual Diseases
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: There is a woman who is pregnant and in the ninth month becomes mute — what is this? Qibo replied: The connecting vessel of the uterus has been cut off. Huangdi said: How can this be explained? Qibo said: The uterine vessel is tied to the kidney; the Shaoyin channel passes through the kidney and connects to the root of the tongue, and therefore the woman cannot speak. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: There is no need to treat it — it will recover of itself by the tenth month. The needling classic says: do not deplete what is already insufficient, and do not supplement what is already in excess, for doing so would bring the affliction to completion; only afterward should one regulate it. What is meant by 'do not deplete what is insufficient' is that when the body is emaciated and weak, one should not use the lancet-stone. What is meant by 'do not supplement what is in excess' is that when there is a mass in the abdomen, one should not purge it, for purging it would cause the essence to escape and leave the disease to dominate alone within — this is what is meant by 'the affliction being brought to completion.'

Huangdi said: There is a disease with fullness below the ribs and rebellious qi that does not resolve for two or three years — what disease is this? Qibo said: This disease is called 'accumulation of breath.' It does not interfere with eating, and it should not be treated with moxibustion or needling; for this accumulation, guided exercises should be combined with medicine, for medicine alone cannot cure it.

Huangdi said: There is a case in which the body, thighs, and shins are all swollen, and there is pain encircling the navel — what disease is this? Qibo said: This disease is called 'hidden beam'; it is rooted in wind. Its qi overflows into the large intestine and lodges in the membrane source; since the origin of the membrane source lies below the navel, the pain encircles the navel. This mass must not be disturbed — if disturbed, it will give rise to a disease of water and difficult urination.

Huangdi said: There is a case in which the cubit pulse is exceedingly rapid, and the sinews are tense and visibly prominent — what disease is this? Qibo said: This is what is called 'sinew affliction.' In such a person, the abdomen must also be tense; if white or black coloring appears in the complexion, the disease is severe.

Huangdi said: There is a case of headache that persists unresolved for several years — how is this contracted, and what is this disease called? Qibo said: It must be that the person was once exposed to severe cold, which penetrated inward as far as the bone marrow. The marrow is governed by the brain, and when the brain is thrown into rebellion, it causes headache, and the teeth ache as well. This disease is called 'reversal counterflow.' Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the mouth tastes sweet — what is this disease called, and how is it contracted? Qibo said: This is an overflow of the qi of the five flavors; it is called 'spleen heat-block.' The five flavors enter the mouth and are stored in the stomach; the spleen circulates their essential qi, and when fluid accumulates in the spleen, it causes the mouth to taste sweet. This is brought on by rich, fatty foods — such a person must frequently eat sweet and rich foods in excess. Fatty food generates internal heat, and sweet food causes fullness in the middle; therefore the qi overflows upward and turns into wasting-thirst. It is treated with orchid, to eliminate the stagnant qi.

Huangdi said: There is a disease with a bitter taste in the mouth, for which one selects the point Yangling Quan — what is this disease called, and how is it contracted? Qibo said: This disease is called 'gallbladder heat-block.' The liver is the general of the interior, and it depends on the gallbladder for its decisions, while the throat serves as their intermediary. Such a person frequently deliberates but cannot reach a decision; hence the gallbladder becomes deficient, its qi overflows upward, and the mouth becomes bitter as a result. It is treated at the alarm and transport points of the gallbladder; the treatment is discussed in the chapter on the Twelve Officials of Yin and Yang and Their Mutual Employment.

Huangdi said: There is a case of urinary blockage in which the patient urinates several dozen times a day — this is a deficiency. Yet the body is hot as burning charcoal, the neck and chest feel as if blocked, the Renying pulse is agitated and full, and there is panting and rebellious qi — this is an excess. And the Taiyin pulse is faint and fine as a hair — this is a deficiency. Where does this disease lie, and what is it called? Qibo said: The disease lies in the Taiyin; its excess is chiefly in the stomach, and somewhat also in the lung. This disease is called reversal, and it is fatal — it cannot be treated. This is what is meant by 'obtaining five excesses and two deficiencies.' Huangdi said: What is meant by 'five excesses and two deficiencies'? Qibo said: The 'five excesses' means that the qi of the diseases is in excess; the 'two deficiencies' likewise means that the disease qi is deficient. Now, if externally one finds the five excesses and internally the two deficiencies, then the body is neither in a treatable exterior nor interior state, and it is clear that death is certain.

Huangdi said: There are people who are born already suffering from an epileptic disorder — what is this disease called, and how is it contracted? Qibo said: This disease is called 'fetal disease.' It is contracted while the child is still in the mother's womb: the mother suffers a great fright, so that her qi rises and does not descend, and the essence-qi of mother and child become lodged together; this causes the child to develop the epileptic disorder.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the face appears swollen and puffy as though there were water beneath the skin; on palpating the pulse it is large and tight; the body has no pain; the form is not emaciated, yet the patient cannot eat, or eats very little — what is this disease called? Qibo said: This disease arises in the kidney and is called 'kidney wind.' When a person with kidney wind cannot eat and is prone to fright, and after the fright the heart qi becomes withered and weak, the patient dies. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Great Anomalies
paragraphs_en: When the liver, kidney, or lung is full, all are cases of excess, and this results in swelling.
