When the lung is congested, there is panting and fullness on both sides beneath the armpits; when the liver is congested, there is fullness on both sides beneath the armpits, and when lying down the patient startles easily, and urination is obstructed. When the kidney is congested, there is fullness from the feet up to the lower abdomen, the two shins become unequal in size, the thigh and shin become enlarged, the patient limps, and this readily turns into hemiplegia.

When the heart pulse is full and large, there is epilepsy with convulsions and sinew spasms. When the liver pulse is small and tight, there is likewise epilepsy with convulsions and sinew spasms. When the liver pulse gallops and races wildly, it indicates the patient has suffered a fright; if the pulse suddenly fails to arrive, or the patient becomes mute, it will resolve of itself without treatment. When the kidney pulse, the liver pulse, or the heart pulse is small and tight, and does not beat vigorously, all these indicate an abdominal mass. When the kidney and liver pulses are both deep, this is 'stone water'; when both are floating, this is 'wind water'; when both are deficient, it is fatal; when both are small and wiry, the patient is about to be startled.

When the kidney pulse is large, tight, and deep, or the liver pulse is large, tight, and deep, in both cases this is a hernia-like disorder. When the heart pulse beats forcefully, is slippery, and tight, this is 'heart hernia.' When the lung pulse is deep and beats forcefully, this is 'lung hernia.' When the three yang pulses are tight, this indicates an abdominal mass; when the three yin pulses are tight, this indicates a hernia-like disorder; when the two yin pulses are tight, this indicates epilepsy with reversal; when the two yang pulses are tight, this indicates fright.

When the spleen pulse bulges outward yet is deep, this is intestinal flux; in time it resolves of itself. When the liver pulse is small and moderate, this is intestinal flux that is easy to treat. When the kidney pulse is small and forceful, yet deep, this is intestinal flux with blood in the stool; if the blood is warm and the body is hot, the patient dies. When both the heart and liver are afflicted with flux, there is also blood in the stool; when the two viscera are diseased together, it can be treated. If the pulse is small, deep, and rough, this is intestinal flux; if the body is hot, the patient dies — and if the heat persists for seven days, death occurs on the seventh day.

When the stomach pulse is deep, bulging, and rough, or bulges outward and is large, or when the heart pulse is small, hard, and tight — in all these cases it results in hemiplegia. In men it tends to appear on the left side, in women on the right; if the patient is not mute and the tongue still moves freely, it can be treated, and the patient will recover in thirty days. If the condition follows its natural course and the patient becomes mute, recovery takes three years. If the patient is not yet twenty years of age, death occurs within three years.

When the pulse arrives beating forcefully, and there is nosebleed with fever, the patient dies. A pulse that comes suspended like a hook, floating, is a normal pulse. When the pulse arrives as though panting, this is called 'sudden reversal'; in sudden reversal, the patient loses the ability to speak with others. When the pulse arrives rapid as in a racing count, it causes the patient to suddenly startle; this resolves of itself in three or four days.

When the pulse arrives floating and merging, floating and merging as though rapid, with ten or more beats to a single breath, this indicates that the channel qi is about to become insufficient; if this is seen only faintly, death occurs within ninety days. When the pulse arrives like a flame from burning firewood, this indicates that the essence of the heart is about to be lost; the patient dies when the grass withers. When the pulse arrives like scattering leaves, this indicates that the qi of the liver is about to become deficient; the patient dies when the leaves fall from the trees. When the pulse arrives like a visiting guest being announced — meaning the pulse is blocked yet still beats — this indicates that the qi of the kidney is about to become insufficient; the patient dies when the jujube flowers fall. When the pulse arrives like a pellet of clay, this indicates that the essence of the stomach is about to become insufficient; the patient dies when the elm pods fall. When the pulse arrives like a crosswise bar, this indicates that the qi of the gallbladder is about to become insufficient; the patient dies when the grain ripens. When the pulse arrives like a taut string, this indicates that the essence of the uterus is about to become insufficient; if the patient talks excessively, death occurs when the frost falls; if the patient does not speak, this can be treated. When the pulse arrives like crossing lacquer — meaning it arrives now from the left, now from the right — if this is seen only faintly, death occurs within thirty days. When the pulse arrives like a gushing spring, floating and beating, this indicates that the Taiyang qi within the flesh is about to become insufficient; when the patient's breath and appetite grow scant, death occurs when the leek flowers bloom. When the pulse arrives like crumbling earth, so that pressing upon it yields nothing, this indicates that the qi of the flesh is about to become insufficient; when the five complexions appear, first black then white in succession, death occurs. When the pulse arrives like the uvula, floating, and pressing and palpating it makes it seem to grow even larger, this indicates that the qi of the twelve transport points is about to become insufficient; death occurs when the water freezes. When the pulse arrives like an overturned blade — meaning that on the surface it is small and tight, but pressing down it is hard, large, and tight — this indicates that the five viscera are congested with heat, and cold and heat converge singly in the kidney; such a patient cannot sit up, and death occurs at the Beginning of Spring. When the pulse arrives like a rolling pellet, slippery, and cannot be caught beneath the hand — meaning that pressing upon it yields nothing — this indicates that the qi of the large intestine is about to become insufficient; death occurs when the jujube leaves sprout. When the pulse arrives like a flower, causing the patient to be constantly fearful, unwilling to sit or lie down, walking and standing while constantly listening as if for something, this indicates that the qi of the small intestine is about to become insufficient; death occurs in the last month of autumn.

素问.title_en: Explanation of the Channels
paragraphs_en: As for the Taiyang disorder in which there is swelling and pain in the lower back and buttocks: in the first month, Taiyang corresponds to the earthly branch yin; yin is Taiyang. In the first month the yang qi is emerging and rising above, while the yin qi is still abundant, so the yang has not yet been able to take its proper place in sequence; hence there is swelling and pain in the lower back and buttocks. As for the disease in which one side becomes deficient, causing lameness: in the first month the yang qi is thawing the frozen ground and the earth-qi is emerging; what is called 'one-sided deficiency' refers to the residual insufficiency left by the winter cold, and hence the one-sided deficiency causes lameness. As for what is called 'stiffness above pulling on the back': the yang qi is rising vigorously upward and contending with the remaining yin, and therefore there is stiffness above. As for what is called 'ringing in the ears': the yang qi, together with the qi of the ten thousand things, is flourishing and rising upward and leaping about, and therefore there is ringing in the ears. As for what is called, in severe cases, 'mania and epileptic disorders': the yang is entirely above while the yin qi follows from below, so that below is deficient and above is in excess, and therefore there is mania and epileptic disorder. As for what is called 'floating pulse causing deafness': this is entirely a matter of qi. As for what is called 'entering the interior and causing muteness': the yang, having been abundant, has now declined, and therefore there is muteness. When there is an internal loss of qi leading to reversal, this becomes 'muteness and flaccidity'; this is a case of kidney deficiency. When the Shaoyin qi fails to arrive, this is reversal.

As for the Shaoyang disorder in which there is pain in the heart region and the ribs: this means that the Shaoyang qi is in excess, and this excess is expressed on the exterior of the heart. In the ninth month the yang qi is exhausted and the yin qi is abundant, and therefore there is pain in the heart region and ribs. As for what is called 'unable to turn from side to side': the yin qi stores away the ten thousand things, and when things are stored away they do not move, and therefore the patient cannot turn from side to side. As for what is called, in severe cases, 'leaping': in the ninth month the ten thousand things are all in decline, the grasses and trees have all shed their leaves and fallen, so that the qi departs from the yang and goes to the yin; when the qi is abundant, the yang grows below, and hence this is called 'leaping.'

As for the Yangming disorder in which there is shivering and chills: Yangming corresponds to the branch wu; the fifth month is when yang is at its most abundant, yet already contains the beginning of yin. When the yang is abundant and yin qi is added to it, there is shivering and chills. As for what is called 'swelling of the shins with inability to draw in the thighs': this too belongs to the fifth month when yang is most abundant yet turning toward yin. The yang begins to decline in the fifth month, and a single yin qi rises and begins to contend with the yang; therefore there is swelling of the shins and inability to draw in the thighs. As for what is called 'panting above and turning into water': the yin qi descends and then rises again; when it rises, evil qi lodges between the viscera and bowels, and therefore water results. As for what is called 'chest pain and shortness of breath': water qi is present in the viscera and bowels; water is yin qi, and when yin qi is in the center, there is chest pain and shortness of breath. As for what is called, in severe cases, 'reversal, aversion to people and to fire, and being startled with alarm on hearing the sound of wood being struck': the yang qi and yin qi are clashing with each other, water and fire mutually detest each other, and therefore the patient starts with alarm. As for what is called 'wishing to shut oneself alone behind closed doors and windows': yin and yang are clashing with each other; the yang is exhausted and the yin is abundant, and therefore the patient wishes to dwell alone behind closed doors and windows. As for what is called, when the disease arrives, 'wishing to climb to a high place and sing, or to cast off one's clothes and run about': yin and yang are once again contending, and on the outside they combine with the yang, and this causes the patient to cast off his clothes and run about. As for what is called 'lodging in the grandchild vessels, causing headache, nasal congestion, and abdominal swelling': the Yangming qi combines above; when it is above, it is because its grandchild-connecting vessels reach the Taiyin, and therefore there is headache, nasal congestion, and abdominal swelling.

As for the Taiyin disorder called 'distension': Taiyin corresponds to the branch zi; in the eleventh month the qi of the ten thousand things is all stored within, and therefore the disease is called 'distension.' As for what is called 'rising to the heart and causing belching': the yin is abundant and rises to the Yangming, and the connecting vessel of the Yangming attaches to the heart, and therefore it is called 'rising to the heart and causing belching.' As for what is called 'vomiting upon eating': things are abundant and full and overflow upward, and therefore there is vomiting. As for what is called 'feeling relieved as though the illness had eased after passing stool or gas': in the twelfth month the yin qi is declining below while the yang qi is about to emerge, and therefore it is said that after passing stool or gas, one feels relieved as though the illness had eased.

As for the Shaoyin disorder called 'lower back pain': Shaoyin is the kidney; in the tenth month the yang qi of the ten thousand things is all injured, and therefore there is lower back pain. As for what is called 'vomiting, coughing, and panting with qi rising': the yin qi is below and the yang qi is above; all the yang qi is floating, with nothing to depend upon or follow, and therefore there is vomiting, coughing, and panting with rising qi. As for what is called 'restlessness, inability to stand for long, and upon rising after sitting long the eyes grow dim and see nothing': the yin and yang of the ten thousand things are unsettled and have not yet found their ruler. As the autumn qi begins to arrive and the first light frost begins to fall, it is just then killing off the ten thousand things; the yin and yang are drawn inward, and therefore the eyes grow dim and see nothing. As for what is called 'shortness of breath and irritability': the yang qi is not governing properly; when the yang qi does not govern, it cannot emerge, and the liver qi, which ought to govern, cannot yet do so, and therefore there is irritability; this condition of being prone to anger is called 'simmering reversal.' As for what is called 'fear as though someone were about to seize the patient': in autumn the qi of the ten thousand things has not yet entirely departed; the yin qi is scant and the yang qi is entering, and yin and yang clash with each other, and therefore there is fear. As for what is called 'aversion to the smell of food': the stomach has no qi, and therefore there is aversion to the smell of food. As for what is called 'the face being black as the color of earth': the autumn qi has been drawn inward, and therefore the complexion changes. As for what is called 'coughing accompanied by blood': the yang vessel has been injured; the yang qi, not yet fully abundant above, causes the vessel to become full, and when it is full there is coughing, and therefore blood appears from the nose.

As for the Jueyin disorder called 'scrotal hernia-swelling and, in women, swelling of the lower abdomen': Jueyin corresponds to the branch chen; in the third month, evil qi lies within the yin that is contained within the yang, and therefore it is called 'hernia-swelling and swelling of the lower abdomen.' As for what is called 'pain in the lower back and spine, unable to bend forward or backward': in the third month all things burst forth in a single wave of flourishing growth, bending down but not rising back up. As for what is called 'hernia-swelling, urinary blockage, and skin swelling': it is said that the yin is also abundant, so that the vessels are distended and impassable, and therefore it is called 'hernia-swelling and urinary blockage.' As for what is called, in severe cases, 'dryness of the throat and heat in the middle': yin and yang clash with each other and produce heat, and therefore the throat is dry.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Essentials of Needling
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I wish to hear about the essentials of needling. Qibo replied: Disease has degrees of superficiality and depth, and needling has corresponding degrees of shallowness and depth; each must reach its proper level, and must not exceed its proper path. If the needle exceeds the proper depth, it injures the interior; if it does not reach far enough, it gives rise to blockage on the exterior, and blockage allows evil qi to follow it in. When shallowness and depth are not properly matched, the needling instead becomes a great harm, stirring the five viscera within and afterward giving rise to serious disease. Therefore it is said: disease may lie in the fine hairs and interstices of the skin, or in the skin, or in the muscles and flesh, or in the vessels, or in the sinews, or in the bones, or in the marrow. Therefore, when needling the fine hairs and interstices, one must not injure the skin; if the skin is injured, it stirs the lung within, and when the lung is stirred, in autumn the patient will suffer warm malaria, with shivering and chills. When needling the skin, one must not injure the flesh; if the flesh is injured, it stirs the spleen within, and when the spleen is stirred, on the seventy-second day of each of the four seasons the patient will suffer abdominal distension and vexation, and loss of appetite. When needling the flesh, one must not injure the vessels; if the vessels are injured, it stirs the heart within, and when the heart is stirred, in summer the patient will suffer heart pain. When needling the vessels, one must not injure the sinews; if the sinews are injured, it stirs the liver within, and when the liver is stirred, in spring the patient will suffer fever with slackening of the sinews. When needling the sinews, one must not injure the bones; if the bones are injured, it stirs the kidney within, and when the kidney is stirred, in winter the patient will suffer distension and lower back pain. When needling the bones, one must not injure the marrow; if the marrow is injured, it will melt away, the shins will ache, and the body will become languid and weak, unable to recover.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Correct Depths of Needling
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I wish to hear about the distinctions between shallow and deep needling. Qibo replied: When needling the bone, do not injure the sinews; when needling the sinews, do not injure the flesh; when needling the flesh, do not injure the vessels; when needling the vessels, do not injure the skin. Conversely, when needling the skin, do not injure the flesh; when needling the flesh, do not injure the sinews; when needling the sinews, do not injure the bone.

Huangdi said: I do not yet understand what this means; I wish to hear it explained. Qibo said: The fault of 'needling the bone yet injuring the sinews' means that the needle reaches only as far as the sinews and then withdraws, not reaching the bone. The fault of 'needling the sinews yet injuring the flesh' means the needle reaches only the flesh and withdraws, not reaching the sinews. The fault of 'needling the flesh yet injuring the vessels' means the needle reaches only the vessels and withdraws, not reaching the flesh. The fault of 'needling the vessels yet injuring the skin' means the needle reaches only the skin and withdraws, not reaching the vessels. As for what is called 'needling the skin yet injuring the flesh': the disease lies within the skin, but the needle, entering the skin, goes on to injure the flesh. 'Needling the flesh yet injuring the sinews' means the needle passes beyond the flesh into the sinews. 'Needling the sinews yet injuring the bone' means the needle passes beyond the sinews into the bone. This is what is meant by 'reversal,' the opposite of correct practice.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Needling Prohibitions
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I wish to hear the rules of prohibition in needling. Qibo replied: The viscera have their critical and vulnerable points, which must not go unexamined. The liver's qi arises on the left; the lung stores its qi on the right; the heart is positioned toward the exterior; the kidney governs the interior; the spleen acts as their envoy; the stomach serves as their marketplace. Above the diaphragm and the membrane source there lie the 'father and mother'; beside the seventh vertebra there lies the 'little heart.' Following these principles brings good fortune; going against them brings calamity.

If needling strikes the heart, death occurs within one day, and its sign is belching. If needling strikes the liver, death occurs within five days, and its sign is talkativeness. If needling strikes the kidney, death occurs within six days, and its sign is sneezing. If needling strikes the lung, death occurs within three days, and its sign is coughing. If needling strikes the spleen, death occurs within ten days, and its sign is swallowing. If needling strikes the gallbladder, death occurs within a day and a half, and its sign is vomiting.

If needling the instep strikes a large vessel, bleeding will not stop, and the patient dies. If needling the face strikes the liu vessel, the unfortunate result is blindness. If needling the head strikes the Naohu point and enters the brain, death is immediate. If needling beneath the tongue strikes the vessel too deeply, bleeding will not stop and causes muteness. If needling beneath the foot damages the connecting vessel and strikes the main vessel, and no blood emerges, swelling results. If needling the Xi-cleft point strikes a large vessel, it causes the patient to collapse and lose color. If needling the Qichong point strikes the vessel and no blood emerges, it causes swelling like a groin abscess. If needling between the vertebrae strikes the marrow, it causes hunching of the back. If needling above the breast strikes the breast tissue, it causes swelling that erodes at its root. If needling the Quepen point sinks in too deep, qi leaks out and causes the patient to pant and cough with rebellious qi. If needling the thenar eminence of the hand sinks in too deep, swelling results. Do not needle a person who is greatly intoxicated, for it will cause the qi to become disordered. Do not needle a person who is greatly enraged, for it will cause the qi to rebel. Do not needle a person who is greatly fatigued; do not needle a person who has just eaten to fullness; do not needle a person who is greatly hungry; do not needle a person who is greatly thirsty; do not needle a person who has been greatly frightened. If needling the inner thigh strikes a large vessel, bleeding will not stop, and the patient dies. If needling Kezhuren sinks in too deep and strikes the vessel, it causes internal leakage and deafness. If needling the kneecap causes fluid to escape, lameness results. If needling the Taiyin vessel of the arm causes excessive bleeding, death is immediate. If needling the Shaoyin vessel of the foot causes severe depletion through bleeding, it causes difficulty moving the tongue and speaking. If needling the chest sinks in too deep and strikes the lung, it causes panting, rebellious qi, and labored breathing with the head thrown back. If needling the elbow sinks in too deep, the qi gathers there, and the joint cannot bend or extend. If needling the inner thigh three cun below sinks in too deep, it causes incontinence of urine. If needling beneath the armpit, in the rib region, sinks in too deep, it causes coughing. If needling the lower abdomen strikes the bladder so that urine escapes, it causes fullness of the lower abdomen. If needling the calf sinks in too deep, swelling results. If needling above the eye socket strikes the vessel within the bony depression, it causes leakage of fluid and blindness. If needling a joint causes fluid to escape, the joint can no longer bend or extend.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Purport of Needling
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I wish to hear the essentials of deficiency and excess. Qibo replied: When the qi is in excess, the body form is correspondingly full; when the qi is deficient, the body form is correspondingly weak — this is the normal pattern, and when it is otherwise, there is disease. When food intake is abundant, the qi is correspondingly abundant; when food intake is scant, the qi is correspondingly scant — this is the normal pattern, and when it is otherwise, there is disease. When the pulse is full, the blood is correspondingly full; when the pulse is deficient, the blood is correspondingly scant — this is the normal pattern, and when it is otherwise, there is disease.

Huangdi said: What constitutes 'otherwise'? Qibo said: When the qi is deficient yet the body is hot, this is called 'otherwise.' When food intake is abundant yet the qi is scant, this is called 'otherwise.' When food does not enter yet the qi is abundant, this is called 'otherwise.' When the pulse is full yet the blood is scant, this is called 'otherwise.' When the pulse is scant yet the blood is abundant, this is called 'otherwise.'

When the qi is abundant yet the body is cold, this is contracted from cold damage. When the qi is deficient yet the body is hot, this is contracted from summerheat damage. When food intake is abundant yet the qi is scant, this is contracted when there has been some loss of blood, or when dampness settles below. When food intake is scant yet the qi is abundant, this is because evil qi lies in the stomach and also in the lung. When the pulse is small yet the blood is abundant, this is heat retained within drink. When the pulse is large yet the blood is scant, this is because wind qi is present in the vessel, and fluids and beverages cannot be taken in — this is what is meant.

Now, excess means that qi is entering; deficiency means that qi is departing. When the qi is in excess, there is heat; when the qi is deficient, there is cold. When inserting the needle for a condition of excess, the left hand should keep the needle hole open. When inserting the needle for a condition of deficiency, the left hand should close the needle hole.

素问.title_en: Explication of the Needles
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I wish to hear the explanation of the nine needles, and the principle of deficiency and excess. Qibo replied: "Needling deficiency so as to make it replete" means that heat appears beneath the needle — once the qi becomes replete, heat follows. "When it is full, drain it" means that cold appears beneath the needle — once the qi becomes deficient, cold follows. "When there is stagnant old blood, remove it" means letting out the malign blood. "When the evil prevails, deplete it" means withdrawing the needle without pressing the opening closed. "Slow then quick makes it replete" means withdrawing the needle slowly while pressing the opening quickly. "Quick then slow makes it deficient" means withdrawing the needle quickly while pressing the opening slowly. To speak of excess and deficiency is to speak of whether the qi's coldness or warmth is abundant or scant. "As if absent, as if present" means that the disease cannot readily be discerned. "Examining what follows and what precedes" means knowing the sequence in which the disease arose. In treating deficiency and excess, the physician must not depart from the proper method; "as if gained, as if lost" describes a departure from that method. The essential point of deficiency and excess is that the nine needles are most marvelous precisely because each is suited to its own particular use. The timing of supplementation and drainage corresponds to the opening and closing of the qi. That the nine needles differ in name and form is because each needle is fashioned to fully accomplish the supplementation or drainage proper to it.

In needling excess, one must wait for it to turn to deficiency: retain the needle until the yin qi arrives in abundance, and only then withdraw it. In needling deficiency, one must wait for it to turn to repletion: retain the needle until the yang qi arrives in abundance and heat appears beneath it, and only then withdraw it. "Once the channel qi has arrived, guard it carefully and do not lose it" means one must not alter the needling thereafter. "Depth and shallowness lie in the intent" means knowing whether the disease lies within or without. "Near and far are treated as one" means that shallow and deep needling await the same signs before completion. "As if approaching a deep abyss" means one dares not be careless. "The hand as if grasping a tiger" means one wishes it to be firm and resolute. "The spirit should not be occupied by the multitude of things" means calming one's intent and observing the patient without glancing left or right. "The purpose should admit no deviant descent of the needle" means wishing the needle to enter upright and true. "One must rectify the spirit" means wishing to gaze into the patient's eyes so as to govern their spirit, thereby making the qi move with ease.

What is called "Three Li" lies three cun below the knee. What is called "the instep point" is found by raising the knee, whereupon the dividing line becomes easy to see. "Great Void" is found by raising the foot, at the place where the shin bone alone forms a hollow. "Lower Ridge" is the place of depression.

Huangdi said: I have heard that the nine needles correspond above to heaven and earth, the four seasons, and yin and yang. I wish to hear their model, so that it may be handed down to later generations as a lasting standard. Qibo said: One is heaven, two is earth, three is man, four is the four seasons, five is the five notes, six is the six pitch-pipes, seven is the seven stars, eight is the eight winds, and nine is the nine fields — the form of the human body corresponds to all of these, and each needle has that to which it is suited; hence they are called the nine needles. Man's skin corresponds to heaven, his flesh to earth, his vessels to man, his sinews to the seasons, his voice to the notes, the union of yin and yang qi within him to the pitch-pipes, his teeth, face, and eyes to the stars, the outgoing and incoming of his breath to the winds, and his nine orifices together with his three hundred sixty-five connecting vessels to the fields. Hence the first needle treats the skin, the second the flesh, the third the vessels, the fourth the sinews, the fifth the bones, the sixth regulates yin and yang, the seventh benefits the essence, the eighth dispels wind, and the ninth opens the nine orifices and clears the qi of the three hundred sixty-five joints — this is what is meant by each having its own particular office.

Man's heart and intent correspond to the eight winds; man's qi corresponds to heaven; man's hair, teeth, ears, eyes, and the five sounds correspond to the five notes and six pitch-pipes; man's yin and yang vessels together with his blood and qi correspond to earth; man's liver and eyes correspond to the number nine, as do his nine orifices and three hundred sixty-five connecting vessels. The unity of man serves to observe motion and stillness; heaven's duality serves to examine the five colors, to which the seven stars correspond in judging whether the hair is lustrous; the oneness of the five notes serves to examine gong, shang, jue, zhi, and yu, to which the surplus and insufficiency of the six pitch-pipes correspond; earth's duality serves to examine height and depth and their surplus; the unity of the nine fields, with its single joint and transport-point, corresponds to this in examining the closing of the joints. The threefold division of man varies: in one part one examines the teeth, where discharge is heavy and blood scant; in its tenfold division, the variation at the corners; in its fivefold division, one examines slowness and urgency; in its sixfold division, insufficiency; in its threefold division, cold at the joints; in its ninth division, the four seasons. Man's cold, warmth, dryness, and dampness correspond each to one of the four seasons, whereby one examines their contrariety; each of the four directions is to be explained in turn.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Extended Needling Regimens
paragraphs_en: The needling specialist does not diagnose but simply listens as the patient reports the pain lying in the head. When there is disease of the head with pain, insert the needle deeply, needling until it reaches the bone, and the disease will end — yet one must not injure the bone, flesh, or skin above; the skin is merely the pathway through which the needle passes.

In yin needling, one needle is inserted at the center and four more around it; this treats cold and heat. When the disease lies deep and concentrated, needle the great viscera; to approach the viscera, needle the back — at the back transport-points. When needling to approach the viscera, at the meeting-points of the viscera, stop once the cold and heat in the abdomen have been dispelled; the essential of this needling is to withdraw the needle so that blood escapes only shallowly.

In treating festering swellings, needle above the festering site; observe whether the abscess is large or small and needle shallow or deep accordingly — for a large one, let out much blood; for a small one, needle deeply. One must insert the needle straight and true, and stop only once this has been properly accomplished.

When the disease lies in the lower abdomen with an accumulation, needle below the pubic region downward, stopping at the lower abdomen; needle the points flanking the spine between the fourth vertebrae, and needle between the iliac points and the free ribs, so as to conduct the hot qi of the abdomen downward until it is dispelled.

When the disease lies in the lower abdomen, with abdominal pain and inability to urinate or defecate, the disease is called shan; it is contracted from cold. Needle between the lower abdomen and the two thighs, and needle between the bones of the lumbar region, needling repeatedly until warmth pervades throughout and the disease ends.

When the disease lies in the sinews, with sinew spasm and joint pain that prevents walking, it is called sinew impediment. Needle above the sinews as the rule, needling between the muscle divisions without striking the bone; the disease resolves once the sinews grow warm, and then one stops.

When the disease lies in the flesh and skin, with all the flesh and skin aching, it is called flesh impediment, arising from injury by cold and dampness. Needle the large and small muscle divisions, inserting the needle repeatedly and deeply, taking warmth as the guide, without injuring sinew or bone — for if sinew or bone is injured, an abscess or other ill transformation may arise. Once all the divisions have grown thoroughly warm, the disease ends and one stops.

When the disease lies in the bones, with the bones so heavy they cannot be lifted and the marrow aching upon the arrival of cold qi, it is called bone impediment. Needle deeply without injuring vessel or flesh as the rule, proceeding along the large and small muscle divisions; once the bones grow warm, the disease ends and one stops.

When the disease lies among the yang vessels generally, with alternating cold and heat, and all the muscle divisions likewise alternating between cold and heat, it is called mania. Needle so as to deplete the vessels; once all the divisions have grown thoroughly warm, the disease ends and one stops.

When the disease first arises, it occurs once a year; if untreated, once a month; if still untreated, four or five times a month — this is called epilepsy. Needle all the muscle divisions and vessels; where there is no cold, regulate with the needle, and the disease stops.

When the disease is wind, with alternating cold and heat and profuse sweating occurring several times a day, first needle the muscle divisions to regulate the connecting vessels; while the sweating with alternating cold and heat continues, needle once every three days, and after a hundred days it will end.

When the disease is great wind, with the bones and joints heavy and the beard and eyebrows falling out, it is called great wind. Needle the flesh and muscles as the rule until sweating occurs, continuing for a hundred days; then needle the bones and marrow until sweating occurs, for another hundred days — two hundred days in all — and when the beard and eyebrows have regrown, cease the needling.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Regions of the Skin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that the skin has its divided regions, the vessels their regulating channels, the sinews their knots and links, and the bones their measurements, and that the diseases arising from each differ. I wish to distinguish these regions — left and right, upper and lower, where yin and yang lie — and to understand the beginning and end of disease within them. I wish to hear this principle. Qibo replied: To know the regions of the skin, one takes the channels and vessels as the guide; this holds true for every channel. The yang aspect of yang brightness is called Hai Fei; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of yang brightness. When their color is predominantly green, there is pain; predominantly black, there is impediment; yellow-red, there is heat; predominantly white, there is cold; and when all five colors appear together, there is cold and heat alternating. When the connecting vessels grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel; yang governs the exterior, yin governs the interior. The yang aspect of lesser yang is called Shu Chi; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of lesser yang. When they grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel; hence when it lies in the yang aspect it tends inward, and when it lies in the yin aspect it tends outward so as to seep inward — this holds true for every channel. The yang aspect of greater yang is called Guan Shu; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of greater yang. When they grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel. The yin aspect of lesser yin is called Shu Ru; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of lesser yin. When they grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel; upon entering the channel it pours in from the yang region, and upon leaving it pours inward from the yin aspect into the bone. The yin aspect of the pericardium is called Hai Jian; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of the pericardium. When they grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel. The yin aspect of greater yin is called Guan Zhe; the rule is the same above and below. Observing the floating connecting vessels within its region — these are all connecting vessels of greater yin. When they grow exuberant, the pathogen enters and lodges in the channel. In sum, the twelve channels and their connecting vessels constitute the regions of the skin.

Therefore, when the hundred diseases first arise, they must begin in the skin and body hair. When a pathogen strikes there, the interstices open; once open, it enters and lodges in the connecting vessels; if it lingers without departing, it is transmitted into the channels; if it lingers there still without departing, it is transmitted into the bowels, where it accumulates in the intestines and stomach. When the pathogen first enters the skin, it produces a shivering that raises the fine hairs and opens the interstices. When it enters the connecting vessels, the connecting vessels grow full and their color changes. When it enters and lodges in the channels, the channels, sensing the deficiency, sink and collapse. When it lingers between sinew and bone, if cold predominates the sinews cramp and the bones ache; if heat predominates the sinews go slack and the bones waste, the flesh melts and the muscle masses break down, and the body hair stands erect and turns lusterless. Huangdi said: You, Master, have spoken of the twelve regions of the skin — how do the diseases arising within them present themselves? Qibo said: The skin is the region of the vessels. When a pathogen lodges in the skin, the interstices open; once open, the pathogen enters and lodges in the connecting vessels; when the connecting vessels become full, it pours into the channel vessels; when the channel vessels become full, it enters and takes up residence in the bowels and viscera. Hence, although the skin has its divided regions, if the disease is not addressed there, it will give rise to grave illness. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Channels and Connecting Vessels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When the connecting vessels become visible, their five colors differ — green, yellow, red, white, and black are not the same. What is the reason for this? Qibo replied: The channels have constant colors, while the connecting vessels change without constancy. Huangdi said: What are the constant colors of the channels? Qibo said: The heart is red, the lung white, the liver green, the spleen yellow, and the kidney black — each corresponds to the color of its own channel vessel. Huangdi said: Do the yin and yang aspects of the connecting vessels likewise correspond to their channels? Qibo said: The color of the yin connecting vessels corresponds to their channel, but the color of the yang connecting vessels changes without constancy, following the movement of the four seasons. When cold predominates, the blood congeals and stagnates, and this congealing and stagnation produces green-black; when heat predominates, the blood becomes moist and flowing, and this produces yellow-red. These are all normal colors, said to indicate the absence of disease. When all five colors appear together, this is said to indicate cold and heat. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Qi Cavities
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that there are three hundred sixty-five qi cavities, corresponding to the days of one year, but I do not know their locations. I wish to hear of them in full. Qibo bowed his head and prostrated himself twice, replying: What a formidable question! Were you not a sagely sovereign, who could exhaust this subject? Allow your servant, then, to speak his mind fully and set forth their locations in their entirety. Huangdi clasped his hands, drew back respectfully, and said: You, Master, have opened this path for me — though my eyes have not yet seen these places nor my ears heard their number, already my eyes feel clearer and my ears sharper. Qibo said: This is what is meant by saying that the sage is easy to instruct, as a fine horse is easy to drive. Huangdi said: I am no sage so easily instructed. It is said in the world that the true numbers open a person's understanding; what I now inquire after are these true numbers, to dispel ignorance and resolve doubt — a matter not yet fit to call settled. Still, I wish to hear you, Master, speak your mind fully and set forth their locations completely, that their meaning may be understood; I shall store this in a golden casket and dare not let it out again. Qibo bowed twice, rose, and said: Your servant will speak of it. When the back and the heart pull upon each other and ache, treat Tiantu together with the tenth vertebra and the Upper Bond — the Upper Bond being the stomach duct, and the Lower Bond being Guanyuan. The back and chest are obliquely linked through yin and yang, left and right. When this disease occurs, there is pain and roughness both front and back, pain in the chest and flank with inability to breathe freely or to lie down, rising qi and shortness of breath with pain to one side; the vessel grows full and rises obliquely to emerge at the sacral vessel, links with the chest and flank, branches to the heart, pierces the diaphragm, ascends to the shoulder to join Tiantu, and then descends obliquely across the shoulder to meet below the tenth vertebra. There are fifty transport-points of the viscera; seventy-two transport-points of the bowels; fifty-nine transport-points for heat; and fifty-seven transport-points for water. Atop the head there are five rows of five points, twenty-five points in all. Flanking the central spine, five points to each side, ten in all. Above the Great Vertebra, one point to each side, two in all. The pupil and Fubai points, two. Between the two hip joints, two. Dubi, two. "Much Heard Within the Ear," two. The roots of the eyebrows, two. Wangu, two. The center of the crown, one. The occipital bone, two. Shangguan, two. Daying, two. Xiaguan, two. Tianzhu, two. The upper and lower ridges of the Great Void, four. Quya, two. Tiantu, one. Tianfu, two. Tianyou, two. Futu, two. Tianchuang, two. The shoulder joints, two. Guanyuan, one. Weiyang, two. Jianzhen, two. Yinmen, one. The navel, one. The chest transport-points, twelve. The back transport-points, two. The chest-front transport-points, twelve. Fenrou, two. The transverse points above the ankle, two. The yin and yang springing vessels, four. The water transport-points lie among the various muscle divisions; the heat transport-points lie at the qi cavities; the cold-heat transport-points are the two points within the hip joints. The twenty-five great forbidden points lie five cun below Tianfu. In all there are three hundred sixty-five points, the course by which the needle proceeds.

Huangdi said: I now know the locations of the qi cavities, the dwelling places through which the needle travels. I wish to hear whether the grandchild-connecting vessels and the valleys and ravines likewise have their correspondences. Qibo said: The grandchild-connecting vessels have three hundred sixty-five meeting points, which likewise correspond to the days of one year; through them unusual pathogens overflow and nutritive and defensive qi are made to circulate. When nutritive and defensive qi become detained, defensive qi disperses and nutritive qi overflows; qi becomes exhausted and blood stagnates, appearing outwardly as fever and inwardly as shortness of breath. Drain promptly without delay, so as to open the passage of nutritive and defensive qi; wherever stagnation is seen, drain it, without needing to ask which meeting point it is. Huangdi said: Good.

I wish to hear of the meeting places of the valleys and ravines. Qibo said: Where the flesh gathers greatly, it forms a "valley"; where the flesh gathers in a small way, it forms a "ravine." Between the divisions of the flesh, at the meeting of valley and ravine, nutritive and defensive qi circulate and the great qi convenes. When pathogenic qi overflows and qi becomes obstructed, the vessels grow hot and the flesh decays; once nutritive and defensive qi cease to circulate, this will inevitably form pus, consuming the marrow within and rupturing the great muscle masses without; if it lingers at the joints and junctures, ruin will inevitably follow. When accumulated cold lodges and lingers, nutritive and defensive qi lose their place; the flesh contracts and the sinews shrink, so that the ribs and elbows cannot extend; internally this becomes bone impediment, externally it becomes numbness — this is called insufficiency, caused by great cold lodging in the valleys and ravines. The valleys and ravines have three hundred sixty-five meeting points, likewise corresponding to one year. When minor impediment spreads and overflows, traveling back and forth along the vessels, the fine needle can reach it, following the same method as for the qi cavities.

Huangdi then dismissed his attendants, rose, and bowed twice, saying: Today my ignorance has been dispelled and my doubts resolved; I shall store this in a golden casket and dare not let it out again — I shall keep it in the Chamber of Gold and Orchid, inscribed "The Locations of the Qi Cavities." Qibo said: Among the grandchild-connecting vessels branching from the channels, those whose blood grows exuberant and ought to be drained likewise number three hundred sixty-five vessels; together they pour into the connecting vessels, transmitting into the twelve connecting vessels — not the fourteen connecting vessels alone. Of these, ten vessels are resolved and drained internally.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Repositories of Qi
paragraphs_en: The points where the qi of the foot greater yang vessel emerges number seventy-eight: one at each end of the eyebrows; from the hairline to the nape, three and a half cun, with five points to each side spaced three cun apart; where the floating qi lies within the skin there are in all five rows of five points, twenty-five in total; one on each side of the great sinew at the nape; one on each side of Fengfu; flanking the spine down to the sacrum and coccyx, one at each of the fifteen spaces among the twenty-one vertebrae; five transport-points for each of the five viscera; six transport-points for each of the six bowels; and from Weizhong down to beside the little toe, six transport-points on each side.

The points where the qi of the foot lesser yang vessel emerges number sixty-two: two at each temple; five straight above each eye within the hairline; one above and one below each ear in front; one below each pointed hairline; one at each Kezhuren; one in the hollow behind each ear; one at each Xiaguan; one behind the jaw below each ear; one at each Quepen; one at each of the eight spaces from three cun below the armpit down along the flank to the hypochondrium; one on each side at the center of the hip joint; and from the knee down to the fourth toe, six transport-points on each side.

The points where the qi of the foot yang brightness vessel emerges number sixty-eight: three on each side of the forehead hairline; one at each cheekbone hollow; one at each Daying bone hollow; one at each Renying; one at each bone hollow outside Quepen; one at each bone space within the chest; flanking the outside of Jiuwei, level with three cun below the nipple, flanking the stomach duct, five on each side; flanking the navel, three cun out, three on each side; two cun below the navel, flanking it, three on each side; one at each pulsating vessel of Qijie; one above each Futu; from Zusanli down to the middle toe, eight transport-points on each side — these are the points located at the muscle divisions.

The points where the qi of the hand greater yang vessel emerges number thirty-six: one at each inner canthus; one at each outer canthus; one below each cheekbone; one above each ear rim; one within each ear; one at each Jugu; one at each bone point above the bend of the armpit; one at each hollow above the neck bone; one four cun above each Tianchuang; one at each shoulder joint; one three cun below each shoulder joint; and from the elbow down to the base of the little finger, six transport-points on each side.

The points where the qi of the hand yang brightness vessel emerges number twenty-two: two at the outer edge of each nostril and above the nape; one at each Daying bone hollow; one at each meeting of the neck bone; one at each meeting of the shoulder bone; and from the elbow down to the base of the index finger, six transport-points on each side.

The points where the qi of the hand lesser yang vessel emerges number thirty-two: one below each cheekbone; one behind each eyebrow; one at each temple; one behind each Wangu below; one at the nape in front of the foot greater yang channel on each side; one flanking each Futu; one at each Jianzhen; one in the muscle division three cun below each Jianzhen; and from the elbow down to the base of the ring finger, six transport-points on each side.

The points where the qi of the governing vessel emerges number twenty-eight: two at the center of the nape; eight at the center behind the hairline; three on the face; and from the Great Vertebra down to the sacrum and coccyx together with the flanking points, fifteen points, reaching down to below the sacrum, making twenty-one segments in all — this is the model of the vertebrae.

The points where the qi of the conception vessel emerges number twenty-eight: two at the center of the throat; one at each bone hollow within the chest; three cun below Jiuwei lies the stomach duct, and at five cun likewise the stomach duct — from the stomach duct down to Henggu, six and a half cun, one point at each interval — this is the model of the abdominal vessel; one at the branch below the genitals; one below each eye; one at the lower lip; and one at Yinjiao.

The points where the qi of the penetrating vessel emerges number twenty-two: flanking Jiuwei, half a cun to each side, down to the navel, one point per cun; flanking below the navel, five fen to each side, down to Henggu, one point per cun — this is the model of the abdominal vessel.

One point each at the foot lesser yin below the tongue and at the reverting yin's Jimai amid the pubic hair; one at each hand lesser yin point; one at each of the yin and yang springing vessels; and, including the points where qi emerges at all the fish-border vessels of the hands and feet, there are in all three hundred sixty-five points.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Bone Cavities
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that wind is the beginning of the hundred diseases. How does one treat it with the needle? Qibo replied: When wind enters from without, it causes a person to shiver with cold, to sweat, to suffer headache, heaviness of the body, and aversion to cold. Treat at Fengfu, regulating the yin and yang: if deficient, supplement; if excessive, drain.

For great wind with pain in the neck and nape, needle Fengfu; Fengfu lies above the first vertebra. For great wind with sweating, moxa Yixi; Yixi lies below the back, flanking the spine some three cun to the side — press it and have the patient call out "Yixi," and the point will respond beneath the hand. For catching wind with aversion to wind, needle the head of the eyebrow. For a stiff neck, the point lies on the shoulder between the chess-piece-shaped bones; have the patient extend the arm level with the elbow, and moxa the center of the spine. For pulling pain and distension from the flank-connecting region and the free ribs drawing into the lower abdomen, needle Yixi. For lumbar pain that prevents turning or swaying, with urgent pulling toward the genitals, needle the eight liao points together with the point above the pain; the eight liao points lie in the region between the lumbar spine and the sacrum.

For scrofula with cold and heat, also needle Hanfu; Hanfu lies at the outer bend beside the knee. To take the points above and outside the knee, have the patient bow; to take the point at the center of the sole, have the patient kneel. The conception vessel arises below Zhongji, ascends to the region of the pubic hair, follows the interior of the abdomen upward to Guanyuan, reaches the throat, ascends to the chin, and follows the face into the eyes. The penetrating vessel arises at Qijie, runs alongside the lesser yin channel, flanks the navel and ascends, reaching the chest, where it disperses. When the conception vessel is diseased, in men it produces the seven kinds of shan bound within; in women it produces vaginal discharge and abdominal masses. When the penetrating vessel is diseased, it produces counterflow qi and urgency within. When the governing vessel is diseased, it produces rigidity of the spine and opisthotonos. The governing vessel arises below the lower abdomen at the center of the pubic bone; in women it enters and connects with the ting orifice, this orifice being the tip of the urinary opening. Its connecting branch follows the genitals, joins at the perineum, and encircles behind it; a separate branch encircles the buttocks and reaches the lesser yin channel, joining there with the connecting branch of greater yang; the lesser yin ascends the inner-rear border of the thigh, pierces the spine, and belongs to the kidney; together with greater yang, it arises at the inner canthus, ascends the forehead, crosses at the crown, enters and connects with the brain, then emerges again, descends the nape, follows the shoulder blades, flanks the spine internally, reaches the lumbar region, enters along the paraspinal muscles, and connects with the kidney. In men it follows the penis down to the perineum, just as in women. Its branch rising directly from the lower abdomen pierces the center of the navel, ascends further to pierce the heart, enters the throat, rises to the chin, encircles the lips, and ascends to connect with the center beneath the two eyes. When disease arises from this vessel, qi rushes upward from the lower abdomen to the heart, causing pain and inability to defecate or urinate — this is called penetrating shan; in women it causes infertility, painful urinary blockage, hemorrhoids, incontinence, and dryness of the throat. When disease arises in the governing vessel, treat the governing vessel, treating at the point above the pubic bone; in severe cases, treat the point below the navel. When there is rising qi accompanied by sound, treat the throat; where its center lies within Quepen, if the disease rushes upward to the throat, treat the "gradual" point, which lies flanking the chin above.

For a stiff knee that extends but will not flex, treat the hip joint. For knee pain while sitting, treat the hip socket. For weakness upon standing, treat the knee joint. For knee pain extending to the big toe, treat the popliteal fossa. For knee pain while sitting, as though something were lodged within, treat the joint. For knee pain that can neither bend nor extend, treat the inner back of the knee. For pain joining the shin as if it were broken, treat the central transport-hollow of yang brightness; failing that, treat separately the spring-points of greater yang and lesser yin. For pervasive weakness and aching of the shin with inability to stand for long, treat the linking vessel of lesser yang, located five cun above the outer ankle. The bone above the assisting bone and below the transverse bone forms the hip joint; flanking the pelvis forms the hip socket; the knee joint forms the 骸关; the bone flanking the knee forms the 连骸; below it is the assisting bone; above the assisting bone is the popliteal fossa; above the popliteal fossa is the joint; and the transverse bone of the head forms the occiput.

As for the fifty-seven transport-points for water: above the sacrum, five rows of five points; above Futu, two rows of five points; to the left and right, one row each of five points; above the ankle, one row on each side of six points.

The marrow cavity behind the brain lies three fen in, below the pointed bone at the edge of the skull; one lies below the base of the gums; one lies below the doubled bone at the center behind the nape; one lies at the bone cavity above the spine, above Fengfu; the bone cavity below the spine lies below the sacral bone. The several marrow cavities of the face flank the nose; another bone cavity lies below the mouth, level with the two shoulders. The two shoulder-blade bone cavities lie on the outer side of the shoulder-blade center. The arm-bone cavity lies on the outer side of the arm, four cun from the wrist, between the two bones. The thigh-bone's upper cavity lies on the outer side of the thigh, four cun above the knee. The shin-bone cavity lies at the upper end of the assisting bone. The cavity near the hip lies below the pulsating point amid the pubic hair. The sacral bone cavity lies behind the hip bone, four cun distant. The shoulder bone has seeping texture and gathering points but no marrow hole — it exchanges marrow without possessing a cavity.

The method of moxibustion for cold and heat disorders: first moxa the Great Vertebra at the nape, using the patient's age as the number of cones; next moxa the coccyx, likewise using the age as the number of cones; observe any depression at the back transport-points and moxa it; moxa the depression that appears on the shoulder when the arm is raised; moxa between the two free ribs; moxa the tip of Juegu above the outer ankle; moxa between the fourth and little toes; moxa the sunken vessel below the calf; moxa behind the outer ankle; where pressing above Quepen reveals a hardness as taut and painful as a sinew, moxa it; moxa the bone hollow within the chest; moxa below the wrist bones; moxa three cun below the navel at Guanyuan; moxa the pulsating vessel amid the pubic hair; moxa the muscle division three cun below the knee; moxa the pulsating vessel atop the instep along the foot yang brightness channel; moxa once at the crown of the head; for a place bitten by a dog, moxa it with three cones and then treat according to the method for dog-bite injury — in all, twenty-nine places should be moxaed. For food-damage, moxa accordingly; if it does not resolve, one must observe which channel the disorder has passed into on the yang side, and needle its transport-point repeatedly while administering medicine.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Water, Heat, and the Acupuncture Points
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Why does the lesser yin govern the kidney, and why does the kidney govern water? Qibo replied: The kidney is the utmost yin; the utmost yin is that which abounds in water. The lung is the greater yin, and the lesser yin is the vessel of winter; thus its root lies in the kidney and its branch lies in the lung, both being places where water accumulates. Huangdi asked: How is it that the kidney can gather water and thereby give rise to disease? Qibo replied: The kidney is the gate of the stomach. When the gate fails to open properly, water gathers and follows its own kind, overflowing above and below into the skin, thus producing swelling. Swelling is disease born of gathered water.

Huangdi asked: Do all forms of water disease arise from the kidney? Qibo replied: The kidney is a yin viscus; the qi of earth that rises upward belongs to the kidney and gives rise to water and fluid, hence it is called the utmost yin. When one exerts great force and labors to excess, the kidney sweats. If this kidney sweat encounters wind, the wind can neither enter inward into the viscera and bowels nor pass outward beyond the skin; it lodges instead in the mysterious gateways, travels within the skin, and is transmitted into swelling. Since it is rooted in the kidney, this is called wind water. What are called the mysterious gateways are the pores through which sweat passes.

Huangdi asked: What do the fifty-seven points for treating water disease govern? Qibo replied: The fifty-seven kidney points are where accumulated yin gathers, the places through which water passes in and out. The five rows of five points above the sacrum are the kidney transport points. In water disease, below there is swelling and abdominal distension, and above there is panting and gasping with inability to lie down; this is disease of both branch and root together, for the lung produces the panting and gasping, the kidney produces the water swelling, and the lung's rebellious qi prevents reclining. Where the two are distinguished yet mutually transmit and both are affected, this is where the water qi lodges. The two rows of five points above the crouching-rabbit point are the kidney's thoroughfare, where the three yin channels of the leg meet and knot. The single rows of six points above the ankle are where the kidney vessel descends; this is called the greater surge. In all, these fifty-seven points are the yin network vessels of the viscera, the places where water lodges as a guest.

Huangdi asked: Why in spring does one needle the network vessels and the interstices between the flesh? Qibo replied: In spring, wood begins to govern and the liver qi begins to be generated; the liver qi is urgent and its wind swift, while the channels and vessels remain deep and their qi scant, unable to penetrate deeply—hence one needles the network vessels and the spaces between the flesh. Huangdi asked: Why in summer does one needle the abundant channels and the interstices of the skin? Qibo replied: In summer, fire begins to govern and the heart qi begins to grow; the vessels are slender and the qi weak, while yang qi lingers and overflows, its heat steaming the interstices and penetrating inward to the channels—hence one needles the abundant channels and the skin interstices, cutting through the skin so that the disease departs, for the pathogen dwells only shallowly. What is called the abundant channel is the yang vessel. Huangdi asked: Why in autumn does one needle the transport and connecting points of the channels? Qibo replied: In autumn, metal begins to govern; the lung is about to gather in and bring things to a close, metal is about to overcome fire, yang qi resides at the uniting points, and yin qi has just begun to prevail. Dampness reaches the body, but since yin qi is not yet abundant it cannot penetrate deeply—hence one selects the transport points to drain yin pathogens, and the uniting points to deplete yang pathogens; as yang qi begins to decline, one therefore selects the uniting points. Huangdi asked: Why in winter does one needle the well and spring points? Qibo replied: In winter, water begins to govern, the kidney is closing up, yang qi is diminished and weak, yin qi is firm and abundant, the great yang lies hidden and sunken, and the yang vessels have departed—hence one selects the well points to bring down rebellious yin, and the spring points to fortify yang qi. Thus it is said: needle the well and spring points in winter so that in spring there will be no nosebleed—this is what is meant.

Huangdi asked: Master, you have spoken of the fifty-nine points for treating heat disease; I have considered their meaning but have not yet been able to distinguish their locations. I wish to hear of their locations, and through that to hear their meaning. Qibo replied: The five rows of five points atop the head serve to disperse the rebellious heat of the various yang channels. The eight points of the great shuttle, the chest transport points, the empty basin, and the back transport points serve to drain the heat within the chest. The eight points of the qi thoroughfare, the leg three li, and the upper and lower great hollows serve to drain the heat within the stomach. The eight points of the cloud gate, the shoulder bone, the bend-middle, and the marrow hollow serve to drain the heat of the four limbs. The ten points flanking the five transport points of the five viscera serve to drain the heat of the five viscera. In all, these fifty-nine points are the points to either side that govern heat. Huangdi asked: When a person is injured by cold, and it is transmitted and transformed into heat, why is this? Qibo replied: When cold is abundant, it gives rise to heat.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Regulating the Channels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard it said in the methods of needling that in cases of excess one should drain, and in cases of deficiency one should supplement. What is meant by excess, and what is meant by deficiency? Qibo replied: There are five kinds of excess, and likewise five kinds of deficiency. What does Your Majesty wish to ask? Huangdi said: I wish to hear of them all. Qibo said: Spirit may be in excess or deficient; qi may be in excess or deficient; blood may be in excess or deficient; form may be in excess or deficient; and will may be in excess or deficient. These ten conditions all involve qi that is unequal. Huangdi said: A person possesses essence, qi, and fluids, the four limbs and nine orifices, the five viscera and sixteen regions, and the three hundred sixty-five joints, from which the hundred diseases are born. And in the birth of the hundred diseases there is always deficiency or excess. Now Master, you speak of five kinds of excess and five kinds of deficiency—how do these arise? Qibo said: They all arise from the five viscera. The heart stores the spirit, the lung stores qi, the liver stores blood, the spleen stores the flesh, and the kidney stores the will; from these the bodily form is completed. Will and intention penetrate within and connect with the marrow and bones, thereby completing the body's form and the five viscera. The pathways of the five viscera all issue from the channel passages, through which blood and qi travel. When blood and qi are not harmonious, the hundred diseases arise through transformation—therefore one must guard the channel passages.

Huangdi asked: What of spirit in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When spirit is in excess, one laughs without cease; when spirit is deficient, one grieves. Before blood and qi have combined with the pathogen and the five viscera remain settled, if a pathogen lodges in the body it produces a shivering that arises from the fine hairs, not yet having entered the channels and network vessels—this is called a subtle affection of the spirit. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain in this case? Qibo said: When spirit is in excess, drain the blood of its small network vessels; let blood out, but do not press too deeply, and do not strike the great channel—then the spirit qi will be leveled. When spirit is deficient, observe its empty network vessels, press upon them to draw qi there, needle to free them, but do not let blood out, do not drain the qi—so as to open the channel, and the spirit qi will be leveled. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Massage without release, insert the needle without dislodging it, and move qi to where it is deficient—then the spirit qi can be restored.

Huangdi said: Good. What of qi in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When qi is in excess there is panting and coughing with qi rising upward; when qi is deficient, breathing is uneven and qi is scant. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, there is a slight affection of the skin, called a subtle leakage of white qi. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When qi is in excess, drain its channel passage, but do not injure the channel, do not let blood out, do not drain the qi. When deficient, supplement its channel passage, but do not let the qi escape. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Massage without release; when withdrawing the needle, observe it, saying, 'I shall insert it deeply'; once it truly enters, the response will surely change—the essential qi will naturally settle, the pathogenic qi will scatter and disperse with nowhere to rest, the qi will leak out through the interstices, and the true qi will thereby find its proper place.

Huangdi said: Good. What of blood in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When blood is in excess there is anger; when deficient there is fear. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, if the grandchild network vessels overflow with fluid, then there is retained blood in the channel. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When blood is in excess, drain its abundant channel and let out its blood. When deficient, observe its empty channel and insert the needle within its vessel, retaining it long while observing; when the vessel becomes full, withdraw the needle quickly, without letting blood escape. Huangdi asked: How does one needle retained blood? Qibo said: Observe its blood network vessels, needle to let out the blood, and do not let malign blood enter the channel, lest it become a chronic ailment.

Huangdi said: Good. What of form in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When form is in excess there is abdominal distension and difficulty in urination and defecation; when deficient, the four limbs cannot be used. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, the flesh twitches faintly—this is called subtle wind. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When form is in excess, drain its yang channel; when deficient, supplement its yang network vessel. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Select the spaces between the flesh, without striking the channel and without injuring the network vessel—then the defensive qi can be restored and the pathogenic qi will disperse.

Huangdi said: Good. What of will in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When will is in excess there is abdominal distension and undigested diarrhea; when deficient there is reversal cold. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, there is stirring in the bones and joints. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When will is in excess, drain the blood at Rangu; when deficient, supplement at Fuliu. Huangdi asked: How does one needle when blood and qi have not yet combined? Qibo said: Needle it at once, without striking the channel, and the place of the pathogen can immediately be made deficient.

Huangdi said: Good. I have now heard the forms of deficiency and excess, but do not know how they arise. Qibo said: When qi and blood have combined with a pathogen, yin and yang tip against each other; qi becomes disordered in the defensive level, blood rebels within the channels, and blood and qi become separated from their proper places—one becomes excess, the other deficient. When blood combines in the yin and qi combines in the yang, this produces fright and mania. When blood combines in the yang and qi combines in the yin, this produces a burning within. When blood combines above and qi combines below, there is vexation in the heart and a tendency to anger. When blood combines below and qi combines above, there is confusion and a tendency to forgetfulness. Huangdi asked: When blood combines in the yin and qi combines in the yang, and thus blood and qi are separated from their places, which is excess and which is deficient? Qibo said: Blood and qi delight in warmth and are averse to cold; when cold, they congeal and cannot flow; when warm, they dissolve and disperse. Therefore, wherever qi combines, blood becomes deficient there; wherever blood combines, qi becomes deficient there. Huangdi said: What a person possesses is only blood and qi. Now Master, you say that where blood combines there is deficiency, and where qi combines there is deficiency—is there then no excess at all? Qibo said: Where something is present, that is excess; where something is absent, that is deficiency. Thus where qi combines there is no blood, and where blood combines there is no qi. Now, since blood and qi have lost each other, this is called deficiency. The network vessels and grandchild vessels all convey into the channels; when blood and qi combine there, this becomes excess. When blood and qi together rush upward, this produces great reversal; in reversal there is sudden death—if the qi returns, the person lives; if it does not return, the person dies. Huangdi asked: By what path does excess arrive, and by what path does deficiency depart? I wish to hear the essentials of deficiency and excess. Qibo said: Yin and yang each have their transport points and meeting places. Yang pours into yin, and yin fills to the outside; when yin and yang are evenly balanced, the body is thereby nourished, and the nine indicators are as one—this is called a balanced person.

Now, in the generation of pathogens, some are generated in the yin, some in the yang. Those generated in the yang are contracted through wind, rain, cold, and summer heat. Those generated in the yin are contracted through diet, dwelling, and excesses of yin and yang or joy and anger. Huangdi asked: How does injury from wind and rain affect a person? Qibo said: When wind and rain injure a person, they first lodge in the skin, then are transmitted inward to the grandchild vessels; when the grandchild vessels are full, they are transmitted into the network vessels; when the network vessels are full, they pour into the great channel vessels. Blood and qi combine with the pathogen and lodge between the interstices of the flesh, so that the vessel becomes firm and large—hence it is called excess. In excess, the exterior is firm and full, and cannot bear pressing; pressing produces pain. Huangdi asked: How does injury from cold and dampness affect a person? Qibo said: When cold and dampness strike a person, the skin fails to contract, the flesh becomes tight and firm, the nutritive blood congeals, and the defensive qi departs—hence it is called deficiency. In deficiency, the flesh is wrinkled and sunken, the qi insufficient; pressing on it allows the qi to warm it, hence it feels pleasant and is not painful. Huangdi said: Good. How does excess arise in the yin? Qibo said: When joy and anger are not moderated, yin qi rebels upward; when it rebels upward, the lower part becomes deficient; when the lower part is deficient, yang qi rushes into it—hence it is called excess. Huangdi asked: How does deficiency arise in the yin? Qibo said: Joy causes qi to descend, grief causes qi to dissipate; when qi dissipates, the vessel becomes empty and hollow. When this is compounded by cold food and drink, cold qi steams and fills the interior, the blood congeals and the qi departs—hence it is called deficiency.

Huangdi asked: The classics say that when yang is deficient there is external cold, when yin is deficient there is internal heat, when yang is in excess there is external heat, and when yin is in excess there is internal cold. I have already heard this, but do not know why it is so. Qibo said: Yang receives its qi from the upper burner, which warms the skin and the space between the flesh. If cold qi lodges in the exterior, then the upper burner becomes obstructed; when the upper burner is obstructed, cold qi alone remains in the exterior—hence there is cold and shivering. Huangdi asked: How does deficiency of yin give rise to internal heat? Qibo said: When there has been overexertion and fatigue, the bodily form and qi decline and diminish, grain qi is not abundant, the upper burner fails to move, and the lower stomach-duct fails to open; the stomach qi becomes heated, and this heat steams the chest—hence there is internal heat. Huangdi asked: How does excess of yang give rise to external heat? Qibo said: When the upper burner is obstructed and fails to flow freely, the skin becomes dense and the interstices close up, the mysterious gateways fail to open, and the defensive qi cannot be released and dispersed—hence there is external heat. Huangdi asked: How does excess of yin give rise to internal cold? Qibo said: When reversal qi rebels upward, cold qi accumulates in the chest without being drained; if it is not drained, warm qi departs and cold alone remains; the blood then congeals, and once congealed the vessels fail to flow freely—the vessel becomes large and rough, hence there is internal cold.

Huangdi asked: When yin and yang combine with pathogens, when blood and qi combine, and the form of disease is thereby completed, how does one needle this? Qibo said: In needling such cases, select the channel passages; draw blood from the nutritive level, draw qi from the defensive level, and make use of the bodily form, according to the season, whether abundant or scant, high or low. Huangdi asked: When blood and qi have combined and the form of disease has been completed, and yin and yang tip against each other, how does one supplement and drain? Qibo said: To drain excess, insert the needle once the qi is abundant; let the needle enter together with the qi, opening its gate as though loosening its door; let the needle withdraw together with the qi, so that the essential qi is not injured and the pathogenic qi is thereby brought down. Do not close the outer gate, so as to let the ailment out; shake wide its passage, as though clearing its path—this is called great draining; one must press firmly as the needle is withdrawn, so that the great pathogenic qi is thereby subdued. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement deficiency?
