Qibo said: Hold the needle without placing it yet, to steady one's intent; await the exhalation and insert the needle; as the qi goes out, the needle goes in; let the needle's hollow be sealed on all four sides, so that the essential qi has nowhere to escape; when it becomes truly replete, withdraw the needle quickly; as the qi enters, the needle comes out, so that heat cannot return; close its gate, so that the pathogenic qi is dispersed and the essential qi may thereby be preserved. Move the qi and await the right moment, so that the near qi is not lost and the distant qi will then arrive—this is called pursuing it.

Huangdi asked: Master, you have spoken of ten kinds of deficiency and excess, arising from the five viscera—the five viscera having five vessels. Yet the twelve channel vessels all give rise to disease; now Master, you speak only of the five viscera. The twelve channel vessels all connect with the three hundred sixty-five joints; when a joint is diseased, it must affect the channel vessel, and the diseases of the channel vessels all involve deficiency or excess—how are these to be reconciled? Qibo said: The five viscera are paired in exterior-interior relation with the six bowels; the channels, network vessels, and joints each give rise to their own deficiency or excess, and one regulates according to where the disease resides. If the disease is in the vessel, regulate the blood. If the disease is in the blood, regulate the network vessels. If the disease is in the qi, regulate the defensive level. If the disease is in the flesh, regulate the spaces between the flesh. If the disease is in the sinews, regulate the sinews. If the disease is in the bone, regulate the bone. Use fire-heated needles for rapid piercing where the disease lies and where there is tension. If the disease is in the bone, use the tempered needle together with medicinal fomentation. If the disease's location of pain cannot be identified, the two springing vessels are best to treat. If the body has pain but the nine indicators show no disease, then apply crossed needling. If the pain is on the left but the affected vessel is diseased on the right, apply great needling. One must carefully examine the nine indicators, and the way of needling will then be complete.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Crossed Needling
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard of crossed needling but have not grasped its meaning—what is meant by crossed needling? Qibo replied: When a pathogen lodges in the body, it must first reside in the skin and hair; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the grandchild vessels; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the network vessels; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the channel vessels, connecting inwardly with the five viscera and dispersing into the intestines and stomach, so that yin and yang are both affected and the five viscera are thereby injured. This is the sequence by which a pathogen enters from the skin and hair to reach its utmost in the five viscera; in such a case one treats the channels. Now, if a pathogen lodges in the skin and hair and enters to reside in the grandchild network vessels, lingering there without departing, it becomes blocked and fails to pass through; unable to enter the channels, it overflows into the great network vessels and gives rise to unusual diseases. When a pathogen lodges in the great network vessels, it pours from the left to the right, from the right to the left, above and below, left and right, interfering with the channels and spreading to the four extremities; its qi has no fixed location and does not enter the channel transport points—this is called crossed needling. Huangdi asked: I wish to hear of crossed needling—of taking the right to treat the left, and the left to treat the right. How is this done, and how does it differ from great needling? Qibo said: When a pathogen lodges in the channel, if the left is abundant, the right will be diseased; if the right is abundant, the left will be diseased—there are also cases where it shifts, where the pain on the left has not yet ceased before the vessel on the right first becomes diseased. In such cases, one must apply great needling, striking precisely the channel, not the network vessel. Thus in network-vessel disease, the pain occurs at a place crossed from the affected channel—hence it is called crossed needling.

Huangdi asked: I wish to hear how crossed needling is performed, and how the points are selected. Qibo said: When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yin, it causes sudden heart pain and abrupt distension, with fullness extending through the chest and rib-sides, without any palpable accumulation. Needle in front of the Rangu point to let blood, and after about the time it takes to eat a meal, it will cease. If it does not cease, take the right side for a left-side ailment, or the left for a right-side ailment. If the disease is newly arisen, it will resolve within five days.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand lesser yang, it causes throat blockage and a curled tongue, dry mouth, and vexation of the heart, with pain along the outer border of the arm such that the hand cannot reach the head. Needle above the nail of the ring finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each hand. In the robust, it resolves at once; in the elderly, it takes a little while. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; this, being a recently arisen disease, resolves within a few days.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot reverting yin, it causes sudden hernial pain of great severity. Needle above the nail of the big toe, at the junction with the flesh, one prick on each side. In males it resolves at once; in females it takes a little while. Take the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yang, it causes pain in the head, neck, and shoulder. Needle above the nail of the little toe, at the junction with the flesh, one prick on each side; it resolves at once. If it does not resolve, needle three times below the outer ankle. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to eat a meal.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand yang brightness, it causes fullness of qi, panting in the chest, with the rib-sides supported and heat in the chest. Needle above the nail of the index finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each side. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to eat a meal.

When a pathogen lodges between the forearm and palm so that it cannot be bent, needle behind the wrist; first press with the finger where it is painful, then needle there, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks: on the first day of the moon's waxing, one prick; on the second day, two pricks; up to the fifteenth day, fifteen pricks; on the sixteenth day, fourteen pricks, and so on decreasing. When a pathogen lodges in the foot yang springing vessel, it causes eye pain beginning from the inner canthus. Needle about half a cun below the outer ankle, two pricks on each side; needle the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to walk ten li. When a person has fallen or been thrown down, so that malign blood remains within, with fullness and distension in the abdomen and inability to relieve oneself, first give a purgative medicine to disperse it. If this fall has injured the reverting yin vessel above and the lesser yin network vessel below, needle below the inner ankle, at the blood vessel in front of Rangu, to let out blood; needle also the pulsing vessel atop the instep. If this does not resolve it, needle above the cluster of hairs near the great toe, one prick on each side; once blood appears, it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right. For one prone to sorrow, fright, and unhappiness, needle according to the method just given.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand yang brightness, it causes deafness, so that at times no sound is heard. Needle above the nail of the index finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each side; hearing is restored at once. If it does not resolve, needle at the junction of the middle finger's nail with the flesh, and hearing is restored at once. In cases where hearing was never present at all, needling is of no avail. For those in whom wind arises within the ear, needle in the same manner as above, the right for the left, the left for the right.

In all cases of painful obstruction that comes and goes without fixed location, needle wherever the pain occurs in the spaces between the flesh, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks. In wielding the needle, follow the abundance or decline of the qi to determine the number of pricks; if the needling exceeds the proper count of days, qi will be lost; if it falls short of the count, the qi will not be drained. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right; when the disease resolves, stop. If it does not resolve, needle again according to the method: on the first day of the moon's waxing, one prick; the second day, two pricks, gradually increasing; up to the fifteenth day, fifteen pricks; the sixteenth day, fourteen pricks, gradually decreasing.

When a pathogen lodges in the channel of the foot yang brightness, it causes nosebleed and cold in the upper teeth. Needle at the junction of the second toe's nail with the flesh, one prick on each side. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yang, it causes rib-side pain with inability to breathe freely, coughing, and sweating. Needle at the junction of the fourth toe's nail with the flesh, one prick on each side; the inability to breathe resolves at once, and the sweating stops at once. For coughing, keep warmly clothed and take food and drink, and it will resolve within a day. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right; the disease resolves at once. If it does not resolve, needle again according to the method.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yin, it causes throat pain such that food cannot be taken in, with causeless irritability and a tendency to anger, with qi rushing up to the diaphragm. Needle the vessel at the center of the sole, three pricks on each side, six pricks in all; it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right. When there is swelling within the throat such that food cannot be taken in, and when at times saliva cannot even be spat out, needle in front of Rangu to let out blood, and it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yin, it causes lower back pain radiating into the lower abdomen and pulling at the flank, so that one cannot bend backward or breathe freely. Needle at the juncture of the lower back and sacrum, above the two muscles beside the spine—this is the lumbar transport point—using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks; upon withdrawing the needle it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yang, it causes spasm and tightness of the back, radiating pain into the rib-sides. Needle beginning from the nape, counting down the vertebrae alongside the spine; press firmly, and wherever the response to pressure feels like pain, needle to the side of it, three pricks; it resolves at once.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yang, it causes pain lodging in the hip joint, so that the thigh cannot be raised. Needle the hip joint with a fine needle; if there is cold, retain the needle for a long while, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks; it resolves at once.

In treating the various channels, if needling their points of passage produces no effect on the disease, then apply crossed needling. For deafness, needle the hand yang brightness; if it does not resolve, needle the connecting vessel that emerges in front of the ear. For dental caries, needle the hand yang brightness; if it does not resolve, needle the vessel that enters the teeth, and it resolves at once. When a pathogen lodges among the five viscera, the disease manifests as pain that pulls along the vessel, coming and going intermittently. Observe where the disease is and apply crossed needling at the nails of the hands and feet; observe the vessel, and let out its blood, needling once every other day; if one needling does not resolve it, five needlings will. When the crossed disease is transmitted to affect the upper teeth, causing cold pain in the teeth and lips, observe the vessel with blood on the back of the hand and remove it; at the foot yang brightness, needle at the nail of the second toe with a great prick, and at the hand, needle above the nail of the index finger, one prick on each; it resolves at once. Take the left for the right, the right for the left.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessels of the hand and foot lesser yin, the hand and foot greater yin, and the foot yang brightness—these five network vessels all converge within the ear and connect above with the left corner of the head. When all five network vessels are exhausted, it causes all the vessels of the body to stir while the body itself has no awareness; its appearance is like that of a corpse, and it is therefore also called corpse reversal. Needle the inner side of the big toe's nail, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip; afterward needle the center of the sole; then needle above the nail of the middle toe, one prick each. Afterward needle the inner side of the thumb's nail, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip; then needle the heart-governing vessel and the tip of the pisiform bone at the lesser yin, one prick each; it resolves at once. If it does not resolve, use a bamboo tube to blow into both ears, shave a square inch of hair at the left corner, burn and grind it, and have the patient drink it down with a cup of fine wine; for one unable to drink, pour it in—it resolves at once.

In general, the method of needling is: first observe the channel vessels, press and follow along them, examine whether there is deficiency or excess, and regulate accordingly. Where the channel is not in harmony, needle the channel itself; where there is pain but the channel is not diseased, apply crossed needling; and observing wherever there are blood network vessels in the skin regions, remove them entirely. This is the method of crossed needling.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Compliance and Contrariety in Needling through the Four Seasons
paragraphs_en: When the terminal yin (jueyin) is in excess, it causes yin impediment (bi); when it is deficient, it causes heat impediment; when the pulse is slippery, it causes fox hernia; when the pulse is rough with wind, it causes accumulation of qi in the lower abdomen. When the lesser yin (shaoyin) is in excess, it causes skin impediment with urticaria; when deficient, it causes lung impediment; when slippery, it causes lung wind-hernia; when rough, it causes accumulations and bloody urine. When the greater yin (taiyin) is in excess, it causes flesh impediment with cold in the middle; when deficient, it causes spleen impediment; when slippery, it causes spleen wind-hernia; when rough, it causes accumulations with periodic fullness of the heart and abdomen. When yang brightness (yangming) is in excess, it causes vessel impediment with periodic fever of the body; when deficient, it causes heart impediment; when slippery, it causes heart wind-hernia; when rough, it causes accumulations with frequent fright. When greater yang (taiyang) is in excess, it causes bone impediment with heaviness of the body; when deficient, it causes kidney impediment; when slippery, it causes kidney wind-hernia; when rough, it causes accumulations with recurrent disorders of the crown of the head. When lesser yang (shaoyang) is in excess, it causes sinew impediment with fullness of the rib-side; when deficient, it causes liver impediment; when slippery, it causes liver wind-hernia; when rough, it causes accumulations with periodic tension of the sinews and eye pain.

Therefore in spring the qi is in the channels; in summer the qi is in the minute connecting vessels; in late summer the qi is in the muscles and flesh; in autumn the qi is in the skin; in winter the qi is in the bone marrow. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the reason for this. Qibo said: In spring, the qi of heaven begins to open and the qi of earth begins to release; the ice thaws and melts, water flows and the channels open, so people's qi is in the vessels. In summer, the channels are full and the qi overflows, entering the minute connecting vessels and receiving blood, so the skin becomes full and firm. In late summer, the channels and connecting vessels are all abundant, and qi overflows internally into the flesh. In autumn, the qi of heaven begins to contract, the interstices close up, and the skin becomes tight and drawn. In winter, qi is stored away; blood and qi reside in the interior, lodging in the bone marrow, and communicate with the five viscera. Therefore pathogenic qi always follows the qi and blood of the four seasons in order to enter and lodge in the body; when it comes to its transformations these cannot be measured by a fixed rule, but one must follow the channel qi proper to the season in order to expel the pathogen — once the pathogen is expelled, disordered qi will not arise.

Huangdi said: How does disordered qi arise from acting contrary to the four seasons? Qibo said: If one needles the connecting vessels in spring, blood and qi will overflow outward, causing the person to lack qi. If one needles the muscles and flesh in spring, blood and qi will circulate in counterflow, causing the person's qi to rise. If one needles the sinews and bones in spring, blood and qi will lodge internally, causing the person's abdomen to distend. If one needles the channels in summer, blood and qi will become exhausted, causing the person to become languid and weak. If one needles the muscles and flesh in summer, blood and qi will withdraw internally, causing the person to become prone to fear. If one needles the sinews and bones in summer, blood and qi will rise in counterflow, causing the person to become prone to anger. If one needles the channels in autumn, blood and qi will rise in counterflow, causing the person to become forgetful. If one needles the connecting vessels in autumn, qi will not move outward, causing the person to lie down and be unwilling to move. If one needles the sinews and bones in autumn, blood and qi will disperse internally, causing the person to shiver with cold. If one needles the channels in winter, blood and qi will both be drained away, causing the person's eyes to become dim. If one needles the connecting vessels in winter, internal qi will leak outward and linger to form a great impediment. If one needles the muscles and flesh in winter, yang qi will become exhausted and cut off, causing the person to become forgetful. In all these cases, needling contrary to the four seasons is a grave transgression that gives rise to disease, and one must not fail to comply with the seasons; acting contrary to them gives rise to disordered qi that spreads and generates disease. Therefore, if in needling one does not know the channel proper to the season and the place where the disease arises, taking compliance for contrariety, the true qi becomes disordered within and clashes with the essence; one must carefully examine the nine indicators of the pulse — then the true qi will not become disordered, and the essential qi will not be diverted.

Huangdi said: Good. When needling wounds the five viscera: if it strikes the heart, death comes in one day, and its sign is belching. If it strikes the liver, death comes in five days, and its sign is disordered speech. If it strikes the lung, death comes in three days, and its sign is coughing. If it strikes the kidney, death comes in six days, and its sign is sneezing and yawning. If it strikes the spleen, death comes in ten days, and its sign is swallowing. Needling that wounds a person's five viscera will invariably result in death; as for its signs, one may know the time of death according to the changing indications proper to each viscus.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Root and Branch and the Transmission of Disease
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: In disease there is root and branch; in needling there is contrariety and compliance. How is this? Qibo replied: In general, the method of needling must distinguish yin and yang, so that front and back correspond to one another and contrariety and compliance can be properly applied, with root and branch shifting into each other. Hence it is said: there are cases where the trouble is in the branch and one seeks it in the branch; there are cases where it is in the root and one seeks it in the root; there are cases where it is in the root and one seeks it in the branch; there are cases where it is in the branch and one seeks it in the root. Therefore in treatment there are cases where taking the branch achieves success, cases where taking the root achieves success, cases where taking it contrary to its course achieves success, and cases where taking it compliant with its course achieves success. Hence, if one understands contrariety and compliance, one may act correctly without hesitation; if one understands root and branch, then every action taken will be fitting; if one does not understand root and branch, this is called acting recklessly.

Now, the contrariety and compliance of yin and yang, and the way of root and branch, extend from the small to the great: speaking of one thing, one can know the harm of a hundred diseases; from the few to the many, from the shallow to the broad, one can speak of one and know a hundred. By the shallow one may know the deep; by observing the near one may know the far. To speak of branch and root is easy, yet to reach its full application is not. Treating against the true condition is contrariety; treating so as to achieve cure is compliance. When disease comes first and counterflow comes afterward, treat the root. When counterflow comes first and disease comes afterward, treat the root. When cold comes first and disease is generated afterward, treat the root. When disease comes first and cold is generated afterward, treat the root. When heat comes first and disease is generated afterward, treat the root. When heat comes first and fullness in the middle is generated afterward, treat the branch. When disease comes first and diarrhea follows, treat the root. When diarrhea comes first and another disease is generated afterward, treat the root — one must first regulate the diarrhea, and only then treat the other disease. When disease comes first and fullness in the middle follows, treat the branch. When fullness in the middle comes first and vexation of the heart follows, treat the root. When a person has both guest qi and host qi together, if urination and defecation are inhibited, treat the branch; if they are free, treat the root. When disease arises with excess, treat the root before the branch. When disease arises with deficiency, treat the branch before the root. One must carefully examine whether the condition is mild or severe and regulate it by judgment: when mild, treat both together; when severe, treat one alone. When inhibited urination and defecation come first and disease is generated afterward, treat the root.

Now, as for the transmission of disease: in heart disease there is first heart pain; after one day, coughing; after three days, pain and distension in the rib-side; after five days, blockage and stoppage of the bowels, with pain and heaviness throughout the body; if this does not resolve after three more days, death follows — in winter at midnight, in summer at midday. In lung disease there is panting and coughing; after three days, distension and pain in the rib-side; after one day, heaviness and pain of the body; after five days, distension; if this does not resolve after ten days, death follows — in winter at sunset, in summer at sunrise. In liver disease there is headache, dizziness, and fullness in the rib-side; after three days, heaviness and pain of the body; after five days, distension; after three more days, pain in the lumbar spine and lower abdomen with aching of the shins; if this does not resolve after three more days, death follows — in winter at sunset, in summer at the morning meal. In spleen disease there is pain and heaviness of the body; after one day, distension; after two days, pain in the lower abdomen and lumbar spine with aching of the shins; after three days, pain in the muscles of the back and spine with blocked urination; if this does not resolve after ten days, death follows — in winter at nightfall, in summer at the late meal. In kidney disease there is pain in the lower abdomen and lumbar spine with aching of the shins; after three days, pain in the muscles of the back and spine with blocked urination; after three more days, abdominal distension; after three more days, pain and distension in both rib-sides; if this does not resolve after three more days, death follows — in winter at early dawn, in summer in the late afternoon. In stomach disease there is fullness and distension; after five days, pain in the lower abdomen and lumbar spine with aching of the shins; after three days, pain in the muscles of the back and spine with blocked urination; after five more days, heaviness of the body; if this does not resolve after six days, death follows — in winter after midnight, in summer in the early afternoon. In bladder disease there is blocked urination; after five days, distension of the lower abdomen and pain in the lumbar spine with aching of the shins; after one day, abdominal distension; after one more day, pain of the body; if this does not resolve after two days, death follows — in winter at the cock's crow, in summer in the late afternoon. All these diseases are transmitted in this sequence, and when this occurs each has a fixed time of death, and one must not needle. But if the transmission skips a viscus and stops, or reaches the third or fourth viscus, then needling may be undertaken.

素问.title_en: Great Discourse on the Celestial Origin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Heaven has the five phases, which govern the five positions to give rise to cold, heat, dryness, dampness, and wind; man has the five viscera, which transform into five qi, giving rise to joy, anger, contemplation, worry, and fear. Treatises say that the five movements succeed one another and each governs in turn, and when the cycle of the year is complete it turns and begins again — this I already understand. I wish to hear how it corresponds with the signs of the three yin and three yang, and how they are joined together. Guiyuqu bowed his head twice and replied: Profound indeed is this question! Now, the five movements and yin-yang are the Way of heaven and earth, the guiding thread of the myriad things, the parents of change and transformation, the root and origin of birth and destruction, the abode of spirit and illumination — how could one fail to comprehend them? Thus when a thing is generated it is called transformation; when a thing reaches its extreme it is called change; when yin and yang cannot be fathomed it is called spirit; when spirit functions without fixed direction it is called sagely. Now as for the working of change and transformation: in heaven it is the mysterious; in man it is the Way; on earth it is transformation. Transformation generates the five flavors, the Way generates wisdom, the mysterious generates spirit. Spirit, in heaven, is wind; on earth, it is wood. In heaven it is heat; on earth, it is fire. In heaven it is dampness; on earth, it is earth. In heaven it is dryness; on earth, it is metal. In heaven it is cold; on earth, it is water. Hence in heaven it forms qi, on earth it forms shape; shape and qi stimulate each other, and thereby the myriad things are transformed and generated. Now heaven and earth are the above and below of the myriad things; left and right are the pathways of yin and yang. Water and fire are the signs and manifestations of yin and yang; metal and wood are the beginning and end of generation and completion. Qi has abundance and paucity, form has flourishing and decline; above and below summon each other, and thereby gain and loss become manifest.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear how the five movements govern the seasons. Guiyuqu said: The five qi move and circulate, each completing its own full cycle of the year — they do not simply govern fixed seasons alone. Huangdi said: Please let me hear what this means. Guiyuqu said: I have gathered from the text of the Investigation of the Grand Origin of Heaven from Remotest Antiquity: 'The Great Void is vast and boundless; it is the foundation from which transformation originates, whence the myriad things draw their beginning. The five movements traverse the heavens, spreading true numinous qi, uniting and governing the origin of earth. The nine stars hang bright, the seven luminaries revolve in turn. There is yin, there is yang; there is the soft, there is the hard. Once the hidden and the manifest have taken their places, cold and heat relax and tighten in turn; birth begets birth, transformation begets transformation, and the categories of things all become manifest.' I have transmitted this through ten generations of my family — this is what it means. Huangdi said: Good. What is meant by 'qi has abundance and paucity, form has flourishing and decline'? Guiyuqu said: The qi of yin and yang each has its abundance and paucity, hence it is said there are three yin and three yang. Form has flourishing and decline, meaning that in the governance of the five phases each has its excess and its insufficiency. Thus at the outset, if there is surplus, insufficiency will follow it; if there is insufficiency, surplus will follow from it. If one knows how to anticipate and how to follow, the movement of qi can be foreseen. When it corresponds to heaven it is called tianfu, celestial correspondence; when it inherits the governing qi of the year it is called suizhi, direct correspondence with the year; when the three coincide it is called governance. Huangdi said: How do above and below summon each other? Guiyuqu said: Cold, heat, dryness, dampness, wind, and fire are the yin and yang of heaven; the three yin and three yang receive them from above. Wood, fire, earth, metal, and water are the yin and yang of earth; growth, development, transformation, harvest, and storage respond to them from below. Heaven, through yang, generates life; through yin, fosters its growth. Earth, through yang, brings death; through yin, stores away. Heaven has yin and yang; earth too has yin and yang. Wood, fire, earth, metal, and water are the yin and yang of earth, governing growth, development, transformation, harvest, and storage. Therefore within yang there is yin, and within yin there is yang. The reason one wishes to know the yin and yang of heaven and earth is this: the qi that corresponds to heaven moves without cease, hence every five years it shifts to the right; the qi that corresponds to earth is still and holds its position, hence every six years it completes a full round. Movement and stillness summon each other, above and below overlook each other, yin and yang interweave, and thus change is generated.

Huangdi said: Do the cycles of above and below have fixed numbers? Guiyuqu said: Heaven takes six as its unit; earth takes five as its measure. In completing the cycle of heaven's qi, six years make one full round; in completing the record of earth, five years make one cycle. The sovereign fire governs through illumination; the ministerial fire governs through position. Five and six combine to give seven hundred twenty qi-periods, forming one era of thirty years with one thousand four hundred forty qi-periods; sixty years form one complete cycle, and within this span insufficiency and excess are both fully manifested. Huangdi said: Master, your words reach upward to encompass the qi of heaven and downward to complete the record of earth — this may truly be called thorough. I wish to hear it and store it away, to use it above in governing the people and below in governing the body, so that the common people may be enlightened, above and below in harmonious accord, virtue and grace flowing down so that descendants need not worry, and so that it may be transmitted to later generations without end. May I hear it? Guiyuqu said: The workings of the utmost calculations are pressing and subtle; their coming can be seen, their going can be traced. Those who revere them will flourish; those who neglect them will perish. To act without the Way, in selfish pursuit, will surely bring calamity from heaven. Reverently upholding the Way of heaven, let me speak its true essentials. Huangdi said: One who speaks well of beginnings must connect them to their endings; one who speaks well of the near must know the far. Only thus can the utmost calculations reach their limit without the Way becoming confounded — this is what is called illumination. I wish the Master to arrange them in sequence and give them order, so that they are concise yet not lacking, enduring yet unbroken, easy to apply and hard to forget, forming a framework and guiding thread. I wish to hear the essentials of the utmost calculations in full. Guiyuqu said: Profound indeed is this question, and clear indeed is the Way! It is like a drum answering the drumstick, like an echo answering a sound. I have heard: in the years of jia and ji, the earth movement governs. In the years of yi and geng, the metal movement governs. In the years of bing and xin, the water movement governs. In the years of ding and ren, the wood movement governs. In the years of wu and gui, the fire movement governs. Huangdi said: How does this correspond with the three yin and three yang? Guiyuqu said: In the years of zi and wu, lesser yin appears above. In the years of chou and wei, greater yin appears above. In the years of yin and shen, lesser yang appears above. In the years of mao and you, yang brightness appears above. In the years of chen and xu, greater yang appears above. In the years of si and hai, terminal yin appears above. Lesser yin is what is called the tip of the cycle; terminal yin is what is called its end. Above terminal yin, wind qi governs; above lesser yin, heat qi governs; above greater yin, damp qi governs; above lesser yang, ministerial fire governs; above yang brightness, dry qi governs; above greater yang, cold qi governs. These are what is called the roots; together they are called the six origins. Huangdi said: Brilliant indeed is this Way, illuminating indeed is this discourse! Let it be inscribed on tablets of jade and stored in a coffer of gold, entitled the Record of the Celestial Origin.

素问.title_en: Great Discourse on the Circulation of the Five Movements
paragraphs_en: Huangdi sat in the Hall of Light; he began to rectify the celestial framework, surveying the eight extremities and examining and establishing the five constants. He summoned the Celestial Master and asked him: Treatises say that the movement and stillness of heaven and earth are governed by spirit and illumination as their guiding thread, and that the rising and falling of yin and yang are shown forth in the signs of cold and heat. I have heard from you, Master, of the numbers of the five movements; what you have said rectifies how the five qi each govern a year, beginning with jia to fix the movements. Now I wish to discuss this further. Guiyuqu said: Earth governs the years of jia and ji; metal governs yi and geng; water governs bing and xin; wood governs ding and ren; fire governs wu and gui. Above zi and wu, lesser yin governs; above chou and wei, greater yin governs; above yin and shen, lesser yang governs; above mao and you, yang brightness governs; above chen and xu, greater yang governs; above si and hai, terminal yin governs. Why do these not match up directly with yin and yang — what is the reason for this? Qibo said: This is a profound principle indeed — it concerns the yin and yang of heaven and earth. Now, numbers that can be counted are the yin and yang within man; these are numbers that can be matched and grasped. As for yin and yang in general, their numbers may be counted to ten, extended to a hundred, counted to a thousand, extended to ten thousand. But the yin and yang of heaven and earth are not to be reckoned by number — they are to be grasped through image.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear how this began. Qibo said: Profound indeed is this question! I have examined the text of the Grand Origin of Heaven from Remotest Antiquity: the cinnabar-red heavenly qi passes through the wu division of the lunar mansions Ox and Girl; the ochre-yellow heavenly qi passes through the ji division of Heart and Tail; the kingfisher-green heavenly qi passes through Rooftop, Encampment, Willow, and Ghost; the plain-white heavenly qi passes through Neck, Foundation, Pleiades, and Net; the mysterious-black heavenly qi passes through Spread, Wing, Bond, and Stomach. What are called the wu and ji divisions are Legs, Wall, Horn, and Chariot — these are the gate and door of heaven and earth. The origin of observation and the source of the Way — one must not fail to comprehend them.

Huangdi said: Good. The treatises speak of heaven and earth as the above and below of the myriad things, and of left and right as the pathways of yin and yang — I do not yet grasp what this means. Qibo said: What is meant by 'above and below' is where yin and yang appear as above or below within the cycle of the year. As for 'left and right': wherever terminal yin appears above, lesser yin lies to its left and greater yang to its right. Where lesser yin appears, greater yin lies to its left and terminal yin to its right. Where greater yin appears, lesser yang lies to its left and lesser yin to its right. Where lesser yang appears, yang brightness lies to its left and greater yin to its right. Where yang brightness appears, greater yang lies to its left and lesser yang to its right. Where greater yang appears, terminal yin lies to its left and yang brightness to its right. This is what is meant by 'facing north to determine position,' referring to what appears above. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'below'? Qibo said: When terminal yin is above, lesser yang is below, with yang brightness to its left and greater yin to its right. When lesser yin is above, yang brightness is below, with greater yang to its left and lesser yang to its right. When greater yin is above, greater yang is below, with terminal yin to its left and yang brightness to its right. When lesser yang is above, terminal yin is below, with lesser yin to its left and greater yang to its right. When yang brightness is above, lesser yin is below, with greater yin to its left and terminal yin to its right. When greater yang is above, greater yin is below, with lesser yang to its left and lesser yin to its right. This is what is meant by 'facing south to determine position,' referring to what appears below. Above and below encounter each other, cold and heat overlook each other; when the qi is compatible there is harmony, when it is not compatible there is disease. Huangdi said: Why does disease occur even when the qi is compatible? Qibo said: Because what is below overlooks what is above, and their positions are not appropriate to each other. Huangdi said: What of movement and stillness? Qibo said: What is above moves to the right, what is below moves to the left; left and right complete the circuit of heaven, and after a surplus they meet again. Huangdi said: I have heard Guiyuqu say that what corresponds to earth is still. Now you, Master, say that what is below moves to the left — I do not understand what this means. I wish to hear how this comes about. Qibo said: The movement and stillness of heaven and earth, the shifting and returning of the five phases — even Guiyuqu observed them only from above, and still could not comprehend them fully. Now as for the working of change and transformation: heaven hangs down its images, earth completes forms; the seven luminaries thread through the void, the five phases are attached to the earth. The earth is that which bears and carries the forms and categories of what is generated and completed. The void is that which arrays the essential qi corresponding to heaven. The movement of form and essence is like the relation of root and trunk to branch and leaf: looking upward to observe their images, even what is far off can be known.

Huangdi said: Is the earth situated below, or is it not? Qibo said: The earth is below man, yet it is suspended within the Great Void. Huangdi said: What upholds it? Qibo said: The great qi upholds it. Dryness dries it, summer-heat steams it, wind moves it, dampness moistens it, cold hardens it, fire warms it. Thus wind and cold lie below, dryness and heat lie above, damp qi lies in the middle, and fire circulates and travels between them; cold and heat enter the cycle six times, and thereby the earth is made to become hollow and to give rise to generation and transformation. Hence when dryness prevails the earth becomes parched; when summer-heat prevails the earth becomes hot; when wind prevails the earth is set in motion; when dampness prevails the earth becomes muddy; when cold prevails the earth cracks; when fire prevails the earth becomes solid and firm.

Huangdi said: How does one observe the qi of heaven and earth? Qibo said: The workings of the qi of heaven and earth, the rise of dominance and counter-dominance, are not manifested in pulse diagnosis. The Pulse Canon says: 'The changes of heaven and earth cannot be diagnosed by the pulse' — this is what is meant. Huangdi said: What of the intervening qi? Qibo said: One observes it according to where the qi resides, expecting it to the left or to the right. Huangdi said: How does one anticipate it? Qibo said: If one follows the qi there is harmony; if one goes against the qi there is disease. If the qi is not in its proper position there is disease. If it shifts repeatedly from its position there is disease. If it loses and fails to hold its position there is danger. If the cubit and inch pulse positions are reversed, there is death. If yin and yang cross, there is death. One must first establish the governing qi of the year in order to know its qi; once the correspondences to left and right are observed, only then may one speak of the contrariety and compliance that determine life and death.

Huangdi said: Cold, heat, dryness, dampness, wind, and fire — how do they correspond in man, and how do they generate and transform the myriad things? Qibo said: The east generates wind; wind generates wood; wood generates sourness; sourness generates the liver; the liver generates the sinews; the sinews generate the heart. In heaven this is the mysterious; in man it is the Way; on earth it is transformation. Transformation generates the five flavors, the Way generates wisdom, the mysterious generates spirit, transformation generates qi. Spirit, in heaven, is wind; on earth, it is wood; in the body, it is the sinews; in qi, it is suppleness; in the viscera, it is the liver. Its nature is warmth; its virtue is harmony; its function is movement; its color is kingfisher-green; its transformation is flourishing growth; its creature is the furred kind; its governance is dispersal; its command is to spread and put forth; its aberration is uprooting and breaking; its calamity is falling and shedding; its flavor is sour; its emotion is anger. Anger damages the liver; grief overcomes anger. Wind damages the liver; dryness overcomes wind. Sourness damages the sinews; pungency overcomes sourness.

The south generates heat; heat generates fire; fire generates bitterness; bitterness generates the heart; the heart generates blood; blood generates the spleen. In heaven this is heat; on earth it is fire; in the body it is the vessels; in qi it is breath; in the viscera it is the heart. Its nature is summer-heat; its virtue is manifestation; its function is restlessness; its color is red; its transformation is luxuriant growth; its creature is the feathered kind; its governance is brightness; its command is sultry steaminess; its aberration is scorching and searing; its calamity is burning; its flavor is bitter; its emotion is joy. Joy damages the heart; fear overcomes joy. Heat damages qi; cold overcomes heat. Bitterness damages qi; saltiness overcomes bitterness.

The center generates dampness; dampness generates earth; earth generates sweetness; sweetness generates the spleen; the spleen generates flesh; flesh generates the lung. In heaven this is dampness; on earth it is earth; in the body it is flesh; in qi it is fullness; in the viscera it is the spleen. Its nature is calm and encompassing; its virtue is moistening; its function is transformation; its color is yellow; its transformation is abundance; its creature is the unshelled, unfurred kind; its governance is quietude; its command is cloud and rain; its aberration is sudden downpour; its calamity is flooding and collapse; its flavor is sweet; its emotion is contemplation. Contemplation damages the spleen; anger overcomes contemplation. Dampness damages the flesh; wind overcomes dampness. Sweetness damages the spleen; sourness overcomes sweetness.

The west generates dryness; dryness generates metal; metal generates pungency; pungency generates the lung; the lung generates the skin and body hair; skin and body hair generate the kidney. In heaven this is dryness; on earth it is metal; in the body it is skin and body hair; in qi it is completion; in the viscera it is the lung. Its nature is coolness; its virtue is clarity; its function is firmness; its color is white; its transformation is contraction; its creature is the shelled kind; its governance is severity; its command is fog and dew; its aberration is harshness and killing; its calamity is withering and shedding; its flavor is pungent; its emotion is worry. Worry damages the lung; joy overcomes worry. Heat damages skin and body hair; cold overcomes heat. Pungency damages skin and body hair; bitterness overcomes pungency.

The north generates cold; cold generates water; water generates saltiness; saltiness generates the kidney; the kidney generates bone marrow; marrow generates the liver. In heaven this is cold; on earth it is water; in the body it is bone; in qi it is firmness; in the viscera it is the kidney. Its nature is severe cold; its virtue is coldness; its function is storage; its color is black; its transformation is solemnity; its creature is the scaled kind; its governance is stillness; its command is frost and snow; its aberration is freezing and bitter cold; its calamity is ice and hail; its flavor is salty; its emotion is fear. Fear damages the kidney; contemplation overcomes fear. Cold damages the blood; dryness overcomes cold. Saltiness damages the blood; sweetness overcomes saltiness.

Qi changes and is established in turn, each with its own precedence: when it is not in its proper position it is pathogenic, when it is in its proper position it is correct. Huangdi said: What are the changes by which disease arises? Qibo said: When the qi is compatible, disease is mild; when it is not compatible, disease is severe. Huangdi said: What of the governance of the year? Qibo said: When qi is in excess, it overcontrols what it normally dominates, and also insults what it cannot dominate. When it is insufficient, then what it cannot dominate will insult and take advantage of it, and what it normally dominates will treat it lightly and insult it as well. The one who insults may in turn receive pathogenic qi in return — when one insults and receives pathogenic qi in return, it is because one has grown too reckless and lost due caution. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Great Treatise on the Subtle Meaning of the Six [Qi]
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: "Ah, how distant and profound is the Way of Heaven! It is like facing a drifting cloud, or like gazing into a deep abyss. A deep abyss can still be fathomed, but facing a drifting cloud, one cannot know its limit. You, Master, have repeatedly told me to reverently uphold the Way of Heaven. I have heard this and kept it in mind, but privately I find it strange, for I do not understand what it means. I wish you would give full expression to your intent and speak exhaustively of this matter, so that it may never be extinguished, but endure long without being cut off. May I hear of the Way of Heaven?" Qibo bowed his head to the ground twice and replied: "How enlightened is this question about the Way of Heaven! It concerns the sequence of Heaven and the times of flourishing and decline."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the six-sixfold divisions of the Way of Heaven and how flourishing and decline occur within them." Qibo said: "Above and below there are fixed positions; to the left and right there are guiding sequences. Thus, to the right of shaoyang, yangming governs. To the right of yangming, taiyang governs. To the right of taiyang, jueyin governs. To the right of jueyin, shaoyin governs. To the right of shaoyin, taiyin governs. To the right of taiyin, shaoyang governs. This is what is called the branch [biao] of qi, awaiting as one who faces south. Hence it is said: following the sequence of Heaven, at the times of flourishing and decline, one shifts the light to fix the position, standing upright and awaiting it — this is what is meant. Above shaoyang, fire qi governs, with jueyin seen as the intermediate. Above yangming, dryness qi governs, with taiyin seen as the intermediate. Above taiyang, cold qi governs, with shaoyin seen as the intermediate. Above jueyin, wind qi governs, with shaoyang seen as the intermediate. Above shaoyin, heat qi governs, with taiyang seen as the intermediate. Above taiyin, dampness qi governs, with yangming seen as the intermediate. These are what is called the roots [ben]. Below the root is the intermediate that is seen; below what is seen is the branch [biao] of qi. Root and branch are not the same, and the correspondences of qi take different forms."

Huangdi said: "There are cases where the qi arrives when it should arrive, cases where it should arrive but does not, and cases where it arrives in excess — why is this?" Qibo said: "When it arrives at the time it should arrive, this is harmony. When it should arrive but does not, the incoming qi is insufficient. When it arrives before it should, the incoming qi is in excess." Huangdi said: "In the cases of arriving before it should and not arriving when it should, what happens?" Qibo said: "If they correspond to the proper season, this is compliance; if not, this is rebellion. Rebellion gives rise to abnormal change, and abnormal change gives rise to disease." Huangdi said: "Good. Please speak of these correspondences." Qibo said: "In living things, their growth is the correspondence. In qi, the pulse is the correspondence."

Huangdi said: "Good. I wish to hear how the patterns of earth correspond to the positions of the six divisions of qi." Qibo said: "To the right of Bright Manifestation is the position of sovereign fire. To the right of sovereign fire, retreating one step, ministerial fire governs; advancing another step, earth qi governs; advancing another step, metal qi governs; advancing another step, water qi governs; advancing another step, wood qi governs; advancing another step, sovereign fire governs again. Beneath ministerial fire, water qi receives and continues it. Beneath the position of water, earth qi receives and continues it. Beneath the position of earth, wind qi receives and continues it. Beneath the position of wind, metal qi receives and continues it. Beneath the position of metal, fire qi receives and continues it. Beneath sovereign fire, yin essence receives and continues it." Huangdi said: "Why is this?" Qibo said: "When something becomes excessive it causes harm; by being received and restrained, it is brought under control. When it is controlled, generation and transformation proceed, and outwardly flourishing and decline are displayed in due order. When harm occurs, ruin and disorder result, and generation and transformation suffer great affliction."

Huangdi said: "What of flourishing and decline?" Qibo said: "When it is not in its proper position, this is pathogenic; when it occupies its proper position, this is correct. When it is pathogenic, the abnormal change is severe; when it is correct, the change is slight." Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'occupying its proper position'?" Qibo said: "When the wood movement falls on the year mao, the fire movement on wu, the earth movement on the four seasonal months, the metal movement on you, and the water movement on zi — these are called 'year conjunctions,' where the qi is balanced." Huangdi said: "What of when it is not in its position?" Qibo said: "Then the year does not conjoin with the movement." Huangdi said: "In years of the earth movement, taiyin appears above; in years of the fire movement, shaoyang or shaoyin appears above; in years of the metal movement, yangming appears above; in years of the wood movement, jueyin appears above; in years of the water movement, taiyang appears above — how is this?" Qibo said: "This is the conjunction of Heaven with the movement. Hence the Tianyuan Ce, the Book of Heavenly Origins, calls this 'Heavenly Correspondence.'" Huangdi said: "What of when Heavenly Correspondence and Year Conjunction occur together?" Qibo said: "This is the conjunction of the Grand Unity with Heavenly Correspondence." Huangdi said: "What of their relative rank, noble or lowly?" Qibo said: "Heavenly Correspondence acts as the Law-Enforcer, the Year Position acts as the Order-Executor, and the Grand Unity Heavenly Correspondence acts as the Noble Personage." Huangdi said: "How does pathogenic qi strike in these cases?" Qibo said: "When it strikes the Law-Enforcer, the disease comes swiftly and is dangerous. When it strikes the Order-Executor, the disease comes slowly but is distinctive. When it strikes the Noble Personage, the disease comes suddenly and is fatal." Huangdi said: "What of the exchange of positions?" Qibo said: "When the sovereign occupies the minister's position, this is compliant; when the minister occupies the sovereign's position, this is rebellious. When rebellious, the disease comes soon and its harm is swift; when compliant, the disease is distant and its harm is slight. This refers to the two fires, sovereign and ministerial."

Huangdi said: "Good. I wish to hear about the 'steps' — how are they?" Qibo said: "What is called a 'step' spans sixty-some degrees. Thus, over twenty-four steps, the accumulated surplus amounts to a hundred ke, forming a full extra day." Huangdi said: "How do the six qi correspond to the changes of the five phases?" Qibo said: "Positions have their endings and beginnings; qi has its early and middle phases. Above and below are not the same, and so what is sought in each also differs." Huangdi said: "How is this sought?" Qibo said: "Heavenly qi begins at jia; earthly qi begins at zi. When zi and jia combine, this is called the establishment of the year. By carefully observing its timing, the qi can be anticipated."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear how the beginning and end of the year's six qi come early or late." Qibo said: "How penetrating is this question! In the year jiazi, the heavenly count of the first qi begins at the water clock's first ke and ends at the eighty-seventh ke and a half. The second qi begins at eighty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at seventy-five ke. The third qi begins at seventy-six ke and ends at sixty-two ke and a half. The fourth qi begins at sixty-two ke and six fen, and ends at fifty ke. The fifth qi begins at fifty-one ke and ends at thirty-seven ke and a half. The sixth qi begins at thirty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at twenty-five ke. This is called the 'first of the six,' the heavenly count. In the year yichou, the heavenly count of the first qi begins at twenty-six ke and ends at twelve ke and a half. The second qi begins at twelve ke and six fen, and ends at the water clock's hundredth ke. The third qi begins at the first ke and ends at eighty-seven ke and a half. The fourth qi begins at eighty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at seventy-five ke. The fifth qi begins at seventy-six ke and ends at sixty-two ke and a half. The sixth qi begins at sixty-two ke and six fen, and ends at fifty ke. This is called the 'second of the six,' the heavenly count. In the year bingyin, the first qi's heavenly count begins at fifty-one ke and ends at thirty-seven ke and a half. The second qi begins at thirty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at twenty-five ke. The third qi begins at twenty-six ke and ends at twelve ke and a half. The fourth qi begins at twelve ke and six fen, and ends at the water clock's hundredth ke. The fifth qi begins at the first ke and ends at eighty-seven ke and a half. The sixth qi begins at eighty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at seventy-five ke. This is called the 'third of the six,' the heavenly count. In the year dingmao, the first qi's heavenly count begins at seventy-six ke and ends at sixty-two ke and a half. The second qi begins at sixty-two ke and six fen, and ends at fifty ke. The third qi begins at fifty-one ke and ends at thirty-seven ke and a half. The fourth qi begins at thirty-seven ke and six fen, and ends at twenty-five ke. The fifth qi begins at twenty-six ke and ends at twelve ke and a half. The sixth qi begins at twelve ke and six fen, and ends at the water clock's hundredth ke. This is called the 'fourth of the six,' the heavenly count. Next, in the year wuchen, the first qi again begins at the first ke. It is always thus, without end, cycling round and beginning again." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear how the yearly signs proceed." Qibo said: "How thorough is this question! When the sun has completed one full circuit, the heavenly qi begins at the first ke. When the sun has completed a second circuit, the heavenly qi begins at the twenty-sixth ke. When the sun has completed a third circuit, the heavenly qi begins at the fifty-first ke. When the sun has completed a fourth circuit, the heavenly qi begins at the seventy-sixth ke. When the sun has completed a fifth circuit, the heavenly qi again begins at the first ke — this is called one full cycle. Therefore the years yin, wu, and xu share a common meeting of qi; the years mao, wei, and hai share a common meeting of qi; the years chen, shen, and zi share a common meeting of qi; and likewise the years si, you, and chou share a common meeting of qi — and so it ends and begins again."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of its application." Qibo said: "In speaking of Heaven, one seeks it in the root; in speaking of Earth, one seeks it in the position; in speaking of Humanity, one seeks it in the intersection of qi." Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'the intersection of qi'?" Qibo said: "Between the positions above and below, in the midst of the intersection of qi, is where humanity dwells. Hence it is said: above the Pivot of Heaven, heavenly qi governs; below the Pivot of Heaven, earthly qi governs; at the boundary of their intersection, human qi follows, and the myriad things depend upon it. This is what is meant."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'the beginning and the middle'?" Qibo said: "The beginning phase spans thirty-some degrees; the middle qi follows the same measure." Huangdi said: "Why distinguish beginning and middle?" Qibo said: "It is the means by which Heaven and Earth are distinguished." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear the whole of it." Qibo said: "The beginning phase is earthly qi; the middle phase is heavenly qi." Huangdi said: "What of their ascending and descending?" Qibo said: "The ascending and descending of qi is the alternating function of Heaven and Earth." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear how this function operates." Qibo said: "When ascent is complete, descent follows — what descends is called Heaven's qi. When descent is complete, ascent follows — what ascends is called Earth's qi. Heavenly qi descends, and its qi flows into the earth; earthly qi ascends, and its qi rises into Heaven. Thus the high and the low call to one another, ascending and descending depend on each other, and change is thereby brought forth."

Huangdi said: "Good. Cold and dampness meet one another, dryness and heat draw near to each other, wind and fire encounter each other — may I hear of this?" Qibo said: "Qi has its dominance and its counter-response. In the arising of dominance and counter-response, there is virtue and there is transformation, there is function and there is change. When change occurs, pathogenic qi resides there." Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'pathogenic'?" Qibo said: "The generation of things follows from transformation; the culmination of things arises from change. The mutual clash of change and transformation is the source from which success and failure arise. Thus qi has its going and returning, and function has its slowness and speed. When these four are present, transformation and change occur, and this is how wind comes to arise." Huangdi said: "Slowness, speed, going, and returning are what give rise to wind, and transformation and change thereby follow from the changes of flourishing and decline. But how is it that success and failure, each latent within the other, roam in the midst of things?" Qibo said: "Success and failure, each latent within the other, are born of movement. When movement does not cease, change is brought forth." Huangdi said: "Is there a fixed time for this?" Qibo said: "Where there is no generation and no transformation, that is the state of stillness." Huangdi said: "Is there truly no generation and transformation anywhere?" Qibo said: "When outward and inward movement cease, the spirit-mechanism is extinguished and transformation perishes. When ascending and descending cease, the establishment of qi is left solitary and imperiled. Therefore, without outward and inward movement, there could be no birth, growth, maturity, aging, and ending; without ascending and descending, there could be no birth, growth, transformation, harvesting, and storage. Thus there is no vessel that does not have ascending, descending, outward, and inward movement. For the vessel is the dwelling place of generation and transformation; when the vessel disperses and scatters, generation and transformation cease. Hence there is nothing that lacks outward and inward movement, and nothing that lacks ascending and descending. Transformation has its greater and lesser degrees, and its fixed times have their nearer and further reach — but where these four are present, it is precious to maintain constancy; when constancy is violated, calamity and harm will arrive. Hence it is said: 'where there is no form, there is no affliction' — this is what is meant." Huangdi said: "Good. Is there anything that neither generates nor transforms?" Qibo said: "How thorough is this question! Only the Realized Person, who is united with the Dao, attains this." Huangdi said: "Good."

素问.title_en: Great Treatise on Mutations in the Exchange of Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: The five movements alternate in governance, corresponding above to the cycle of heaven; yin and yang go and return, cold and heat follow one another in succession; the true qi and the pathogenic contend with each other, so that inner and outer become separated; the six channels surge and billow, the five qi tilt and shift; there is excess and deficiency, singular domination and mutual encroachment. I wish to hear of their origin, and of the constant names by which they are known — may I hear of this? Qibo bowed his head twice and replied: How illuminating is this question — it concerns the manifest Dao. This is what the High Sovereigns of old prized, and what my former teachers transmitted to me. Though I am unworthy, I have in the past heard its essential meaning. Huangdi said: I have heard that to find the right person and not teach him is called losing the Dao, while to transmit it to one who is not the right person is to squander the treasures of Heaven carelessly. My virtue is truly meager, insufficient to receive the utmost Dao; yet I grieve for the many who lack an end to their teaching, and I wish that you, Master, would preserve this knowledge without end, transmitting it without limit, so that I, charged with this office, may model myself upon it and put it into practice. What should be done? Qibo said: Allow me to speak of it fully. The Upper Canon states: 'As for the Dao, one who above understands the patterns of heaven, below understands the patterns of earth, and in the middle understands the affairs of man, may endure long' — this is what is meant. Huangdi said: What does this mean? Qibo said: The root of qi lies in position. What occupies the position of heaven is the pattern of heaven; what occupies the position of earth is the pattern of earth; and what penetrates the transformations of human qi is the affairs of man. Therefore, when a movement is in excess, it arrives ahead of the heavenly cycle; when it is deficient, it arrives behind the heavenly cycle — this is what is meant by governance and transformation to which man responds.

Huangdi said: When the transformations of the five movements are in excess, what happens? Qibo said: When in a given year wood is in excess, wind qi circulates prevalently, and the spleen, [governed by] earth, receives the pathogenic influence. People suffer from undigested-food diarrhea and diminished appetite, heaviness of the body and oppressive vexation, intestinal rumbling and distension of the abdomen and flanks; above, this corresponds to the Year Star [Jupiter]. In severe cases there is absent-mindedness and irascibility, dizziness and clouding of vision, and disease at the vertex; the transforming qi fails to govern, while the generating qi rules alone; clouds and objects fly and stir, grasses and trees are unsettled, and in extreme cases they are shaken down and shed; conversely there is pain in the flanks with severe vomiting. If the Chongyang pulse is cut off, death ensues and cannot be treated; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star [Venus]. When fire is in excess, blazing summer-heat circulates prevalently, and the lung, governed by metal, receives the pathogenic influence. People suffer from malarial disorders, shortness of breath, coughing and panting, overflow and discharge of blood pouring downward, dryness of the throat and deafness, heat in the center of the body and heat in the shoulders and back; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder Star [Mars]. In severe cases there is pain in the chest, distension and pain in the flanks, pain between the chest, back, and shoulder blades, pain in the inner aspect of both arms, generalized heat and bone pain, and this develops into spreading sores. The gathering qi of autumn fails to move, while the growing qi of summer alone is manifest; rain, frost, and cold appear; above, this corresponds to the Hour Star [Mercury]. When lesser yin or lesser yang preside above, fire scorches and burns, ice and springs dry up, and things are parched and withered; the disease turns instead to delirious raving and manic excess, coughing and panting with wheezing sounds, and below, severe unremitting overflow and discharge of blood. If the Taiyuan pulse is cut off, death ensues and cannot be treated; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder Star. When earth is in excess, rain and dampness circulate prevalently, and the kidney, governed by water, receives the pathogenic influence. People suffer from abdominal pain, cold reversal of the limbs and despondency of mind, heaviness of the body and oppressive vexation; above, this corresponds to the Quelling Star [Saturn]. In severe cases there is wasting of the flesh, weakness of the feet that cannot be controlled, a tendency to spasms while walking, pain in the soles of the feet, fluid retention giving rise to fullness in the center and diminished appetite, and the four limbs cannot be raised. When the mutation arises and gains its position, the qi of the other viscera is suppressed, and the transforming qi alone governs; springs gush forth and rivers overflow, and dried-up marshes give rise to fish; wind and rain arrive in great force, the earth crumbles and collapses, and scaled creatures appear on dry land. The disease is abdominal fullness, sloppy diarrhea, and intestinal rumbling; conversely, if the downward flux is severe and the Taixi pulse is cut off, death ensues and cannot be treated; above, this corresponds to the Year Star. When metal is in excess, dryness qi circulates prevalently, and the liver, governed by wood, receives the pathogenic influence. People suffer from pain below both flanks and in the lesser abdomen, redness and pain of the eyes, ulceration of the canthi, and deafness. When the stern, killing qi is severe, there is heaviness of the body and oppressive vexation, chest pain radiating to the back, and fullness of both flanks with pain radiating to the lesser abdomen; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star. In severe cases there is panting, coughing, and counterflow qi, pain in the shoulders and back, and disease throughout the buttocks, genitals, thighs, knees, hips, calves, shins, and feet; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder Star. The gathering qi becomes severe, the generating qi is pressed downward, grasses and trees contract, and their green foliage withers and falls; the disease instead turns to sudden severe pain, such that one cannot turn from side to side because of pain in the ribs and flanks, coughing and counterflow become severe with overflow of blood. If the Taichong pulse is cut off, death ensues and cannot be treated; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star. When water is in excess, cold qi circulates prevalently, and the pathogenic influence harms the heart, governed by fire. People suffer from generalized heat, vexation of the heart, restlessness and palpitation, yin reversal with cold above, below, and in the center, delirious raving and heart pain; cold qi arrives early; above, this corresponds to the Hour Star. In severe cases there is abdominal distension and swelling of the shins, panting and coughing with profuse sweating and aversion to wind; heavy rains arrive, and dust and fog lie thick and murky; above, this corresponds to the Quelling Star. When greater yang presides above, rain, ice, snow, and frost descend out of season, and damp qi transforms living things; the disease instead turns to abdominal fullness, intestinal rumbling, and sloppy diarrhea, undigested food, thirst with delirious dizziness. If the Shenmen pulse is cut off, death ensues and cannot be treated; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder and Hour Stars.

Huangdi said: Good. What of when they are deficient? Qibo said: How thorough is this question! When wood is deficient, dryness then prevails greatly; the generating qi fails to respond, and grasses and trees flourish late. When the stern, killing qi is severe, hard timber splits and cracks, and all vegetation withers and turns dry and green-brown; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star. People suffer coldness in the center, pain in the ribs and flanks and in the lesser abdomen, intestinal rumbling and sloppy diarrhea; cool rains arrive in season; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star; its grain is the green one. When yang brightness presides above, the generating qi loses its governance, grasses and trees flourish a second time, and the transforming qi becomes urgent; above, this corresponds to the Great White and Quelling Stars; its ruling grain is the green one, maturing early. When the retaliatory qi arrives, blazing summer-heat and flowing fire appear, the nature of dampness becomes dry, soft and brittle grasses and trees become scorched and withered, their lower parts regrow, and flowers and fruit ripen together; the disease is cold and heat, sores and ulcers, heat rashes and papules, and carbuncles and boils; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder and Great White Stars; its grain is the white and hard one. White dew descends early, the gathering and killing qi moves, cold rain harms living things, insects devour the sweet-yellow crops, and the spleen, governed by earth, receives the pathogenic influence; the red qi transforms belatedly, and the heart qi governs late; wood above overcomes the lung, governed by metal, and the white qi is thereby subdued, so its grain fails to ripen; there is coughing and nasal congestion; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder and Great White Stars. When fire is deficient, cold then prevails greatly; the governance of growth is not exercised, so that living things flourish above but congeal below; when the bleakness is severe, yang qi fails to transform, and this cuts short flourishing and beauty; above, this corresponds to the Hour Star. People suffer pain in the chest, distension and pain in both flanks, pain between the chest, back, and shoulder blades and in the inner aspect of both arms, oppressive dizziness and clouded confusion, heart pain and sudden loss of voice, distension of the chest, abdomen, and flanks pulling downward with the lower back so that they ache together; in severe cases one cannot straighten after bending, and the hip and thigh feel as if separated; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder and Hour Stars; its grain is the red one. When the retaliatory qi arrives, dust hangs oppressive and heavy rains also come, and black qi is thereby debased; the disease is duck-dropping diarrhea and abdominal fullness, inability to take in food or drink, cold in the center with intestinal rumbling, pouring diarrhea and abdominal pain, sudden cramping and wilting numbness such that the feet cannot bear the body; above, this corresponds to the Quelling and Hour Stars; the black grain fails to ripen. When earth is deficient, wind then prevails greatly; the transforming qi fails to issue its command, so grasses and trees grow lush and flourishing; when the tossing and blowing is severe, they blossom but do not bear fruit; above, this corresponds to the Year Star. People suffer from undigested-food diarrhea and sudden turmoil disorders, heaviness of the body and abdominal pain, shaking and twitching of the sinews and bones, twitching and soreness of the flesh, and a tendency to anger; the yearly qi takes charge, hibernating creatures burrow in early, and all suffer from cold in the center; above, this corresponds to the Year Star and the Quelling Star; its grain is the yellow one. When the retaliatory qi arrives, the governance of gathering becomes severe and stern, and prized trees wither in their green; there is sudden pain in the chest and flanks pulling down to the lesser abdomen, with frequent sighing; insects devour the sweet-yellow crops, and that qi lodges in the spleen, so the yellow grain diminishes; people eat little and lose their sense of taste, and the green grain is also damaged; above, this corresponds to the Great White and Year Stars. When reverting yin presides above, flowing water does not freeze, hibernating creatures come out and appear, the storing qi is not exercised, and the white metal qi does not return to dominance; above, this corresponds to the Year Star, and the people are thereby healthy. When metal is deficient, blazing fire then moves; the generating qi is thereby exercised, and the growing qi alone predominates, so the multitude of things flourish, while scorching dryness also moves; above, this corresponds to the Sparkling Deluder Star. People suffer from heaviness and dizziness of the shoulders and back, nasal congestion and sneezing, and bloody stool pouring down; the gathering qi arrives late; above, this corresponds to the Great White Star; its grain is the hard, bearded one. When the retaliatory qi arrives, cold rain suddenly comes, and ice, hail, frost, and snow fall, killing living things; yin reversal then repels yang, which instead flows upward, causing pain at the Brain's Door, extending to the fontanelle and vertex with fever; above, this corresponds to the Hour Star; the red grain fails to ripen; people suffer mouth sores, and in severe cases, heart pain. When water is deficient, dampness then prevails greatly; the growing qi is instead exercised, and its transformation becomes rapid; summer-heat and rain arrive repeatedly; above, this corresponds to the Quelling Star. People suffer from abdominal fullness and heaviness of the body, watery diarrhea, cold sores that weep fluid, onset of pain in the lower back and thighs, difficulty moving the popliteal fossa, calves, thighs, and knees, oppressive vexation, wilting weakness of the feet and cold reversal, pain in the soles of the feet, and in severe cases swelling of the instep; the storing qi does not govern properly, and the kidney qi is unbalanced; above, this corresponds to the Hour Star; its grain is the black millet. When greater yin presides above, then great cold repeatedly arises, hibernating creatures store away early, the ground accumulates hard ice, and the light of yang fails to govern; people suffer from cold ailments in the lower body, and in severe cases abdominal fullness and floating swelling; above, this corresponds to the Quelling Star; its ruling grain is the yellow one. When the retaliatory qi arrives, great winds burst forth suddenly, grasses are bent flat and trees shed their leaves, growth is no longer fresh, the complexion changes from time to time, sinews and bones both become bent and crooked, the flesh twitches and quivers, the vision becomes dim and blurred, things become sparse and cracked, papules break out on the flesh, qi collects in the diaphragm region, and there is pain in the heart and abdomen; the yellow qi is thereby damaged, and its grain fails to mature; above, this corresponds to the Year Star.

Huangdi said: Good. I wish to hear about their seasons. Qibo said: How thorough is this question! When wood is deficient, if spring has the transformation of whistling branches and rhythmic ease, then autumn will have the governance of mist, dew, and clear coolness. If spring has the domination of bleak desolation and destructive harm, then summer will have the retaliation of blazing heat and scorching fire. Its calamity is in the east, its viscus is the liver, its disease internally lodges in the ribs and flanks, and externally is in the joints. When fire is deficient, if summer has the transformation of brightness and radiant splendor, then winter will have the governance of severe sternness, frost, and cold.
