If it arrives in a balanced manner, this is normal; if it arrives excessively, this is disease; if it arrives in a contrary manner, this is disease; if it fails to arrive when it should, this is disease; if it arrives before it should, this is disease; and if yin and yang are reversed in their expected arrival, the condition is critical.'

The Emperor said: 'The root and branch of the six qi are followed differently, how is this?' Qibo said: 'Among the qi, some follow the root, some follow both root and branch, and some follow neither root nor branch.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear the whole of it.' Qibo said: 'Lesser yang and greater yin follow the root; lesser yin and greater yang follow both the root and the branch; yang brightness and reverting yin follow neither root nor branch, but follow the intermediate paired qi instead. Hence, for those that follow the root, transformation is generated from the root; for those that follow both root and branch, there is transformation of both root and branch; for those that follow the intermediate qi, transformation occurs by means of the intermediate qi.' The Emperor said: 'When the pulse is compliant yet the disease is contrary, how does one diagnose this?' Qibo said: 'The pulse arrives in a seemingly compliant manner, yet upon pressing it does not beat vigorously, this holds true for all the yang conditions.' The Emperor said: 'What of the pulse in the contrariety of the yin conditions?' Qibo said: 'The pulse arrives in a seemingly compliant manner, yet upon pressing it beats vigorously and is full. Hence, in the arising of the hundred diseases, some are generated from the root, some from the branch, some from the intermediate qi; some are resolved by treating the root, some by treating the branch, some by treating the intermediate qi, some by treating both branch and root; some are resolved by contrary treatment, some by compliant treatment. What appears contrary may in truth be compliant; what appears compliant may in truth be contrary. Hence it is said: he who knows branch and root can apply treatment without peril; he who clearly knows contrariety and compliance can act correctly without hesitation. This is what is meant. He who does not know this is unfit to speak of diagnosis, and is apt to throw the classics into confusion. Hence the Da Yao states: the crude practitioner chatters glibly, believing he knows it all; he says the heat has not yet ceased, when in fact a cold disease has already begun anew. Though the qi is the same, its manifestation differs, and he becomes confused in diagnosis and throws the classics into disorder. This is what is meant. Now, the way of branch and root is at once concise and broad, small yet vast, one may speak of a single principle and thereby know the harm of the hundred diseases. To speak of branch and root is easy, provided nothing is lost; to examine root and branch enables the qi to be brought into balance. He who clearly understands dominance and return becomes a model for the myriad people, and the Way of heaven is thereby fulfilled.'

The Emperor said: 'In the changes of dominance and return, how do early and late timing come about?' Qibo said: 'As for the dominant qi: once dominance arrives, disease has already set in; and once the disease has become sullenly established, the return has already begun to sprout. As for the return: it arises once the dominance has run its course, and becomes severe once it attains its proper seasonal position. Dominance may be slight or severe, and return may be little or great; where the dominance is mild, the return is mild; where the dominance is depleted, the return is depleted, this is the constancy of heaven.' The Emperor said: 'When dominance and return occur, their movement sometimes fails to match the proper seasonal position, or arrives later than the expected time, what is the reason for this?' Qibo said: 'The generation of qi differs from the waxing and waning of its transformation. The functioning of the waxing and waning of cold, heat, warmth, and coolness lies in the four intercalary corners. Hence the movement of yang begins with warmth and reaches its height in summer heat; the movement of yin begins with clearness and reaches its height in cold. Spring, summer, autumn, and winter each vary somewhat in their divisions. Hence the Da Yao states: the mildness of spring becomes the heat of summer; the harshness of autumn becomes the fury of winter. Carefully examine the four intercalary corners, and all the signs of observation converge there, their culmination can be seen, and their inception can be known. This is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'Does this variance have a fixed measure?' Qibo said: 'It amounts in all to about thirty degrees.' The Emperor said: 'How does the pulse respond in each case?' Qibo said: 'The variance follows the same regular pattern, awaiting its proper time before departing. The Mai Yao states: in spring the pulse should not be deep; in summer it should not be wiry; in winter it should not be choppy; in autumn it should not be rapid, this is called the four blockages. If deepness is excessive, this is disease; if wiriness is excessive, this is disease; if choppiness is excessive, this is disease; if rapidity is excessive, this is disease. If these signs appear intermingled, this is disease; if they reappear out of season, this is disease; if a sign departs before it should, this is disease; if a sign fails to depart when it should, this is disease; and if the reverse of the seasonal norm occurs, death results. Hence it is said: the mutual custody of the qi between seasons and pulses is like a balance scale that must not lose its equilibrium. As for the qi of yin and yang: when clear and still, generation and transformation proceed in order; when disturbed, grave illness arises. This is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'What of the obscure and the bright?' Qibo said: 'Where the two yin meet and are exhausted, it is called obscure; where the two yang combine into brightness, it is called bright. The pairing of the obscure and the bright corresponds to the distinction between cold and heat.' The Emperor said: 'What of the equinoxes and solstices?' Qibo said: 'The point where the qi culminates is called a solstice; the point where the qi divides is called an equinox. At a solstice, the qi is the same; at an equinox, the qi is differentiated, this is what is called the proper regulation of heaven and earth.' The Emperor said: 'You, Master, have said that the qi of spring and autumn begins beforehand, while the qi of winter and summer begins afterward, this I already understand. Yet the coming and going of the six qi and their governance of the year are not constant, how, then, does one supplement and drain?' Qibo said: 'What is governed above and below, follow what is beneficial to each; rectifying the flavor used is the essential point, the same method applies to left and right. The Da Yao states: for the governance of lesser yang, use sweetness first and saltiness afterward; for yang brightness, use acridity first and sourness afterward; for greater yang, use saltiness first and bitterness afterward; for reverting yin, use sourness first and acridity afterward; for lesser yin, use sweetness first and saltiness afterward; for greater yin, use bitterness first and sweetness afterward. Assist with what is beneficial and nourish with what generates the needed qi, this is called attaining the qi.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. Now, the hundred diseases that arise are all generated from wind, cold, summer-heat, dampness, dryness, and fire, through their transformations and changes. The classics say: what is in excess, drain it; what is deficient, supplement it. I have granted this to the practitioners, yet the practitioners' application of it has still not achieved complete success. I wish to make the essential Way infallible in practice, responding like drumstick to drum, as swift and sure as pulling out a thorn or washing away a stain, the skill of the crafty, the subtle, the spiritlike, and the sagely. May I hear of it?' Qibo said: 'To carefully examine the pivotal mechanism of disease without losing what is proper to the qi, this is what is meant.' The Emperor said: 'I wish to hear what the pivotal mechanisms of disease are.' Qibo said: 'All conditions of wind with trembling and dizziness belong to the liver. All conditions of cold with contraction and tension belong to the kidney. All conditions of qi with oppression and stifling belong to the lung. All conditions of dampness with swelling and fullness belong to the spleen. All conditions of heat with clouded vision and convulsion belong to fire. All diseases of itching and sores belong to the heart. All conditions of reversal, retention, and discharge belong to the lower region. All conditions of wilting, panting, and vomiting belong to the upper region. All conditions of chattering teeth and trembling, as though the spirit had lost its guard, belong to fire. All conditions of spasm and stiffness of the nape belong to dampness. All conditions of contrary surging upward belong to fire. All conditions of distension with enlarged abdomen belong to heat. All conditions of agitation, mania, and reckless behavior belong to fire. All conditions of sudden rigidity and stiffness belong to wind. All conditions with sound, such that percussion of the abdomen resounds like a drum, belong to heat. All diseases of swelling, aching pain and soreness, fright and alarm belong to fire. All conditions of twisting, contortion, and turbid bodily fluids belong to heat. All diseases with clear, cold, and limpid bodily fluids belong to cold. All conditions of vomiting of sour fluid and sudden pouring diarrhea with urgent tenesmus belong to heat. Hence the Da Yao states: carefully hold to the pivotal mechanisms of disease, and attend to each according to its category. Where a sign is present, seek its cause; where it is absent, still seek its cause. Where there is excess, hold it accountable; where there is deficiency, hold it accountable. One must first determine the dominance among the five phases, unblock the blood and qi, bring about their smooth flow, and achieve harmony and balance. This is what is meant.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of the yin-yang functions of the five flavors?' Qibo said: 'Acrid and sweet flavors, which effuse and disperse, are yang; sour and bitter flavors, which surge upward and drain downward, are yin; the salty flavor, which surges upward and drains downward, is yin; the bland flavor, which percolates and drains, is yang. Of these six flavors, some astringe, some disperse, some moderate, some quicken, some dry, some moisten, some soften, some firm, one applies them according to what is beneficial, regulating the qi so as to bring it into balance.' The Emperor said: 'For conditions not resolved by regulating the qi alone, how does one treat them? Among substances toxic and non-toxic, which comes first and which comes after? I wish to hear this principle.' Qibo said: 'Whether toxic or non-toxic, what is treated takes precedence, and the formula is regulated according to the disease's magnitude.' The Emperor said: 'Please tell me of this regulation.' Qibo said: 'One sovereign and two ministers constitute a small formula; one sovereign, three ministers, and five assistants constitute a medium formula; one sovereign, three ministers, and nine assistants constitute a large formula. What is cold, heat it; what is hot, cool it; what is mild, oppose it directly; what is severe, follow it with paradoxical treatment; what is hardened, whittle it away; what is a lodged pathogen, expel it; what is fatigued from taxation, warm it; what is bound, disperse it; what is retained, attack it; what is dry, moisten it; what is urgent, moderate it; what is dispersed, gather it; what is damaged, warm it; what is sluggish, move it; what is startled, calm it. Treat by raising or by lowering, by rubbing or by bathing, by pressing or by forceful seizure, by opening or by venting, in each case, fit the treatment to the matter at hand.'

The Emperor said: 'What is meant by contrary and compliant treatment?' Qibo said: 'Contrary treatment is direct treatment; compliant treatment is paradoxical treatment. Whether one follows it a little or a great deal depends on observing the particular case.' The Emperor said: 'What is meant by paradoxical treatment?' Qibo said: 'Heat medicine is used by means of cold symptoms, cold medicine is used by means of heat symptoms, blocking agents are used for conditions of blockage, and freely flowing agents are used for conditions of free flow. One must subdue the governing pathology, while first addressing its underlying cause. At the outset the treatment and symptom appear alike, but in the end they differ. In this way one may cause accumulations to be broken up, hardness to be dissolved, the qi to be harmonized, and the disease to be surely resolved.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What of cases resolved simply by regulating the qi?' Qibo said: 'Treat by contrariety, or treat by compliance; treat contrarily yet in a compliant manner, or treat compliantly yet in a contrary manner. Unblocking the qi so as to bring it into regulation is the principle.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of disease located internally and externally?' Qibo said: 'When disease proceeds from within to without, regulate the interior; when it proceeds from without to within, treat the exterior. When it proceeds from within to without and becomes predominant in the exterior, first regulate the interior and afterward treat the exterior; when it proceeds from without to within and becomes predominant in the interior, first treat the exterior and afterward regulate the interior. When the interior and exterior are unrelated to each other, treat the governing disease directly.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. When fire-heat is followed by aversion to cold, with fever resembling malaria, occurring sometimes once daily and sometimes once every several days, what is the reason for this?' Qibo said: 'This is because, at the time when the dominant and returning qi meet, there is a difference in their relative abundance. If yin qi is abundant and yang qi scant, the onset occurs at longer intervals; if yang qi is abundant and yin qi scant, the onset occurs at shorter intervals. This is the rhythm of the mutual clash and waxing-waning of dominance and return, malaria follows the same pattern.' The Emperor said: 'The classic texts say to treat cold with heat and heat with cold, and practitioners cannot abandon this rule and alter this Way. Yet there are cases of heat disease that, when treated with cold, remain hot, and cases of cold disease that, when treated with heat, remain cold, in both cases a new disease arises anew. How should these be treated?' Qibo said: 'In all cases where cooling treatment fails to relieve heat, seek the root in yin; where warming treatment fails to relieve cold, seek the root in yang. This is what is meant by seeking its category.' The Emperor said: 'Good. Why is it that taking a cold medicine instead produces heat, and taking a hot medicine instead produces cold?' Qibo said: 'This is because one has treated the already dominant qi, and hence the result is contrary.' The Emperor said: 'What of when this occurs even without treating the dominant qi?' Qibo said: 'How thorough is this question! This is due not to treating the dominant qi, but to the affinities of the five flavors. Now, when the five flavors enter the stomach, each returns to what it favors: sourness enters the liver first, bitterness the heart first, sweetness the spleen first, acridity the lung first, and saltiness the kidney first. Over time, this augments the corresponding qi, this is the constant pattern by which substances transform the body. Yet if the augmentation of qi persists too long, this becomes the cause of premature death.'

The Emperor said: 'Good. What is meant by sovereign and minister in the composition of a formula?' Qibo said: 'That which governs the disease is called the sovereign; that which assists the sovereign is called the minister; that which corresponds to the minister is called the envoy, this is not the same as the three grades of superior, middle, and inferior in classifying materia medica.' The Emperor said: 'What are the three grades?' Qibo said: 'They serve to clarify the distinct categories of the beneficial and the harmful.' The Emperor said: 'Good. What of disease internal and external?' Qibo said: 'In the method of regulating the qi, one must distinguish yin and yang, determine what is internal and external, and let each keep to its own domain. What is internal, treat internally; what is external, treat externally. What is mild, regulate it; what is more than mild, level it; what is exuberant, seize it. For excess that would be treated by sweating, instead purge it when appropriate. Cold, heat, warmth, and coolness are each diminished according to their category. Follow what is beneficial, adhere carefully to the Way as prescribed, and every action will attain complete success; the qi and blood will be rightly balanced, and one will long preserve the mandate of heaven.' The Emperor said: 'Good.'

素问.title_en: Discourse on Elucidating the Utmost Teaching
paragraphs_en: Huangdi, seated in the Hall of Light, summoned Lei Gong and asked him: Do you know the Way of medicine? Lei Gong replied: I can recite it and understand it fairly well, but having understood it I cannot yet distinguish its fine points; having distinguished them I cannot yet make them clear; having made them clear I cannot yet make them brilliant. This is enough to treat the various officials, but not enough to reach lords and kings. I wish to receive instruction in the measures established by Heaven, uniting them with the yin and yang of the four seasons, distinguishing the stars and constellations from the light of sun and moon, so as to make the arts of the classics brilliant, so that later generations may become ever more enlightened, reaching up to connect with Shennong, and elucidating the utmost teaching to rival the two sovereigns. The Emperor said: Good. Do not lose hold of this. All of this concerns the mutual transmission and correspondence of yin and yang, exterior and interior, above and below, male and female. As for the Way, one must know astronomy above, geography below, and human affairs in between; only then can it endure long and be used to teach the multitude without doubt or danger. The treatises on the Way of medicine can then be transmitted to later generations and treasured as a precious legacy. Lei Gong said: I ask to receive the Way, that I may recite and understand it.

The Emperor said: Have you not heard of the Transmission of Yin and Yang? He replied: I do not know it. The Emperor said: The three yang take Heaven as their domain; they move up and down without constancy, and when they combine, disease arrives, injuring yin and yang unevenly. Lei Gong said: I cannot fathom the three yang; I ask to hear their explanation. The Emperor said: When the three yang arrive alone, this means the three yang arrive together; arriving together they are like wind and rain — above they cause disease of the vertex, below they cause the disease of leakage. Outwardly there is no fixed period, inwardly there is no regularity, they do not conform to the order of the channels, and in diagnosis there is no distinguishing above from below; one must distinguish them by the writings. Lei Gong said: In my treatments, recovery has been sparse; I have only grasped the general idea. The Emperor said: The three yang are the utmost yang; when they accumulate and combine, they cause fright. The disease arises like a swift wind, arriving like a thunderclap; all nine orifices become blocked, yang qi overflows in torrents, the throat dries and the gullet is obstructed. When they combine with yin, then above and below lose their constancy, and pressing together they cause dysenteric intestinal disorder. This is called the three yang striking straight at the heart: sitting, the patient cannot rise, while lying down the body remains whole — this is the disease of the three yang. Moreover, by this one may know how all under heaven distinguishes yin and yang, corresponds to the four seasons, and unites with the five phases. Lei Gong said: In speaking of yang I cannot distinguish it, in speaking of yin I cannot order it; I ask to rise and receive the explanation, to make it the utmost Way. The Emperor said: If you receive this transmission yet do not know how to unite it with the utmost Way, you will confuse your teacher's instruction. I tell you the essentials of the utmost Way: when disease injures the five viscera, the sinews and bones waste away; if your words are unclear and cannot distinguish this, then the learning of the rulers of the age is exhausted. The kidney is on the verge of being cut off, one grieves and sighs, at dusk one is languid and does not go out, and human affairs are no longer attended to with vigor.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Demonstrating Unhurried Deliberation
paragraphs_en: Huangdi, seated at leisure, summoned Lei Gong and asked him: You who have received the arts and recited the books — if you are able to survey the various branches of learning and extend them by analogy, comprehending and uniting their principles, then tell me what you excel in: the five viscera and six bowels — gallbladder, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, spleen, uterus, and bladder — the brain and marrow, tears and saliva, weeping and sorrowful grief, and the paths by which fluids travel. All of these belong to what gives rise to human life, along with the errors made in treating them. You must strive to understand them clearly; then you may achieve complete success in ten cases out of ten. If you are unable to know them, you will be resented by the world. Lei Gong said: I have recited the upper and lower chapters of the Canon of the Pulse extensively, distinguishing differences and comparing categories, yet I have still not been able to achieve complete success in ten cases out of ten — how then could I be sufficient to make these things clear? The Emperor said: Then try instead to master the faults of the five viscera, the disharmonies of the six bowels, the failures of needle and stone, the proper uses of potent drugs, and the flavors of decoctions — describe their manifestations fully, and answer me completely; I will ask you about what you do not know. Lei Gong said: Deficiency of the liver, deficiency of the kidney, and deficiency of the spleen all cause the body to feel heavy and the mind vexed and oppressed. When one administers potent drugs, needling, moxibustion, stone-lancing, or decoctions, sometimes the disease is cured and sometimes it is not. I wish to hear the explanation for this. The Emperor said: Sir, why is it that though your years are advanced, your question is so trifling? I have truly asked in a way that exposes your own error. I asked you about the deep and obscure, and you answer me by citing the upper and lower chapters — why is that? Now, when the spleen is deficient its pulse floats and resembles that of the lung; when the kidney is small and floating it resembles that of the spleen; when the liver is tense, sunken, and scattered it resembles that of the kidney — these are precisely the cases that regularly confuse the physician, yet through unhurried, careful deliberation one can grasp them correctly. As for the fact that the three viscera of earth, wood, and water dwell intermingled — this is something even a child knows to ask about; why do you ask it?

Lei Gong said: Suppose there is a person with headache, spasm of the sinews, heaviness of the bones, timidity and shortness of breath, hiccupping and belching, fullness of the abdomen, periodic fright, and disinclination to lie down — from which viscus does this arise? The pulse is floating and wiry, yet on pressing it feels as hard as stone. I do not understand the explanation, and so I ask again about the three viscera, in order to understand their analogous categories. The Emperor said: This is precisely what is meant by unhurried deliberation. In the elderly, one seeks the cause in the bowels; in the young, one seeks it in the channels; in those in their prime, one seeks it in the viscera. Now everything you have said misses the mark. The eight winds accumulate and turn to heat, the five viscera waste and are scorched, and the pathogenic evil is transmitted and received from one to another. Now, a pulse that is floating and wiry indicates insufficiency of the kidney. One that is sunken and hard as stone indicates that kidney qi has become lodged within. Timidity and shortness of breath indicate that the water pathways are not moving and that the body's qi is being dissipated. Coughing and vexed oppression indicate the counterflow of kidney qi. In one person's qi, the disease lies in a single viscus; to say that the three viscera are all acting together is not in accordance with proper method. Lei Gong said: Suppose there is a person whose four limbs are limp and languid, who pants and coughs and has bloody discharge. In my ignorance I diagnosed this as injury to the lung. On pressing the pulse it was floating, large, and tight. In my ignorance I dared not treat it. A crude physician applied stone-lancing, and the disease was cured with much bleeding; once the bleeding stopped, the body felt light. What is the nature of this? The Emperor said: What you are able to treat, and what you know, is indeed extensive, yet with this disease you have erred — even a wild goose in flight also soars up to the sky. Now, the sage in treating disease follows method and holds to measure, draws on things and compares categories, and transforms his understanding in the realm of the profound and obscure, tracing from above down to below — why must one cling rigidly to the fixed channels? Now, a pulse that is floating, large, and empty indicates that spleen qi has become cut off externally, departing from the stomach and reverting outward to the yangming. Two fires cannot overcome three waters, and thus the pulse is chaotic and without constancy. Limpness of the four limbs is due to the failure of spleen essence to circulate. Panting and coughing occur when water qi combines with the yangming. Bloody discharge occurs when the vessels are tense and the blood has nowhere to flow. As for taking this to be injury of the lung, that is an error verging on recklessness. Failing to draw on analogous categories shows that one's understanding is not clear. Now, in true injury of the lung, the spleen qi fails to hold its post, the stomach qi is not clear, the channel qi cannot be employed as it should, the true viscus is broken and breached, the channels and vessels are severed at their sides, and the five viscera leak and drain — if there is no nosebleed, there is vomiting. These two conditions do not belong to the same category. It is like Heaven having no form and Earth having no pattern — white and black are as far apart as that. This lapse is my own fault: since you already possessed this knowledge, I did not spell it out further for you, so that by clearly drawing analogies through unhurried deliberation you might arrive at it yourself. This is called diagnosing with ease, and this is what is meant by the utmost Way.

素问.title_en: Discourse Elucidating the Five Errors
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: Ah, how far-reaching this is! How vast and obscure, as if gazing into a deep abyss, as if facing drifting clouds. Gazing into a deep abyss one can still fathom its depth, but facing drifting clouds one cannot know their limit. The art of the sage serves as a model for the myriad people; in judging and shaping his intent he must have laws and standards, following the classics and holding to their methods, conducting the affairs of medicine in accordance with them, so as to serve as a support for the myriad people. Now, in practice there are five errors and four virtues — do you know them? Lei Gong rose from his mat, bowed twice, and said: I am young in years, ignorant and confused; I have not heard of the five errors and the four virtues. In comparing categories and forms and names I have only vainly cited the classics without substance, and my mind has nothing with which to answer.

The Emperor said: Whenever one is about to diagnose a patient, one must always ask whether he was once noble and afterward became lowly. Even if he was not struck by an external pathogen, disease arises internally in such a case, and this is called 'desertion of the camp qi.' If he was once wealthy and afterward became poor, this is called 'loss of essence'; the five qi linger and stagnate, and disease develops from their accumulation. When the physician examines such a patient, the disease is not located in the viscera or bowels, nor does it alter the bodily form; on diagnosis he is puzzled and cannot name the disease. The body wastes day by day, the qi becomes deficient and essence is lacking; when the disease is deep there is no qi, and the patient is repeatedly startled as if chilled. When the disease is deep, it is because the defensive qi is exhausted externally and the nutritive qi is seized internally. This is what even a good physician may miss, failing to know the true nature of the disease — this is the first error in treatment. Whenever one wishes to diagnose a disease, one must always ask about diet and dwelling, about sudden joy and sudden suffering, about beginning in joy and ending in suffering — all of these injure the essential qi; when essential qi is exhausted and cut off, the bodily form is destroyed and ruined. Sudden rage injures yin, sudden joy injures yang; counterflow qi rises upward, filling the vessels and departing from the body. An ignorant physician treating such a case, not knowing whether to supplement or drain, and not knowing the true nature of the disease, allows the essential flower of qi to be lost day by day, so that pathogenic qi then gathers in — this is the second error in treatment. One who is skilled in pulse-taking must know it by comparing categories, the unusual and the constant, through unhurried deliberation. If one acts as a physician without knowing this Way, his diagnoses are not worth honoring — this is the third error in treatment. In diagnosis there are three constants: one must always ask about noble or lowly status, whether an enfeoffed lord has suffered ruin, and whether one aspires to be a marquis or a king. Thus, when the noble loses his position, even without being struck by an external pathogen, his essence is disturbed and his spirit is injured within, so that his body must decline and perish. When one begins wealthy and later becomes poor, even without injury from external pathogens, the skin becomes scorched and dry and the sinews contract, leading to wasting, lameness, and spasm. If the physician cannot be strict and cannot move the patient's spirit, but instead behaves outwardly with weak compliance, disorder arises and constancy is lost; the disease cannot be shifted, and so the physician's work does not succeed — this is the fourth error in treatment. Whenever diagnosing wind-conditions, one must know their beginning and end, and understand as well their subsidiary threads; feeling the pulse and asking the name of the disease, one must also consider whether the patient is male or female. Separation and severance, stagnation and knotting, worry and fear, joy and rage — these cause the five viscera to become empty and hollow, and blood and qi to lose their proper station. If the physician cannot know this, of what use is any technique he speaks of? Someone who was once wealthy and suffered great loss, with sinews severed and vessels cut off, whose body once again moves about while its depletion does not cease — thus injury and ruin accumulate and linger, pressing inward to return to yang, forming pus accumulations with cold and heat. A crude physician treating this hastily needles yin and yang; the body becomes dissolved and scattered, the four limbs cramp and twist, and the day of death has its appointed time. If the physician cannot make this clear, and without asking what brought on the disease merely pronounces the day of death, he too is but a crude physician — this is the fifth error in treatment.

In all these five, the physician has received the art without mastering it, and is not clear about human affairs. Hence it is said: when the sage treats disease, he must know Heaven and Earth, yin and yang, the order of the four seasons, the five viscera and six bowels, male and female, exterior and interior, and what governs needling, moxibustion, stone-lancing, and potent drugs. He deliberates unhurriedly on human affairs so as to clarify the Way of the channels. Noble and lowly, poor and rich, each have their own distinct nature and principle. He asks about age, whether young or old, and the principle of courage or timidity; he examines carefully the division of the body's regions, and knows the root and origin of the disease. With the eight rectitudes and nine indicators, his diagnosis will surely be confirmed. In the Way of treating disease, the qi within is treasured above all; one follows and seeks out its principle. If one seeks it and does not find it, the error lies in exterior and interior. Holding to the methods and treating accordingly, without losing the principle of the transport points, one who can practice this art will go through life without peril. If one does not know the principle of the transport points, the five viscera accumulate and turn to heat, and abscesses erupt in the six bowels. If diagnosis is not thorough, this is called losing the constant order. Carefully hold to this method of treatment, and let it be illuminated together with the classics — the Upper Canon and the Lower Canon, the weighing and measuring of yin and yang, the unusual and the constant of the five centers, judged by the Hall of Light, examined in its beginning and end — and one may then travel freely everywhere without hindrance.

素问.title_en: Discourse Verifying the Four Faults
paragraphs_en: Huangdi was in the Hall of Light, and Lei Gong sat in attendance. Huangdi said: Master, the books you have mastered and the affairs you have received are many indeed. Try to speak of the meaning of success and failure — how success is achieved, and how failure comes about. Lei Gong replied: Following the classics in receiving my training, all say one should achieve complete success in ten cases out of ten; yet there are times when errors and failures occur — I ask to hear the explanation for this. The Emperor said: Is it that you are young in years and your understanding has not yet reached far enough, or do you speak in a mixed and confused manner? Now, the twelve channels and the three hundred sixty-five connecting vessels are things that people clearly know and that physicians follow and employ. The reason complete success is not achieved is that the spirit is not concentrated, the intent is not well ordered, and outer and inner lose their coordination with each other — hence there are times of doubt and danger. In diagnosis, not knowing the principle of the counterflow and favorable flow of yin and yang — this is the first fault in treatment. Receiving instruction from a teacher without completing it, recklessly practicing a mixture of techniques, mistakenly calling error the true Way, changing names to claim credit for oneself, recklessly using stone-lancing — this leaves lasting fault upon oneself; this is the second fault in treatment. Not attending to whether the patient dwells in poverty or wealth, nobility or lowliness, the thinness or thickness of his seat and dwelling, the coldness or warmth of his physical condition, not attending to what is proper in diet, not distinguishing whether a person is bold or timid, and not knowing how to compare categories — this is enough to throw oneself into confusion, and not enough to make oneself clear; this is the third fault in treatment. In diagnosing disease, not asking about its origin — worry and distress, immoderate eating and drinking, excess in daily activity, or perhaps injury from poison — without first inquiring into these, but abruptly taking hold of the wrist pulse, how could one hit upon the disease? Speaking recklessly and inventing names for the disease, one ends up exhausted by one's own crudeness; this is the fourth fault in treatment. Thus, when people of the age speak of these things, their words race a thousand li beyond the mark; not understanding the discussion of the cun and chi pulse positions, they diagnose without regard to human affairs. As for the Way of the methods of treatment and the treasure of unhurried deliberation, they merely sit and hold the wrist pulse, and their diagnosis fails to match the five pulses. When the hundred diseases arise from this, they at first blame themselves, and then pass the fault on to their teacher. Therefore, when treatment cannot follow proper principle, the art is discarded and held cheap in the marketplace; when reckless treatment occasionally succeeds, the ignorant heart is self-satisfied. Ah, how deep and obscure — who truly knows this Way? The greatness of the Way may be likened to Heaven and Earth, and matched to the Four Seas. You do not understand the Way's instruction, and you turn what is meant to bring clarity into darkness.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Categories of Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: When early spring had just arrived, Huangdi, seated at leisure, looked out over the eight extremities, rectifying the qi of the eight winds, and asked Lei Gong: Among the categories of yin and yang and the Way of the channels and vessels, and what the five centers govern, which viscus is most honored? Lei Gong replied: In spring, the stems jia and yi correspond to green, and within the body govern the liver, which presides for seventy-two days; this is the viscus that governs the pulse in its season, and so I consider this viscus to be the most honored. The Emperor said: Yet consider the Upper and Lower Canons on yin and yang and unhurried deliberation — what you call honored is in fact the lowest of all. Lei Gong purified himself by fasting for seven days, and at dawn again sat in attendance. The Emperor said: The greater yang forms the warp; the yang brightness forms the linking vessel; the lesser yang forms the roaming region — by this one knows the beginning and end of the five viscera. The three yang form the exterior, the two yin form the interior, and the one yin, reaching its extremity, is cut off and becomes like the darkness of the new moon; one must gather all of this together to rectify its principle. Lei Gong said: Having received this instruction, I am still not able to understand it clearly. The Emperor said: What is called the three yang — the greater yang forms the warp. When the pulse of the three yang reaches the hand taiyin, it is wiry and floating, not sunken; one determines this by measure and examines it with the mind, uniting it with the discourse on yin and yang. What is called the two yang is the yangming; when its pulse reaches the hand taiyin, it is wiry, sunken, and hasty, without a proper beat; if heat arrives, in such disease the patient will die. The one yang is the shaoyang; when its pulse reaches the hand taiyin, connecting upward with the renying pulse, it is wiry, tense, suspended, and unbroken — this is the disease of the shaoyang; if it becomes purely yin, the patient will die. The three yin govern the six channels; their pulse crosses at the taiyin, hidden and beating without floating, and above it leaves the heart and its resolve empty. The two yin reach the lung, their qi returning to the bladder, and externally connecting with the spleen and stomach. When the one yin arrives alone, the channel is severed, the qi floats without a proper beat, and the pulse is hooked and slippery. These six pulses are now yin, now yang; they cross, connect, and combine with one another, intricately linking through the five viscera, uniting with yin and yang. Whichever arrives first is the host; whichever arrives later is the guest.

Lei Gong said: I have exhausted my understanding; having received the transmission of the channels and vessels, and having recited and grasped the Way of unhurried deliberation, in applying it to unhurried deliberation, I still do not know yin and yang, nor do I know male and female. The Emperor said: The three yang is the father, the two yang is the guard, the one yang is the regulator. The three yin is the mother, the two yin is the female, the one yin is the sole envoy. When the two yang and one yin are involved together, the yangming governs the disease; if it cannot overcome the one yin, the pulse becomes soft and stirring, and all nine orifices sink. When the three yang and one yin are involved, if the taiyang pulse dominates and the one yin cannot restrain it, there is disorder within the five viscera and fright and alarm without. When the two yin and two yang are involved, the disease is in the lung; the shaoyin pulse is sunken, overcoming the lung and injuring the spleen, and injuring the four limbs externally. When the two yin and two yang both arrive together in crossing, the disease is in the kidney, causing cursing and reckless behavior, disease of the vertex, and mania. When the two yin and one yang are involved, the disease arises from the kidney; yin qi lodges and roams into the heart, the region below the epigastrium becomes hollow at its orifices, its embankments closed and blocked without passage, and the four limbs become estranged from proper function. When the one yin and one yang alternate and are cut off, this means yin qi reaches the heart; above and below lose their constancy, and its coming and going cannot be known; the throat becomes dry, and the disease lies in the earth, the spleen. When the two yang and three yin are involved, and the utmost yin is entirely present, yin does not exceed yang, yet yang qi cannot restrain yin; yin and yang are both cut off. If the pulse floats, this produces blood accumulations; if it sinks, this produces festering pus. When yin and yang are both vigorous, reaching down to yin and yang below, uniting above with what is bright and clear, and uniting below with what is dark and obscure, the diagnosis determines the appointed time of death or life, and one must correlate this with the beginning of the year.

Lei Gong said: I ask about the imminent time of death. Huangdi did not answer. Lei Gong asked again. Huangdi said: It is contained within the discourse of the classics. Lei Gong said: I ask to hear of the imminent time. Huangdi said: For diseases of the three months of winter, if the disease unites with yang, by the first month of spring the pulse will show signs of death, all converging to appear as spring emerges. For diseases of the three months of winter, when their course of principle is already exhausted, and the grasses and willow leaves are all killed by frost, while in spring yin and yang are both cut off, the appointed time is in early spring. For diseases of the three months of spring, this is called the killing of yang; yin and yang are both cut off, and the appointed time is when the grass withers and dries. For diseases of the three months of summer, when it reaches the utmost yin, it does not exceed ten days; when yin and yang cross, the appointed time is when the waters turn cold. For diseases of the three months of autumn, when the three yang all rise together, the disease will resolve of itself without treatment. But if yin and yang cross and combine, the patient, once standing, cannot sit, and once sitting, cannot rise. When the three yang arrive alone, the appointed time is when the water freezes hard as stone. When the two yin arrive alone, the appointed time is when the waters are at their fullest.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Waxing and Waning of the Four Directions
paragraphs_en: Lei Gong asked to inquire about the abundance and paucity of qi — which is counterflow and which is favorable flow. Huangdi answered: Yang follows the left, yin follows the right; the old follow above, the young follow below. Thus, in spring and summer, returning to yang means life, and returning to autumn and winter means death; conversely, returning to autumn and winter means life. Thus, whether qi is abundant or scant, if it is counterflow, it becomes reversal. He asked: Is it that qi in excess becomes reversal? The answer: When qi rises and does not descend, cold reversal reaches the knees; in the young this brings death in autumn and winter, while in the old it brings life in autumn and winter. When qi rises and does not descend, there is headache and disease of the vertex; seeking yang, one cannot find it, seeking yin, one cannot discern it clearly; the five regions are cut off without any sign, as if dwelling in a vast wilderness, as if lying hidden in an empty chamber — faint and continuous, the patient's life does not last out the day. Thus, reversal from scant qi causes people to have disordered dreams, reaching an extreme of confusion. When the three yang are cut off and the three yin are faint, this is called scant qi. Thus, when lung qi is deficient, it causes people to dream of white objects, or of seeing people cut down with blood scattered about; if it occurs in the corresponding season, one dreams of soldiers in battle. When kidney qi is deficient, it causes people to dream of boats and ships and people drowning; if it occurs in the corresponding season, one dreams of lying submerged in water as if in fear and dread. When liver qi is deficient, one dreams of mushrooms, fragrant plants, and growing grass; if it occurs in the corresponding season, one dreams of lying beneath a tree, not daring to rise. When heart qi is deficient, one dreams of putting out fires or of bright, fiery things; if it occurs in the corresponding season, one dreams of burning and scorching. When spleen qi is deficient, one dreams of insufficient food and drink; if it occurs in the corresponding season, one dreams of building walls and roofing houses. All of these are cases where the qi of the five viscera is deficient, yang qi is in excess, and yin qi is insufficient. One must unite this with the five methods of diagnosis, harmonize it with yin and yang, and locate it within the channels and vessels.

In diagnosis there are ten measures: measuring a person's pulse measure, visceral measure, flesh measure, sinew measure, and transport-point measure. When the qi of yin and yang is exhausted, the person's disease manifests of itself. When the pulse moves without constancy, scattering yin and favoring yang unduly, the pulse loses its proper form and is incomplete; diagnosis then has no constant procedure. In diagnosis one must always consider above and below, and gauge whether the person is a commoner, a ruler, or a minister. If one receives instruction from a teacher without completing it, this causes one's art to remain unclear; not examining counterflow and favorable flow, one acts recklessly — holding to the female aspect while losing the male, discarding yin and clinging to yang, not understanding how to combine and unite them. Diagnosis is therefore unclear, yet when it is transmitted to later generations, its contrary conclusions become self-evident. When the utmost yin is deficient, the qi of Heaven is cut off; when the utmost yang is excessive, the qi of Earth is insufficient. The crossing and combining of yin and yang is what the Perfected Person practices. In the crossing and combining of yin and yang, yang qi arrives first and yin qi arrives afterward. Thus the sage, in holding to the Way of diagnosis, attends to the sequence of yin and yang, first and last, in his examination. The configurations of the unusual and the constant number sixty in all. In diagnosis one must unite with subtle matters, trace the transformations of yin and yang, illuminate the true condition of the five centers; within this discourse, one grasps the essentials of deficiency and excess, and fixes the matters of the five measures. Only when one knows all this is one sufficient to diagnose. Thus, if in palpating one grasps yin but not yang, the diagnosis dissolves into nothing; if one grasps yang but not yin, one's learning remains shallow and unrefined. To know the left but not the right, to know the right but not the left, to know above but not below, to know what comes first but not what follows after — for these reasons, treatment cannot endure. To know the bad and know the good, to know disease and know absence of disease, to know high and know low, to know sitting and know rising, to know movement and know stillness — applying this with proper order, the Way of diagnosis is then complete, and it will not fail for ten thousand generations. Rise to what is in excess, and know what is insufficient. Measure the affairs of above and below; the affairs of the pulse follow their proper framework. Thus, when the bodily form is weak and the qi is deficient, this means death. When the form and qi are in excess but the pulse qi is insufficient, this means death. When the pulse qi is in excess but the form and qi are insufficient, this means life.

Thus, in diagnosis there is a great method: sitting and rising have their constant order, going out and coming in have their proper conduct, so as to turn one's spirit toward clarity. One must be pure and clear, observing above and observing below, governing the eight rectitudes and the pathogenic evils, distinguishing the five central regions, examining the movement and stillness of the pulse, following the smoothness or roughness at the chi position, and the sense of cold or warmth; one observes whether it is large or small, unites this with the capacity of the disease, and thereby grasps counterflow and favorable flow, and further knows the name of the disease. Then one's diagnosis can achieve complete success in ten cases out of ten, without losing touch with human circumstances. Therefore, in diagnosing, one observes both the patient's breath and his intent, and thus does not lose the proper order; the Way becomes exceedingly clear and discernible, and thus can endure long. One who does not know this Way loses the classics and severs their principles, speaking recklessly and making baseless predictions of the time of death — this is called losing the Way.

素问.title_en: Discourse Explaining the Subtle and Profound
paragraphs_en: Huangdi was in the Hall of Light. Lei Gong requested, saying: I have received this learning and transmitted it, carrying out instruction by means of the classical discourses — unhurried deliberation, the forms and models, yin and yang, needling and moxibustion, and what is nourished by decoctions and medicines. Yet in carrying out treatment there is skill and lack of skill, and one cannot always be certain of complete success in ten cases out of ten. If one first speaks of sorrow, grief, joy, and anger, of dryness, dampness, cold, and summer-heat, of yin and yang, and of women, I ask about the reasons why these are so — the lowly and the noble, the poor and the wealthy, and what the human body follows accordingly; how those below are made to serve, and how, when facing affairs, one may make them fit the Way and its art — I have respectfully received these instructions. I ask further to raise a foolish and lowly question, one not found in the classics, and wish to hear its explanation. The Emperor said: This is a great matter indeed. Lei Gong then asked: Why is it that when a person weeps and cries, sometimes no tears come out, or if tears come out there is little nasal mucus — what is the reason for this? The Emperor said: This is found in the classics. He asked further: I do not know where this water arises from, nor where the mucus comes from. The Emperor said: As for this question, it is of no benefit to treatment; yet it is something the physician should know, for it is born of the Way.

Now, the heart is that which concentrates the essence of the five viscera; the eyes are its orifice, and the radiant complexion is its outward flourishing. Thus, when a person is in a state of well-being, the qi is harmonious in the eyes; when there is loss, grief is made known in the complexion. Thus, when there is sorrow and grief, tears fall; the falling of tears is where this water arises. The ancestral source of this water is the accumulated water within the body; the accumulated water is the utmost yin; the utmost yin is the essence of the kidney. The reason this ancestral essential water does not normally flow out is that the essence holds it in check; it supports and encloses it, and thus the water does not flow. Now, the essence of water is the will, and the essence of fire is the spirit; when water and fire resonate with each other, spirit and will are both moved to sorrow, and thus the water of the eyes is produced. Hence the common saying goes: 'sorrow of the heart is called sorrow of the will,' for the will and the essence of the heart converge together at the eyes. Thus, when both are sorrowful, the spirit qi is transmitted to the heart, but the essence does not rise to be transmitted to the will, so that the will alone is sorrowful, and hence tears come forth.

Tears and mucus arise from the brain. The brain belongs to yin; the marrow is what fills the bones, and thus the brain seeps forth as mucus. The will governs the bones; thus, when the water flows, the mucus follows it, for they move together as members of the same category. Now, mucus and tears are like brothers to one another: in urgency they perish together, in life they live together. When the will is already sorrowful, mucus and tears both come forth and flow freely together. That mucus and tears both come forth and follow one another is because they belong to the same category.

Lei Gong said: This is profound indeed! I ask further: why is it that when a person weeps and cries, sometimes tears do not come out, or if they come out there is little mucus that does not follow along with them? The Emperor said: When tears do not come out, it is because the crying is not truly sorrowful. When one does not weep, it is because the spirit is not compassionate. If the spirit is not compassionate, then the will is not sorrowful; yin and yang hold each other in check, so how could tears come forth alone? Now, when the will is sorrowful, there is grieved distress; when there is grieved distress, it surges into yin; when it surges into yin, the will departs from the eyes; when the will departs, the spirit no longer guards the essence; when essence and spirit depart from the eyes, mucus and tears come forth. Moreover, have you alone not recited and pondered the words of the classics? In reversal, the eyes see nothing. Now, when a person experiences reversal, yang qi combines above and yin qi combines below. When yang combines above, it is like the dazzling light of fire; when yin combines below, the feet become cold, and when the feet are cold, there is distension. Now, a single water cannot overcome five fires, and so the corners of the eyes become obscured. Thus, when facing the wind, tears fall without ceasing. When wind strikes the eyes, yang qi guards the essence within, and this is fire qi scorching the eyes; hence, on encountering wind, tears fall. There is an analogy for this: when fire is fierce, wind is generated, and this can then produce rain — this belongs to the same category of phenomena.

灵枢经.title_en: Nine Needles and the Twelve Source Points
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I care for the myriad people as my children, nurturing the common folk and collecting their taxes and levies. Yet I grieve that they lack sufficiency and so fall prey to illness. I wish to spare them from being subjected to poisonous medicines and from the use of stone needles, and instead to use the fine needle to open through their channels, to regulate their qi and blood, and to govern the confluences where these flow contrary to or in accord with their proper course, entering and emerging. I wish this to be transmitted to later generations, and it must therefore be set forth with a clear method, so that it may come to an end without being extinguished, endure long without being severed, be easy to use yet not easily forgotten. Let it be given order and system, let its sections be distinguished, its exterior and interior differentiated, and let it be given a beginning and an end. Let each part have its proper form, and let a Canon of Needling first be established. I wish to hear its particulars.'

Qibo replied: 'Allow your servant to set these forth in proper sequence, so that they may have order and system, beginning with one and ending with nine. Let me speak of their principles. The essentials of the fine needle are easy to state but difficult to master. The unskilled practitioner holds fast to the outward form; the superior practitioner holds fast to the spirit. How subtle is the spirit — the invading evil lodges at the gate! Without first perceiving the illness, how can one know its origin? The subtlety of needling lies in its timing — whether swift or slow. The unskilled practitioner holds fast to the joints and bony landmarks; the superior practitioner holds fast to the pivotal moment. The movement of this pivot never departs from the aperture. Within the aperture the pivot is pure, still, and subtle — one cannot meet it as it comes, nor pursue it as it goes. One who understands the way of the pivot will not be off by so much as a hair's breadth; one who does not understand the way of the pivot, though he presses upon it, will not release it. To know its coming and going, one must engage it at the precise moment. How obscure this is to the unskilled — how marvelous! — and it is the skilled practitioner alone who possesses it. What departs is called contrary; what arrives is called compliant. Once one clearly knows the contrary and the compliant, one may proceed directly without hesitation. If one meets the qi head-on and seizes it, how can this fail to create deficiency? If one follows behind and reinforces it, how can this fail to create excess? Whether meeting it or following it, one harmonizes it through the intention of the mind — and the way of the needle is then complete.'

In general, in the use of the needle: where there is deficiency, one fills it; where there is fullness, one drains it; where there is stagnant old blood, one removes it; where evil qi prevails, one depletes it. The great essential is this: to insert slowly and withdraw swiftly produces repletion; to insert swiftly and withdraw slowly produces depletion. In speaking of repletion and depletion, the sensation is as though something were present, as though nothing were there. One examines what comes after and what comes before, as though something remained, as though it had vanished. In bringing about depletion and repletion, it is as though one had grasped something, as though one had lost it.

Of the essentials of depletion and repletion, the nine needles are the most marvelous, and the timing of supplementation and drainage is accomplished through the needle. In drainage, one must grasp the needle firmly and insert it, then release and withdraw it; as the yang is dispersed the needle is felt to loosen, and the evil qi is thereby drained. If instead one presses down and draws the needle out, this is called 'warming within' — the blood cannot disperse, and the qi cannot be released. In supplementation, one follows the qi again and again, applying the mind as though heedlessly, now moving, now pressing, alighting as lightly as a mosquito or gadfly, lingering as though staying and returning, withdrawing the needle as abruptly as a snapped bowstring, causing the left hand to press where the right hand withdraws, so that the qi is thereby retained. Once the outer gate is closed, the qi within becomes replete, and there must be no lingering blood — if there is, one must quickly remove it.

The way of holding the needle: firmness is its treasure. Point the finger straight and puncture directly, without slanting the needle to the left or right. Let the spirit be fixed on the finest hair's tip, and fasten the mind upon the patient. Only after carefully examining the blood vessels may one needle without danger. At the moment of needling, one's attention must rest upon the 'suspended yang' and its two guardian points. Keep the spirit fastened there without wavering, so as to know whether the disease is present or gone. Where the blood vessels lie crosswise over the transport points, on inspection they alone appear clear and distinct, and on palpation they alone feel firm.

The names of the nine needles, each differing in form: the first is called the chan needle, one cun six fen in length (a cun being a proportional unit of body measurement, and a fen one-tenth of a cun); the second is called the yuan needle, one cun six fen in length; the third is called the di needle, three cun and a half in length; the fourth is called the feng needle, one cun six fen in length; the fifth is called the pi needle, four cun in length and two fen and a half in width; the sixth is called the yuanli needle, one cun six fen in length; the seventh is called the hao needle, three cun six fen in length; the eighth is called the chang needle, seven cun in length; the ninth is called the da needle, four cun in length. The chan needle has a large head and a sharp point, and is used to drain the yang qi. The yuan needle is shaped like an egg, and is rubbed between the intervals of the flesh without injuring the muscles, so as to drain the qi of the interstices. The di needle has a tip as sharp as a millet grain, and its chief use is to press upon the vessel without letting it sink in, so as to bring the qi to arrive. The feng needle has a three-sided blade, and is used to release stubborn and chronic disease. The pi needle has a tip like the edge of a sword, and is used to remove large accumulations of pus. The yuanli needle is as thick as a horsehair, both round and sharp, slightly thicker toward the middle of its shaft, and is used to draw out sudden and violent qi. The hao needle has a tip as fine as the proboscis of a mosquito or gadfly; it is inserted quietly and slowly, and retained gently for a long while so as to nourish the qi, and is used to treat painful impediment. The chang needle is sharp-edged and thin in body, and can be used to treat impediment lodged deep within. The da needle has a tip like a stick, its point slightly rounded, and is used to drain water accumulated at the joints. Thus the nine needles are complete.

Now, as for the qi within the vessels: evil qi lodges above, turbid qi lodges in the middle, and clear qi lodges below. Thus if one needles the sunken vessel, the evil qi is expelled; if one needles the middle of the vessel, the turbid qi is expelled; but if one needles too deeply, the evil qi is instead driven further down, and the disease worsens. Hence it is said: the skin, flesh, sinews, and vessels each have their proper location, and each disease has its proper treatment. Since the nine needles each differ in form, each should be employed according to what is proper to it — one should neither create excess nor deficiency. To further diminish what is already insufficient, or to further augment what is already in excess, is called aggravating the disease. When disease is thereby aggravated, drawing qi from the vessels of all five viscera causes death, and drawing qi from the vessels of three of the viscera causes debility. To rob a patient of yin causes death; to rob a patient of yang causes derangement — such are the harms of reckless needling, now fully stated.

When one needles and the qi does not arrive, do not count the number of insertions. When one needles and the qi arrives, then withdraw the needle, and do not needle again. Each needle has that to which it is suited, each differing in form, each employed according to its proper place — this is the essential of needling. When the qi arrives there is efficacy, and the sign of this efficacy is as when the wind blows the clouds away, leaving the blue sky clearly visible — and the way of needling is thus complete.

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the places from which the qi of the five viscera and six bowels emerges.' Qibo said: 'The five viscera each have five transport points, five times five making twenty-five transport points in all; the six bowels each have six transport points, six times six making thirty-six transport points in all. There are twelve channel vessels and fifteen network vessels, making in all twenty-seven qi that travel up and down the body. The point where the qi emerges is called the well point; where it flows is called the spring point; where it pours in is called the stream point; where it travels is called the river point; and where it enters is called the sea point. The travels of these twenty-seven qi all take place among these five transport points.'

The junctures of the joints number three hundred and sixty-five confluences in all. One who knows the essential of this may sum it up in a single phrase; one who does not know the essential will be scattered and adrift without end. What is meant by 'joints' here are the places where the spirit qi travels, emerges, and enters — they are not merely the skin, flesh, sinews, and bones.

Observe the patient's complexion, examine the eyes, and thereby know whether the spirit is scattered or restored. Steady the patient's posture, attend to his movement and stillness, and thereby know whether the qi is evil or upright. The right hand governs the advancing of the needle, the left hand holds and guides it; once the qi arrives, withdraw the needle.

Whenever one is about to use the needle, one must first diagnose the pulse, observe whether the qi's condition is severe or mild, and only then may one proceed to treat. If the qi of the five viscera has already been cut off within, and the practitioner instead reinforces the exterior, this is called 'redoubled exhaustion.' Redoubled exhaustion must result in death, and such a death is quiet and still. Yet the practitioner who does this instead reverses the qi still further, by needling the armpit and chest. If the qi of the five viscera has already been cut off in the exterior, and the practitioner instead reinforces the interior, this is called 'contrary reversal.' Contrary reversal must also result in death, and such a death is agitated and restless. Yet the practitioner who does this instead needles the four extremities.

In needling, if harm reaches the interior and the needle is not withdrawn, the essence is thereby leaked away; if harm reaches the interior and the needle is promptly withdrawn, the qi is thereby brought to arrive. If the essence is leaked away, the disease grows more severe and the patient becomes debilitated; if the qi is brought to arrive in this manner, welling-abscesses and sores may result.

The five viscera are matched with the six bowels, and the six bowels have twelve source points. These twelve source points emerge at the four joints of the limbs, and the four joints govern the treatment of the five viscera. When the five viscera fall ill, one should select points among the twelve source points, for these twelve source points are the means by which the five viscera receive the qi and flavor of the three hundred and sixty-five joints of the body. When the five viscera fall ill, this will manifest at the twelve source points, and each of the twelve source points has its own place of emergence. By clearly knowing these source points and observing their responses, one may know the harm done to the five viscera. Within yang, the lesser yin is the lung, whose source point emerges at Taiyuan — there being two Taiyuan points. Within yang, the greater yang is the heart, whose source point emerges at Daling — there being two Daling points. Within yin, the lesser yang is the liver, whose source point emerges at Taichong — there being two Taichong points. Within yin, the utmost yin is the spleen, whose source point emerges at Taibai — there being two Taibai points. Within yin, the greater yin is the kidney, whose source point emerges at Taixi — there being two Taixi points. The source point of the fat membranes emerges at Jiuwei, there being one Jiuwei point. The source point of the vital membranes emerges at Boyang, there being one Boyang point. All these twelve source points govern the treatment of illnesses of the five viscera and six bowels.
For distention, select points among the three yang; for diarrhea with undigested food, select points among the three yin.

Now, when the five viscera fall ill, it is like being pierced by a thorn, like being stained by filth, like being tied in a knot, like being blocked and obstructed. Though the thorn has been embedded long, it can still be drawn out; though the stain has lingered long, it can still be washed clean; though the knot has been tied long, it can still be untied; though the obstruction has persisted long, it can still be cleared. Some say that long-standing illness cannot be treated — this is not a correct teaching. Now, one who is skilled in the use of the needle, in removing illness, does so as one draws out a thorn, as one washes away a stain, as one unties a knot, as one clears an obstruction. Though the illness be long-standing, it can still be brought to an end. To say that it cannot be treated is simply to have not yet mastered the technique.

In needling for various heat conditions, one should withdraw the needle as swiftly as if testing scalding water with the hand; in needling for cold conditions, one should linger as if a person were reluctant to walk. When there is a yang-type illness within the yin, select the point Xiaxiling — that is, Zusanli; proceed directly and without hesitation, and stop once the qi descends; if it does not descend, begin again. When the illness is located high in the body but affects the interior, select the point Yinlingquan; when the illness is located high in the body but affects the exterior, select the point Yanglingquan.

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'In the way of needling generally, one must thoroughly understand the beginning and end of the twelve channels and their network vessels, the places where the network vessels branch off, the places where the five transport points reside, the bowels with which each channel unites, the seasonal points of emergence and entry, the places where the qi of the five viscera flows, the measure of their breadth and number, the manner of their shallowness and depth, and the heights and depths to which they extend. I wish to hear this explained.'

Qibo said: 'Allow me to set them forth in order. The lung channel emerges at Shaoshang — Shaoshang lies at the inner side of the tip of the thumb — and is the wood well point. It flows into Yuji — Yuji lies at the 'fish' of the hand, the fleshy eminence at the base of the thumb — and is the spring point. It pours into Taiyuan — Taiyuan lies in the depression one cun behind the fish — and is the stream point. It travels through Jingqu — Jingqu lies at the radial pulse of the wrist, where the pulse beats without ceasing — and is the river point. It enters at Chize — Chize lies at the pulsating vessel in the crook of the elbow — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Taiyin channel.'

The heart channel emerges at Zhongchong — Zhongchong lies at the tip of the middle finger — and is the wood well point. It flows into Laogong — Laogong lies in the palm, at the inner side of the base joint of the middle finger — and is the spring point. It pours into Daling — Daling lies just below, between the two bones behind the palm — and is the stream point. It travels through Jianshi — the path of Jianshi lies between the two sinews, three cun above the wrist; when there is a disorder, the pulse arrives there, and when there is none, it does not — and this is the river point. It enters at Quze — Quze lies in the depression on the inner border below the elbow, found by bending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Shaoyin channel.

The liver channel emerges at Dadun — Dadun lies at the tip of the big toe, at the cluster of three hairs — and is the wood well point. It flows into Xingjian — Xingjian lies between the big toe and the second toe — and is the spring point. It pours into Taichong — Taichong lies in the depression two cun above Xingjian — and is the stream point. It travels through Zhongfeng — Zhongfeng lies in the depression one and a half cun in front of the inner ankle bone; if manipulated against the current the qi becomes congested, if manipulated in accord with the current it flows freely, and the point is found by rotating the foot — and this is the river point. It enters at Ququan — Ququan lies below the rounded bone and above the great sinew, and is found by bending the knee — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Jueyin channel.

The spleen channel emerges at Yinbai — Yinbai lies at the inner side of the tip of the big toe — and is the wood well point. It flows into Dadu — Dadu lies in the depression below and behind the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Taibai — Taibai lies below the wrist-bone of the foot — and is the stream point. It travels through Shangqiu — Shangqiu lies in the depression below the inner ankle bone — and is the river point. It enters at Yinlingquan — Yinlingquan lies in the depression below the rounded bone, and is found by extending the leg — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Taiyin channel.

The kidney channel emerges at Yongquan — Yongquan lies at the center of the sole of the foot — and is the wood well point. It flows into Rangu — Rangu lies below the 'blazing bone,' the navicular tuberosity — and is the spring point. It pours into Taixi — Taixi lies in the depression behind the inner ankle bone and above the heel bone — and is the stream point. It travels through Fuliu — Fuliu lies two cun above the inner ankle bone, where the pulse beats without ceasing — and is the river point. It enters at Yingu — Yingu lies behind the rounded bone, below the great sinew and above the small sinew; pressing there the pulse responds to the hand, and it is found by bending the knee — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Shaoyin channel.

The bladder channel emerges at Zhiyin — Zhiyin lies at the tip of the small toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Tonggu — Tonggu lies at the outer side, in front of the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Shugu — Shugu lies in the depression behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Jinggu — Jinggu lies below the great bone on the outer side of the foot — and is the source point. It travels through Kunlun — Kunlun lies behind the outer ankle bone and above the heel bone — and is the river point. It enters at Weizhong — Weizhong lies at the center of the popliteal crease — and is the sea point, found with the knee bent. This is the Foot Taiyang channel.

The gallbladder channel emerges at Qiaoyin — Qiaoyin lies at the tip of the fourth toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Xiaxi — Xiaxi lies between the fourth toe and the small toe — and is the spring point. It pours into Linqi — Linqi lies one and a half cun further up, in a depression — and is the stream point. It passes through Qiuxu — Qiuxu lies in the depression in front of and below the outer ankle bone — and is the source point. It travels through Yangfu — Yangfu lies above the outer ankle bone, in front of the rounded bone, at the tip of the 'severed bone' — and is the river point. It enters at Yanglingquan — Yanglingquan lies in the depression at the outer side of the knee, found by extending the leg — and is the sea point. This is the Foot Shaoyang channel.

The stomach channel emerges at Lidui — Lidui lies at the tip of the second toe, on the side next to the big toe — and is the metal well point. It flows into Neiting — Neiting lies between the second and third toes — and is the spring point. It pours into Xiangu — Xiangu lies two cun further up, on the inner side of the middle toe, in a depression — and is the stream point. It passes through Chongyang — Chongyang lies five cun up on the instep, in a depression, and is found by rotating the foot — and is the source point. It travels through Jiexi — Jiexi lies one and a half cun above Chongyang, in a depression — and is the river point. It enters at Xialing, that is, Zusanli — which lies three cun below the knee, at the outer side of the shinbone — and is the sea point. Three cun further below Sanli lies Juxu Shanglian, the Upper Great Void, and three cun further below that lies Juxu Xialian, the Lower Great Void. The large intestine is associated with the upper of these two points, and the small intestine with the lower — this is the Foot Yangming stomach channel. Both the large intestine and the small intestine belong to the stomach, which is the Foot Yangming.

The triple burner, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Shaoyang channel, emerging at Guanchong — Guanchong lies at the tip of the fourth finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Yemen — Yemen lies between the fourth and small fingers — and is the spring point. It pours into Zhongzhu — Zhongzhu lies in the depression behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Yangchi — Yangchi lies in the depression above the wrist — and is the source point. It travels through Zhigou — Zhigou lies three cun above the wrist, in the depression between the two bones — and is the river point. It enters at Tianjing — Tianjing lies in the depression above the great bone on the outer side of the elbow, found by bending the elbow — and is the sea point. The lower transport point of the triple burner lies in front of the big toe of the foot and behind the Shaoyang channel, emerging at the outer side of the center of the popliteal fossa, at the point called Weiyang; this is the network point of the Taiyang, and belongs to the Hand Shaoyang channel. The triple burner is that which the Foot Shaoyang and Taiyin channels bring forth, a branch of the Taiyang; five cun above the ankle it branches off, penetrates through the calf, and emerges at Weiyang, joining the main pathway of the Taiyang, entering to network with the bladder, and binding the lower burner. When it is in excess there is closure and difficulty in urination; when it is deficient there is incontinence of urine. For incontinence, one should supplement; for closure and difficulty in urination, one should drain.

The Hand Taiyang small intestine channel, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Taiyang, emerging at Shaoze — Shaoze lies at the tip of the small finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Qiangu — Qiangu lies in the depression on the outer side of the hand, in front of the base joint — and is the spring point. It pours into Houxi — Houxi lies on the outer side of the hand, behind the base joint — and is the stream point. It passes through Wangu — Wangu lies on the outer side of the hand, in front of the wrist bone — and is the source point. It travels through Yanggu — Yanggu lies in the depression below the pointed bone — and is the river point.
