It enters at Xiaohai — Xiaohai lies outside the great bone of the inner elbow, half a cun from its tip, in a depression, found by extending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Taiyang channel.

The large intestine channel, in its upper association, unites with the Hand Yangming, emerging at Shangyang — Shangyang lies at the tip of the index finger — and is the metal well point. It flows into Erjian, in front of the base joint, and is the spring point. It pours into Sanjian, behind the base joint, and is the stream point. It passes through Hegu — Hegu lies between the forking bones of the thumb — and is the source point. It travels through Yangxi — Yangxi lies in the depression between the two sinews — and is the river point. It enters at Quchi, which lies in the depression by the rounded bone on the outer side of the elbow, found by bending the arm — and is the sea point. This is the Hand Yangming channel.

These are called the transport points of the five viscera and six bowels: five times five making twenty-five transport points, and six times six making thirty-six transport points. The six bowels all emerge from the three yang channels of the foot, and in their upper association unite with the channels of the hand.

At the center of the supraclavicular fossa lies the Conception Vessel, at the point called Tiantu. The first vessel beside the Conception Vessel is the pulsating vessel of the Foot Yangming, at the point called Renying. The second vessel is that of the Hand Yangming, at the point called Futu. The third vessel is that of the Hand Taiyang, at the point called Tianchuang. The fourth vessel is that of the Foot Shaoyang, at the point called Tianrong. The fifth vessel is that of the Hand Shaoyang, at the point called Tianyou. The sixth vessel is that of the Foot Taiyang, at the point called Tianzhu. The seventh vessel, at the center of the nape of the neck, is the Governing Vessel, at the point called Fengfu. The pulsating vessel within the armpit belongs to the Hand Taiyin, at the point called Tianfu. Three cun below the armpit belongs to the Hand Jueyin, at the point called Tianchi.

When needling Shangguan, the patient can open the mouth wide but cannot close it. When needling Xiaguan, the patient can close the mouth but cannot open it wide. When needling Dubi, the patient can bend the knee but cannot extend it. When needling both Guan points together, the patient can extend the limb but cannot bend it.

The Foot Yangming channel has its pulsating vessel beside the throat, and its transport point lies within the chest. The Hand Yangming's point lies next to it, outside this transport point, one cun short of the angle of the jaw. The Hand Taiyang's point lies right at the angle of the jaw. The Foot Shaoyang's point lies below the ear, behind the angle of the jaw. The Hand Shaoyang's point emerges behind the ear, above and resting upon the 'completion bone,' the mastoid process. The Foot Taiyang's point lies beside the great sinews of the nape of the neck, at the hairline.
The pulsating vessel of the yin at the forearm lies at Wuli — this is a point among the five transport points that is forbidden to needle.

The lung unites with the large intestine, and the large intestine is the bowel that transmits and conveys. The heart unites with the small intestine, and the small intestine is the bowel that receives and holds. The liver unites with the gallbladder, and the gallbladder is the bowel of central essence. The spleen unites with the stomach, and the stomach is the bowel of the five grains. The kidney unites with the bladder, and the bladder is the bowel of fluids. The Shaoyang is associated with the kidney, and the kidney above connects with the lung, and so the kidney governs these two viscera together. The triple burner is the bowel of the central drainage-ditch, from which the waterways emerge; it is associated with the bladder, and is the solitary bowel among the six bowels — these are the bowels with which the six bowels are united.

In spring, select points among the network vessels, the spring points, and the intervals between the flesh along the great channels; where the disease is severe, needle deeply, and where it is mild, needle shallowly. In summer, select points among the stream points and the grandchild network vessels, at the surface of the muscles, flesh, and skin. In autumn, select points among the sea points, otherwise following the method of spring. In winter, select points among the well points and the stream points, needling deeply and retaining the needle long. This is the proper sequence of the four seasons, corresponding to where the qi resides, where the disease lodges, and what is suited to the viscera. For cramping of the sinews, needle the patient standing, and the cramp may thereby be resolved at once. For wilting weakness and reversal, needle the patient with the limb stretched out, and relief may thereby be obtained immediately.

What is meant by "easy to state" is that it is easy to put into words. What is meant by "difficult to enter" is that it is difficult to make it take hold in a person. "The unskilled practitioner guards the form" means that he adheres only to the technique of puncturing. "The superior practitioner guards the spirit" means that he attends to whether a person's blood and qi are in excess or deficient, so that he may know when to supplement or drain. "Spirit and guest meeting together" means that the true qi and the evil qi converge together. "Spirit" refers to the true qi; "guest" refers to the evil qi. "At the gate" means that the evil qi follows the very pathway by which the true qi goes out and comes in. "Not yet having seen the illness" means that one must first know in which channel the evil qi or the true qi has caused the illness. "How could one know its origin" means that one must first know in which channel the disease lies, in order to know where to select the point of treatment.

The subtlety of needling lying in speed and slowness means the intention of moving the needle swiftly or slowly. "The unskilled practitioner guards the joints" means that he attends only to the four limbs, without knowing the coming and going of blood and qi, of true qi and evil qi. "The superior practitioner guards the pivot" means that he knows how to hold and attend to the qi. "The movement of the pivot never departs from the point's aperture" means that he knows the deficiency and excess of the qi, and applies the needle swiftly or slowly accordingly. "The pivot within the aperture is pure, tranquil, and subtle" means that once the needle has obtained the qi, one must attend closely and hold the qi without letting it be lost. "What is coming cannot be met head-on" means that when the qi is exuberant, one must not supplement it. "What is departing cannot be pursued" means that when the qi is deficient, one must not drain it. "It cannot be suspended by a single hair" means that the qi is easily lost. "Tapping and yet it does not respond" means that the practitioner does not understand the proper intent of supplementing and draining — the blood and qi are already exhausted, and the qi will not descend.

"Knowing its coming and going" means knowing whether the qi is moving contrary to or in accord with its course, and whether it is exuberant or deficient. "One must engage it at the proper moment" means knowing the precise moment when the qi may be seized. "The unskilled practitioner is in the dark" means he is benighted and does not perceive the subtlety and secrecy of the qi. How marvelous — that the skilled practitioner alone possesses this, for he fully understands the intent of the needle. "What is departing is contrary" means that when the qi is deficient and diminished, this diminishment is contrary to its proper course. "What is coming is accordant" means that when the form and qi are level and balanced, this levelness is in accord with its proper course. "Clearly knowing the contrary and the accordant, and applying the needle correctly without error" means knowing precisely where to select the point of treatment. "To meet the qi head-on and seize it" is to drain; "to pursue the qi and reinforce it" is to supplement.

What is meant by "when deficient, replenish it" is that when the qi opening (the radial pulse) is deficient, one ought to supplement it. "When full, drain it" means that when the qi opening is exuberant, one ought to drain it. "When there is stagnant old blood, remove it" means clearing away the stagnant blood in the vessels. "When evil qi prevails, deplete it" means that in any channel where there is exuberance, one must drain out the evil qi. "Insert slowly and withdraw swiftly, and this brings about replenishment" means inserting the needle slowly and withdrawing it swiftly. "Insert swiftly and withdraw slowly, and this brings about depletion" means inserting the needle swiftly and withdrawing it slowly. Speaking of excess and deficiency as "as if present, as if absent" means that in excess there is qi present, while in deficiency there is no qi. "Examining what comes after and what came before, as if lost, as if preserved" means observing the deficiency and excess of the qi, and the proper sequence of supplementing and draining, examining whether the qi has already descended or still remains constant. "Being deficient, being excess, as if gained, as if lost" means that in supplementing, one feels a sense of fullness as though something had been gained, while in draining, one feels a vague sense as though something had been lost.

Now, as for the qi within the vessels: "evil qi lies in the upper part" means that when evil qi strikes a person, it strikes high, and so evil qi is said to lie in the upper part. "Turbid qi lies in the middle" means that water and grain both enter the stomach, and their essential qi rises to be poured into the lung, while the turbid portion flows into the intestines and stomach; it means that when one fails to adapt to cold and warmth, and is intemperate in eating and drinking, disease arises in the intestines and stomach — hence this is called turbid qi lying in the middle. "Clear qi lies in the lower part" means that when the clear, damp qi of the earth strikes a person, it must begin from the feet — hence it is said that clear qi lies in the lower part. "Needling the collapsed vessel so that evil qi is expelled" means selecting points in the upper part of the body. "Needling the central vessel so that turbid qi is expelled" means selecting the He-Sea point of the Yangming. "If the needle is inserted too deeply, the evil qi is instead driven deeper" means that in illnesses that are shallow and superficial, one should not needle deeply, for if one needles deeply, the evil qi will follow the needle inward — hence it is said the evil qi is instead driven deeper. "The skin, flesh, sinews, and vessels each have their own location" means that the channels and network vessels each govern their own particular region. "Selecting the five yin vessels causes death" means that when the disease lies within and the central qi is insufficient, if one nonetheless uses the needle to drain heavily all the yin vessels, the patient will die. "Selecting the three yang vessels" means draining entirely the qi of the three yang, causing the patient to become enfeebled and unable to recover. "Robbing the yin causes death" refers to needling the Wuli point of the forearm repeatedly, five times in succession. "Robbing the yang causes madness" is stated exactly as it is.

"Observing the complexion, examining the eyes, knowing whether the spirit is scattered or restored, unifying the physical form, and listening to the patient's movement and stillness" means that the superior practitioner knows how to read the five colors in the eyes. There is also the knowledge of adjusting the chi and cun pulse positions — their smallness and largeness, slowness and rapidity, smoothness and roughness — in order to determine the nature of the disease. "Knowing the evil and the true" means understanding the distinction between the vacuous evil wind and the true evil wind. "The right hand governs the thrusting, the left hand holds and controls it" means holding the needle as it is inserted and withdrawn. "When the qi arrives, withdraw the needle" means that once supplementing or draining has regulated the qi, one withdraws the needle. "Regulating the qi lies in making the beginning and the end as one" means holding the mind steady throughout. "The three hundred and sixty-five meeting places at the joints" refers to the places where the network vessels seep into and irrigate all the joints.

What is meant by "the qi of the five viscera already cut off within" is that the qi at the vessel opening is cut off internally and does not arrive; if, contrary to proper practice, one instead selects the diseased site on the exterior and the He-Sea points of the yang channels, retaining the needle so as to summon the yang qi, then when the yang qi arrives, the interior becomes doubly exhausted, and this double exhaustion brings death. In such death, there is no qi to stir movement, and so the body remains still. What is meant by "the qi of the five viscera already cut off without" is that the qi at the vessel opening is cut off externally and does not arrive; if, contrary to proper practice, one instead selects the transport points at the four extremities, retaining the needle so as to summon the yin qi, then when the yin qi arrives, the yang qi is instead driven inward; once driven inward it becomes rebellious, and this rebellion brings death. In such death, the yin qi is in excess, and so the body is restless.

The reason for examining the eyes is that the five viscera cause the five colors to follow their proper brightness. When they follow their proper brightness, the voice becomes distinct. When the voice is distinct, this means the voice differs from what it was in ordinary health.

灵枢经.title_en: Evil Qi and the Disease Patterns of the Viscera and Bowels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'How does evil qi strike a person?' Qibo replied: 'When evil qi strikes a person, it strikes high.'

Huangdi said: 'Is there a fixed measure to this high and low?' Qibo said: 'Above the midpoint of the body, it is evil qi that strikes; below the midpoint of the body, it is dampness that strikes. Hence it is said that when evil qi strikes a person, there is no constant rule — if it strikes the yin, it flows into the bowels; if it strikes the yang, it flows into the channels.'

Huangdi said: 'Yin and yang, though differing in name, belong to the same category; above and below they meet together, and the channels and network vessels interconnect like a ring without end. Yet when evil qi strikes a person, sometimes it strikes the yin, sometimes the yang, sometimes above, sometimes below, sometimes to the left, sometimes to the right, with no constant pattern — what is the reason for this?'

Qibo said: 'All the meeting points of the yang channels lie in the face. When evil qi strikes a person, it does so at a time when the body is vacuous, or when one has just exerted great physical effort, or after eating and drinking so that sweat is released — at such times the interstices of the skin open, and the evil qi strikes in. If it strikes the face, it descends into the Yangming; if it strikes the nape of the neck, it descends into the Taiyang; if it strikes the cheek, it descends into the Shaoyang. If it strikes the chest, the back, or the two rib-sides, it likewise enters their respective channels.'

Huangdi said: 'How does it strike the yin?' Qibo replied: 'When evil qi strikes the yin, it invariably begins from the arm or the shin. Now, on the arm and the shin, the skin over the yin aspect is thin and the flesh moist and glossy; hence, although both the yin and yang aspects are equally exposed to wind, it is the yin alone that is injured.'

Huangdi said: 'Does this, then, necessarily injure the viscera?' Qibo replied: 'When the body is struck by wind, this does not necessarily disturb the viscera. Thus, when evil qi enters a yin channel, if the qi of its associated viscus is replete, the evil qi enters but cannot lodge there, and so it returns instead to the corresponding bowel. Hence, when the yang is struck, the evil flows into the channels; when the yin is struck, the evil flows into the bowels.'

Huangdi said: 'How does evil qi strike a person's viscera?' Qibo said: 'Sorrow, anxiety, fear, and fright injure the heart. Exposure of the body to cold combined with drinking cold beverages injures the lung, for when the two colds affect each other, both the interior and exterior are injured, and so the qi rebels and moves upward. If one has suffered a fall, malign blood lodges within; or if one has been overcome by great rage, the qi rises and does not descend, accumulating below the rib-side — this injures the liver. If one has been struck or has fallen, or has entered the bedchamber while intoxicated, and then sweats while exposed to wind, this injures the spleen. If one has exerted great force in lifting a heavy weight, or has entered the bedchamber to excess, and then sweats and bathes in water, this injures the kidney.'
Huangdi said: 'How does wind strike the five viscera?' Qibo said: 'Only when both yin and yang are affected together can the evil qi make its way in.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The head and face, and the body as a whole, are joined by bone and linked by sinew, and share in the same blood united with qi. Yet when the weather turns cold, so cold that the earth cracks and ice forms, and the sudden chill causes the hands and feet to grow numb and sluggish, the face alone is left uncovered by clothing and yet is unharmed — why is this?' Qibo replied: 'The twelve channel vessels and the three hundred and sixty-five network vessels all send their blood and qi upward to the face, coursing through its open orifices. Their essential yang qi rises to the eyes and becomes vision; their branch qi courses to the ears and becomes hearing; their ancestral qi rises out through the nose and becomes smell; their turbid qi issues from the stomach and courses to the lips and tongue, becoming taste. The fluids carried by this qi all rise to steam and warm the face, and since the skin there is also thick and the flesh firm, even when the weather is extremely cold, it cannot overcome the face.'

Huangdi said: 'When evil qi strikes a person, what are the forms of the resulting illness?' Qibo said: 'When vacuous evil strikes the body, it causes shivering and trembling that disturbs the physical form. When true evil strikes a person, it is subtle — it first appears in the complexion, without being felt in the body, as if present, as if absent, as if lost, as if preserved, having form yet without form, so that none can discern its true nature.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard that to see the complexion and thereby know the disease is called clarity of vision; to press the pulse and thereby know the disease is called spiritual perception; to ask about the illness and thereby know its location is called skill. I wish to hear how one comes to know by seeing, to grasp by pressing, and to exhaust the matter by questioning — how is this done?'

Qibo replied: 'Now the complexion, the pulse, and the skin of the forearm (chi) correspond to one another just as the drumstick corresponds to the drum's sound, or the form to its shadow — they cannot fail to correspond. This is likewise the manifestation of root and branch, root and leaf: when the root dies, the leaves wither. The complexion, the pulse, the bodily form, and the flesh cannot fail to correspond to one another. Thus, one who knows how to read one of these is a skilled practitioner; one who knows two is a spiritually perceptive practitioner; one who knows all three possesses both spiritual perception and clarity of vision.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear this in full.' Qibo replied: 'When the complexion is green-blue, the pulse is string-like; when red, the pulse is hook-like; when yellow, the pulse is intermittent; when white, the pulse is feather-like; when black, the pulse is stone-like. If one observes the complexion but finds a pulse that does not correspond to it, and instead finds a pulse belonging to the phase that overcomes it, the patient will die; but if one finds a pulse belonging to the phase that generates it, the illness will resolve.'
Huangdi asked Qibo: 'What are the forms of disease produced by transformation and change within the five viscera?'
Qibo replied: 'One must first establish the correspondence of the five colors and the five pulses; only then can the disease be distinguished.'
Huangdi said: 'Once the complexion and pulse have been established, how does one go about distinguishing the disease?'
Qibo said: 'By examining whether the pulse is moderate or tense, small or large, slippery or rough, the transformation of the disease can be determined.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one examine this?' Qibo replied: 'When the pulse is tense, the skin of the chi (forearm) is likewise tense; when the pulse is moderate, the skin of the chi is likewise relaxed; when the pulse is small, the skin of the chi is likewise diminished and the qi scant; when the pulse is large, the skin of the chi likewise swells and rises; when the pulse is slippery, the skin of the chi is likewise slippery; when the pulse is rough, the skin of the chi is likewise rough. In all these transformations, there are degrees, some slight and some severe. Thus one who is skilled in examining the chi need not wait to examine the cun; one who is skilled in examining the pulse need not wait to examine the complexion. One who can bring all three together and apply them in unison may be called a superior practitioner — a superior practitioner cures nine out of ten. One who applies two of the three is a mediocre practitioner — a mediocre practitioner cures seven out of ten. One who applies only one is an inferior practitioner — an inferior practitioner cures six out of ten.'
Huangdi said: 'May I ask what are the forms of disease corresponding to the pulse being moderate or tense, small or large, slippery or rough?'

Qibo said: 'Allow your servant to speak of the disease transformations of the five viscera. When the heart pulse is extremely tense, this causes convulsions with tetany; when only somewhat tense, this causes heart pain radiating to the back, and inability to swallow food. When extremely moderate (slack), this causes maniacal laughter; when only somewhat moderate, this causes fu-liang, a mass lodged below the heart that moves up and down, with occasional spitting of blood. When extremely large, this causes obstruction of the throat; when only somewhat large, this causes heart impediment radiating to the back, with frequent tearing of the eyes. When extremely small, this causes frequent retching; when only somewhat small, this causes wasting-thirst disease. When extremely slippery, this causes frequent thirst; when only somewhat slippery, this causes heart mounting-disorder radiating to the navel, with rumbling in the lower abdomen. When extremely rough, this causes loss of voice; when only somewhat rough, this causes overflow of blood, linking reversal, ringing in the ears, and epilepsy.'

When the lung pulse is extremely tense, this causes epilepsy; when only somewhat tense, this causes cold and heat in the lung, with lassitude, coughing and spitting of blood, radiating to the lower back, spine, and chest, and sometimes nasal polyps obstructing the breath. When extremely moderate, this causes profuse sweating; when only somewhat moderate, this causes wilting weakness — wilting weakness combined with hemilateral wind, with sweat pouring from the head downward without cease. When extremely large, this causes swelling of the shin; when only somewhat large, this causes lung impediment radiating to the chest and back, with the patient rising and averting the eyes from sunlight. When extremely small, this causes diarrhea; when only somewhat small, this causes wasting impediment. When extremely slippery, this causes panting with rising qi; when only somewhat slippery, this causes bleeding from both the upper and lower orifices. When extremely rough, this causes vomiting of blood; when only somewhat rough, this causes 'rat lump' swellings between the neck and the armpit, in which the lower part cannot bear the weight of the upper, and one is prone to soreness in response.

When the liver pulse is extremely tense, this causes malicious speech; when only somewhat tense, this causes 'fat qi' — a mass below the rib-side like an overturned cup. When extremely moderate, this causes frequent vomiting; when only somewhat moderate, this causes water-conglomeration impediment. When extremely large, this causes internal welling-abscess, with frequent vomiting and nosebleed; when only somewhat large, this causes liver impediment, retraction of the genitals, and coughing radiating to the lower abdomen. When extremely small, this causes excessive thirst and drinking; when only somewhat small, this causes wasting-thirst disease. When extremely slippery, this causes protruding mounting-disorder; when only somewhat slippery, this causes involuntary urination. When extremely rough, this causes overflow-fluid disease; when only somewhat rough, this causes convulsive spasm and sinew impediment.

When the spleen pulse is extremely tense, this causes convulsions with tetany; when only somewhat tense, this causes obstruction of the diaphragm, in which food and drink, once swallowed, are brought back up, followed by the discharge of frothy fluid. When extremely moderate, this causes wilting weakness and reversal; when only somewhat moderate, this causes wind wilting, in which the four limbs cannot be used, though the mind remains clear as though there were no illness. When extremely large, this causes sudden collapse as though struck down; when only somewhat large, this causes mounting-qi disorder, with a large accumulation of pus and blood within the abdomen, outside the intestines and stomach. When extremely small, this causes cold and heat; when only somewhat small, this causes wasting-thirst disease. When extremely slippery, this causes protruding stranguria; when only somewhat slippery, this causes parasitic toxin from roundworms and other worms with heat in the abdomen. When extremely rough, this causes intestinal protrusion; when only somewhat rough, this causes internal protrusion, with copious discharge of pus and blood.

When the kidney pulse is extremely tense, this causes bone epilepsy; when only somewhat tense, this causes sinking reversal and running-piglet disorder, in which the feet cannot be drawn back and neither urination nor defecation can be accomplished. When extremely moderate, this causes a broken-feeling in the spine; when only somewhat moderate, this causes 'hollow-flux,' in which food is not digested and passes straight down the throat and out again. When extremely large, this causes impotence; when only somewhat large, this causes stone-water disease, in which swelling rises from the navel down to the lower abdomen in a heavy, sluggish manner, and up to the stomach cavity — when it reaches this point, it is fatal and beyond treatment. When extremely small, this causes hollow-flux diarrhea; when only somewhat small, this causes wasting-thirst disease. When extremely slippery, this causes stranguria and protrusion; when only somewhat slippery, this causes bone wilting, in which the patient cannot rise once seated, and upon rising the eyes see nothing. When extremely rough, this causes large welling-abscess; when only somewhat rough, this causes amenorrhea and sunken hemorrhoids.

Huangdi said: 'For these six transformations of disease, how should one needle?' Qibo replied: 'In all cases of tenseness, there is generally cold; in moderateness, there is generally heat; in largeness, there is generally abundant qi but scant blood; in smallness, both blood and qi are scant; in slipperiness, the yang qi is exuberant, with a slight degree of heat; in roughness, there is abundant blood but scant qi, with a slight degree of cold. Therefore, when needling tenseness, insert the needle deeply and retain it long. When needling moderateness, insert the needle shallowly and withdraw it swiftly, so as to dispel the heat. When needling largeness, drain the qi only slightly, without letting blood escape. When needling slipperiness, withdraw the needle swiftly after a shallow insertion, so as to drain the yang qi and dispel the heat. When needling roughness, one must strike precisely into the vessel, and, following whether the qi runs contrary or accordant, retain the needle for a long time; one must first press and trace along the vessel, and once the needle has been withdrawn, press upon the site of the disorder so as to prevent blood from escaping, thereby harmonizing the vessel. In all cases of smallness, the yin, yang, physical form, and qi are all insufficient, and one should not treat with the needle, but should regulate instead with sweet-flavored medicinals.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that regarding the qi of the five viscera and six bowels, the Ying and Shu points lead in to become the He point. By what pathway does the qi enter, and once entered, how does it connect and pass through? I wish to hear the reason for this.' Qibo replied: 'This refers to the branch of the yang vessel that enters within and belongs to the bowels.'

Huangdi said: 'Do the Ying, Shu, and He points each have their own distinct function?' Qibo replied: 'The Ying and Shu points treat the exterior channels, while the He points treat the internal bowels.'
Huangdi said: 'How does one treat the internal bowels?' Qibo said: 'By selecting the He point.'

Huangdi said: 'Do the He points each have their own particular name?' Qibo replied: 'The He point of the stomach is Zusanli; the He point of the large intestine enters at Shangjuxu; the He point of the small intestine enters at Xiajuxu; the He point of the triple burner enters at Weiyang; the He point of the bladder enters at the center of Weizhong; the He point of the gallbladder enters at Yanglingquan.'

Huangdi said: 'How are these points located?' Qibo replied: 'To locate Sanli, lower the instep and take the point; to locate Juxu, raise the foot and take the point; to locate Weiyang, bend and extend the leg and search for the point; to locate Weizhong, bend the leg and take the point; to locate Yanglingquan, have the patient sit with the knee held upright, level with it, and take the point below, on the yang side toward Weiyang; to locate all the points of the exterior channels, extend and stretch the limb and follow it to find the point.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the diseases of the six bowels.' Qibo replied: 'Heat in the face indicates disease of the foot Yangming; a congested network vessel over the thenar eminence indicates disease of the hand Yangming; a sunken, taut vessel over the two insteps indicates disease of the foot Yangming — this is the stomach vessel.'

In disease of the large intestine, there is cutting pain within the intestines, with loud rumbling sounds. If in winter one is further affected by cold, diarrhea results, with pain centered at the navel, and one is unable to stand for long. Its signs are shared with those of the stomach; select Shangjuxu.

In disease of the stomach, there is distension of the abdomen, and pain in the stomach cavity centered below the heart, radiating up to affect the two rib-sides; the diaphragm and throat fail to communicate, and food and drink cannot descend. Select Sanli.

In disease of the small intestine, there is pain in the lower abdomen, with pain in the lower back and spine pulling toward the testicles, and at times urgent difficulty in defecation; there is heat in front of the ear, or else severe cold, or heat concentrated solely on the shoulder, and heat between the little finger and the finger next to it; a sunken vessel is also a sign of this condition. This is disease of the hand Taiyang; select Xiajuxu.

In disease of the triple burner, there is fullness of qi in the abdomen, with the lower abdomen especially hard, inability to urinate, and urgent distress; if the condition overflows, water is retained, producing distension. Its sign lies in the great network vessel on the outer aspect of the foot Taiyang, this great network vessel lying between the Taiyang and Shaoyang, and it also appears in the vessel there; select Weiyang.

In disease of the bladder, there is swelling and pain to one side of the lower abdomen; when pressed with the hand, it produces an urge to urinate that cannot be satisfied; there is heat on the shoulder, or a sunken vessel there, and heat over the outer border of the little toe and behind the ankle of the shin, or a sunken vessel there; select the center of Weizhong.

In disease of the gallbladder, there is frequent sighing, bitterness in the mouth, vomiting of stale fluid, an uneasy fluttering sensation below the heart, and a fear as though someone were about to seize the patient, with frequent hawking and spitting in the throat. The condition lies at the root and tip of the foot Shaoyang; one should also examine its vessel, and where it is sunken, apply moxibustion; where there is cold and heat, select Yanglingquan.

Huangdi said: 'Is there a proper method to needling these points?' Qibo replied: 'In needling these, one must strike precisely into the qi aperture, and must not strike into the flesh at the joints. If one strikes the qi aperture, the needle moves as though traveling through an open lane; if one strikes the flesh at the joints, the skin becomes painful. If supplementing and draining are reversed, the illness will only grow worse. If one strikes a sinew, the sinew becomes slack, the evil qi fails to be expelled, and, contending in disorder with the true qi without being dispelled, it instead returns and lodges within the body. This is the result of applying the needle without due care, turning what should accord into what instead opposes.'

Qibo said: 'Heaven and Earth mutually affect one another, and cold and warmth shift back and forth in turn. As for the Way of yin and yang, which is the lesser and which the greater? The way of yin is even in number, the way of yang is odd. When illness arises in spring and summer, yin qi is scant and yang qi is abundant; yin and yang are then out of balance — in such a case, what should be supplemented and what should be drained? When illness arises in autumn and winter, yang qi is scant and yin qi is abundant; yin qi is exuberant while yang qi wanes, so that the stalks and leaves wither and dry, the damp rains sink downward and return to the earth, and yin and yang shift places with one another — in such a case, what should be drained and what should be supplemented? Aberrant evils that depart from the channels are too numerous to count. If one does not understand the root and knot of the channels and of the five viscera and six bowels, the pivots and hinges will break and fail, and the opening and closing will run astray; yin and yang will be gravely lost and cannot be restored. The mystery of the nine needles lies essentially in the beginning and end (zhongshi) of the channels — the point at which the channel qi arises and terminates. If one is able to understand this beginning and end, it can be summed up in a single phrase; if one does not understand the beginning and end, the Way of needling is entirely severed.'

The Taiyang channel is rooted at Zhiyin and knotted at Mingmen — Mingmen here refers to the eye. The Yangming channel is rooted at Lidui and knotted at Sangda — Sangda refers to the region that clasps the ear. The Shaoyang channel is rooted at Qiaoyin and knotted at Chuanglong — Chuanglong refers to the interior of the ear. Taiyang serves as the opening, Yangming serves as the closing, and Shaoyang serves as the pivot. Hence, when the opening breaks down, the joints of the flesh become leaky and sudden illness arises; for sudden illness, one should select points along the Taiyang channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient. 'Leaky' here means that the skin and flesh become wasted, shrunken, and feeble. When the closing breaks down, the qi has nowhere to rest and comes to a halt, and wilting disease (wei) arises; for wilting disease, one should select points along the Yangming channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient. By 'having nowhere to rest' is meant that the true qi becomes detained and stagnant, and evil qi comes to dwell there. When the pivot breaks down, the bones sway loosely and cannot settle firmly upon the ground; for such swaying of the bones, one should select points along the Shaoyang channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient. By 'swaying of the bones' is meant that the joints become slack and cannot draw themselves in. This so-called swaying of the bones comes from unsteadiness, and one must seek out its root.

The Taiyin channel is rooted at Yinbai and knotted at Taicang. The Shaoyin channel is rooted at Yongquan and knotted at Lianquan. The Jueyin channel is rooted at Dadun and knotted at Yuying, with a network vessel connecting to Danzhong. Taiyin serves as the opening, Jueyin serves as the closing, and Shaoyang serves as the pivot. Hence, when the opening breaks down, the granary has nowhere to transport its contents, and diaphragmatic flux (getong) arises; for diaphragmatic flux, one should select points along the Taiyin channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient — for when the opening breaks down, it is because the qi is insufficient that illness is generated. When the closing breaks down, the qi becomes cut off and one is prone to sorrow; for sorrow, one should select points along the Jueyin channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient. When the pivot breaks down, the vessel becomes knotted at some point and fails to flow through; for such non-flow, one should select points along the Shaoyin channel, examining whether it is in excess or deficient — wherever there is a knot, in every case one should treat it as a deficiency and select points accordingly.

The Foot Taiyang channel is rooted at Zhiyin, flows at Jinggu, pours into Kunlun, and enters at Tianzhu and Feiyang. The Foot Shaoyang channel is rooted at Qiaoyin, flows at Qiuxu, pours into Yangfu, and enters at Tianrong and Guangming. The Foot Yangming channel is rooted at Lidui, flows at Chongyang, pours into Xialing, and enters at Renying and Fenglong. The Hand Taiyang channel is rooted at Shaoze, flows at Yanggu, pours into Xiaohai, and enters at Tianchuang and Zhizheng. The Hand Shaoyang channel is rooted at Guanchong, flows at Yangchi, pours into Zhigou, and enters at Tianyou and Waiguan. The Hand Yangming channel is rooted at Shangyang, flows at Hegu, pours into Yangxi, and enters at Futu and Pianli. These are what are called the twelve channels; wherever their network vessels are exuberant and full, one should in every case select points there for treatment.

In the course of a single day and night, the qi makes fifty circuits (ying), so as to nourish the essence of the five viscera. When the pulse fails to correspond to this number, this is called a reckless survival — life sustained against the proper norm. What is meant by the fifty circuits is this: the five viscera all receive qi in turn, and one holds the pulse at the wrist opening and counts its arrivals. If in fifty beats there is not a single intermittent, skipped beat (dai), the five viscera are all receiving qi properly. If in forty beats there is one skipped beat, one viscus is without qi. If in thirty beats there is one skipped beat, two viscera are without qi. If in twenty beats there is one skipped beat, three viscera are without qi. If in ten beats there is one skipped beat, four viscera are without qi. If there is a skipped beat before ten beats have passed, all five viscera are without qi. In assigning the patient's remaining span of life, the essential point lies in the beginning and end of the pulse. What is meant by 'fifty beats without a single skipped beat' is taken as the normal, healthy pattern. It is by this that one comes to know the appointed time of the five viscera; in assigning a short remaining span of life, one observes whether the pulse is now rapid, now sparse and irregular.

Huangdi said: 'As for the compliance and resistance of the five bodily constitutions — that is, the largeness or smallness of a person's bones and joints, the firmness or fragility of the flesh, the thickness or thinness of the skin, the clarity or turbidity of the blood, the smoothness or roughness of the qi, the length or shortness of the vessels, the abundance or scantiness of the blood, and the number of the channels and network vessels — I already understand these matters, but they concern only the common people, the men in plain cloth. Now, kings, dukes, and great men, rulers who feast on meat and blood, have bodies that are soft and delicate, flesh that is tender and weak, and blood and qi that are brisk, keen, and smoothly flowing. Can the slowness or quickness, shallowness or depth, and the extent of needling be made the same for them as for the common people?' Qibo answered: 'How could the flavor of rich meats and fine grains be compared to that of beans and coarse greens? When the qi is smooth, it emerges quickly upon needling; when the qi is rough, it emerges slowly. When the qi is brisk and keen, the needle should be small and inserted shallowly; when the qi is rough, the needle should be large and inserted deeply — when inserted deeply, one should retain the needle; when inserted shallowly, one should withdraw it quickly. From this it can be seen that in needling the common people, one should insert deeply and retain the needle, while in needling the great and noble, one should needle lightly and withdraw quickly — all this follows from the briskness, keenness, and smooth flow of their qi.'

Huangdi said: 'What of the compliance and resistance between the body's constitution and its qi?' Qibo said: 'When the body's constitution is deficient while the disease qi is in excess, this means the evil qi is prevailing, and one should urgently drain it. When the body's constitution is in excess while the disease qi is deficient, one should urgently supplement it. When both the body's constitution and the disease qi are deficient, this means that both yin and yang qi are deficient, and one must not needle — for needling would only compound the deficiency. When the deficiency is compounded, yin and yang are both exhausted, the blood and qi are both spent, the five viscera become empty and hollow, the sinews, bones, and marrow wither, the old will perish entirely, and the young and vigorous will never recover. When both the body's constitution and the disease qi are in excess, this is called both yin and yang being in excess; one should urgently drain the evil and regulate its deficiency and excess. Hence it is said: what is in excess should be drained, and what is deficient should be supplemented — this is precisely what is meant.'

Hence it is said: if in needling one does not understand compliance and resistance, the true qi and the evil qi will clash with one another. If one is already full and yet is further supplemented, then yin and yang will overflow in all four directions, the intestines and stomach will become swollen and distended, the liver and lung will become congested within, and yin and yang will fall into disorder and cross one another. If one is already deficient and yet is further drained, then the channel vessels become empty and hollow, the blood and qi become utterly exhausted, the intestines and stomach shrivel and fold in upon themselves, the skin becomes thin and clings to the flesh beneath, the body hair and interstices wither and shrink — and this fixes the patient's date of death.

Hence it is said: the essential point in the practice of needling lies in knowing how to regulate yin and yang. When yin and yang are regulated, the essence qi shines forth in brilliance; when the body's form and its qi are united in harmony, the spirit is caused to be stored within. Hence it is said: the superior physician levels the qi; the mediocre physician throws the pulse into disorder; the inferior physician cuts off the qi and endangers life. Hence it is said: the inferior physician must be especially cautious — he must carefully examine the transformations and diseases of the five viscera, the responses of the five pulses, the excess or deficiency of the channels and network vessels, and the softness or coarseness of the skin, and only afterward select the proper points for treatment.

灵枢经.title_en: Longevity and Premature Death, Firmness and Softness
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Shaoshi, saying: 'I have heard that in the generation of human beings, some are firm and some are soft, some are weak and some are strong, some are short and some are long, some are yin and some are yang. I wish to hear the proper method concerning this.'

Shaoshi replied: 'Within yin there is yin, and within yang there is yang. Only by carefully discerning yin and yang can one needle according to the proper method. By grasping the point at which the illness originated, one's needling will have its proper rationale. One must carefully gauge the origin of the disease and respond to it in accordance with the season. Within, the body corresponds to the five viscera and six bowels; without, it corresponds to the sinews, bones, and skin. Hence, just as there is yin and yang within the body, so too is there yin and yang without. Of what lies within, the five viscera are yin and the six bowels are yang; of what lies without, the sinews and bones are yin and the skin is yang. Hence it is said: when disease lies in the yin aspect of yin, one should needle the brook and stream points (ying and shu) of the yin channels; when disease lies in the yang aspect of yang, one should needle the sea points (he) of the yang channels; when disease lies in the yin aspect of yang, one should needle the river points (jing) of the yin channels; when disease lies in the yang aspect of yin, one should needle the network vessels. Hence it is said: disease located in the yang is called wind, disease located in the yin is called impediment (bi), and disease afflicting both yin and yang together is called wind impediment. Disease that has visible form yet is not painful belongs to the category of yang; disease that has no visible form yet is painful belongs to the category of yin. When there is no visible form yet there is pain, this means the yang is whole while the yin has been injured — one should urgently treat the yin and not attack the yang. When there is visible form yet no pain, this means the yin is whole while the yang has been injured — one should urgently treat the yang and not attack the yin. When yin and yang are both stirred and disturbed, so that the disease is now with visible form, now without, accompanied further by vexation in the heart, this is called yin overcoming yang. Such a condition is neither in the exterior nor the interior alone, and the patient's bodily form will not long endure.'

Huangdi asked Bogao, saying: 'I have heard that in disease, the body's form and its qi may be affected in a certain order, one before the other — what is the correspondence between exterior and interior in this?' Bogao answered: 'Wind and cold injure the form, while worry, fear, and anger injure the qi. When the qi is injured, the viscera become diseased, and the viscera then respond; when cold injures the form, the form then responds; when wind injures the sinews and vessels, the sinews and vessels then respond. This is the mutual correspondence between exterior and interior, form and qi.'

Huangdi said: 'How, then, should one needle in such cases?' Bogao answered: 'If the disease has lasted nine days, three needlings will resolve it; if the disease has lasted one month, ten needlings will resolve it. The number of treatments, whether many or few, near or far apart, should be graduated according to this proportion. If a lingering impediment has remained in the body for a long time and will not depart, one should examine its blood network vessels and drain out all of the blood.'

Huangdi said: 'Between diseases of the exterior and the interior, how does the difficulty or ease of treatment compare?' Bogao answered: 'If the body's form is diseased first and the illness has not yet entered the viscera, one should needle it for half the number of days corresponding to its duration. If the viscera are diseased first and the form only responds afterward, one should needle it for double the number of days. This is the correspondence of difficulty and ease within a month's treatment.'

Huangdi asked Bogao, saying: 'I have heard that the body's form may be slack or tense, the qi may be flourishing or declining, the bones may be large or small, the flesh may be firm or fragile, and the skin may be thick or thin — how, from these, is longevity or premature death determined?' Bogao answered: 'When the form and the qi are matched to one another in strength, one lives long; when they are not matched, one dies young. When the skin and flesh are bound closely together, one lives long; when they are not bound together, one dies young. When the blood, qi, channels, and network vessels are more than sufficient to sustain the body's form, one lives long; when they are not sufficient to sustain it, one dies young.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by the slackness or tension of the form?' Bogao answered: 'If the body's form is full and the skin is relaxed and pliant, one lives long; if the body's form is full and the skin is tense and taut, one dies young. If the body's form is full and the pulse is firm and large, this is a favorable sign; if the body's form is full and the pulse is small and weak, the qi is declining, and when it declines the patient is in danger. If the body's form is full yet the cheekbones do not stand out prominently, this indicates that the bones are small, and small bones mean early death. If the body's form is full and the large flesh is firm, well-defined, and shows clear divisions, this indicates that the flesh is firm, and firm flesh means long life; if the body's form is full but the large flesh lacks clear divisions and is not firm, this indicates that the flesh is fragile, and fragile flesh means early death. This is the life bestowed by Heaven. As for establishing the body's form and fixing its qi in order to foresee longevity or premature death, one must be thoroughly clear about how to establish the form and fix the qi, and only then approach the patient and decide upon life and death.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard of longevity and premature death, but I have no way to measure them.' Bogao answered: 'If the foundation walls of the face are low, so that their height does not rise above the level of the surrounding terrain, such a person will die before reaching thirty years of age. If, in addition, such a person contracts a serious illness, he will die before reaching twenty.'

Huangdi said: 'How is longevity or premature death established through the mutual dominance of form and qi?' Bogao answered: 'In a healthy person, if the qi dominates over the form, this indicates long life. In a person who is ill, if the flesh has wasted away from the form and the qi dominates over the form, this indicates death; if the form dominates over the qi, this indicates grave danger.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that there are three variations in needling — what are these three variations?' Bogao answered: 'There is needling that targets the nutritive qi, needling that targets the defensive qi, and needling that targets cold impediment lingering within the channels.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one carry out these three variations of needling?' Bogao answered: 'When needling the nutritive qi, one lets out blood; when needling the defensive qi, one lets out qi; when needling cold impediment, one introduces heat into the body.'

Huangdi said: 'What are the disease manifestations of the nutritive qi, the defensive qi, and cold impediment?' Bogao answered: 'When disease arises from the nutritive qi, there is cold and heat, shortness of qi, and blood moving erratically upward and downward. When disease arises from the defensive qi, there is qi pain that comes and goes intermittently, along with oppressive fullness and rumbling in the abdomen, as wind and cold lodge within the intestines and stomach. When disease takes the form of cold impediment, the evil lingers and does not depart, causing intermittent pain and numbness of the skin.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one needle to introduce heat in the treatment of cold impediment?' Bogao answered: 'When needling the common people, one sears the needle with fire before insertion; when needling the great and noble, one applies a medicinal compress.'

Huangdi said: 'How is the medicinal compress prepared and applied?' Bogao answered: 'Take twenty catties of pure wine, one catty of Sichuan pepper, one catty of dried ginger, and one catty of cinnamon heartwood — four ingredients in all. Chew or crush each of them and steep them in the wine. Take one catty of silk floss and four zhang of fine white cloth, and place these together into the wine as well. Set the wine within a container of warm, smoldering horse dung, seal it thoroughly with a covering, and do not allow the vapor to escape. After five days and five nights, remove the cloth and floss and dry them in the sun; once dried, steep them again, so as to draw out the liquid fully. Each steeping must last a full day and night before the material is taken out to dry again. Once dry, combine the dregs together with the floss, and wrap them in the cloth to make compress-cloths six or seven chi in length, forming six or seven such cloths. Then heat the cloths over charcoal made from fresh mulberry wood, and use them to apply moist heat to the site where cold impediment has been needled, so as to cause the heat to penetrate to the site of the disease. When a cloth cools, heat another over the charcoal and apply it in turn, continuing for thirty rounds in all. Once sweating occurs, wipe the body with a cloth, likewise thirty times. Then have the patient rise and walk about indoors, taking care not to be exposed to wind. Each time one needles, one must also apply this compress — treated in this way, the disease will be resolved. This is what is meant by introducing heat.'

In all needling, the essential point is the proper matching of needle to disease — the standard of official needling (guanzhen) is the most refined of all. The nine needles are each suited to a particular purpose; long, short, large, and small, each has its own proper application. If one fails to use the needle appropriately, the disease cannot be moved or resolved. If the disease is shallow but the needle is inserted deep, this will injure the healthy flesh within, and the skin will develop an abscess. If the disease is deep but the needle is inserted shallow, the disease qi will not be drained, and it will instead branch out and form a great festering sore. If the disease is slight but a large needle is used, the qi will be drained excessively, and this will certainly cause harm. If the disease is severe but a small needle is used, the qi cannot be released and drained, and this too will result in failure. To lose the proper match of the needle in this way — where the disease is large, using too small a needle fails to move it. Having already spoken of these errors, let me now speak of the proper applications of each needle.

When disease lies in the skin without a fixed location, one should select the arrow-head needle (chanzhen) at the site of disease; if the skin is pale, do not needle. When disease lies between the divisions of the flesh, one should select the round needle (yuanzhen) at the site of disease. When disease lies in the channels and network vessels as a stubborn, chronic impediment, one should select the pointed needle (fengzhen). When disease lies in the vessels and the qi is scant, requiring supplementation, one should select the round-sharp needle (dizhen) at the well, brook, and other transport points. When disease has formed a great festering sore, one should select the lancet needle (pizhen). When disease is an impediment with qi erupting suddenly, one should select the round-and-sharp needle (yuanlizhen). When disease is an impediment with lingering pain that does not depart, one should select the filiform needle (haozhen). When disease lies deep within the body, one should select the long needle (changzhen). When disease is water swelling that cannot pass through the joints, one should select the great needle (dazhen). When disease lies fixed and lodged within the five viscera, one should select the pointed needle, draining at the well, brook, and other transport points, choosing the points according to the four seasons.

In all, there are nine methods of needling, corresponding to the nine variations of disease. The first is called transport-point needling (shuci): transport-point needling means needling the brook and transport points of the various channels, as well as the transport points of the viscera. The second is called far-reaching needling (yuandaoci): far-reaching needling means that when disease is located in the upper body, one selects points in the lower body, needling the transport points of the bowels. The third is called channel needling (jingci): channel needling means needling the knotted network vessels and channel divisions of the great channels. The fourth is called network needling (luoci): network needling means needling the blood vessels of the small network vessels. The fifth is called divisional needling (fenci): divisional needling means needling between the divisions of the flesh. The sixth is called great-draining needling (daxieci): great-draining needling means using the lancet needle to drain a great festering sore. The seventh is called hair needling (maoci): hair needling means needling floating impediment in the skin. The eighth is called great opposite-side needling (juci): great opposite-side needling means selecting the right side when the disease is on the left, and the left side when the disease is on the right. The ninth is called searing needling (cuici): searing needling means using a fire-heated needle to treat impediment.

In all, there are twelve techniques of needling, corresponding to the twelve channels. The first is called paired needling (ouci): paired needling means placing one hand directly over the heart or over the back, aligned with the exact site of pain, and inserting one needle in front and one behind, in order to treat impediment of the heart. When needling in this manner, the needle is angled obliquely. The second is called repeated needling (baoci): repeated needling is used when pain has no fixed location, moving up and down. One inserts the needle straight in without withdrawing it, and with the left hand presses along the site of the disease as it shifts, then withdraws the needle and needles again at the new location. The third is called relaxing needling (huici): relaxing needling means inserting the needle straight in and then to the side of it as well, lifting and moving it forward and backward so as to relax sinew tension, in order to treat impediment of the sinews. The fourth is called aligned needling (qici): aligned needling means inserting one needle straight in and two needles to either side, in order to treat cold qi that is somewhat deep; some call this triple needling (sanci), used to treat impediment qi that is somewhat deep. The fifth is called scattered needling (yangci): scattered needling means inserting one needle straight in the center and four needles around it, all shallowly, in order to treat cold qi that has spread over a large area. The sixth is called straight needling (zhizhenci): straight needling means drawing up the skin and then inserting the needle, in order to treat cold qi that lies shallow. The seventh is called transport needling (shuci): transport needling means inserting the needle straight in and withdrawing it straight out, needling infrequently but deeply, in order to treat qi that is exuberant and hot. The eighth is called brief needling (duanci): brief needling means, in treating bone impediment, gently rocking the needle while advancing it deeper, so that the needle reaches the bone, rubbing the bone up and down with the needle tip. The ninth is called floating needling (fuci): floating needling means inserting the needle to the side and keeping it shallow, in order to treat tension of the muscles accompanied by cold. The tenth is called yin needling (yinci): yin needling means needling both the left and right sides together, in order to treat cold reversal; this targets the Shaoyin point behind the medial ankle in cases of cold reversal. The eleventh is called flanking needling (pangzhenci): flanking needling means inserting one needle straight in and one needle to the side, in order to treat a lingering impediment that has resided in the body for a long time. The twelfth is called assisting needling (zanci): assisting needling means inserting the needle straight in and withdrawing it straight out repeatedly, keeping it shallow, so as to let out blood — this is what is meant by treating welling-abscess and swelling.

Where a vessel lies deep and cannot be seen, one should needle it by inserting the needle slightly and retaining it for a long while, so as to draw forth the qi of the vessel's cavity. Where a vessel lies shallow, one should not needle it directly; rather, one should press upon it to block off the flow of the vessel, and only then needle it, so as not to let the essential qi escape, but to let only the evil qi be released.

What is called the three insertions (sanci) is the method by which grain qi is made to emerge. First one needles shallowly, penetrating only the skin, so as to release yang evil; the second insertion releases yin evil, going a little deeper past the skin, reaching the flesh but not yet entering between its divisions; once the needle has entered between the divisions of the flesh, the grain qi emerges. Hence the method of needling states: at the first insertion, needle shallowly, so as to expel the evil qi and bring forth the blood and qi; at the second insertion, needle more deeply, so as to draw out the evil residing in the yin qi; and at the final insertion, needle to the greatest depth, so as to bring down the grain qi. This is precisely what is meant.

Hence, one who practices needling but does not understand the increase brought by the patient's age, the flourishing or decline of the qi, and the origin of deficiency and excess, cannot be considered a skilled physician.

In all, there are five methods of needling, corresponding to the five viscera. The first is called half needling (banci): half needling means inserting the needle shallowly and withdrawing it quickly, without letting the needle injure the flesh, resembling the plucking of a hair, in order to draw upon the qi of the skin — this corresponds to the lung.

The second is called leopard-pattern needling (baowenci): leopard-pattern needling means needling to the left and right, front and back, aiming deliberately to strike the vessel, in order to draw out the blood of the channels and network vessels — this corresponds to the heart.

The third is called joint needling (guanci): joint needling means needling straight in on the left and right, exactly at the point where the sinew ends, in order to treat impediment of the sinews, taking care not to let blood escape — this corresponds to the liver; it is also called deep-pool needling (yuanci), or, in another rendering, qici.

The fourth is called converging-valley needling (hegu ci): converging-valley needling means needling in a chicken-foot pattern to the left and right, between the divisions of the flesh, in order to treat impediment of the muscles — this corresponds to the spleen.

The fifth is called transport needling (shuci): transport needling means inserting the needle straight in and withdrawing it straight out, inserting it deeply until it reaches the bone, in order to treat impediment of the bones — this corresponds to the kidney.

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'In all the methods of needling, one must first take the spirit (shen) as the root. Blood, the vessels, nutritive qi, defensive qi, and essence-spirit — these are what the five viscera store. When these overflow in excess and depart from the viscera, so that the essence is lost, the ethereal and corporeal souls (hun and po) go flying off, the will and intent become confused and disordered, and wisdom and deliberation depart from the body — what is the cause of this? Is it the fault of Heaven, or the transgression of man? And what, precisely, are meant by virtue (de), qi, life (sheng), essence (jing), spirit (shen), the ethereal soul (hun), the corporeal soul (po), the heart-mind (xin), intent (yi), the will (zhi), reflection (si), wisdom (zhi), and deliberation (lü)? I ask to hear the explanation of these matters.'

Qibo answered: 'What Heaven bestows in me is virtue; what Earth bestows in me is qi. When virtue flows down and qi presses close together, life is thereby generated. Thus that from which life comes is called essence; the mutual interaction of the two essences is called spirit; that which follows the spirit in its coming and going is called the ethereal soul (hun); that which moves in and out together with the essence is called the corporeal soul (po); that by which one takes charge of and responds to external things is called the heart-mind; that which the heart-mind holds in recollection is called intent; that which intent retains and preserves is called the will; that which, arising from the will, undergoes deliberate change is called reflection; that which, arising from reflection, reaches far into distant aspiration is called deliberation; and that by which, arising from deliberation, one manages and deals with external affairs is called wisdom.'

Hence, the way in which the wise nourish life is always to follow the four seasons and accommodate themselves to cold and heat, to harmonize joy and anger and dwell in a settled and peaceful abode, and to moderate yin and yang and regulate firmness and softness. When one does this, deviant evils will not arrive, and one will enjoy long life and lasting vision.

Hence, one who is fearful, apprehensive, and given to excessive reflection and deliberation injures the spirit; when the spirit is injured, fear and dread arise, flowing unchecked and unceasing. One whose inner being is stirred by grief and sorrow becomes exhausted and cut off, and thereby loses their life. One who indulges excessively in joy and pleasure causes the spirit to become scattered and dissipated, no longer stored within. One who is beset by worry and anxiety causes the qi to become blocked and obstructed, no longer flowing freely. One who is given to violent rage becomes confused and disordered, beyond governing. One who is overcome by fear and dread causes the spirit to become agitated and unsettled, no longer able to be gathered in.

In the heart, fearfulness, apprehension, and excessive reflection and deliberation injure the spirit; when the spirit is injured, fear and dread arise and the person loses command of themselves. The rounded muscles waste and the flesh falls away, the body hair withers, and the complexion fades — such a person will die in winter.

In the spleen, when worry and anxiety go unresolved, this injures the intent; when the intent is injured, there is oppressive confusion and disorder, the four limbs cannot be lifted, the body hair withers, and the complexion fades — such a person will die in spring.

In the liver, when grief and sorrow stir the interior, this injures the ethereal soul (hun); when the hun is injured, there is manic forgetfulness and lack of clarity, and when there is no clarity, one loses proper self-command — one's genitals will contract and the sinews will cramp, the ribs on both sides cannot be lifted, the body hair withers, and the complexion fades — such a person will die in autumn.

In the lung, when joy and pleasure are indulged without limit, this injures the corporeal soul (po); when the po is injured, there is mania, and one in this manic state no longer recognizes other people; the skin becomes scorched and dry, the body hair withers, and the complexion fades — such a person will die in summer.

In the kidney, when violent rage does not cease, this injures the will; when the will is injured, one is prone to forgetting one's own previous words, the lower back and spine can no longer bend forward or back, flex or extend, the body hair withers, and the complexion fades — such a person will die in late summer.

When fear and dread go unresolved, this injures the essence; when the essence is injured, the bones ache, and there is wilting weakness and reversal, with essence spontaneously leaking downward from time to time. This is why the five viscera, whose governing function is to store the essence, must not be injured — for when injured, they lose their proper guard and yin becomes deficient; when yin is deficient there is no qi, and when there is no qi, death follows.

Hence, one who practices needling must observe and examine the patient's condition and bearing, so as to discern the presence or loss of the essence, spirit, ethereal soul, and corporeal soul, and the meaning of what has been gained or lost. If all five of these have already been injured, needling cannot treat the condition.
The liver stores the blood, and the blood houses the ethereal soul (hun); when the liver qi is deficient, one is fearful, and when it is in excess, one is prone to anger.

The spleen stores the nutritive qi, and the nutritive qi houses the intent; when the spleen qi is deficient, the four limbs cannot be used and the five viscera are unsettled, and when it is in excess, there is abdominal distension and difficulty in menstruation and urination.
The heart stores the vessels, and the vessels house the spirit; when the heart qi is deficient, one is prone to sorrow, and when it is in excess, one laughs without ceasing.

The lung stores the qi, and the qi houses the corporeal soul (po); when the lung qi is deficient, the nose becomes congested and functions poorly, with shortness of breath, and when it is in excess, there is panting and wheezing, fullness in the chest, and labored breathing with the head tilted back.

The kidney stores the essence, and the essence houses the will; when the kidney qi is deficient, there is reversal, and when it is in excess, there is distension. When the five viscera are unsettled in this way, one must carefully examine the disease patterns of the five viscera, so as to discern the deficiency or excess of their qi, and treat them with careful attention and regulation.

The Way of needling in its entirety is comprehended within the doctrine of the End and the Beginning (zhongshi — the complete course of a disease and its treatment, from onset to resolution). When one clearly understands the End and the Beginning, the five viscera serve as the guiding framework, and yin and yang become fixed and determinate. Yin governs the viscera, and yang governs the bowels; yang receives its qi from the four extremities, and yin receives its qi from the five viscera. Hence in draining one meets the qi head-on, and in supplementing one follows along with it; only by knowing how to meet it and how to follow it can the qi be brought into harmony. The method for harmonizing the qi must penetrate through to yin and yang. The five viscera are yin, and the six bowels are yang; this teaching has been handed down to later generations sealed with a covenant of blood. Those who revere it will flourish; those who treat it with contempt will perish. To act without the Way, in pursuit of private ends, will surely bring premature death and calamity.

In reverent accord with the Way of Heaven, let me speak of the End and the Beginning. In this doctrine, the channel vessels serve as the guiding framework. By taking hold of the wrist pulse (cunkou) and the renying pulse (taken at the neck), one may know whether yin and yang are in surplus or insufficiency, and whether the body is level or unlevel — and thus the Way of Heaven is fully comprehended. The one called the 'level person' is a person free of illness; being free of illness means that the wrist pulse and the renying pulse respond in accordance with the four seasons, that upper and lower correspond to one another and move back and forth together, that the vessels of the six channels do not knot or stir abnormally, and that the cold and warmth of root and branch maintain their mutual charge over one another. The flesh, blood, and qi must all be commensurate with one another — this is called the level person. In one with insufficient qi, the wrist pulse and the renying pulse are both diminished, and fail to match the proper measure at the cubit and the inch. In such a case, both yin and yang are insufficient; to supplement the yang would exhaust the yin, and to drain the yin would cause the yang to collapse. In such a case, one may treat with sweet, mild medicinals, but must not administer strong, potent decoctions, nor should one apply moxibustion. If the condition does not resolve and one goes on to drain it regardless, the qi of the five viscera will be ruined.

When the renying pulse is one degree greater than the wrist pulse, the disease is located in the foot lesser yang; if it is one degree greater and also agitated, the disease is located in the hand lesser yang. When the renying pulse is two degrees greater, the disease is located in the foot greater yang; if it is two degrees greater and also agitated, the disease is located in the hand greater yang. When the renying pulse is three degrees greater, the disease is located in the foot yangming; if it is three degrees greater and also agitated, the disease is located in the hand yangming. When the renying pulse is four degrees greater, and moreover large and rapid, this is called 'overflowing yang'; overflowing yang results in outer blockage (waige).

When the wrist pulse is one degree greater than the renying pulse, the disease is located in the foot jueyin; if it is one degree greater and also agitated, the disease is in the hand ruler of the heart (the pericardium channel). When the wrist pulse is two degrees greater, the disease is located in the foot lesser yin; if it is two degrees greater and also agitated, the disease is in the hand lesser yin. When the wrist pulse is three degrees greater, the disease is located in the foot greater yin; if it is three degrees greater and also agitated, the disease is in the hand greater yin. When the wrist pulse is four degrees greater, and moreover large and rapid, this is called 'overflowing yin.' Overflowing yin results in inner closure (neiguan); when inner closure fails to open, the case is fatal and cannot be treated. When the renying pulse and the greater yin wrist pulse are both enlarged fourfold or more, this is called 'closure and blockage' (guange). One afflicted with closure and blockage will be given but a short span of time to live.

When the renying pulse is one degree greater, drain the foot lesser yang and supplement the foot jueyin — two parts draining to one part supplementing — treating once a day. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious. When the renying pulse is two degrees greater, drain the foot greater yang and supplement the foot lesser yin — two parts draining to one part supplementing — treating once every two days. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious. When the renying pulse is three degrees greater, drain the foot yangming and supplement the foot greater yin — two parts draining to one part supplementing — treating twice a day. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious.

When the wrist pulse is one degree greater, drain the foot jueyin and supplement the foot lesser yang — two parts supplementing to one part draining — treating once a day. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious. When the wrist pulse is two degrees greater, drain the foot lesser yin and supplement the foot greater yang — two parts supplementing to one part draining — treating once every two days. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious. When the wrist pulse is three degrees greater, drain the foot greater yin and supplement the foot yangming — two parts supplementing to one part draining — treating twice a day. One must press the pulse and verify it, needling only intermittently, and stop once the qi above has become harmonious. The reason one treats twice daily in this case is that the greater yin and the yangming govern the stomach, and are exceptionally rich in grain qi; hence it is permissible to treat them twice a day.

When the renying pulse and the wrist pulse are both enlarged threefold or more, this is called 'yin and yang both overflowing.' In such a case, if the blockage is not opened, the blood vessels become closed and obstructed, the qi has nowhere to travel, and it flows and spreads excessively within, injuring the five viscera internally. If, in such a case, one goes on to apply moxibustion, the condition will change and transform into some other disease.

In the general Way of needling, one stops once the qi has been regulated. By supplementing yin and draining yang, the voice and qi become ever more vigorous, and the ears and eyes become sharp and clear. To act contrary to this causes the blood and qi to cease flowing.

What is called the arrival of qi with therapeutic effect is this: when draining, the condition becomes ever more deficient — deficient meaning that the pulse remains as large as before but is no longer firm. If the pulse remains as firm as before, then even though the patient may say he feels as he did, the disease has not yet been removed. When supplementing, the condition becomes ever more replete — replete meaning that the pulse remains as large as before but becomes ever firmer. But if the pulse remains as before without becoming firmer, then even though the patient may say he feels pleased, the disease has not yet been removed. Hence supplementing should result in repletion, and draining should result in depletion; even though the pain may not vanish the instant the needle is withdrawn, the disease is bound to wane and depart. One must first thoroughly understand the diseases that arise from each of the twelve channel vessels, and only then can one attain transmission in the doctrine of the End and the Beginning. Thus, when yin and yang do not encroach upon one another, and deficiency and excess do not overturn one another, one should select points along the corresponding channel.
