Among the general categories of needling, there is the method of three punctures to bring the grain qi (guqi — the nourishing qi derived from digested food and drink) to arrive. When deviant evil qi has wrongly commingled with the body, yin and yang exchange their proper places, contrary and compliant movements run counter to each other, and sinking and floating occupy each other's positions, so that the qi fails to correspond to the four seasons, and evil lingers and stagnates in excess — this must be removed by the needle. Thus at the first puncture, the yang evil is expelled; at the second puncture, the yin evil is expelled; at the third puncture, the grain qi arrives, and once the grain qi arrives, one should stop. What is called the arrival of grain qi is this: once supplementing has been done, the area becomes replete; once draining has been done, the area becomes deficient — and thus one knows that the grain qi has arrived. If only the evil qi departs while yin and yang have not yet become harmonized, the disease is nevertheless known to be on the way to recovery. Hence it is said: supplementing results in repletion, and draining results in depletion; even though the pain may not vanish the instant the needle is withdrawn, the disease is bound to wane and depart.

When yin is in excess and yang is deficient, first supplement the yang, and afterward drain the yin, so as to bring about harmony. When yin is deficient and yang is in excess, first supplement the yin, and afterward drain the yang, so as to bring about harmony.

Three vessels pulsate between the great toes; one must carefully discern whether they are excess or deficient. To drain a vessel that is deficient is called 'doubling the deficiency,' and doubling the deficiency makes the disease all the more severe. In needling this region, one should press it with the finger; where the pulsation is replete and rapid, drain it swiftly; where it is deficient and slow, supplement it. To act contrary to this makes the disease all the more severe. Of these pulsations, the yangming is uppermost, the jueyin is in the middle, and the lesser yin is lowest.

For disorders of the chest, select the chest transport points; for disorders of the back, select the back transport points; where the shoulder and upper back are deficient, select points in the upper region.
For 'doubled tongue' (a swelling beneath the tongue resembling a second tongue), puncture the sublingual frenulum with the pi needle (a broad, sword-like lancet used for lancing swellings).

When the hand can flex but not extend, the disease lies in the sinews; when it can extend but not flex, the disease lies in the bones. When the disease is in the bone, treat the bone; when the disease is in the sinew, treat the sinew.

In supplementing, one method calls for this: where the region is replete, needle deeply, and press the puncture wound only sparingly, so as to allow the evil qi to be expelled fully. Another method calls for this: where the region is deficient, needle shallowly, so as to nourish the vessel, and press the puncture wound promptly, so as not to let evil qi gain entry. The arrival of evil qi feels tight and rapid; the arrival of grain qi feels slow and mild. Where the pulse is replete, needle deeply, so as to drain off its qi; where the pulse is deficient, needle shallowly, so that the essence qi is not drained away, thereby nourishing the vessel while expelling only the evil qi.
In all cases of needling for pain, the pulse is invariably replete.
Hence it is said: for regions from the waist upward, the hand greater yin and yangming both govern; for regions from the waist downward, the foot greater yin and yangming both govern.

When the disease is located above, select points below; when the disease is located below, select points above; when the disease is in the head, select points in the foot; when the disease is in the lower back, select points in the popliteal fossa.

When the disease arises in the head, the head feels heavy; when it arises in the hand, the arm feels heavy; when it arises in the foot, the foot feels heavy. In treating disease, one must first needle the place from which the disease arose.

In spring, the qi resides in the body hair; in summer, the qi resides in the skin; in autumn, the qi resides in the interstices of the flesh; in winter, the qi resides in the sinews and bones. In needling disease, one should in each case take the corresponding season as the standard for depth. Thus in needling a stout, fleshy person, one uses the standard of autumn and winter; in needling a thin, lean person, one uses the standard of spring and summer.

Disease marked by pain is yin in nature; pain that does not yield when pressed with the hand is also yin, and should be needled deeply. Disease located above is yang; disease located below is yin. Itching is yang in nature, and should be needled shallowly.
When a disease first arises in yin, first treat the yin, and afterward treat the yang; when a disease first arises in yang, first treat the yang, and afterward treat the yin.

In needling heat reversal, retaining the needle will, on the contrary, produce cold; in needling cold reversal, retaining the needle will, on the contrary, produce heat. In needling heat reversal, one should needle yin twice for every once one needles yang; in needling cold reversal, one should needle yang twice for every once one needles yin. What is meant by 'twice yin' is puncturing the yin points twice; 'once yang' means puncturing the yang points once.

In chronic, long-standing disease, the evil qi has penetrated deeply. In needling such disease, insert the needle deeply and retain it for a long time, needling again every other day. One must first regulate the left and right sides, and remove any stagnant blood vessels — and thus the Way of needling is complete.

In the general method of needling, one must examine the patient's physical form and qi. If the flesh has not yet wasted away, but the qi is scant and the pulse is agitated — a case of agitated reversal — one must apply contralateral needling (miu ci, needling points on the side opposite the disease); by this means, scattered qi can be gathered in, and pent-up qi can be dispersed and spread.

The practitioner should dwell in a deep and quiet place, observing the coming and going of the patient's spirit; doors and windows should be closed, so that the ethereal and corporeal souls do not scatter. He should focus his intention and unify his spirit, attending to the movement of essence qi, and should not be disturbed by the voices of others, so as to gather in the patient's essence. He must unify his own spirit, and fix his will upon the needle.
Needle shallowly and retain the needle; needle gently and float it near the surface, so as to shift the patient's spirit — and only when the qi arrives should one stop.

In men, the qi tends inward; in women, it tends outward. Guard it firmly so that it does not escape outward; watch over it carefully so that nothing further enters — this is called 'obtaining the qi.'

The general prohibitions of needling are these: one who has just engaged in sexual intercourse should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not engage in intercourse. One who is already intoxicated should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not become intoxicated. One who has just been enraged should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not become enraged. One who has just exerted himself to exhaustion should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not exert himself to exhaustion. One who has just eaten to satiety should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not eat to satiety. One who is hungry should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not be allowed to become hungry. One who is thirsty should not be needled; one who has just been needled should not be allowed to become thirsty. One who has suffered great fright or great fear must have his qi settled before being needled. One who has just arrived by carriage should lie down and rest, and only be needled after a period equal to the time it takes to eat a meal. One who has just arrived on foot from a distant journey should sit down and rest, and only be needled after a period equal to the time it would take to walk a thousand li. In all of these twelve prohibited circumstances, the pulse is disordered and the qi is scattered, the nutritive and defensive qi run contrary to their proper course, and the qi of the channels loses its proper sequence. If one goes on to needle under such conditions, a disease in the yang will enter into the yin, and a disease in the yin will emerge as yang, so that evil qi is generated anew. If the unskilled practitioner fails to discern this, it is called 'assaulting the body' — the physical form becomes excessive and disordered, the brain and marrow are thereby depleted, the body fluids fail to transform properly, the essence of the five flavors is lost, and this is called 'losing the qi.'

When the vessel of the greater yang reaches its termination, the eyes roll upward and stare fixedly, the back arches backward, there is tugging and slackening convulsion, and the complexion turns white; the skin gives out, and then the sweat gives out — and when the sweat gives out, life comes to its end.

When the lesser yang reaches its termination, the ears become deaf, all the joints of the body go completely slack, and the connecting vessel of the eye is severed. Once the connecting vessel of the eye is severed, death follows within a day and a half. At the moment of death, the complexion turns green-blue and white, and then death occurs.

When the yangming reaches its termination, the mouth and eyes twitch and move, the patient is prone to fright and to speaking wildly, and the complexion turns yellow. When the channels above and below become full and cease to flow, life comes to its end.

When the lesser yin reaches its termination, the face turns black, the teeth appear to lengthen and become caked with grime, the abdomen becomes distended and blocked, and the upper and lower parts of the body fail to communicate — and thus life comes to its end.

When the jueyin reaches its termination, heat fills the interior and the mouth and throat become parched and dry, the patient is prone to frequent urination, and the heart is vexed; in severe cases the tongue curls and the testicles draw upward — and thus life comes to its end.

When the greater yin reaches its termination, the abdomen becomes distended and blocked, breathing becomes impossible, belching occurs, and the patient is prone to vomiting; when vomiting occurs, the qi rebels upward, and when it rebels the face turns red; when it does not rebel, the upper and lower parts of the body fail to communicate, and when the upper and lower fail to communicate, the face turns black, and the skin and body hair wither — and thus life comes to its end.

Leigong asked Huangdi, saying: "Regarding the teaching handed down in the Jin Mai ('On the Forbidden Vessels') — that in all the principles of needling, the channel vessels are the starting point: one must trace the courses along which they travel, establish their proper measurements, correlate them internally with the five viscera, and distinguish them externally from the six bowels. I wish to hear the whole of this doctrine."

Huangdi said: "When a person is first brought into being, the essence is formed first; once the essence is formed, the brain and marrow are generated. The bones serve as the trunk, the vessels as the enclosing network, the sinews as the binding strength, and the flesh as the wall. Once the skin is firm and the body hair has grown, grain enters the stomach, the pathways of the vessels become open, and qi and blood are thereby set in motion."

Leigong said: "I wish to hear in full how the channel vessels first arise." Huangdi said: "The channel vessels are that by which one determines death and life, addresses the hundred diseases, and regulates deficiency and excess — one must not fail to master them."

The Hand Taiyin Lung channel arises in the middle burner, descends to network with the large intestine, turns back to follow the opening of the stomach, ascends through the diaphragm, and belongs to the lung. From the lung system it emerges transversely to below the axilla, descends along the inner aspect of the upper arm, running in front of the Hand Shaoyin Heart channel and the Xin Zhu ("heart ruler," i.e. the Pericardium) channel, descends to the middle of the elbow, follows the inner border of the forearm along the radial bone, enters the cunkou, ascends over the thenar eminence, follows the border of the thenar eminence, and emerges at the tip of the thumb. Its branch separates from behind the wrist and runs straight out to the inner border of the index finger, emerging at its tip.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is fullness and distension of the lung, a bloated, panting cough, pain within the supraclavicular fossa, and in severe cases the two hands are crossed over the chest with a feeling of blurred, clouded vision — this is called "arm reversal." Among the diseases governed by the lung, there is coughing with qi rising, panting, thirst, vexation of the heart, fullness of the chest, pain and reversal-cold along the anterior inner border of the upper arm and forearm, and heat in the palm. When the qi is in excess, there is pain in the shoulder and back; if wind and cold bring on sweating, one is struck by wind, with frequent, scanty urination. When the qi is deficient, there is pain in the shoulder and back, cold, shortness of breath insufficient to sustain respiration, and change in the color of the urine. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if the vessel is sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse (felt at the wrist) is three-fold larger than the renying pulse (felt at the neck); in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

The Hand Yangming Large Intestine channel arises at the tip of the index finger, follows the upper border of the finger, emerges between the two bones at hegu (the web between thumb and index finger), ascends into the depression between the two tendons, follows the upper border of the forearm, enters the outer border of the elbow, ascends along the anterior outer border of the upper arm, ascends to the shoulder, emerges in front of the acromion, ascends further to the meeting point above the great pillar bone at the base of the neck, descends into the supraclavicular fossa, networks with the lung, descends through the diaphragm, and belongs to the large intestine. Its branch ascends from the supraclavicular fossa to the neck, passes through the cheek, enters the lower gums, then turns back out to flank the mouth, crosses at renzhong (the philtrum) — the left channel crossing to the right, the right to the left — and ascends to flank the nostril.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is toothache and swelling of the neck. Among the diseases governed by the body fluids, there is yellowing of the eyes, dryness of the mouth, nosebleed, throat impediment, pain in the anterior shoulder and upper arm, and pain and disuse of the index finger. When the qi is in excess, the region through which the channel passes becomes hot and swollen; when deficient, there is chill and shivering that does not recover. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is three-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Yangming Stomach channel arises where the nose meets the bridge between the eyes, joins laterally with the Taiyang channel, descends along the outer side of the nose, enters the upper gums, turns back out to flank the mouth and encircle the lips, descends to cross at chengjiang below the lower lip, then withdraws to follow the lower border behind the jaw, emerges at daying, follows jiache along the jaw, ascends in front of the ear, passes kezhuren, follows the hairline, and reaches the forehead. Its branch descends from in front of daying to renying, follows the throat, enters the supraclavicular fossa, descends through the diaphragm, belongs to the stomach, and networks with the spleen. Its direct branch descends from the supraclavicular fossa along the inner border of the breast, descends to flank the navel, and enters qichong. Another branch arises at the lower opening of the stomach, descends inside the abdomen, and reaches down to qichong, where it joins with the direct branch; from there it descends past biguan, reaches futu, descends to the kneecap, descends along the outer border of the shin, descends to the instep, and enters between the toes at the middle toe. A further branch separates three cun below the knee and descends to enter between the outer side of the middle toe. Yet another branch separates from the instep and enters between the big toe and its neighbor, emerging at its tip.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is shivering with cold as though drenched in cold water, frequent groaning, repeated yawning, a darkened forehead, and when the illness arrives the person dislikes other people and fire; on hearing the sound of wood, he is startled with fright, his heart wants to race, and he wishes only to shut the doors and windows and stay alone. In severe cases he wishes to climb to a high place and sing, to cast off his clothes and run about, with rumbling and distension in the abdomen — this is called "shin reversal." Among the diseases governed by the blood, there is mania, malaria, excess of warm-disease, sweating, nosebleed, deviation of the mouth, eruptions on the lips, swelling of the neck, throat impediment, swelling and water accumulation in the great abdomen, swollen and painful kneecaps, and pain running along the chest, breast, qichong, thigh, futu, the outer border of the shin, and the instep, with disuse of the middle toe. When the qi is in excess, the entire front of the body feels hot; if there is a surplus of qi in the stomach, this causes rapid digestion and easy hunger, with yellow urine. When the qi is deficient, the entire front of the body feels chilled and shivering, and cold in the stomach brings distension and fullness. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is three-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Taiyin Spleen channel arises at the tip of the big toe, follows the border between the red and white flesh along the inner side of the toe, passes behind the core bone, ascends along the anterior border of the inner ankle, ascends the inner aspect of the calf, follows behind the shinbone, crosses to emerge in front of the Jueyin channel, ascends the anterior inner border of the knee and thigh, enters the abdomen, belongs to the spleen, and networks with the stomach; it ascends through the diaphragm, flanks the throat, connects with the root of the tongue, and disperses beneath the tongue. Its branch again separates from the stomach, ascends through the diaphragm, and pours into the heart.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is stiffness at the root of the tongue, vomiting after eating, pain in the stomach duct, abdominal distension, frequent belching; upon defecation or the passing of gas one feels relieved as though the illness had eased, yet the whole body feels heavy. Among the diseases governed by the spleen, there is pain at the root of the tongue, inability to move the body, inability to swallow food, vexation of the heart, acute pain below the heart, sloppy diarrhea and abdominal masses, water retention, jaundice, inability to lie down, an inability to remain still though standing, swelling and reversal-cold in the inner thigh and knee, and disuse of the big toe. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse is three-fold larger than the renying; in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

The Hand Shaoyin Heart channel arises within the heart, emerges to belong to the heart system, descends through the diaphragm, and networks with the small intestine. Its branch ascends from the heart system to flank the throat and connect with the eye system. Its direct branch again ascends from the heart system to the lung, descends and emerges below the axilla, descends along the posterior inner border of the upper arm, running behind the Taiyin and Xin Zhu channels, descends inside the elbow, follows the posterior inner border of the forearm, reaches the tip of the small bone behind the palm, enters the posterior inner border of the palm, follows the inner side of the little finger, and emerges at its tip.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is dryness of the throat, pain in the heart, and thirst with a desire to drink — this is called "arm reversal." Among the diseases governed by the heart, there is yellowing of the eyes, pain in the rib-side, pain and reversal-cold along the posterior inner border of the upper arm and forearm, and heat and pain in the palm. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse is two-fold larger than the renying; in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

The Hand Taiyang Small Intestine channel arises at the tip of the little finger, follows the outer side of the hand, ascends the wrist, emerges at the bony prominence of the wrist, ascends straight along the lower border of the forearm bone, emerges between the two tendons on the inner side of the elbow, ascends along the posterior outer border of the upper arm, emerges at the shoulder joint, encircles the shoulder blade, crosses at the top of the shoulder, enters the supraclavicular fossa, networks with the heart, follows the throat, descends through the diaphragm, reaches the stomach, and belongs to the small intestine. Its branch ascends from the supraclavicular fossa along the neck to the cheek, reaches the outer canthus of the eye, and then enters the ear. Another branch separates at the cheek, ascends to zhuo (the region below the eye), reaches the nose, arrives at the inner canthus of the eye, and slants to network with the cheekbone.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is pain in the throat, swelling of the jaw, inability to turn the head to look back, a shoulder that feels as if wrenched out, and an upper arm that feels as if broken. Among the diseases governed by the body fluids, there is deafness, yellowing of the eyes, swelling of the cheek, and pain in the neck, jaw, shoulder, upper arm, elbow, and the posterior outer border of the forearm. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is two-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Taiyang Bladder channel arises at the inner canthus of the eye, ascends the forehead, and crosses at the vertex. Its branch runs from the vertex to the upper corner of the ear. Its direct branch enters from the vertex to network with the brain, then returns and emerges to descend the nape, following the inner border of the shoulder blade, flanking the spine, reaching the lower back, entering to follow the paraspinal muscles, networking with the kidney, and belonging to the bladder. Its branch descends from the lower back, flanks the spine, pierces the buttock, and enters the popliteal fossa. Another branch, from within the shoulder blades on both sides, separates and descends, pierces the scapula, flanks the inside of the spine, passes the hip joint, follows the outer side of the thigh along its posterior border, descends to join at the popliteal fossa, and from there descends to pierce the inner aspect of the calf, emerges behind the outer ankle, follows jinggu, and reaches the outer side of the little toe.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is a surging headache, eyes that feel as if bulging outward, a nape that feels as if wrenched, pain in the spine, a lower back that feels as if broken, inability to bend the hip, a popliteal fossa that feels bound up, and a calf that feels as if splitting apart — this is called "ankle reversal." Among the diseases governed by the sinews, there is hemorrhoids, malaria, mania, epilepsy-like disorders, pain at the crown of the head and nape, yellowing of the eyes, tearing, nosebleed, and pain throughout the nape, back, lower back, sacrum, popliteal fossa, calf, and foot, with disuse of the little toe. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is two-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Shaoyin Kidney channel arises beneath the little toe, runs obliquely to the sole of the foot, emerges below rangu, follows behind the inner ankle, separates to enter the heel, ascends the inner calf, emerges at the inner border of the popliteal fossa, ascends the posterior inner border of the thigh, pierces the spine, belongs to the kidney, and networks with the bladder. Its direct branch ascends from the kidney, pierces the liver and diaphragm, enters the lung, follows the throat, and flanks the root of the tongue. Its branch emerges from the lung, networks with the heart, and pours into the chest.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is hunger without any desire to eat, a face as dark and withered as lacquered wood, coughing with blood in the spittle, wheezing and panting, wanting to rise the moment one sits, dim and blurred vision as though seeing nothing, and a heart that feels suspended as if in hunger. When the qi is deficient, one is prone to fear, the heart pounds anxiously as though someone were about to seize him — this is called "bone reversal." Among the diseases governed by the kidney, there is heat in the mouth, dryness of the tongue, swelling of the throat, qi rising upward, dryness and pain of the throat, vexation of the heart, pain in the heart, jaundice, dysenteric diarrhea, pain along the posterior inner border of the spine and thigh, wilting weakness and reversal-cold, excessive drowsiness, and heat and pain in the soles of the feet. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion. When treated with moxibustion, the patient should be made to eat well so as to generate flesh, to loosen the belt and let the hair hang free, and to walk with a stout staff and heavy shoes. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse is two-fold larger than the renying; in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

The Xin Zhu, the Hand Jueyin Pericardium channel, arises within the chest, emerges to belong to the pericardium, descends through the diaphragm, and successively networks with the triple burner. Its branch follows the chest and emerges at the rib-side, descends three cun below the axilla, ascends back up to the axilla, follows the inner aspect of the upper arm, running between the Taiyin and Shaoyin channels, enters the elbow, descends the forearm, runs between the two tendons, enters the palm, follows the middle finger, and emerges at its tip. Another branch separates within the palm, follows the ring finger, and emerges at its tip.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is heat in the center of the palm, spasm and tension of the forearm and elbow, swelling of the axilla, and in severe cases fullness and distension of the chest and rib-sides, violent throbbing within the heart, redness of the face, yellowing of the eyes, and incessant laughter. Among the diseases governed by the vessels, there is vexation of the heart, pain in the heart, and heat in the palm. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse is one-fold larger than the renying; in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

The Hand Shaoyang Triple Burner channel arises at the tip of the ring finger, ascends between the two fingers, follows the back of the wrist, emerges between the two bones on the outer forearm, ascends to pierce the elbow, follows the outer upper arm, ascends to the shoulder, and crosses to emerge behind the Foot Shaoyang channel, enters the supraclavicular fossa, spreads through danzhong at the center of the chest, disperses to network with the pericardium, descends through the diaphragm, and follows down to belong to the triple burner. Its branch ascends from danzhong, emerges at the supraclavicular fossa, ascends the nape, connects behind the ear, ascends straight up to emerge at the upper corner of the ear, then bends downward to the cheek, reaching zhuo. Another branch enters the ear from behind it, emerges to run in front of the ear, passes in front of kezhuren, crosses the cheek, and reaches the outer canthus of the eye.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is deafness with a dull, muffled roaring in the ears, swelling of the throat, and throat impediment. Among the diseases governed by the qi, there is sweating, pain at the outer canthus of the eye, pain in the cheek, and pain behind the ear and in the shoulder, upper arm, elbow, and outer forearm, with disuse of the ring finger. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is one-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Shaoyang Gallbladder channel arises at the outer canthus of the eye, ascends to the corner of the head, descends behind the ear, follows the neck in front of the Hand Shaoyang channel, reaches the top of the shoulder, then crosses back out behind the Hand Shaoyang channel, and enters the supraclavicular fossa. Its branch enters the ear from behind it, emerges to run in front of the ear, and reaches behind the outer canthus of the eye. Another branch separates at the outer canthus, descends to daying, joins with the Hand Shaoyang channel, reaches below zhuo, adds on at jiache, descends the neck, joins at the supraclavicular fossa, and from there descends into the chest, pierces the diaphragm, networks with the liver, belongs to the gallbladder, follows the inside of the rib-side, emerges at qichong, encircles the margin of the pubic hair, and enters transversely into the hip joint. Its direct branch descends from the supraclavicular fossa to the axilla, follows the chest, passes the free ribs, descends to join at the hip joint, and from there descends along the outer side of the thigh, emerges at the outer border of the knee, descends in front of the fibula, continues straight down to reach juegu at the end of the fibula, descends to emerge in front of the outer ankle, follows the top of the instep, and enters between the fourth toe and the little toe. Another branch separates from the instep, enters between the big toe and its neighbor, follows the inner side of the cleft bone of the big toe, emerges at its tip, turns back to pierce the nail, and emerges at the tuft of three hairs.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is bitterness in the mouth, frequent sighing, pain in the heart and rib-side, inability to turn the body from side to side, and in severe cases the face appears as though covered with fine dust, the body lacks its usual luster, and the outer side of the foot becomes abnormally hot — this is called "yang reversal." Among the diseases governed by the bones, there is headache, pain in the jaw, pain at the outer canthus of the eye, swelling and pain within the supraclavicular fossa, swelling below the axilla, scrofulous swellings resembling a saber-clam shell strung along the neck, sweating with shivering, malaria, and pain throughout the chest, rib-side, flank, thigh, and from the outer knee down to the shin, juegu, and the region in front of the outer ankle, and pain in all the joints, with disuse of the fourth toe. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the renying pulse is one-fold larger than the cunkou; in cases of deficiency, the renying is instead smaller than the cunkou.

The Foot Jueyin Liver channel arises at the tuft of hair on the big toe, ascends along the upper border of the instep, one cun from the inner ankle, ascends eight cun above the ankle, crosses to emerge behind the Taiyin channel, ascends the inner border of the popliteal fossa, follows the inner side of the thigh, enters the pubic hair, passes around the genitals, reaches the lower abdomen, flanks the stomach, belongs to the liver, and networks with the gallbladder; it ascends to pierce the diaphragm, spreads through the rib-side, follows behind the throat, ascends into the nasopharynx, connects with the eye system, emerges at the forehead, and meets the Governing Vessel at the vertex. Its branch descends from the eye system into the cheek, and encircles the inside of the lips. Another branch again separates from the liver, pierces the diaphragm, and ascends to pour into the lung.

When this channel is disturbed, the resulting disease is lower back pain such that one cannot bend forward or backward, scrotal hernia swelling in men and swelling of the lower abdomen in women, and in severe cases dryness of the throat, a dust-covered face, and loss of complexion. Among the diseases governed by the liver, there is fullness in the chest, retching and vomiting, diarrhea with undigested food, fox hernia, incontinence of urine, and urinary blockage. For all these diseases: if excess, drain it; if deficient, supplement it; if hot, needle it swiftly; if cold, retain the needle; if sunken and collapsed, apply moxibustion; if neither excess nor deficient, take it by way of the channel itself. In cases of excess, the cunkou pulse is one-fold larger than the renying; in cases of deficiency, the cunkou is instead smaller than the renying.

When the qi of the Hand Taiyin is exhausted, the skin and body hair wither. The Taiyin is that which circulates qi to warm the skin and body hair. Hence when the qi fails to nourish, the skin and hair wither; when the skin and hair wither, the body fluids depart from the skin and joints; when the body fluids depart from the skin and joints, the nails become dry and brittle and the hair breaks off; when the hair breaks off, this means the hair dies first. Such a person will worsen on a bing day and die on a ding day, for fire overcomes metal.

When the qi of the Hand Shaoyin is exhausted, the vessels no longer flow freely; when the vessels no longer flow freely, the blood no longer circulates; when the blood no longer circulates, the hair and complexion lose their luster. Hence when the face turns black like withered lacquer wood, this means the blood dies first. Such a person will worsen on a ren day and die on a gui day, for water overcomes fire.

When the qi of the Foot Taiyin is exhausted, the vessels no longer nourish the flesh and muscles. The lips and tongue are the root of the flesh and muscles. When the vessels fail to nourish, the flesh and muscles become flaccid; when the flesh and muscles become flaccid, the tongue withers and the philtrum becomes full; when the philtrum becomes full, the lips turn outward; when the lips turn outward, this means the flesh dies first. Such a person will worsen on a jia day and die on a yi day, for wood overcomes earth.

When the qi of the Foot Shaoyin is exhausted, the bones wither. The Shaoyin is the vessel of winter; it travels hidden within and moistens the bones and marrow. Hence when the bones are not moistened, the flesh cannot adhere to them; when the bones and flesh no longer cling to one another, the flesh becomes flaccid and recedes; when the flesh becomes flaccid and recedes, the teeth appear to lengthen and grow coated with grime, and the hair loses its luster; when the hair loses its luster, this means the bone dies first. Such a person will worsen on a wu day and die on a ji day, for earth overcomes water.

When the qi of the Foot Jueyin is exhausted, the sinews give out. The Jueyin is the vessel of the liver; the liver corresponds with the sinews, and the sinews gather at the genital region, with the vessel networking at the root of the tongue. Hence when the vessel fails to nourish, the sinews become tense; when the sinews become tense, they pull upon the tongue and the genitals, so that the lips turn blue, the tongue curls up, and the genitals contract — this means the sinews die first. Such a person will worsen on a geng day and die on a xin day, for metal overcomes wood.

When the qi of all five yin channels is exhausted together, the eye system rotates; when it rotates, the eyes become disoriented and everything appears to spin — this means the will dies first; when the will dies first, death follows within a day and a half at most. When the qi of the six yang channels is exhausted, yin and yang separate from each other; when they separate, the interstices of the skin open and leak, and "severance sweat" pours out. Hence if this is observed in the morning, death will come by evening; if observed in the evening, death will come by the following morning.

The twelve channel vessels travel hidden between the layers of flesh, running deep and thus invisible. The one that is normally visible is the Foot Taiyin, where it passes above the outer ankle, for there it has no place to hide. All the vessels that float near the surface and are habitually visible are network vessels. Among the network vessels of the six channels, the great network vessels of the Hand Yangming and Hand Shaoyang arise between the five fingers and ascend to join within the elbow. In a person who has been drinking wine, the defensive qi travels first through the skin, first fills the network vessels, and the network vessels become replete first. Hence once the defensive qi has become level and calm, the nutritive qi then becomes full, and the channel vessels become greatly replete. Whenever a vessel suddenly throbs and stirs, this is invariably because evil qi has lodged within it, lingering at its root or its tip; if it does not throb, there is heat; if it is not firm, there is sinking and hollowness — and where it does not resemble the vessels around it, one can thereby know which particular vessel is stirring.

Leigong said: "How can one know the difference between the channel vessels and the network vessels?" Huangdi said: "The channel vessels are ordinarily invisible; their deficiency and excess are known through the qikou (the pulse felt at the wrist, equivalent to the cunkou). Vessels that are visible are, in every case, network vessels."

Leigong said: "This lowly disciple is still unable to grasp the reason for this." Huangdi said: "None of the network vessels are able to pass directly through the large joints; they must instead travel by cut-off pathways, emerging and entering, and then rejoin within the skin, so that their confluences are all visible on the surface. Hence, whenever one needles a network vessel, one must needle the place where it knots and where the blood is most abundant. Even where there is no knot, one should still take it up promptly, so as to drain the evil qi and let out its blood; if it is allowed to linger, it will develop into impediment (bi) disease. In examining the network vessels generally: if the color of the vessel is green-blue, there is cold and pain; if red, there is heat. When there is cold in the stomach, the network vessels at the thenar eminence of the hand are mostly green-blue; when there is heat in the stomach, the network vessel at the thenar border is red. Where it has suddenly turned black, this is impediment disease that has lingered long. Where there is a mixture of red, black, and green-blue, this indicates alternating cold and heat qi. Where it is green-blue and short, this indicates scanty qi. In all cases of needling for cold and heat disorders, when the blood-filled network vessels are numerous, one must take them up once every other day, stopping only once the blood is exhausted, and then regulate the deficiency and excess. Where the vessel is small and short, this indicates scanty qi; in severe cases, if one drains it, the patient will feel oppressed and stifled, and if the oppression is severe he will collapse and be unable to speak — when this oppression occurs, one must promptly sit the patient upright."

The connecting vessel of the Hand Taiyin is called Lieque. It arises above the wrist, in the cleft between the tendons, runs alongside the Taiyin channel, enters directly into the palm, and disperses into the thenar eminence. When this vessel is in excess, the disease is heat in the wrist and palm; when deficient, the disease is yawning and stretching, with frequent involuntary urination. It is needled one and a half cun above the wrist. It branches off to communicate with the Yangming channel.

The connecting vessel of the Hand Shaoyin is called Tongli. It lies one and a half cun above the wrist, branches off and ascends, follows the channel to enter the heart, connects with the root of the tongue, and belongs to the eye system. When in excess, the disease is a propped-up, obstructed feeling in the diaphragm; when deficient, the disease is inability to speak. It is needled one cun behind the palm. It branches off to communicate with the Taiyang channel.

The connecting vessel of the Hand Xin Zhu is called Neiguan. It lies two cun above the wrist, emerges between the two tendons, follows the channel upward, and connects with the pericardium. When the heart system is in excess, the disease is pain in the heart; when deficient, the disease is stiffness of the head. It is needled between the two tendons.

The connecting vessel of the Hand Taiyang is called Zhizheng. It lies five cun above the wrist, and pours inward into the Shaoyin channel; its branch ascends to the elbow and networks with jianyu at the shoulder. When in excess, the disease is slackness of the joints and disuse of the elbow; when deficient, the disease is warts, small ones resembling scabby, itchy skin lesions. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Hand Yangming is called Pianli. It lies three cun above the wrist, and branches off to enter the Taiyin channel; another branch ascends along the forearm, mounts jianyu, ascends to the curve of the cheek, and reaches the teeth; yet another branch enters the ear and joins with the ancestral vessels. When in excess, the disease is dental caries and deafness; when deficient, the disease is cold in the teeth and a blocked, obstructed feeling in the chest. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Hand Shaoyang is called Waiguan. It lies two cun above the wrist, encircles the outer forearm, pours into the chest, and joins with the Xin Zhu channel. When the disease is one of excess, there is spasm of the elbow; when deficient, there is inability to draw it in. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Taiyang is called Feiyang. It lies seven cun above the ankle, and branches off to communicate with the Shaoyin channel. When in excess, the disease is nasal congestion, with pain in the head and back; when deficient, the disease is nosebleed. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Shaoyang is called Guangming. It lies five cun above the ankle, branches off to communicate with the Jueyin channel, and descends to network with the instep of the foot. When in excess, the disease is reversal-cold; when deficient, the disease is wilting weakness and lameness, so that one cannot rise once seated. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Yangming is called Fenglong. It lies eight cun above the ankle, and branches off to communicate with the Taiyin channel; another branch follows the outer border of the shinbone, ascends to network with the head and nape, joins with the qi of the various channels, and descends to network with the throat. When its qi rebels, the disease is throat impediment with sudden loss of voice. When in excess, the disease is mania and epilepsy; when deficient, the disease is inability to draw up the foot, with withering of the shin. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Taiyin is called Gongsun. It lies one cun behind the base joint of the big toe, and branches off to communicate with the Yangming channel; another branch enters to network with the intestines and stomach. When reversal qi surges upward, the disease is sudden turmoil disorder, an acute affliction of vomiting and diarrhea; when in excess, the disease is cutting pain within the intestines; when deficient, the disease is drum-like distension. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Shaoyin is called Dazhong. It lies behind the ankle, encircling the heel, and branches off to communicate with the Taiyang channel; another branch runs alongside the channel, ascending to below the pericardium, and pierces outward through the lower back and spine. When its qi rebels, the disease is vexation and oppression; when in excess, the disease is urinary blockage; when deficient, the disease is lower back pain. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Foot Jueyin is called Ligou. It lies five cun above the inner ankle, and branches off to communicate with the Shaoyang channel; another branch follows the shin, ascends to the testicles, and knots at the penis. When its qi rebels, the disease is swelling of the testicles and sudden hernia; when in excess, the disease is prolonged erection; when deficient, the disease is sudden itching. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Conception Vessel is called Weiyi. It descends from jiuwei and disperses through the abdomen. When in excess, the disease is pain in the skin of the abdomen; when deficient, the disease is itching. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The connecting vessel of the Governing Vessel is called Changqiang. It flanks the paraspinal muscles, ascends the nape, disperses over the top of the head, and descends to correspond with the shoulder blades on both sides, branching off to communicate with the Taiyang channel and entering to pierce the paraspinal muscles. When in excess, the disease is stiffness of the spine; when deficient, the disease is heaviness of the head, and shaking it produces dizziness — in this case the fault lies where the vessel flanks the spine. It is needled at the point where it branches off.

The great connecting vessel of the spleen is called Dabao. It emerges three cun below yuanye and spreads through the chest and rib-side. When in excess, the disease is pain throughout the entire body; when deficient, the disease is slackness in all the joints. Whenever this vessel is engorged and stagnant with blood as though exhausted, one should needle it at the great connecting vessel of the spleen.

In all of these fifteen connecting vessels, when there is excess the vessel will necessarily become visible, and when there is deficiency it will necessarily sink and become depressed. If on inspection it is not visible, one should search for it above and below. Since people's channels are not uniform, the network vessels likewise differ from person to person in where they branch off.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard that the human being accords with the Way of Heaven: within, there are the five viscera, which correspond to the five notes, the five colors, the five seasons, the five flavors, and the five positions; without, there are the six bowels, which correspond to the six pitch-pipes. The six pitch-pipes establish the several channels of yin and yang and unite them with the twelve months, the twelve double-hours, the twelve joints, the twelve channel waters, the twelve periods of the day, and the twelve channels — this is how the five viscera and six bowels correspond to the Way of Heaven. Now the twelve channels are that by which a person is born, that by which disease is formed, that by which a person is treated, that from which disease arises, the starting point of study, and the endpoint of the practitioner's skill. They are what the unskilled find easy and what the superior physician finds difficult. May I ask about their separating and joining, their emerging and entering?' Qibo bowed his head twice and said: 'How penetrating is this question! This is what the unskilled pass over and what the superior physician dwells upon. Allow me to explain it fully.'

The primary vessel of Foot Taiyang branches off and enters the popliteal fossa; one of its branches descends five inches below the coccyx and branches off to enter the anus, connects with the bladder, and disperses into the kidney; it follows the muscles alongside the spine and, on reaching the region of the heart, enters and disperses there. Its straight-running branch ascends from the muscles alongside the spine and emerges at the nape of the neck, rejoining the Taiyang channel — this constitutes the channel of Taiyang. The primary vessel of Foot Shaoyin reaches the popliteal fossa, branches off to run to Taiyang and joins with it, ascends to the kidney, and emerges at the fourteenth vertebra to connect with the Girdling Vessel; its straight-running branch attaches to the root of the tongue, emerges again at the nape of the neck, and joins with Taiyang — this constitutes the first confluence. It is by virtue of such divergent branches of the several yin channels that they, too, are termed 'primary vessels.'

The primary vessel of Foot Shaoyang circles around the thigh and enters the pubic hairline, joining with Jueyin; its branch enters the region of the free ribs, follows the interior of the chest, connects with the gallbladder, and disperses upward into the liver; it penetrates the heart and ascends alongside the throat, emerges between the chin and jaw, disperses over the face, and connects with the eye system, joining Shaoyang again at the outer canthus. The primary vessel of Foot Jueyin branches off above the instep, ascends to the pubic hairline, and joins with Shaoyang, traveling together with its branch — this constitutes the second confluence.

The primary vessel of Foot Yangming ascends to the spleen, enters the interior of the abdomen, connects with the stomach, and disperses into the spleen; it communicates upward with the heart, ascends alongside the throat and emerges at the mouth, rises to the bridge of the nose and the region beneath the eye, and returns to connect with the eye system, joining Yangming again. The primary vessel of Foot Taiyin ascends to the thigh and joins with Yangming, traveling together with its branch; it ascends and knots at the throat, penetrating through the middle of the tongue — this constitutes the third confluence.

The primary vessel of Hand Taiyang points downward toward the earth; it branches off at the shoulder joint, enters the axilla, and runs to the heart, connecting with the small intestine. The primary vessel of Hand Shaoyin branches off and enters between the two sinews at the depths of the axilla, connects with the heart, ascends to run to the throat, emerges at the face, and joins at the inner canthus — this constitutes the fourth confluence.

The primary vessel of Hand Shaoyang points upward toward the heavens; it branches off at the vertex, enters the supraclavicular fossa, descends to run to the triple burner, and disperses within the chest. The primary vessel of Hand Jueyin, the Pericardium channel, branches off three inches below the depths of the axilla, enters the chest, branches off to connect with the triple burner, emerges to follow the throat, and comes out behind the ear, joining Shaoyang below the mastoid bone — this constitutes the fifth confluence.

The primary vessel of Hand Yangming runs from the hand along the chest and breast, branches off at the shoulder, enters at the great bone of the neck, descends to run to the large intestine, and connects with the lung; it ascends along the throat and emerges at the supraclavicular fossa, joining Yangming again. The primary vessel of Hand Taiyin branches off and enters the depths of the axilla in front of Shaoyin, enters to run to the lung, and disperses into the large intestine; it ascends and emerges at the supraclavicular fossa, follows the throat, and rejoins Yangming — this constitutes the sixth confluence.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The twelve channels correspond outwardly to the twelve channel waters — the great rivers of the land — and connect inwardly with the five viscera and six bowels. Now these twelve channel waters differ from one another in size, depth, breadth, and distance; and the five viscera and six bowels likewise differ in their height, size, and the amount of grain they receive. How do these correspondences work? The channel waters receive water and conduct it onward; the five viscera unite the spirit, qi, ethereal soul, and corporeal soul, and store them; the six bowels receive grain and conduct it onward, receive qi and disperse it abroad; the channels receive blood and nourish the body with it. How are these united in treatment? May I hear of the proper depth for needling and the number of cones for moxibustion?'

Qibo replied: 'An excellent question! Heaven is so high it cannot be measured, and Earth so vast it cannot be gauged — this is what your question touches upon. Now, human beings are born between Heaven and Earth, within the six directions; and the height of Heaven and the breadth of Earth are not things that human effort can measure and attain. But as for a person of eight spans in height, his skin and flesh are right here before us — externally they can be measured, palpated, and traced by hand, and after death the body can be dissected and examined. The firmness or fragility of the viscera, the size of the bowels, the amount of grain they hold, the length of the vessels, the clarity or turbidity of the blood, and the abundance or scarcity of qi — as well as which of the twelve channels have much blood and little qi, which have little blood and much qi, which have abundant blood and qi in both, and which have scant blood and qi in both — all of these follow fixed general measures. In treating with needle and moxa, one regulates the channel qi of each accordingly; surely there is a constant correspondence to be found here.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard this, and it pleases my ear, yet it has not settled in my mind — I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo replied: 'This is precisely how a person joins with Heaven and Earth and corresponds to yin and yang; it cannot be left unexamined. Foot Taiyang corresponds outwardly to the Qing River, and connects inwardly with the bladder, through which the waterways pass. Foot Shaoyang corresponds outwardly to the Wei River, and connects inwardly with the gallbladder. Foot Yangming corresponds outwardly to the sea, and connects inwardly with the stomach. Foot Taiyin corresponds outwardly to the Hu, or Lake, waters, and connects inwardly with the spleen. Foot Shaoyin corresponds outwardly to the Ru River, and connects inwardly with the kidney. Foot Jueyin corresponds outwardly to the Sheng River, and connects inwardly with the liver. Hand Taiyang corresponds outwardly to the Huai River, and connects inwardly with the small intestine, through which the waterways emerge. Hand Shaoyang corresponds outwardly to the Ta River, and connects inwardly with the triple burner. Hand Yangming corresponds outwardly to the Yangtze, and connects inwardly with the large intestine. Hand Taiyin corresponds outwardly to the Yellow River, and connects inwardly with the lung. Hand Shaoyin corresponds outwardly to the Ji River, and connects inwardly with the heart. Hand Jueyin, the Pericardium, corresponds outwardly to the Zhang River, and connects inwardly with the pericardium. In all these cases, the twelve channel waters of the five viscera and six bowels have their springs and sources outwardly, and their inward endowments within; the inner and outer are threaded through one another like a ring without end, and the channels of the human body are just the same. Thus Heaven is yang and Earth is yin; above the waist corresponds to Heaven, and below the waist corresponds to Earth. Thus the region north of the sea is yin, and the region north of the lake is yin within yin; the region south of the Zhang is yang, and the region north of the Yellow River up to the Zhang is yin within yang; the region south of the Ta as far as the Yangtze is the greater yang within yang. This is but one corner of the pattern of yin and yang, by which the human being joins with Heaven and Earth.'

Huangdi said: 'Now, since the channel waters correspond to the channels, and their distance, depth, and the abundance of water and blood each differ, how does one unite these principles in needling?' Qibo replied: 'Foot Yangming is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; its vessel is large, its blood abundant and its qi vigorous, and its heat is strong. In needling it, if one does not go deep the qi will not disperse, and if one does not retain the needle it will not be drained. Foot Yangming should be needled to a depth of six fen, and the needle retained for ten breaths. Foot Taiyang, to a depth of five fen, retained for seven breaths. Foot Shaoyang, to a depth of four fen, retained for five breaths. Foot Taiyin, to a depth of three fen, retained for four breaths. Foot Shaoyin, to a depth of two fen, retained for three breaths. Foot Jueyin, to a depth of one fen, retained for two breaths. As for the yin and yang channels of the hand, their pathways of receiving qi are shorter, and their qi arrives swiftly; in needling them, the depth should never exceed two fen, and the needle should never be retained beyond one breath. As for a person's youth or age, size, fatness or thinness, one must judge with careful attention — this is called following the constant pattern of Heaven, and the same holds true for moxibustion. If moxibustion exceeds these measures, harmful fire will cause the bones to wither and the vessels to become choppy; if needling exceeds these measures, the qi will be drained away.'

Huangdi said: 'Now, the size of the channels, the abundance of blood, the thickness or thinness of the skin, the firmness or fragility of the flesh, and the size of the popliteal fossa — can these be taken as fixed measures?' Qibo replied: 'What can be taken as a measure is the person of medium build — one who has not greatly wasted in flesh, and whose blood and qi have not declined. But as for a person to be measured who is emaciated and has lost his flesh, how could one apply these measurements to needling him? One must examine, palpate, trace along, feel, and press, observing whether the person is cold or warm, replete or deficient, and regulate accordingly — this is what is meant by adapting to the individual to achieve the true method.'

The sinew channel of Foot Taiyang originates at the little toe, ascends and knots at the ankle, then slants upward to knot at the knee; its lower portion follows the outer side of the foot and knots at the heel, ascends along the heel-cord and knots in the popliteal fossa. A branch knots on the outer side of the calf, ascends the inner border of the popliteal region, and together with the popliteal branch ascends to knot at the buttock, then rises alongside the spine to the nape of the neck. One offshoot branches off and enters to knot at the root of the tongue. The straight-running portion knots at the occipital bone, ascends over the head, descends over the forehead, and knots at the nose. One branch forms the upper network of the eye and descends to knot beside the cheekbone. Another branch runs from behind the axilla along its outer border to knot at the shoulder. Another branch enters below the axilla, ascends and emerges at the supraclavicular fossa, and ascends to knot at the mastoid bone. Yet another branch emerges from the supraclavicular fossa and slants upward to emerge beside the cheekbone. Its disorders include swelling and pain radiating from the little toe to the heel, spasm of the popliteal region, opisthotonos of the spine, tension of the sinews at the nape, inability to raise the shoulder, and twisting pain radiating from the axilla to the supraclavicular fossa, such that one cannot turn from side to side. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count of treatments the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of mid-spring.

The sinew channel of Foot Shaoyang originates at the fourth toe, ascends to knot at the outer ankle, then rises along the outer border of the shin to knot at the outer side of the knee. A branch arises separately from the fibula, ascends to run to the thigh; its anterior portion knots above the 'crouching hare,' and its posterior portion knots at the sacrum. The straight-running portion ascends over the flank and free ribs, rises to run along the front border of the axilla, connects with the chest and breast, and knots at the supraclavicular fossa. The straight-running branch ascends and emerges at the axilla, penetrates the supraclavicular fossa, emerges in front of the Taiyang sinew channel, follows behind the ear, ascends to the corner of the forehead, crosses at the vertex, descends to run to the jaw, and ascends to knot beside the cheekbone. A branch knots at the outer canthus of the eye, forming the 'outer maintaining' network. Its disorders include cramping that radiates from the fourth toe, cramping that draws upon the outer knee such that the knee cannot bend or extend, tension of the sinews in the popliteal region drawing forward on the thigh and backward on the sacrum, pain radiating upward over the flank and free ribs, and tension radiating up to the supraclavicular fossa, chest, breast, and neck-maintaining sinews. When the disorder passes from left to right, the right eye cannot open; because the sinew channel passes above the right corner of the forehead and travels together with the Yangqiao vessel — the yang stepping vessel — with the left connecting to the right, an injury to the left corner of the forehead causes the right foot to lose its function — this is called 'the crossing of the maintaining sinews.' Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of early spring.

The sinew channel of Foot Yangming originates at the three middle toes, knots on the instep, slants outward and upward to add onto the fibula, ascends to knot at the outer side of the knee, and rises straight up to knot at the hip joint; it then ascends along the flank and connects with the spine. The straight-running portion ascends along the shin and knots at the knee. A branch knots at the fibula and joins with Shaoyang. Another straight-running portion ascends along the 'crouching hare,' rises to knot at the thigh, gathers at the genitals, spreads upward over the abdomen, and knots at the supraclavicular fossa; it then ascends the neck, rises alongside the mouth, joins beside the cheekbone, descends to knot at the nose, and ascends to join with Taiyang. Taiyang forms the upper network of the eye, and Yangming forms the lower network of the eye. A branch runs from the cheek to knot in front of the ear. Its disorders include cramping radiating from the middle toe to the shin, hardness with jumping of the foot, cramping of the 'crouching hare,' swelling at the front of the thigh, hernial swelling, tension of the abdominal sinews drawing on the supraclavicular fossa and the cheek, and sudden deviation of the mouth. When the affected side is in spasm, the eye on that side cannot close; when there is heat, the sinews become slack and the eye cannot open. When the cheek sinews are affected by cold, they contract, drawing the cheek and shifting the mouth; when affected by heat, the sinews become loose and slack, too relaxed to draw back, and so the mouth deviates. Treatment is by applying horse fat as an ointment to the contracted side, and by anointing the slack side with white wine mixed with cinnamon; one then hooks the area with a mulberry hook, placing fresh mulberry charcoal in a pit, its height matched to the level of the patient when seated. One applies hot compresses of the ointment to the contracted cheek, and has the patient drink fine wine and eat richly roasted meat; if the patient does not drink wine, he should force himself to do so all the same. This treatment is applied with three gentle pattings, and that suffices. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of late spring.

The sinew channel of Foot Taiyin originates at the medial tip of the great toe, ascends and knots at the inner ankle. Its straight-running portion connects with the inner aspect of the knee, ascends along the medial thigh, knots at the thigh, gathers at the genitals, ascends over the abdomen and knots at the navel, follows the interior of the abdomen, knots at the ribs, and disperses within the chest; its inner portion attaches to the spine. Its disorders include pain radiating from the great toe to the inner ankle, cramping pain, pain at the inner aspect of the knee, pain radiating along the medial thigh to the hip, twisting pain in the genitals, pain radiating upward to the navel and both flanks, and pain radiating to the chest and the interior of the spine. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of early autumn.

The sinew channel of Foot Shaoyin originates beneath the little toe, runs together with the sinew channel of Foot Taiyin, slants toward the area beneath the inner ankle, and knots at the heel, joining with the sinew channel of Taiyang; it ascends to knot beneath the inner side of the knee, runs together with the Taiyin sinew channel, ascends along the medial thigh, and knots at the genitals; it then follows the interior of the spine, flanks the muscles of the back, ascends to the nape, and knots at the occipital bone, joining with the sinew channel of Foot Taiyang. Its disorders include cramping in the sole of the foot, and pain and cramping at every point where the channel knots along its course. When disease lies in this channel, it governs epilepsy, convulsions, and tetany; when the disorder is on the outside, the body cannot be drawn forward, and when on the inside, it cannot be extended backward. Thus in yang disease, the lower back becomes rigidly arched and cannot bend forward; in yin disease, the body cannot be extended backward. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. When the disorder is on the inside, one applies hot compresses, guided stretching exercises, and medicinal decoctions. When the sinews become twisted and knotted in this way, and the twisting recurs frequently and severely, it is fatal and cannot be treated. This is called the impediment of mid-autumn.

The sinew channel of Foot Jueyin originates above the great toe, ascends and knots in front of the inner ankle, ascends along the shin, ascends to knot beneath the inner side of the knee, ascends along the medial thigh, and knots at the genitals, where it connects with all the other sinew channels. Its disorders include pain radiating from the great toe to the area in front of the inner ankle, pain at the inner knee, pain and cramping along the medial thigh, and impotence of the genitals; if injured internally, the member cannot rise; if injured by cold, it retracts inward; if injured by heat, it becomes flaccid and extended, unable to draw back. Treatment lies in draining the water and clearing the yin qi; when the disorder involves cramping, treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of late autumn.

The sinew channel of Hand Taiyang originates above the little finger, knots at the wrist, ascends along the inner border of the forearm, and knots behind the bony prominence on the inner side of the elbow — striking this point produces a sensation felt above the little finger — then enters and knots below the axilla. A branch runs backward along the posterior border of the axilla, ascends and winds around the scapula, follows the neck, and emerges to run in front of the Taiyang sinew channel of the foot, knotting behind the ear at the mastoid bone. Another branch enters the ear. The straight-running portion emerges above the ear, descends to knot at the jaw, and ascends to connect with the outer canthus of the eye. Its disorders include pain radiating from the little finger to the posterior border of the bony prominence at the inner elbow, following the inner side of the forearm and entering below the axilla, with pain below the axilla, pain at the posterior border of the axilla, and pain winding around the scapula and radiating to the neck, accompanied by ringing and pain in the ear radiating to the jaw, and blurred vision that clears only after a long while. When the sinews of the neck become tense, this produces sinew fistula and swelling of the neck, with cold and heat lodged there. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. Where there is swelling, one repeats the piercing with a sharp needle. As for the original branch that ascends to the bent tooth at the angle of the jaw, follows in front of the ear, connects with the outer canthus, ascends to the jaw and knots at the corner of the forehead — pain occurring anywhere along its course produces radiating cramping. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of midsummer.

The sinew channel of Hand Shaoyang originates at the tip of the fourth finger, knots at the wrist, runs centrally along the forearm, knots at the elbow, ascends around the outer border of the upper arm, rises over the shoulder, and runs to the neck, joining with Hand Taiyang. A branch, at the bend of the jaw, enters and connects with the root of the tongue. Another branch ascends to the bent tooth, follows in front of the ear, connects with the outer canthus of the eye, rises over the jaw, and knots at the corner of the forehead. Its disorders, wherever the channel passes, produce radiating cramping and curling of the tongue. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of late summer.

The sinew channel of Hand Yangming originates at the tip of the index finger, knots at the wrist, ascends along the forearm, ascends to knot at the outer elbow, rises up the upper arm, and knots at the shoulder. A branch winds around the scapula and flanks the spine. The straight-running portion goes from the shoulder up to the neck. Another branch ascends to the cheek and knots beside the cheekbone. The straight-running portion ascends and emerges in front of Hand Taiyang, ascends to the left corner of the forehead, connects with the head, and descends to the right jaw. Its disorders, wherever the channel passes, produce radiating pain and cramping, inability to raise the shoulder, and inability to turn the neck from side to side. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of early summer.

The sinew channel of Hand Taiyin originates above the thumb, follows the digit upward, and knots behind the thenar eminence; it travels along the outer side of the radial pulse position, ascends along the forearm, knots at the elbow, ascends along the inner border of the upper arm, enters below the axilla, emerges at the supraclavicular fossa, and knots in front of the shoulder; it ascends to knot at the supraclavicular fossa, and descends to knot within the chest, dispersing to penetrate the diaphragm, and joining below the diaphragm to reach the free ribs. Its disorders, wherever the channel passes, produce radiating cramping; when severe, this becomes 'panting mass' — a mass of rebellious qi lodged in the chest — with tension of the rib-sides and vomiting of blood. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of midwinter.

The sinew channel of Hand Jueyin, the Pericardium, originates at the middle finger, runs alongside the sinew channel of Taiyin, and knots at the inner side of the elbow; it ascends the inner side of the upper arm and knots below the axilla, then descends and disperses forward and backward, flanking the rib-sides. A branch enters the axilla, disperses within the chest, and knots at the arm. Its disorders, wherever the channel passes, produce radiating cramping in front and chest pain with 'panting mass.' Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. This is called the impediment of early winter.

The sinew channel of Hand Shaoyin originates at the inner side of the little finger, knots at the bony prominence of the wrist, ascends to knot at the inner elbow, ascends further to enter the axilla, crosses with Taiyin, flanks the interior of the breast, and knots within the chest; it follows the forearm downward and connects with the navel. Its disorders include internal tension, with the heart harboring 'hidden beam' — an abdominal mass — and below this, contracture of the elbow. Its disorders, wherever the channel passes, produce radiating cramping and sinew pain. Treatment lies in the swift piercing with a heated needle, taking as the count the point at which relief is felt, and taking the site of pain as the transport point. When 'hidden beam' develops to the point of spitting blood and pus, it is fatal and cannot be treated. In disorders of the sinew channels generally: when caused by cold, the body arches backward and the sinews grow tense; when caused by heat, the sinews become slack and cannot contract, and yin atrophy renders the member useless. When the yang sinews are tense, the body arches backward; when the yin sinews are tense, the body bends forward and cannot straighten. 'Quenched needling' is used to treat cold tension; when heat causes the sinews to be slack and unable to contract, one should not use the heated needle. This is called the impediment of late winter.

When the sinew channels of Foot Yangming and Hand Taiyang become tense, the mouth and eyes become deviated; when the canthus becomes tense, one cannot suddenly see clearly. Treatment in all these cases follows the methods described above.

Huangdi asked Bogao: 'The chapter on Vessel Measurements speaks of the length of the channels — how were these established?' Bogao replied: 'One first measures the size, breadth, and length of the bones and joints, and from this the measurements of the vessels are determined.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear the standard measurements for the ordinary person. In a person seven feet five inches in height, what are the sizes and lengths of the bones and joints?' Bogao replied: 'The circumference of the great bone of the head is two feet six inches; the circumference of the chest is four feet five inches; the circumference of the waist is four feet two inches. The area covered by hair, from the crown to the nape, measures one foot two inches. From the hairline downward to the chin measures one foot — this is the final figure by which a person of good proportion is reckoned.'

From the laryngeal prominence downward to the center of the supraclavicular fossa measures four inches. From the supraclavicular fossa downward to the xiphoid process measures nine inches; if it exceeds this, the lung is large, and if it falls short, the lung is small. From the xiphoid process downward to the level of the navel measures eight inches; if it exceeds this, the stomach is large, and if it falls short, the stomach is small. From the navel downward to the pubic bone measures six and a half inches; if it exceeds this, the coiled intestine is broad and long, and if it falls short, it is narrow and short.
