The pubic bone itself measures six and a half inches. From the upper border of the pubic bone downward to the upper border of the inner condyle of the knee measures one foot eight inches. From the upper border of the inner condyle downward to its lower border measures three and a half inches. From the lower border of the inner condyle downward to the inner ankle measures one foot three inches. From the inner ankle downward to the ground measures three inches. From the popliteal crease downward to the ankle measures one foot six inches. From the ankle downward to the ground measures three inches. Thus, if the circumference of the bone is large, this exceeds the standard measure; if small, it falls short of it.

From the corner of the forehead downward to the great bone of the neck measures one foot. The portion running through the axilla that is not visible measures four inches. From the axilla downward to the free ribs measures one foot two inches. From the free ribs downward to the hip joint measures six inches. From the hip joint downward to the middle of the knee measures one foot nine inches. From the knee downward to the outer ankle measures one foot six inches. From the outer ankle downward to the prominence of the fifth metatarsal measures three inches. From that prominence downward to the ground measures one inch.

The breadth between the mastoid bones behind the ears measures nine inches. The breadth between the points in front of the ears at the ear gate measures one foot three inches. The distance between the two cheekbones measures seven inches. The breadth between the two nipples measures nine and a half inches. The breadth between the two hip bones measures six and a half inches.

The foot measures one foot two inches in length and four and a half inches in breadth. From the shoulder to the elbow measures one foot seven inches; from the elbow to the wrist measures one foot two and a half inches. From the wrist to the base joint of the middle finger measures four inches. From the base joint to its tip measures four and a half inches.

From the hairline at the nape downward to the first vertebra of the spine measures two and a half inches. From the vertebrae of the back downward to the coccyx there are twenty-one segments, measuring three feet in total; the uppermost segments each measure one and a quarter inches, with the remaining fraction lodged in the lower segments, so that the upper seven segments down to the first vertebra of the back measure nine and seven-eighths inches. These are the standard bone measurements for the ordinary person, by which the length of the channels is established. Therefore, in examining a person's channels
as they appear on the body: where they appear floating and firm, or bright and large, there is an abundance of blood; where they appear fine and deep, there is an abundance of qi.

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear about the fifty circuits — how do they proceed?' Qibo replied: 'Heaven's circuit consists of the twenty-eight lunar lodges, each lodge divided into thirty-six fen; thus a single circuit of the human qi covers one thousand and eight fen, corresponding to the sun's traversal of the twenty-eight lodges. The channels of the human body, above and below, left and right, front and back, number twenty-eight vessels, and their total circuit around the body measures sixteen zhang and two chi, corresponding to the twenty-eight lodges.'

The water clock drips down one hundred markings to divide day and night. Thus, with each exhalation of a person, the pulse beats twice and the qi travels three inches; with each inhalation, the pulse likewise beats twice and the qi travels three inches. With a complete breath — inhalation and exhalation together — the qi travels six inches. In ten breaths, the qi travels six feet, while the sun travels two fen. In two hundred and seventy breaths, the qi travels sixteen zhang and two chi, threading through and communicating within, completing one circuit of the body; the water clock drips down two markings, while the sun travels twenty-five fen. In five hundred and forty breaths, the qi completes two circuits of the body; the water clock drips down four markings, while the sun travels forty fen. In two thousand seven hundred breaths, the qi completes ten circuits of the body; the water clock drips down twenty markings, while the sun travels five lodges and twenty fen. In thirteen thousand five hundred breaths, the qi completes fifty circuits of the body; the water clock has dripped down its full one hundred markings, and the sun has traversed all twenty-eight lodges — the water clock is exhausted and the channels have completed their circuit.

What is called 'threading through and communicating' means that the qi travels together as a single reckoning. Thus when the fifty circuits are complete, one has fully lived out the allotted span granted by Heaven and Earth for that day; in all, the qi travels eight hundred and ten zhang.

Huangdi said: 'The way of nutritive qi (yingqi) is such that grain intake is its treasure. Grain enters the stomach and is thereupon transmitted to the lung; it overflows within and disperses without, and its refined and concentrated portion travels through the channel passageways, circulating constantly without cease, ending and beginning again — this is called the guiding thread of Heaven and Earth. Thus the qi emerges from the Taiyin and pours into the Hand Yangming; it ascends and pours into the Foot Yangming, descends to the instep, and pours into the cleft between the great toes, there joining with the Taiyin. It then ascends to reach the thigh, and from the spleen pours into the heart. Following the Hand Shaoyin, it emerges at the armpit, descends the arm, and pours into the little finger, joining with the Hand Taiyang. It ascends, rides over the armpit, emerges within the cheek beneath the eye, pours into the inner canthus, ascends to the vertex, descends the nape, and joins with the Foot Taiyang. It follows the spine, descends to the sacrum, descends further to pour into the tip of the little toe, follows the sole of the foot, and pours into the Foot Shaoyin. It ascends and pours into the kidney, and from the kidney pours outward into the heart, dispersing through the chest. Following the Pericardium vessel [Hand Jueyin], it emerges at the armpit, descends the arm, passes between the two sinews, enters the palm, emerges at the tip of the middle finger, then returns to pour into the tip of the ring finger, joining with the Hand Shaoyang. It ascends and pours into danzhong (the Center of the Chest), disperses through the triple burner, and from the triple burner pours into the gallbladder, emerges at the rib-side, and pours into the Foot Shaoyang. It descends to the instep, and once again from the instep pours into the cleft between the great toes, joining with the Foot Jueyin. It ascends to the liver, and from the liver rises to pour into the lung, ascends along the throat, enters the openings of the nasopharynx, and terminates at the nostril gate (xumen). Its branching offshoot ascends the forehead, follows the vertex, descends the middle of the nape, follows the spine and enters the sacrum — this is the Governing Vessel (Du Mai). It sends a network to the genitals, ascends past the pubic hair, enters the navel, rises along the interior of the abdomen, enters the Empty Basin (quepen), descends to pour into the lung, and once more emerges at the Taiyin. This is the course traveled by nutritive qi, the constant order of its forward and reverse flow.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear about the measurements of the vessels.' Qibo replied: 'The six yang channels of the hand run from the hand to the head, a length of five chi (a chi being roughly a Chinese "foot"); five sixes make three zhang (a zhang being ten chi). The six yin channels of the hand run from the hand to the chest, three chi five cun (a cun being roughly a Chinese "inch"); three sixes make one zhang eight chi, and five sixes make three chi, together two zhang one chi. The six yang channels of the foot run from the foot up to the head, eight chi; six eights make four zhang eight chi. The six yin channels of the foot run from the foot to the chest, six chi five cun; six sixes make three zhang six chi, and five sixes make three chi, together three zhang nine chi. The qiaomai (Springing Vessel) runs from the foot to the eye, seven chi five cun; two sevens make one zhang four chi, and two fives make one chi, together one zhang five chi. The Governing Vessel (Du Mai) and the Conception Vessel (Ren Mai) are each four chi five cun; two fours make eight chi, and two fives make one chi, together nine chi. In all, the total sum is sixteen zhang two chi — this is the great thoroughfare of the channel passageways through which qi travels.'

The channels lie in the interior; their branching, transverse offshoots are the network vessels; and the further branches of the network vessels are the grandchild vessels (sunluo). Where fullness and blood-stasis occur, one should swiftly eliminate them by needling; where there is excess, one should drain it; where there is deficiency, one should supplement it by administering medicinal decoctions. The five viscera constantly make themselves manifest above, at the seven orifices. Thus the lung qi communicates with the nose, and when the lung is harmonious, the nose is able to distinguish foul and fragrant odors. The heart qi communicates with the tongue, and when the heart is harmonious, the tongue is able to distinguish the five flavors. The liver qi communicates with the eyes, and when the liver is harmonious, the eyes are able to distinguish the five colors. The spleen qi communicates with the mouth, and when the spleen is harmonious, the mouth is able to distinguish the five grains. The kidney qi communicates with the ears, and when the kidney is harmonious, the ears are able to hear the five notes. When the five viscera are not harmonious, the seven orifices fail to communicate; when the six bowels are not in accord, [qi and fluids] linger and form welling-abscesses. Thus, when evil qi resides in the bowels, the yang vessels become disharmonious; when the yang vessels are disharmonious, qi lingers there, and when qi lingers there, the yang qi becomes excessive. When yang qi becomes excessively full, the yin fails to flow freely; when the yin vessels fail to flow freely, blood lingers there, and when blood lingers there, the yin qi becomes excessive. When yin qi becomes excessively full, the yang qi can no longer nourish it — this is called 'closure' (guan). When yang qi becomes excessively full, the yin qi can no longer nourish it — this is called 'blockage' (ge). When yin and yang are both excessively full and can no longer nourish each other, this is called 'closure and blockage' (guange). One who suffers closure and blockage will die before living out his allotted years.

Huangdi said: 'Where does the qiaomai arise and where does it terminate, and what qi nourishes it?' Qibo replied: 'The qiaomai is a branch of the Shaoyin; it arises behind Rangu (Blazing Valley) point. It ascends above the medial malleolus, rises directly along the inner thigh, enters the genital region, ascends along the interior of the chest, enters the Empty Basin, emerges above in front of Renying (Man's Prognosis) point, enters the cheek, attaches to the inner canthus of the eye, and joins with the Taiyang; together with the yang qiaomai it ascends, and when the qi converges and returns, it provides moisture — when the qi that nourishes the eyes fails to arrive, the eyes will not close [in sleep].'

Huangdi said: 'Why does qi travel through the five viscera alone and not nourish the six bowels?' Qibo replied: 'It is not that qi fails to travel [to the bowels] — it flows like water, like the sun and moon in their motion, without ceasing. Thus the yin vessels nourish the viscera, and the yang vessels nourish the bowels, circling like a ring without end, so that none can discern where it begins; it ends and begins again. The overflowing qi that spills forth irrigates the viscera and bowels within, and moistens the interstices of the flesh without.'

Huangdi said: 'The qiaomai has both a yin and a yang branch — which vessel should be counted in the reckoning?' Qibo said: 'In counting for a man, one counts his yang [qiaomai]; in counting for a woman, one counts her yin [qiaomai]. The one that is counted is regarded as the [primary] channel, while the one not counted is regarded as a network vessel.'

灵枢经.title_en: The Generation and Meeting of Nutritive and Defensive Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'From where does a person receive qi? Where do yin and yang meet? What qi becomes nutritive qi, and what qi becomes defensive qi? Whence is nutritive qi generated, and where does defensive qi converge? The qi of the old differs from that of the vigorous, and yin and yang occupy different positions — I wish to hear of their meeting.' Qibo replied: 'A person receives qi from grain. Grain enters the stomach and is transmitted to the lung, and the five viscera and six bowels all receive qi from it. Its clear portion becomes nutritive qi, and its turbid portion becomes defensive qi. Nutritive qi travels within the vessels, and defensive qi travels outside the vessels. Nutritive qi circulates ceaselessly, and after fifty circuits it once again meets in a great convergence; yin and yang are strung through one another like a ring without end. Defensive qi travels twenty-five circuits through the yin and twenty-five circuits through the yang, dividing into day and night; thus when the qi reaches yang it rouses one to wake, and when it reaches yin it brings one to rest. Hence it is said that at midday yang is at its height, and this is called double yang; at midnight yin is at its height, and this is called double yin. Thus the Taiyin governs the interior and the Taiyang governs the exterior, each traveling twenty-five circuits, dividing into day and night. At midnight yin is at its height; after midnight yin begins to decline, and at dawn, when yin is exhausted, yang receives the qi. At midday yang is at its height; as the sun moves westward yang declines, and at sunset, when yang is exhausted, yin receives the qi. At midnight the great convergence occurs, when the myriad people all lie asleep — this is called the closing of yin. At dawn, when yin is exhausted, yang receives the qi once more, and so it continues without cease, following the same order as Heaven and Earth.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes old people to be unable to sleep soundly at night? What qi causes the young and vigorous to be unable to sleep during the day?' Qibo replied: 'In the vigorous, qi and blood are abundant, the flesh is smooth and supple, and the pathways of qi are open and unobstructed, so that the movement of nutritive and defensive qi does not lose its normal course; hence they are alert by day and sleep soundly by night. In the aged, qi and blood have declined, the flesh has withered, and the pathways of qi have become rough and impeded; the qi of the five viscera contends within, the nutritive qi has grown scant and weak, while the defensive qi assails the interior — hence they are not alert by day and cannot sleep soundly by night.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear by what pathway nutritive and defensive qi travel, and whence they come.' Qibo replied: 'Nutritive qi emerges from the middle burner, and defensive qi emerges from the lower burner.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the triple burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The upper burner emerges from the upper opening of the stomach; it runs alongside the throat and ascends, passes through the diaphragm, and spreads through the chest; it courses to the armpit, follows the division of the Taiyin, and travels along, then returns to reach the Yangming; it ascends to the tongue and descends to the Foot Yangming. It constantly travels together with nutritive qi twenty-five circuits through yang and twenty-five circuits through yin, completing one full cycle; thus after fifty circuits it once again meets in the great convergence at the Hand Taiyin.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person has heat, and food and drink descend into the stomach before its qi has become settled, sweat will issue forth — sometimes from the face, sometimes from the back, sometimes from half the body — failing to follow the pathway of defensive qi as it emerges. Why is this?' Qibo said: 'This occurs when one is injured by wind from without, so that the interstices of the flesh open within; the body hair steams and the interstices leak, and defensive qi rushes toward the opening, and so cannot follow its proper pathway. This qi is swift, fierce, slippery, and rapid — wherever an opening appears, it escapes through it, and so cannot follow its regular course. This is called "leakage" (louxie).'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the middle burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The middle burner likewise lies alongside the stomach, emerging just after the upper burner. What it receives of qi, it separates out the waste and dregs, steams the body fluids, and transforms their refined essence, sending it upward to pour into the lung vessel, where it is transformed into blood, in order to nourish and sustain the body. Nothing is more precious than this; hence it alone is able to travel through the channel passageways, and it is called nutritive qi.'

Huangdi said: 'Now, blood and qi have different names yet belong to the same category — what does this mean?' Qibo replied: 'Nutritive and defensive qi are the essential qi; blood is the spirit qi. Hence blood and qi, though differently named, belong to the same category. Thus one who has lost blood should not be made to sweat, and one who has lost sweat should not be depleted of blood; hence in human life there are two ways to die from their loss, but no way to gain twice from preserving them.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear whence the lower burner emerges.' Qibo replied: 'The lower burner branches off at the coiled intestine, pours into the bladder, and seeps into it. Water and grain ordinarily reside together within the stomach; they form waste and dregs, which together descend into the large intestine and become the lower burner, seeping downward together. The clear fluid is filtered and separated out, and follows the lower burner to seep into the bladder.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person drinks wine, the wine also enters the stomach; yet even before the grain has been digested, the urine is passed first — why is this?' Qibo replied: 'Wine is the fluid of digested grain. Its qi is fierce and clear; hence, although it enters after the grain, its fluid is discharged before the grain's.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent. I have heard it said that the upper burner is like a mist, the middle burner is like a froth, and the lower burner is like a drainage ditch — this is precisely what is meant.'

灵枢经.title_en: The Qi of the Four Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The qi of the four seasons each takes a different form, and the hundred diseases that arise each have their own origin — in moxibustion and needling, what is the fixed rule?' Qibo replied: 'The qi of the four seasons each has its own location, and the fixed rule of moxibustion and needling is to obtain the proper qi-point (qixue, acupoint). Thus in spring one selects the channels, the blood vessels, and the spaces between the flesh divisions — in severe cases, one needles deeply; in milder cases, one needles shallowly. In summer one selects the flourishing channels and the grandchild network vessels, needling at the flesh divisions so as to sever [the pathogen] from the skin. In autumn one selects the channel transport points (jingshu); when evil qi resides in the bowels, one selects the He-Sea point. In winter one selects the Well and Spring points, and must needle deeply and retain the needle.'

For warm malaria with no sweating, apply fifty-nine punctures; for wind-water swelling with distension of the skin, apply fifty-seven punctures. Wherever blood appears in the skin, remove all of it by needling. For diarrhea with undigested food, supplement above the three yin channels of the leg, supplementing Yinlingquan (Yin Mound Spring); in all these cases retain the needle for a long time, until the heat has dispersed, and then stop.

For cramping of the sinews on the yang aspect, treat the yang; for cramping of the sinews on the yin aspect, treat the yin — in both cases needle promptly. For plain water-swelling (edema without other complication), first select the point three cun below Huangu (Ring Valley), and needle it with the pi needle (a flat, blade-like needle); having needled, insert a tube [to drain the fluid], and let it flow in and be drawn out repeatedly, so as to exhaust the swelling fluid entirely — the abdomen must become firm. If the fluid drains slowly, the patient will feel vexed and oppressed; if it drains rapidly, the patient will remain calm. Needle once every other day until the swelling fluid is exhausted, and then stop. Give the patient a fluid-restricting decoction; at the time of needling, let the patient drink only this, without eating; when eating, allow no drinking; and permit no other food — this regimen continues for one hundred and thirty-five days.

For fixed impediment (zhuobi) that does not resolve, and chronic cold that does not abate, promptly select Zusanli (Leg Three Li); when it reaches the bone, the bone becomes withered and dry. For irregularity in the bowels, select Zusanli — if replete, drain it; if deficient, supplement it. For wind-malignity (leprosy-wind, lifeng), needle directly over the swelling as a matter of course. After needling, use a sharp needle to needle the site again, pressing to expel the malign qi, and stop only when the swelling is entirely gone. Let the patient eat only the prescribed diet, and no other food.

When the abdomen constantly rumbles and qi surges up to strike the chest, causing panting so severe that one cannot stand for long, the evil qi resides in the large intestine — needle the Huang Yuan (Source point of the Vitals), Shangjuxu (Upper Great Hollow), and Zusanli. When the lower abdomen pulls upon the testicles, drawing on the lower back and spine, and surges up to strike the heart, the evil qi resides in the small intestine, connecting with the system of the testicles, attaching to the spine, penetrating the liver and lung, and linking with the system of the heart. When this qi becomes excessive, reversal occurs, surging upward to strike the intestines and stomach, steaming the liver, dispersing into the huang (the region between the heart and diaphragm), and knotting at the navel. Hence one selects Huang Yuan to disperse it, needles the Taiyin to supply it, selects the Jueyin to bring it downward, selects Xiajuxu (Lower Great Hollow) to eliminate it, and presses along the channel through which it has passed in order to regulate it.

When a person is prone to vomiting, vomits bitter fluid, sighs deeply and repeatedly, feels a fluttering unease within the heart, and fears as though someone were about to seize him — the evil qi resides in the gallbladder, and the rebellion occurs in the stomach. When gallbladder fluid leaks out, the mouth becomes bitter; when stomach qi rebels, one vomits bitter fluid — hence this is called 'vomiting gallbladder [fluid].' Select Zusanli to bring the qi downward. If stomach qi rebels, needle the blood network vessels of the Shaoyang to close off the gallbladder's rebellion, and then regulate its deficiency and excess so as to eliminate the evil qi.

When food and drink fail to descend, and the diaphragm is stopped up and impassable, the evil qi resides in the stomach duct. If it is in the upper duct, needle to press it down and cause it to descend; if it is in the lower duct, needle to disperse and remove it. When the lower abdomen aches and swells, and urination is obstructed, the evil qi resides in the triple burner; restrain it by selecting the great network vessel of the Taiyang. Observe the network vessels where they knot together with the small network vessels of the Jueyin and contain stagnant blood — if the swelling extends up to the stomach duct, select Zusanli.

To observe the complexion, examine its manner, and know whether the qi is dispersing or gathering again — one observes the color of the eyes, thereby knowing whether the disease persists or has resolved. To unify one's attention on the bodily form and listen for its stirring or stillness — one holds the Qikou (Qi Mouth) pulse and the Renying (Man's Prognosis) pulse to observe the vessels. If the pulse is firm, full, and slippery, the disease is daily advancing; if the pulse is soft, the disease is about to subside; if all the channels are replete, the disease will resolve within three days. The Qikou pulse reflects yin, and the Renying pulse reflects yang.

When evil qi resides in the lung, the disease manifests as pain in the skin, alternating cold and heat, qi rising with panting, sweating, and coughing that moves the shoulders and back. Select the outer transport points of the chest, and the points beside the third vertebra [associated with] the five viscera on the back; press quickly with the hand, and where the patient feels relief, then needle there. Also select the point within the Empty Basin (quepen) to disperse the evil qi outward.

When evil qi resides in the liver, there is pain within both rib-sides, cold in the middle, malign blood lodged within, a tendency for the joints to twitch and pull during movement, and the feet sometimes swell. Select Xingjian (Moving Between) to draw the qi down from beneath the rib-side; supplement Zusanli to warm the middle of the stomach; select the blood vessels to disperse the malign blood; and select the green-blue vessel between the ears to relieve the twitching.

When evil qi resides in the spleen and stomach, the disease manifests as pain in the flesh and muscles. If yang qi is in excess and yin qi is insufficient, there is heat in the middle and a tendency toward hunger; if yang qi is insufficient and yin qi is in excess, there is cold in the middle, intestinal rumbling, and abdominal pain; if yin and yang are both in excess, or both insufficient, there will be both cold and heat — in every case, regulate at Zusanli.

When evil qi resides in the kidney, the disease manifests as pain in the bones, and yin impediment. In yin impediment, pressing on the affected area yields nothing definite; there is abdominal distension, lower back pain, difficulty with bowel movement, pain in the shoulders, back, neck, and nape, and periodic dizziness. Select Yongquan (Bubbling Spring) and Kunlun. Wherever blood appears, remove it entirely.

When evil qi resides in the heart, the disease manifests as heart pain, a tendency toward sorrow, and periodic dizziness and collapse. Observe whether there is excess or deficiency, and regulate accordingly at its transport points.

In skin cold-heat disease, the patient cannot bear to lie against the mat, the body hair is scorched and dry, and the nose is withered and parched. If sweating does not occur, select the network vessels of the three yang, and supplement the Hand Taiyin. In flesh cold-heat disease, there is pain in the flesh, the body hair is scorched, and the lips are withered and parched. If sweating does not occur, select the three yang points in the lower body to remove the stagnant blood, and supplement the Foot Taiyin so as to induce sweating.

In bone cold-heat disease, the patient can find no comfort, and sweat pours out without cease. If the teeth have not yet become withered, select the network vessel of the Shaoyin on the inner thigh; if the teeth have already withered, the condition is fatal and cannot be treated. Bone reversal follows the same rule. In bone impediment, all the joints are unusable and painful, with pouring sweat and vexation in the heart — select the channels of the three yin and supplement them.

When the body has been injured, with much loss of blood, and is also struck by wind and cold, or when one has suffered a fall from a height, so that the four limbs become slack and feeble and cannot be controlled — this is called 'bodily languor' (tiduo). Select the point of the three-fold junction below the navel in the lower abdomen. The three-fold junction is where the Yangming and Taiyin meet, three cun below the navel, at Guanyuan (Gate of the Origin). In reversal-impediment, reversal qi rises up to reach the abdomen. Select the network vessels of yin and yang, observe which governs the disease, and drain the yang channel while supplementing the yin channel.

The pulsating vessel at the side of the neck is Renying (Man's Prognosis). Renying belongs to the Foot Yangming, and lies in front of the 'binding sinew.' Behind the binding sinew is the Hand Yangming, at the point named Futu (Support the Prominence). The next vessel is that of the Foot Shaoyang, at the point named Tianyou (Celestial Window). The next vessel is that of the Foot Taiyang, at the point named Tianzhu (Celestial Pillar). The pulsating vessel beneath the armpit belongs to the Hand Taiyin, at the point named Tianfu (Celestial Storehouse).

For yang-type headache with fullness in the chest and inability to breathe freely, select Renying. For sudden loss of voice with hardened qi in the throat, select Futu and bleed the root of the tongue. For sudden invasion causing clouded qi, with dimness of hearing and sight, select Tianyou. For sudden spasm, seizure, and dizziness, with the legs unable to support the body, select Tianzhu. For sudden impediment with internal rebellion, in which liver and lung contend with each other and blood overflows from the nose and mouth, select Tianfu. These are the five regions of the 'Celestial Window' group.

The Hand Yangming has a branch that enters the cheek and passes among the teeth, at the point named Daying (Great Welcome). For decay of the lower teeth, select this point on the arm; if the patient is averse to cold, supplement it, and if not averse to cold, drain it. The Foot Taiyang has a branch that enters the cheek and passes among the teeth, at the point named Jiaosun (Angle Vertex). For decay of the upper teeth, select the point located in front of the nose and cheek. At the time the disease is active, its pulse will be full — if full, drain it; if deficient, supplement it. One account says to select the point just outside the nose.

The Foot Yangming has a branch that flanks the nose and enters the face, at the point named Xuanlu (Suspended Skull). It connects with the mouth and, opposite, links with the root of the eye. Observe where the fault lies and select accordingly — reduce what is in excess, supplement what is deficient; where the condition is reversed, it will only increase the disorder. The Foot Taiyang has a branch that passes through the nape and enters the brain, directly connecting with the root of the eye, called the 'eye system' (yanxi). For pain and distress in the head and eyes, select the point located between the two sinews in the middle of the nape. Upon entering the brain, this channel separates into the yin qiaomai and yang qiaomai; yin and yang intersect there, with yang entering yin and yin emerging into yang, meeting at the outer canthus of the eye. When yang qi is excessive, the eyes stay wide open; when yin qi is excessive, the eyes close in sleep.

For heat reversal, select the Foot Taiyin and Foot Shaoyang, retaining the needle in both; for cold reversal, select the Foot Yangming and Foot Shaoyin at the foot, retaining the needle in both. For a slack, protruding tongue with drooling saliva and vexed oppression, select the Foot Shaoyin. For shivering with cold, chattering teeth as though struck by a chill, inability to sweat, abdominal distension, and vexed oppression, select the Hand Taiyin. When needling deficiency, needle as the qi is departing; when needling excess, needle as the qi is arriving.

In spring select the network vessels; in summer select the flesh divisions and interstices; in autumn select the Qikou (Qi Mouth); in winter select the channel transport points. In all these, the four seasons each serve as the standard for timing. The network vessels govern the skin, the flesh divisions and interstices govern the muscles, the Qikou governs the sinews and vessels, and the channel transport points govern the bones and marrow. Corresponding to the five viscera, the body has five regions: first, Futu (Crouching Rabbit); second, the calf; third, the back; fourth, the transport points of the five viscera; fifth, the nape. If a welling-abscess or flat-abscess arises in any of these five regions, it is fatal.

When disease begins in the hand or arm, first select the Hand Yangming and Hand Taiyin so as to induce sweating; when disease begins in the head, first select the Taiyang at the nape so as to induce sweating; when disease begins in the foot or shin, first select the Foot Yangming so as to induce sweating. The arm's Taiyin can be made to sweat, and the Foot Yangming can be made to sweat; thus, if selecting a yin point causes excessive sweating, stop it by needling a yang point, and if selecting a yang point causes excessive sweating, stop it by needling a yin point.

Now, the harms of needling are these: if the needle strikes its mark but is not withdrawn, the essence will leak out; if it fails to strike its mark yet is withdrawn, it will only draw evil qi to the site. When essence leaks out, the disease worsens and the patient becomes despondent; when evil qi is drawn in, it gives rise to welling-abscess and flat-abscess.

Where the canthus of the eye opens outward toward the face, this is the outer canthus (the 'sharp canthus'); where it lies to the inside, near the nose, this is the inner canthus. The upper is the outer canthus; the lower is the inner canthus.

When epilepsy (dianji) first begins to arise, the patient first feels unhappy, with heaviness and pain in the head; on looking upward the eyes appear red. When the attack reaches its extreme, and it has passed, the patient feels vexation in the heart. Examine this at the forehead. Select the Hand Taiyang, Yangming, and Taiyin, and stop once the blood [drawn by needling] changes color.

When epilepsy first breaks out, with the mouth drawn taut, crying out, panting, and palpitations, examine the Hand Yangming and Taiyang. If the left side is more rigid, treat the right; if the right side is more rigid, treat the left, and stop once the blood changes color. When epilepsy first breaks out with the body first arching backward rigidly, and consequently pain in the spine, examine the Foot Taiyang, Yangming, Taiyin, and Hand Taiyang, and stop once the blood changes color.

In treating epilepsy, one should remain constantly with the patient, observing the points that ought to be selected. When the disease attacks, observe the site where there is a fault, and drain it; place the blood [drawn] into a gourd vessel — when the next attack occurs, the blood alone will stir. If it does not stir, apply moxibustion twenty times to Qionggu (the 'utmost bone'). Qionggu is the sacrococcygeal bone.

In bone epilepsy, the jaw, the teeth's transport points, and the flesh divisions are all full and swollen, while the bones remain fixed and unmoving, with sweating, vexation and oppression, frequent vomiting of frothy phlegm, and qi leaking downward — this cannot be treated.

In sinew epilepsy, the body is fatigued, with severe contraction and spasm; needle the great channel of the nape at Dazhu (Great Shuttle). If there is frequent vomiting of frothy phlegm and qi leaking downward, this cannot be treated.

In vessel epilepsy, there is sudden collapse, and the vessels of the four limbs are all distended and slack. If the vessels are full, needle all of them to let blood; if not full, apply moxibustion at the Taiyang points flanking the nape, and at the Belt Vessel (dai mai) points three cun apart at the lower back, and at the root transport points of all the flesh divisions. If there is vomiting of frothy phlegm and qi leaking downward, this cannot be treated. In epilepsy, when the attack manifests like mania (kuang), this is fatal and cannot be treated.

When mania first arises, the patient first becomes sorrowful, forgetful, prone to anger, and prone to fear; it is contracted through worry and hunger. Treat by selecting the Hand Taiyang and Yangming, stopping once the blood changes color, and also select the Foot Taiyin and Yangming. When mania first breaks out, the patient sleeps little and does not feel hunger, considers himself lofty and worthy, considers himself eloquent and wise, considers himself honored and noble, and is given to cursing and reviling others without ceasing day or night. Treat by selecting the Hand Yangming, Taiyang, Taiyin, the point beneath the tongue, and the Shaoyin; wherever the pulse is found to be full, select it, and where it is not full, release [leave it untreated].

Manic raving, being easily startled, prone to laughter, fond of singing and merriment, and wandering aimlessly without cease — this is contracted through great fright. Treat by selecting the Hand Yangming, Taiyang, and Taiyin. Mania in which the eyes see falsely, the ears hear falsely, and the patient is prone to shouting — this arises from depleted qi. Treat by selecting the Hand Taiyang, Taiyin, Yangming, the Foot Taiyin, and the two sides of the head at the jaw.

Mania in which the patient eats excessively, is prone to seeing ghosts and spirits, and is prone to laughing without any outward provocation — this is contracted through some occasion of great joy. Treat by selecting the Foot Taiyin, Taiyang, and Yangming, then afterward selecting the Hand Taiyin, Taiyang, and Yangming. When mania is newly arisen and has not yet developed to this degree, first select the pulsating vessels to the left and right of Ququan (Spring at the Bend), and wherever full, let blood; after a short while the condition will resolve. If it does not resolve, treat according to the standard method, and apply moxibustion twenty times to the sacrococcygeal bone.

In wind reversal (fengni), there is sudden swelling of the four limbs, the body feels drenched with sweat, and the patient sighs and shivers with periodic chills; when hungry, there is vexation, and when full, the condition readily changes for the worse. Select the exterior and interior channels of the Hand Taiyin, and the pathways of the Foot Shaoyin and Yangming. If the flesh is cold, select the Spring points; if the bone is cold, select the Well and channel points.

As a disease, reversal-counterflow (jueni) causes the feet to suddenly turn cold, the chest to feel as though it were splitting apart, and the intestines to feel as though cut with a knife; the patient is vexed and cannot eat, and the pulse, whether large or small, is rough and choppy throughout. If there is warmth, select the Foot Shaoyin; if there is cold, select the Foot Yangming — where cold, supplement; where warm, drain. In reversal-counterflow with abdominal distension and fullness, intestinal rumbling, and fullness in the chest with inability to breathe freely, select the points below the chest on both rib-sides — where coughing causes the hand placed there to be moved, along with the back transport points; press with the hand, and the point that brings immediate relief is the correct one.

For internal blockage with inability to urinate, needle the Foot Shaoyin and Taiyang, together with the point reaching up toward the lower abdomen, using a long needle. When qi rebels, select the Taiyin, Yangming, and Jueyin; in severe cases, select the Shaoyin and Yangming — these being the channels where the pulse stirs.

In depleted qi, the body feels drenched with sweat, speech comes in short gasping breaths, the bones ache, the body feels heavy, and the patient is listless and unable to move — supplement the Foot Shaoyin. In shortness of breath, where the breath is short and discontinuous, and exertion exhausts the qi entirely, supplement the Foot Shaoyin, and remove [stagnant blood from] the blood network vessels.

In hemiplegia, one side of the body is disabled and painful, yet speech is unaffected and the mind remains unclouded; the disease lies between the interstices of the flesh. One should select points with the large needle, supplementing what is deficient and draining what is in excess, and recovery can then be achieved.

In the disease called fei (flaccid wind-strike), the body is without pain, yet the four limbs cannot be controlled. If the mind is not gravely disturbed and speech remains faintly intelligible, it can be treated; but if it is severe and the patient cannot speak at all, it cannot be treated. When the disease first arises in the yang and then enters the yin, one should first select the yang, and afterward the yin, needling shallowly to reach them.

When heat disease has lasted three days and the qi opening (the wrist pulse) is calm while the renying pulse (at the neck) is agitated, one should select the yang channels, applying the fifty-nine punctures to drain the heat and bring out sweat, while making the yin replete so as to supplement what is deficient. When body heat is severe and both yin and yang pulses are calm, one must not needle; where needling is permissible, one should apply it urgently — if sweat does not appear, the patient will suffer a discharging flux. The cases in which needling is forbidden are those bearing signs of impending death.

When heat disease has lasted seven or eight days and the pulse at the wrist is agitated, with panting and shortness of breath, one should needle urgently, and sweat will then appear of itself; needle shallowly between the thumb and the hand.

When heat disease has lasted seven or eight days and the pulse is faint and small, and the patient passes blood in the urine with a dry mouth, death will come within a day and a half. If the pulse is intermittent (dai), death will come within a day.
When heat disease has already produced sweating, yet the pulse remains agitated, with panting and a return of heat, one must not needle the skin; if the panting is severe, the patient will die.

When heat disease has lasted seven or eight days, if the pulse is not agitated, or if agitated it is not scattered and rapid, then within the following three days sweat should appear; if after three days there is still no sweat, death will come on the fourth day. In one who has not yet sweated, do not needle the interstices of the flesh.

When in heat disease the skin aches first, with the nose obstructed and the face swollen and full, select points at the skin level, using the first needle, applying the fifty-nine punctures. If harsh rashes appear about the nose, seek the disorder of the skin in the lung; if this fails, seek it in fire, for fire is the heart.

When in heat disease the body first feels rough and heavy, leaning and feverish, with vexation and a bowed head, and the lips are dry with excess saliva at the mouth, select points at the skin level, using the first needle, applying the fifty-nine punctures. If the skin becomes swollen, the mouth dry, and cold sweat appears, seek the disorder of the vessels in the heart; if this fails, seek it in water, for water is the kidney.

When in heat disease there is welling dryness and excessive drinking, frequent fright, and lying down unable to rise, select points at the flesh, using the sixth needle, applying the fifty-nine punctures; when the canthi of the eyes turn blue-green, seek the disorder of the flesh in the spleen; if this fails, seek it in wood, for wood is the liver.

When in heat disease the face turns blue-green, with pain in the brain and agitation of the hands and feet, select points between the sinews, using the fourth needle for the four reversals (coldness in the four limbs). When the sinews become lame and the eyes water, seek the disorder of the sinews in the liver; if this fails, seek it in metal, for metal is the lung.

When in heat disease there is frequent fright, convulsions and mania, select points at the vessels, using the fourth needle, urgently draining what is in excess; when epilepsy has caused the hair to fall out, seek the disorder of the blood in the heart; if this fails, seek it in water, for water is the kidney.

When in heat disease the body feels heavy with pain in the bones, deafness, and a tendency to keep the eyes closed, select points at the bones, using the fourth needle, applying the fifty-nine punctures to needle the bones. When the patient cannot eat, gnashes the teeth, and the ears turn blue-green, seek the disorder of the bones in the kidney; if this fails, seek it in earth, for earth is the spleen.

When in heat disease the patient does not know where the pain is, is deaf, unable to control the body, with a dry mouth, severe yang heat, yet the yin shows some degree of cold — the heat lies in the marrow, and death is inevitable; it cannot be treated.

When in heat disease there is headache, throbbing at the temples, and the eyes feel as though pulled, with pain in the vessels and frequent nosebleeds — this is a reversal-heat disease. Treat it with the third needle, observing whether there is excess or deficiency, and whether there is cold, heat, or hemorrhoids.

When in heat disease the body feels heavy and there is heat in the intestines, treat with the fourth needle at the transport points, and between the toes below, seeking the qi at the network vessel of the stomach until qi is obtained.
When in heat disease there is acute pain flanking the navel, with fullness in the chest and rib-sides, select Yongquan and Yinlingquan, using the fourth needle, and needle within the throat.

When in heat disease sweat is about to appear, and the pulse is favorable for sweating, select Yuji, Taiyuan, Dadu, and Taibai. Draining these points causes the heat to leave; supplementing them causes sweat to appear. If sweating becomes excessive, select the transverse vessel above the medial malleolus to stop it.

When heat disease has already produced sweating yet the pulse remains agitated and forceful, this is the extreme of the yin vessels, and the patient will die. If, having sweated, the pulse becomes calm, the patient will live.

In heat disease, if the pulse remains forceful and agitated and sweat cannot be obtained, this is the extreme of the yang vessels, and the patient will die. If the pulse is forceful and agitated but becomes calm once sweat is obtained, the patient will live.

There are nine conditions of heat disease in which needling is forbidden: first, when sweat does not appear, and the great cheekbones flush red with hiccupping, the patient will die; second, when there is diarrhea with severe abdominal fullness, the patient will die; third, when the eyes are dim and the heat does not abate, the patient will die; fourth, when the elderly or infants have heat with abdominal fullness, the patient will die; fifth, when sweat does not appear and there is vomiting of blood from below, the patient will die; sixth, when the root of the tongue rots and the heat does not abate, the patient will die; seventh, when there is coughing with nosebleed, and sweat does not appear, or appears but does not reach the feet, the patient will die; eighth, when the marrow is heated, the patient will die; ninth, when there is heat with tetany, the patient will die — the lower back snaps, there are convulsions, and the teeth are clenched and grinding. In all these nine conditions, needling is forbidden.

What is called the fifty-nine punctures is this: on the outer and inner sides of both hands, three points each, making twelve punctures in all. Between each of the five fingers, one point each, making eight punctures in all, and likewise on the feet. On the head, one cun within the hairline, three fen to each side, three points each, making six punctures in all. Further in, three cun within the hairline, five points to each side, making ten punctures in all. In front of and behind the ears, and below the mouth, one point each; at the nape of the neck, one point — making six punctures in all. On the crown of the head, one point; at Conghui, one point; at the hairline, one point; at Lianquan, one point; at Fengchi, two points; at Tianzhu, two points.

When qi fills the chest with panting breath, select the tip of the big toe on the Foot Taiyin channel, at a distance from the nail like the width of a scallion leaf; if there is cold, retain the needle, if there is heat, withdraw it quickly — stop once the qi descends.
For sudden pain of cardiac mounting-disorder (xin shan), select the Foot Taiyin and Jueyin channels, needling thoroughly to remove blood from the network vessels.

For throat impediment with the tongue curling, dryness of the mouth, vexation of the heart, heart pain, and pain along the inner border of the arm such that the hand cannot reach the head, select the point below the nail of the fourth finger, at a distance from the tip like the width of a chive leaf.
For redness and pain in the eye beginning from the inner canthus, select the Yinqiao (Yin Heel Vessel).
For wind-tetany with the body arched backward, first select the Foot Taiyang channel, the popliteal fossa, and the network vessels, letting blood; if there is cold within, select Zusanli.
For dribbling urinary block, select the Yinqiao, the point above the three hairs (of the great toe), and the network vessels, letting blood.

When a man's condition resembles gu-toxin disorder, or a woman's resembles ju-disorder, with the body, lower back, and spine feeling as though coming apart, and no desire for food or drink, first select Yongquan until blood appears; observe where the vessels on the instep are full and let blood from all of them.

In reversal headache, when the face seems swollen and there is vexation of the heart, select the Foot Yangming and Taiyin channels. In reversal headache with pain in the head's vessels, sorrow of the heart, and frequent weeping, if the pulsing vessels of the head are observed to be abnormally forceful, needle thoroughly to remove the blood, and afterward regulate the Foot Jueyin channel.

In reversal headache with a steady, persistent heaviness and aching of the head, drain the five rows atop the head, five points to each row; first select the Hand Shaoyin channel, then the Foot Shaoyin channel. In reversal headache with forgetfulness, where pressing fails to locate the source, select the pulsing vessels to the left and right of the head and face, then select the Foot Taiyin channel.

In reversal headache where the nape of the neck aches first, with the lower back and spine responding in kind, first select Tianzhu, then the Foot Taiyang channel. In reversal headache with severe head pain, and the vessels in front of and behind the ears surging with heat, drain them to let blood, then select the Foot Shaoyang channel.
In true headache, the pain is severe, with the entire brain aching, and coldness of the hands and feet reaching to the joints — this is fatal and cannot be treated.

Headache that should not be treated at the transport points is that caused by a blow or a fall, where malign blood remains lodged within, or where the flesh has been injured and the pain has not subsided — in such cases one may needle, but not at distant points. Headache that should not be needled at all is that of severe impediment turned malignant; if it recurs daily, needling can bring some slight improvement, but cannot cure it entirely. For headache with cold affecting one half of the head, first select the Hand Shaoyang and Yangming channels, then the Foot Shaoyang and Yangming channels.

In reversal heart pain that pulls against the back, with frequent convulsions, as though something were reaching from behind to touch the heart, and the body stooped and hunched — this is kidney heart pain; first select Jinggu and Kunlun. If mania develops and does not subside, select Rangu. In reversal heart pain with abdominal distension and chest fullness, the heart pain being especially severe — this is stomach heart pain; select Dadu and Taibai.
In reversal heart pain where the pain feels as though the heart were being pierced with an awl or needle, and the heart pain is severe — this is spleen heart pain; select Rangu and Taixi.
In reversal heart pain where the complexion is ashen, as though lifeless, and the patient cannot sigh deeply throughout the day — this is liver heart pain; select Xingjian and Taichong.

In reversal heart pain where, lying down or sitting still, the heart pain eases, but upon moving the pain grows worse, while the complexion remains unchanged — this is lung heart pain; select Yuji and Taiyuan.

In true heart pain, the hands and feet are cold to the joints, and the heart pain is severe — if it arises in the morning, death comes by evening; if it arises in the evening, death comes by morning. Heart pain that must not be needled is that in which there is a substantial accumulation within; in such a case one must not select the transport points.

When there are worm concretions or coiling roundworms within the intestines, none of these should be treated with the fine needle. When there is pain of the heart region and intestines, with gnawing pain, swelling accumulations moving up and down, pain that comes and goes, abdominal heat, a tendency to thirst, and drooling — this is caused by coiling roundworms. Press and hold the area firmly with the hands, not allowing it to shift, then needle it with the large needle, holding it in place for a long while; once the worms cease moving, withdraw the needle. As for gnawing, aching abdominal pain, this applies where the mass is located in the upper body.

For deafness with no hearing at all, select within the ear. For tinnitus, select the pulsing vessel in front of the ear. Ears that should not be needled are those with pus within, or with dry blockage — in such cases there is no hearing at all. For deafness, select the point where the nail of the fourth finger meets the flesh, first on the hand side, then on the foot side. For tinnitus, select the point on the nail of the middle finger — for the left ear select the right hand, for the right ear select the left hand — first on the hand side, then on the foot side.

When the thigh cannot be lifted, treat it by lying the patient on the side, needling at the joining of the hip socket, using the round-sharp needle — the large needle must not be used here. For disease with pouring downward of blood, select Ququan.

In wind impediment with numb, gritty sensations that cannot be cured, the feet feel as though treading on ice, at times as though immersed in scalding water; the thighs and shins feel numb and gritty; there is vexation of the heart and headache, alternating with vomiting and oppression; after dizziness, sweating appears, and in time this leads to blurred vision, sorrow alternating with fear and fright, shortness of breath, and lack of joy — death will occur within three years.

When illness comes first and reversal follows, treat the root; when reversal comes first and illness follows, treat the root; when cold comes first and illness follows, treat the root; when illness comes first and cold follows, treat the root; when heat comes first and illness follows, treat the root.

When diarrhea comes first and other illness follows, treat the root — one must first regulate this, and only then treat the other illness. When illness comes first and fullness in the middle follows, treat the branch. When illness comes first and diarrhea follows, treat the root. When fullness in the middle comes first and vexation of the heart follows, treat the root.

There is guest qi (an intruding pathogen), and there is qi of the same kind (arising from the body's own disorder). When urination and defecation are unfavorable, treat the branch; when urination and defecation are favorable, treat the root.

When disease arises and is one of excess, proceed from root to branch — treat the root first, then the branch. When disease arises and is one of deficiency, proceed from branch to root — treat the branch first, then the root. One must carefully and thoroughly observe whether the condition is mild or severe, and regulate it with judgment: in mild cases, root and branch may be treated together; in severe cases, treat one alone. When urination and defecation are first unfavorable and other illness follows afterward, treat the root.

When reversal causes pain flanking the spine, reaching to the crown of the head, with a heavy, sinking sensation in the head, dimness of vision, and stiffness of the lower back and spine, select the network vessels in the popliteal fossa on the Foot Taiyang channel, letting blood.

When reversal causes fullness of the chest and swelling of the face, with the lips moist and drooping, sudden difficulty speaking, and in severe cases inability to speak at all, select the Foot Yangming channel.
When reversal qi rushes to the throat causing inability to speak, with cold hands and feet and difficult defecation, select the Foot Shaoyin channel.
When reversal causes rumbling fullness of the abdomen, with abundant cold qi, gurgling sounds within the abdomen, and difficulty in urination and defecation, select the Foot Taiyin channel.
For dryness of the throat, with the mouth hot as though filled with glue, select the Foot Shaoyin channel.
For pain within the knee, select Dubi, using the round-sharp needle, releasing it and treating at intervals. The needle should be as thick as a coarse hair — needle the knee without hesitation.
For throat impediment with inability to speak, select the Foot Yangming channel; if speech is still possible, select the Hand Yangming channel.
For malarial disorder without thirst, occurring every other day, select the Foot Yangming channel; if there is thirst and it occurs daily, select the Hand Yangming channel.
For toothache without aversion to cold drinks, select the Foot Yangming channel; with aversion to cold drinks, select the Hand Yangming channel.
For deafness without pain, select the Foot Shaoyang channel; for deafness with pain, select the Hand Yangming channel.

For nosebleed that will not stop, with blood flowing freely, select the Foot Taiyang channel; for lesser nosebleed, select the Hand Taiyang channel. If it does not stop, needle below the wrist bone (Wangu); if it still does not stop, needle the popliteal fossa to let blood.

For lower back pain, if there is cold above the site of pain, select the Foot Taiyang and Yangming channels; if there is heat above the site of pain, select the Foot Jueyin channel. If the patient cannot bend forward or backward, select the Foot Shaoyang channel. If there is heat within with panting, select the network vessels in the popliteal fossa on the Foot Shaoyin channel.
For irritability with no desire to eat, and speech becoming ever fainter, needle the Foot Taiyin channel; for anger with excessive talking, needle the Foot Shaoyang channel.
For pain of the cheek and jaw, needle the Hand Yangming channel and the forceful vessel of the cheek to let blood.
For pain of the nape that prevents bending forward or backward, needle the Foot Taiyang channel; if the patient cannot turn to look back, needle the Hand Taiyang channel.

For fullness and distension of the lower abdomen, rushing upward to the stomach and reaching the heart, with a shivering sensation and periodic chills and fever, and difficulty urinating, select the Foot Jueyin channel.
For abdominal fullness with difficult defecation, distension of the abdomen that likewise rushes upward to the chest and throat, with wheezing, rasping breath, select the Foot Shaoyin channel.
For abdominal fullness with undigested food, rumbling fullness of the abdomen, and inability to defecate, select the Foot Taiyin channel.
For heart pain pulling toward the lower back and spine, with a desire to vomit, select the Foot Shaoyin channel.
For heart pain with abdominal distension, a wall-like tautness within, and difficult defecation, select the Foot Taiyin channel.
For heart pain pulling toward the back so that the patient cannot breathe freely, needle the Foot Shaoyin channel; if it does not resolve, select the Hand Shaoyang channel.
For heart pain pulling toward a full lower abdomen, with the site shifting without fixed location above or below, and difficulty in urination and defecation, needle the Foot Jueyin channel.
For heart pain with only shortness of breath, insufficient to sustain respiration, needle the Hand Taiyin channel.

For heart pain, needle at the ninth vertebra, then press; once needled and pressed, it will resolve at once. If it does not resolve, search above and below for the point — once found, it will resolve at once.

For pain of the cheek and jaw, needle the pulsing vessel at the curve of the jaw on the Foot Yangming channel until blood appears, and it will resolve at once. If it does not resolve, press Renying along the channel, and it will resolve at once.
For qi that rebels upward, needle the depression in the center of the chest, and the pulsing vessel below the chest.

For abdominal pain, needle the pulsing vessels to the left and right of the navel; once needled, press, and it will resolve at once. If it does not resolve, needle Qijie; once needled, press, and it will resolve at once.

For wilting and reversal causing binding oppression of the four extremities, one should promptly loosen this binding, twice daily; where there is numbness, improvement will come within ten days if treatment continues without interruption, until the disease has ceased.

Alternatively, use a straw to tickle the nose so as to induce sneezing; sneezing will bring resolution. If breath fails to stir, one should quickly draw and guide it, and this will resolve it at once; a great fright may also resolve it.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'In encircling impediment (zhoubi) as it occurs in the body, it moves up and down, shifting and traveling along the vessels, so that its manifestations above and below, left and right, correspond to one another without the least gap between them. I wish to hear whether this pain lies within the blood vessels, or whether it lies between the interstices of the flesh, and how it comes to be so. When the pain shifts, there is not even time to insert the needle; by the time the aching pain is felt, there is not even time to settle on a treatment before the pain has already ceased. What is the principle that causes this? I wish to hear the reason.' Qibo replied: 'This is manifold impediment (zhongbi), not encircling impediment.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear about manifold impediment.' Qibo replied: 'This condition occurs each time in its own particular location, arising and ceasing in turn, settling and stirring in turn — the right responding to the left, the left responding to the right, but it is not able to encircle the whole body; it merely arises and rests in turn.' Huangdi said: 'Good. How should it be needled?' Qibo replied: 'In needling this condition, even though the pain has already ceased, one must still needle the affected site, so as not to let it arise again.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. I wish to hear what encircling impediment is like.' Qibo replied: 'Encircling impediment lies within the blood vessels, following the vessels upward and following the vessels downward; it cannot move to left or right, but remains confined to its own place along the vessel.' Huangdi said: 'How should it be needled?' Qibo replied: 'When the pain moves from above downward, first needle below to intercept it, then needle above to release it. When the pain moves from below upward, first needle above to intercept it, then needle below to release it.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. Where does this pain arise, and for what reason does it bear this name?' Qibo replied: 'Wind, cold, and damp qi lodge externally between the interstices of the flesh, where they are forced together and form froth. When this froth encounters cold, it congeals, and once congealed it pushes the interstices of the flesh apart and splits them; this splitting causes pain, and pain draws the spirit to that place. Once the spirit arrives there, heat is generated; once heat arises, the pain is dispersed; once the pain is dispersed, reversal occurs; once reversal occurs, another impediment breaks out elsewhere — and it develops in just this way.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I have now grasped its meaning. This condition does not reside within the viscera, nor has it yet broken out upon the skin externally; it dwells alone between the interstices of the flesh, so that the true qi cannot circulate all around — hence it is called encircling impediment. Therefore, in needling impediment disorders, one must first press along and follow the six channels below the affected region, observing whether they are deficient or excess, and whether the great network vessels have blood stagnation that fails to flow freely, or are so deficient that the vessel sinks and becomes hollow — and regulate accordingly, applying heat therapy to open the passage. Where there is convulsive tautness and contraction, guide it along its course.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I have now grasped both its meaning and its practice. This encompasses the principle governing the nine varieties, and the disorders of yin and yang among the twelve channels.'

Huangdi, at leisure, dismissed his attendants and asked Qibo: 'I have already heard the classic of the nine needles, discussed the following and opposing of yin and yang, and completed the study of the six channels. I now wish to receive the oral instructions.' Qibo left his mat, bowed twice, and said: 'How excellent is this question! These are teachings handed down orally by the former masters.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear these oral teachings.' Qibo replied: 'Now, the hundred diseases all first arise from wind, rain, cold, and summer-heat; from yin and yang, joy and anger; from food and drink, and dwelling; and from great fright and sudden fear. These cause the blood and qi to separate, yin and yang to be broken and ruined, the channels and network vessels to be cut off, the pathways of the vessels to become obstructed, yin and yang to rebel against each other, the defensive qi to stagnate and linger, the channel vessels to become empty and hollow, the blood and qi to lose their proper sequence — and thus their normal state is lost. Since these matters are not addressed within the classics, allow me to explain their method.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person yawns, what qi causes this?' Qibo replied: 'The defensive qi travels through the yang by day, and at midnight it travels through the yin. Yin governs the night, and night is for lying down to rest; yang governs the upper body, and yin governs the lower body. Hence yin qi accumulates below while yang qi has not yet been exhausted; the yang draws upward while the yin draws downward, and as yin and yang draw against each other, frequent yawning results. When yang qi is exhausted and yin qi is abundant, the eyes close in sleep; when yin qi is exhausted and yang qi is abundant, one awakens. Drain the Foot Shaoyin channel and supplement the Foot Taiyang channel.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person hiccups, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Grain enters the stomach, and the stomach qi rises to pour into the lung. Now, if old cold qi and newly ingested grain qi both return together into the stomach, the new and the old throw each other into disorder, true qi and evil qi attack one another, the qi converges and rebels, and bursts back out from the stomach — hence hiccupping results. Supplement the Hand Taiyin channel and drain the Foot Shaoyin channel.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person sobs with a catch in the breath (xi), what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'This is when yin qi is abundant and yang qi is deficient, yin qi is swift and yang qi is sluggish, yin qi is abundant while yang qi is nearly cut off — hence this sobbing catch of breath results. Supplement the Foot Taiyang channel and drain the Foot Shaoyin channel.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person shivers with cold, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Cold qi lodges in the skin; yin qi is abundant and yang qi is deficient — hence shivering with cold and chills results. Supplement all the yang channels.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person belches, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Cold qi lodges in the stomach; reversed qi rises from below and disperses upward, then bursts back out from the stomach — hence belching results. Supplement the Foot Taiyin and Yangming channels.' Some say: supplement the root of the eyebrow.

Huangdi said: 'When a person sneezes, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'When the yang qi is harmonious and smooth, filling the heart and issuing out through the nose, sneezing results. Supplement the Foot Taiyang channel and the point at the root of the eyebrow.' Some say: above the eyebrow.

Huangdi said: 'When a person's limbs hang limp and listless (duo), what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'When the stomach is not replete, all the vessels become deficient; when all the vessels are deficient, the sinews and vessels become slack and languid; when the sinews and vessels become slack and languid, and one exerts oneself in intercourse, the qi cannot recover — hence limpness results. Supplement between the interstices of the flesh, according to the location affected.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person weeps from sorrow, with tears and mucus flowing, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'The heart is the ruler of the five viscera and six bowels; the eyes are where the ancestral vessels converge, and the pathway by which fluid rises; the mouth and nose are the gateways of qi. Hence when sorrow, grief, worry, and anxiety stir the heart, the heart's stirring causes the five viscera and six bowels all to shake; their shaking affects the ancestral vessels, and when the ancestral vessels are affected, the pathway of fluid opens; when the pathway of fluid opens, tears and mucus flow out. Fluid is that which irrigates the essence and moistens the orifices of the body; hence when the upper pathway of fluid opens, weeping occurs, and if the weeping does not stop, the fluid becomes exhausted; when the fluid is exhausted, the essence can no longer be irrigated, and when the essence cannot be irrigated, the eyes lose their sight — hence this is called the loss of essence. Supplement Tianzhu and the channel flanking the neck.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person sighs deeply, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Worry and anxious thought cause the ties of the heart to become taut; when the ties of the heart become taut, the pathway of qi becomes constricted; when constricted, it becomes unfavorable, and so one sighs deeply to release and expel it. Supplement the Hand Shaoyin channel, the Pericardium (xin zhu), and the Foot Shaoyang channel, retaining the needle.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person drools, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Food and drink all enter the stomach; when there is heat in the stomach, the worms are stirred, and when the worms are stirred, the stomach becomes slack; when the stomach becomes slack, Lianquan opens, and so drooling results. Supplement the Foot Shaoyin channel.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person has ringing in the ears, what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'The ear is where the ancestral vessels converge; hence when the stomach is empty, the ancestral vessels become deficient, and when deficient they sink downward, so that the vessels are somehow depleted — hence ringing in the ears results. Supplement Kezhuren, and the point where the nail of the thumb meets the flesh.'

Huangdi said: 'When a person bites their own tongue, what qi causes this?' This is when reversal qi rushes upward and the qi of several channels arrives together. When Shaoyin qi arrives, the tongue is bitten; when Shaoyang qi arrives, the cheek is bitten; when Yangming qi arrives, the lip is bitten. Observe which channel governs the disease, and supplement it accordingly.

In all these twelve evils, they are extraordinary evils that travel to the body's orifices. Wherever such evil qi is located, there is always a deficiency. Thus, when qi in the upper body is deficient, the brain fails to be filled, the ears suffer from ringing, the head suffers from tilting, and the eyes suffer from dizziness. When qi in the middle of the body is deficient, urination and defecation become abnormal, and the intestines suffer from rumbling. When qi in the lower body is deficient, wilting, reversal, and oppression of the heart result. Supplement below the outer ankle and retain the needle.

Huangdi said: 'How should these be treated?' Qibo said: 'The kidney governs yawning; select the Foot Shaoyin channel. The lung governs hiccupping; select the Hand Taiyin and Foot Shaoyin channels. In sobbing with a catch of breath, yin and yang are cut off from each other; hence supplement the Foot Taiyang channel and drain the Foot Shaoyin channel. In shivering with cold, supplement all the yang channels. In belching, supplement the Foot Taiyin and Yangming channels. In sneezing, supplement the Foot Taiyang channel at the root of the eyebrow. In limpness, according to the location affected, supplement between the interstices of the flesh. In weeping, supplement Tianzhu and the channel flanking the neck — flanking the neck being the dividing line at the back of the head. In deep sighing, supplement the Hand Shaoyin channel, the Pericardium, and the Foot Shaoyang channel, retaining the needle. In drooling, supplement the Foot Shaoyin channel. In ringing of the ears, supplement Kezhuren, and the point where the nail of the thumb meets the flesh. In biting one's own tongue, observe which channel governs the disease and supplement it accordingly. For dizziness of the eyes and tilting of the head, supplement below the outer ankle and retain the needle. For wilting, reversal, and oppression of the heart, needle two cun above the point between the big toes, retaining the needle' — some say: retain it below the outer ankle.

灵枢经.title_en: Teachings Handed Down by the Master
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I have heard that the former masters kept certain things in their hearts that were never set down in prescriptions. I wish to hear them, store them in mind, and pattern my conduct upon them — using them above to govern the people, and below to govern my own body — so that the common people might be free of illness, high and low might live in harmony and affection, virtue and beneficence might flow down to them, their descendants might be free of care, and this might be handed down to later generations without end. May I hear of this?' Qibo said: 'How far-reaching a question this is! Governing the people and governing oneself, governing others and governing this body, governing the small and governing the great, governing a state and governing a household — in none of these can one succeed by going against what is proper; only by following it can one succeed. And "following" does not refer solely to yin and yang and the channels, to discoursing on the compliance or rebellion of qi — the common people, too, all wish to have their own intentions followed.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one follow this?' Qibo said: 'On entering a state, ask about its customs; on entering a household, ask about its taboos; on ascending the hall, ask about its rites; on approaching a patient, ask what brings them ease.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one bring ease to a patient?' Qibo said: 'When there is heat in the middle causing wasting-thirst disorder, coolness brings ease; in conditions of cold in the middle, warmth brings ease. When there is heat in the stomach, it consumes food quickly, causing the person to feel an emptiness at the heart and to be constantly hungry. When the skin above the navel is hot and there is heat in the intestines, the stool comes out yellow and like gruel. When the skin below the navel is cold and there is cold in the stomach, the abdomen becomes distended; when there is cold in the intestines, there is rumbling in the intestines and diarrhea with undigested food. When there is cold in the stomach and heat in the intestines, there is both distension and diarrhea; when there is heat in the stomach and cold in the intestines, one drinks rapidly and there is pain and distension in the lower abdomen.'

Huangdi said: 'When the stomach desires a cold drink but the intestines desire a hot drink, the two are in mutual opposition — how then does one bring ease? Moreover, princes and nobles and great men, rulers who feast on meat, are arrogant and self-indulgent, following their desires and slighting others, so that no one is able to restrain them; to restrain them would go against their will, yet to comply with them would aggravate their illness. How then does one bring them ease? And in treating them, what should come first?' Qibo said: 'It is the nature of people that none fail to hate death and love life. Tell them of the ruin that awaits them, speak to them of the benefit that is available, guide them toward what brings them ease, and open their understanding to what causes them suffering — even one who is unreasonable, how could such a person fail to listen?'
Huangdi said: 'How, then, does one treat them?' Qibo said: 'In spring and summer, treat the branch first and the root afterward; in autumn and winter, treat the root first and the branch afterward.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one bring ease when the two desires are in mutual opposition?' Qibo said: 'To bring ease in such cases, one's food and drink and clothing should also be suited to cold and warmth — in cold weather one should not be so chilled as to shiver, and in hot weather one should not sweat excessively. As for food and drink, that which is hot should not be scorchingly hot, and that which is cold should not be icy cold. When cold and warmth are moderated and suitable, the qi will thereby be sustained, and evil and deviant influences will not be brought about.'

Huangdi said: 'The chapter on the Root Viscera uses the bodily form, the limbs and joints, and the muscles to observe the size of the five viscera and six bowels. But now, in the case of princes and nobles and great men, rulers who attend court and hold the throne, when they are questioned, who would be permitted to touch and examine them before giving an answer?' Qibo said: 'The bodily form and the limbs and joints are the outer covering of the viscera and bowels — this is not the same as inspection of the face.'

Huangdi said: 'I already know how the qi of the five viscera may be inspected in the face — but how does one come to know and inspect them by means of the limbs and joints?' Qibo said: 'Among the five viscera and six bowels, the lung serves as their canopy; a broad, prominent shoulder and a sunken throat are signs by which its condition can be observed externally.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Qibo said: 'Among the five viscera and six bowels, the heart serves as their ruler; the supraclavicular fossa is its pathway, and a well-developed breastbone allows one to observe the condition of the xiphoid process.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'
Qibo said: 'The liver serves as the general among the viscera, sending its influence to be observed externally; to know its firmness, observe the size of the eyes.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'
