Qibo said: 'The spleen serves as a guard, receiving grain and provisions; observe the condition of the lips and tongue, whether favorable or unfavorable, to know whether the outlook is fortunate or unfortunate.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Qibo said: 'The kidney governs the exterior, extending hearing into the distance; observe the condition of the ears, whether favorable or unfavorable, to know its nature.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I wish to hear of the signs of the six bowels.'

Qibo said: 'Among the six bowels, the stomach serves as their sea; a broad frame, a thick neck, and an expansive chest allow the five grains to be well contained. A long nasal passage indicates the condition of the large intestine. Thick lips and a long philtrum indicate the condition of the small intestine. Full pouches beneath the eyes indicate that the gallbladder is vigorous. Nostrils that flare outward indicate that the bladder leaks and fails to hold urine. A prominent ridge at the center of the nasal bridge indicates that the triple burner is well restrained. These are the signs by which the six bowels are observed. When the upper, middle, and lower thirds of the body are all in proportion, the viscera are at peace and in good condition.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that the human being possesses essence, qi, fluid, humor, blood, and vessels. In my own thinking I had supposed these to be but a single qi; yet now they are distinguished into six separate names, and I do not understand the reason for this.' Qibo said: 'When the two spirits contend and unite to form the body, that which is always generated prior to the body itself is called essence. What is called qi?' Qibo said: 'When the upper burner opens and disperses, spreading abroad the flavors of the five grains, warming the skin, filling the body, and moistening the body hair — like the irrigation of mist and dew — this is called qi. What is called fluid?' Qibo said: 'When the interstices release and open, so that sweat pours out profusely, this is called jin — the thin, readily-flowing fluid that vents outward as sweat. What is called humor?' Qibo said: 'When grain enters and the qi becomes full, and a rich moistness pours into the bones, so that the joints can flex and extend, this moistness supplementing and benefiting the brain and marrow so that the skin becomes lustrous, this is called ye — the thicker, denser humor. What is called blood?' Qibo said: 'When the middle burner receives qi and, taking up the fluid, transforms it and turns it red, this is called blood. What is called the vessel?' Qibo said: 'That which contains and restrains the nutritive qi, so that it has nowhere to escape, this is called the vessel.'

Huangdi said: 'Among these six qi there is surplus and insufficiency, abundance and scarcity of qi, deficiency and excess of the brain and marrow, and clarity and turbidity of the blood vessels — how does one come to know these?' Qibo said: 'When essence is lost, there is deafness. When qi is lost, the eyes become dim. When fluid is lost, the interstices open and sweat pours out excessively. When humor is lost, the joints lose their ease of flexion and extension, the complexion becomes withered, the brain and marrow waste away, the lower legs become impeded, and the ears ring repeatedly. When blood is lost, the complexion turns pale, withered and without luster, and the pulse becomes hollow and empty — these are the signs of loss.'

Huangdi said: 'As for these six qi, which is more precious and which is less so?' Qibo said: 'Each of the six qi has its own region and governance, and their relative precedence, whether favorable or unfavorable, may each be taken as a constant standard; yet it is the five grains together with the stomach that serve as their great sea.'

Huangdi asked Bogao: 'I wish to hear, among the six bowels that transmit grain, of the size and length of the intestines and stomach, and how much grain they are able to receive.' Bogao said: 'Allow me to describe this fully — the measurements of depth, distance, and length by which grain passes in and out. From the lips to the teeth is nine fen in length, and the mouth is two cun and a half in breadth. From behind the teeth to the epiglottis is three cun and a half in depth, with a capacity of five ge. The tongue weighs ten liang, is seven cun in length and two cun and a half in breadth. The throat-gate weighs ten liang and is one cun and a half in breadth. From there to the stomach is one chi and six cun in length; the stomach itself is coiled and bent, and when straightened out measures two chi and six cun in length, one chi and five cun in breadth, with a diameter of five cun, and a capacity of three dou and five sheng. The small intestine lies behind, attached to the spine, coiling to the left and folding back upon itself in layered turns; where it empties into the large intestine, it is attached externally above the navel. It winds through sixteen coils in all, two cun and a half in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than eight fen, and a length of three zhang and three chi. The large intestine coils to the left of the navel, winding round and folding downward in layered turns, circling back through sixteen coils, four cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than one cun, and a length of two zhang and one chi. The rectum runs along the spine, receiving what comes from the large intestine, coiling to the left and folding above and below the spine, eight cun across, with a diameter of a little more than two cun, and a length of two chi and eight cun. From the point where the intestines and stomach receive food to the point where it is expelled, the total length is six zhang, four chi and four fen, winding through thirty-two coils in all.'

灵枢经.title_en: On the Ordinary Person Going Without Food
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear why it is that when a person does not eat, death follows within seven days.' Bogao said: 'Allow your servant to explain the reason.'

The stomach is one chi and five cun in breadth, with a diameter of five cun and a length of two chi and six cun; coiled crosswise, it holds three dou and five sheng of water and grain. Of this, the grain that ordinarily remains within amounts to two dou, and the water one dou and five sheng, when full. The upper burner releases qi, sending forth the essential and subtle portion, swift and smooth and rapid in its movement, while the lower burner irrigates the intestines below.

The small intestine is two cun and a half in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than eight fen, and a length of three zhang and two chi; it holds two dou and four sheng of grain, and a little more than six sheng and three ge of water.
The large intestine is four cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than one cun, and a length of two zhang and one chi; it holds one dou of grain and seven sheng and a half of water.
The rectum is eight cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little more than two cun, and a length of two chi and eight cun; it holds nine sheng, three ge, and an eighth of a ge of grain.

Altogether, the length of the intestines and stomach is five zhang, eight chi, and four cun, and together they hold nine dou, two sheng, and a little more than one ge of water and grain — this is the total measure of water and grain that the intestines and stomach can receive. In the healthy person, however, matters do not remain fixed in this way: when the stomach is full the intestines are empty, and when the intestines are full the stomach is empty; emptiness and fullness alternate, and so the qi is able to move up and down, the five viscera remain settled and at peace, the blood and vessels are harmonious and free-flowing, and the essence and spirit thereby find their dwelling place. Hence spirit is nothing other than the essential qi of water and grain. Thus, within the intestines and stomach, there ordinarily remains two dou of grain and one dou and five sheng of water. Hence the healthy person has two bowel movements each day, each releasing two and a half sheng, five sheng in the course of a day; over seven days, five times seven gives three dou and five sheng, and the retained water and grain would then be entirely exhausted. This is why, when a healthy person goes without food or drink for seven days, death follows — because the essential qi and fluid and humor derived from grain have all become exhausted.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard the methods of needling from you, Master, and what you have spoken of has never strayed from nutritive qi, defensive qi, blood, and qi. Now the twelve channel vessels are internally connected to the bowels and viscera, and externally netted throughout the limbs and joints — do you, Master, also correlate these with the four seas?' Qibo replied: 'The human being likewise has four seas, corresponding to the twelve channel-rivers. The channel-rivers all flow into the sea, and the sea has its eastern, western, southern, and northern portions, which are called the four seas.' Huangdi said: 'How does the human being correspond to this?' Qibo said: 'The human being has a Sea of Marrow, a Sea of Blood, a Sea of Qi, and a Sea of Water and Grain — these four correspond to the four seas.'

Huangdi said: 'How far-reaching this is, Master, that you correlate the human being with the four seas of Heaven and Earth! I wish to hear how this correspondence works.' Qibo replied: 'One must first clearly understand the location of yin and yang, exterior and interior, and the ying and shu transport points; only then can the four seas be determined.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one determine them?' Qibo said: 'The stomach is the Sea of Water and Grain; its transport points lie above at Qichong and below at Zusanli. The Penetrating Vessel is the sea of the twelve channels; its transport points lie above at Dazhu and emerge below at the upper and lower borders of Juxu. The chest center is the Sea of Qi; its transport points lie above and below the pillar-bone, and in front, at Renying. The brain is the Sea of Marrow; its transport point lies above at its crown, and below at Fengfu.'

Huangdi said: 'As for these four seas, what benefits them and what harms them? What allows them to flourish and what causes them to fail?' Qibo said: 'To obtain accord brings flourishing; to fall into opposition brings failure. To know how to regulate them is beneficial; not to know how to regulate them is harmful.'

Huangdi said: 'What are the signs of accord and opposition in the four seas?' Qibo said: 'When the Sea of Qi is in surplus, the qi fills the chest, breathing is oppressed, and the face is flushed red; when the Sea of Qi is insufficient, the qi is scant and insufficient even for speech. When the Sea of Blood is in surplus, one constantly feels as though one's body were enlarged, oppressed and vexed without knowing where the illness lies; when the Sea of Blood is insufficient, one likewise constantly feels as though one's body were diminished, constricted, without knowing where the illness lies. When the Sea of Water and Grain is in surplus, the abdomen is full; when the Sea of Water and Grain is insufficient, one is hungry yet cannot take in food. When the Sea of Marrow is in surplus, the body is light and vigorous with abundant strength, exceeding its usual measure; when the Sea of Marrow is insufficient, the brain spins and the ears ring, the lower legs ache and the vision swims, the eyes see nothing, and one becomes languid and inclined to lie down at ease.'

Huangdi said: 'I have now heard of accord and opposition — how does one regulate them?' Qibo said: 'Carefully attend to their transport points, and regulate their deficiency and excess, taking care not to commit any harm; when accord is restored, recovery follows, but when opposition persists, failure is certain.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Huangdi said: 'The twelve channel vessels are distinguished according to the five phases and divided according to the four seasons — what causes their loss and disorder, and what allows them to be well ordered?' Qibo said: 'The five phases have their sequence, and the four seasons have their divisions; when these accord with one another, order results, and when they oppose one another, disorder results.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by according with one another?' Qibo said: 'The twelve channel vessels correspond to the twelve months. The twelve months are divided into the four seasons. The four seasons are spring, autumn, winter, and summer, each with its own distinct qi. When nutritive qi and defensive qi follow each other properly, when yin and yang are already understood, and when clear and turbid do not interfere with one another — when this is so, all is in accord, and order results.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by opposition and disorder?' Qibo said: 'When clear qi lodges in yin and turbid qi lodges in yang, when the nutritive qi follows the vessels properly but the defensive qi moves in reverse, then clear and turbid interfere with one another, throwing the chest into disorder — this is called great oppression. Thus when qi is disordered in the heart, there is vexation of the heart, a silent inwardness, and a hanging of the head in quiet stillness; when disordered in the lung, there is labored breathing with the head bowed and raised, panting and gasping,

clutching at the hands with each exhalation; when disordered in the intestines and stomach, this is sudden turmoil disorder; when disordered in the arms and legs, this brings about reversal-cold of the four limbs; when disordered in the head, this brings about reversal-ascent, with heaviness of the head, dizziness, and collapse.

Huangdi said: 'In needling these five disorders, is there a proper method?' Qibo said: 'There is a proper way by which the disorder is brought on, and a proper way by which it is dispelled; to examine and understand this way is called the treasure of the body.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I wish to hear this way.' Qibo said: 'When the qi disorder is in the heart, select the shu-transport points of the Hand Shaoyin Heart channel and of the Heart's ruling minister, the Pericardium channel; when the qi disorder is in the lung, select the ying-spring point of the Hand Taiyin Lung channel and the shu-transport point of the Foot Shaoyin Kidney channel; when the qi disorder is in the intestines and stomach, select points of the Foot Taiyin Spleen and Yangming Stomach channels, and if this does not relieve it, select Zusanli; when the qi disorder is in the head, select Tianzhu and Dazhu, and if this does not resolve it, select the ying-spring and shu-transport points of the Foot Taiyang Bladder channel; when the qi disorder is in the arms and legs, first remove stagnant blood from the vessels, and afterward select the ying-spring and shu-transport points of the Yangming and Shaoyang channels.'

Huangdi said: 'How is supplementation and drainage carried out in this case?' Qibo said: 'To insert the needle slowly and withdraw it slowly is called guiding the qi. Supplementation and drainage that leave no visible trace are called uniting with the essence. For this condition is not one of surplus or insufficiency, but of disordered qi rebelling against itself.' Huangdi said: 'How true this way is, how clear this discourse! I ask that it be inscribed upon jade tablets, and let it be called "Governing Disorder."'

Huangdi said: 'How does distension manifest in the pulse at the inch opening?' Qibo said: 'When the pulse is large, firm, and rough, this indicates distension.' Huangdi said: 'How does one know whether the distension is of the viscera or of the bowels?' Qibo said: 'Yin corresponds to the viscera, and yang corresponds to the bowels.'

Huangdi said: 'When qi causes a person to become distended, does this occur within the blood vessels, or within the viscera and bowels?' Qibo said: 'All three are involved, yet none of these is the true dwelling place of distension.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the dwelling place of distension.' Qibo said: 'Distension always occurs outside the viscera and bowels; it pushes the viscera and bowels aside, encases the chest and rib-sides, and distends the skin — hence it is called distension.'

Huangdi said: 'The viscera and bowels lie within the chest, rib-sides, and abdomen, stored away like precious vessels within a case or chest; each has its own place and dwelling, and though they bear different names they occupy the same region, and within this one region their qi each differ — I wish to hear the reason for this.' Huangdi said: 'I have not yet grasped the meaning of this; let me ask again.' Qibo said: 'The chest and abdomen form the outer wall of the viscera and bowels. The chest center is the palace-city of the pericardium, the heart's ruling minister; the stomach is the great granary; the throat and the small intestine serve as the conduits of transmission; the five orifices of the stomach are the gates of the villages and wards; and Lianquan and Yuying are the pathways of fluid and humor. Thus the five viscera and six bowels each have their own borders and boundaries, and their diseases each take their own distinct forms. When the nutritive qi follows the vessels properly, but the defensive qi moves in reverse, this produces distension of the vessels; when the defensive qi merges with the vessels and moves along the interstices, this produces distension of the skin. Draining at Zusanli: for cases nearby, one draining suffices; for cases more distant, three drainings are needed. Without inquiring whether the condition is deficient or excess, the skilled physician's task lies in swift drainage.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear the manifestations of distension.' Qibo said: 'In heart distension, there is vexation of the heart, shortness of breath, and restless sleep. In lung distension, there is an empty fullness together with wheezing and coughing. In liver distension, there is fullness beneath the rib-sides with pain drawing into the lower abdomen. In spleen distension, there is a tendency toward foul eructation, the four limbs feel vexed and heavy, the body feels so heavy that one cannot bear the weight of one's own clothing, and sleep is restless. In kidney distension, there is fullness of the abdomen drawing into the back, a feeling of oppressive discomfort, and pain in the lower back and hips. Among the distensions of the six bowels: in stomach distension, there is fullness of the abdomen, pain in the epigastrium, a scorched odor perceived at the nose, interference with eating, and difficulty in defecation. In large intestine distension, there is rumbling and gurgling pain in the intestines, and if additionally struck by cold in winter, this brings diarrhea with undigested food. In small intestine distension, there is a cautious, guarded distension of the lower abdomen, with pain drawing into the lower back. In bladder distension, there is fullness of the lower abdomen together with painful urinary blockage. In triple burner distension, the qi fills the skin, giving a sensation of lightness and puffiness without firmness. In gallbladder distension, there is pain and distension beneath the rib-sides, bitterness in the mouth, and a tendency to frequent sighing.'

In all these various forms of distension, the underlying principle is one and the same: one must clearly understand accord and opposition, and not err in the number of needlings. To drain what is deficient and supplement what is already replete drives the spirit from its dwelling, brings about evil and the loss of what is proper, and leaves the true qi unable to settle — this is the failure of the unskilled practitioner, and when it occurs, it is attributed to fate. To supplement what is deficient and drain what is replete allows the spirit to return to its dwelling, and by long sealing up the void, this is called the mark of the skilled physician.

Huangdi said: 'How does distension arise? From what cause does it come about?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi, in its ordinary course through the body, runs alongside the vessels and travels through the interstitial flesh, its movement having its proper accord and opposition; when yin and yang follow each other properly, harmony with Heaven is attained, the five viscera renew themselves in turn, the four seasons proceed in their proper sequence, and the five grains are thereby transformed. But when reversal qi settles below, the nutritive and defensive qi become lodged and stagnant, cold qi rebels upward, the true qi and the evil qi assail one another, and these two qi contend together — this is what combines to form distension.' Huangdi said: 'Good. But how may one resolve remaining doubt in this matter?' Qibo said: 'By verifying it against the true condition — when the three aspects converge, the diagnosis is confirmed.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The Discourse on Distension states that, without inquiring whether the condition is deficient or excess, the physician's task lies in swift drainage — for cases nearby, one draining suffices, and for cases more distant, three drainings are needed. But now there are cases in which three drainings fail to relieve the distension — where lies the fault?' Qibo replied: 'This refers to needling that has sunk into the fatty membrane yet has struck the qi-point precisely. If the needle does not strike the qi-point precisely, the qi becomes internally sealed off; if the needle does not sink into the membrane, the qi does not move; and if it passes above and strikes only the flesh, the defensive qi becomes thrown into disorder, and yin and yang chase after one another erratically. In the case of distension, when drainage is called for but is not properly carried out, the qi therefore fails to descend; if three drainings fail to relieve it, one must certainly change the approach — once the qi descends, the treatment should stop, and if it has not yet descended, one begins again. In this way complete success can be assured — how then could there be failure? In treating distension, one must carefully examine its full extent, draining when drainage is called for and supplementing when supplementation is called for, responding as a drum responds to the drumstick — how then could there be a case that fails to yield?'

灵枢经.title_en: Urinary Blockage and the Five-Fold Differentiation of Fluid and Humor
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'When water and grain enter the mouth and are transported to the intestines and stomach, their fluid separates into five kinds. When the weather is cold and one's clothing is thin, it becomes urine and qi; when the weather is hot and one's clothing is heavy, it becomes sweat; when grief and sorrow cause the qi to converge, it becomes tears; when there is heat in the middle and the stomach becomes slack, it becomes saliva. When evil qi rebels internally, the qi becomes blocked and fails to move, and when it fails to move, this becomes water-swelling. I understand that this is so, but I do not know from what it arises — I wish to hear the explanation.'

Qibo said: 'Water and grain both enter through the mouth; their flavors are five in number, and each flows into its own sea. Fluid and humor each travel along their own pathway. Thus the triple burner releases qi, warming the muscles and flesh and filling out the skin — this is jin, the fluid; that which flows but does not circulate freely is ye, the humor.'
When the weather is hot and one's clothing is heavy, the interstices open and so sweat is released; if cold lingers between the interstitial flesh, it gathers into froth and this produces pain.
When the weather is cold, the interstices close, and the qi and dampness fail to circulate; the water then descends and lodges in the bladder, becoming urine and qi.

Among the five viscera and six bowels, the heart serves as their ruler, the ears as their hearing, the eyes as their watch, the lung as their minister, the liver as their general, the spleen as their guard, and the kidney as the governor of the exterior. Thus the fluid and humor of the five viscera and six bowels all seep upward into the eyes. When the heart is stricken with grief and the qi converges, the heart's connecting vessel becomes tense; when the heart's connecting vessel becomes tense, the lung is drawn upward; when the lung is drawn upward, the humor overflows upward. Now the heart's connecting vessel and the lung cannot remain constantly drawn upward — they rise and fall by turns — and so one coughs and tears flow forth.

When there is heat in the middle, the stomach digests food rapidly; when food is digested rapidly, parasites are stirred and move up and down. The intestines and stomach become distended and full, and so the stomach becomes slack; when the stomach becomes slack, the qi rebels upward, and so saliva is expelled.

The fluid and humor of the five grains, blended and harmonized into a rich, oily essence, seeps internally into the hollows of the bones, supplementing and benefiting the brain and marrow, and flows downward into the inner thighs.

When yin and yang lose their harmony, this causes the humor to overflow and pour downward into the genital region; the marrow and humor both diminish and descend, and when this descent is excessive, deficiency results — hence there is pain in the lower back and back, and aching in the lower legs.

When the pathways of yin and yang qi fail to communicate, the four seas become blocked and closed, the triple burner fails to drain properly, and fluid and humor fail to be transformed; water and grain then travel together through the intestines and stomach, separate out at the large intestine, and lodge in the lower burner. If they cannot seep into the bladder, the lower burner becomes distended, and when the water overflows, this becomes water-swelling. This is the accord and opposition in the five-fold differentiation of fluid and humor.

灵枢经.title_en: The Five Inspections and Five Offices
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard that in needling there are five offices and five inspections, by which the five qi are observed. The five qi are the messengers of the five viscera, the deputies of the five seasons. I wish to hear from where these five messengers ought to be observed.' Qibo said: 'The five offices are the means by which the five viscera may be inspected.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear where they emerge, so that this may serve as a constant standard.' Qibo said: 'The pulse emerges at the qi opening, and the complexion appears at the bright hall, the bridge of the nose; the five colors emerge in turn, each corresponding to its own season, each according to its constant pattern. When the channel qi enters the viscera in disorder, it must certainly be treated and set in order.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. Are the five colors determined by the bright hall alone?' Qibo said: 'When the five offices are already clearly discernible, the glabella and forehead must be broad and expansive, and only then is the bright hall well established. When the bright hall is broad and expansive, when the flanking screens of the cheeks can be seen from without, when the walls are squared and the foundation high, and the drooping structures extend outward properly, then the five colors will be well ordered — level, full, and broad — and such a person may live to a hundred years. When one sees such signs, needling will certainly succeed, for a person of this kind has abundant blood and qi, and firm, dense flesh and muscle, and so can withstand vigorous needling.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the five offices.' Qibo said: 'The nose is the office of the lung; the eyes are the office of the liver; the mouth and lips are the office of the spleen; the tongue is the office of the heart; the ears are the office of the kidney.'

Huangdi said: 'What do these offices allow one to observe?' Qibo said: 'They allow one to observe the five viscera. Thus when the lung is diseased, there is labored breathing with flaring of the nostrils; when the liver is diseased, the corners of the eyes turn green-blue; when the spleen is diseased, the lips turn yellow; when the heart is diseased, the tongue curls and shortens, and the cheekbones turn red; when the kidney is diseased, the cheekbones and forehead turn black.'

Huangdi said: 'From where do the five pulses emerge, and where do the five colors appear, and what is it like when even the ordinary complexion is precarious?' Qibo said: 'When the five offices cannot be clearly discerned, when the glabella and forehead are not broad, when the bright hall is small, when the flanking screens cannot be seen, when moreover the walls are low, the foundation beneath the walls absent, and the drooping corners fall away outward — in such a case, even in ordinary health the person is precarious, how much more so when illness is added.'

Huangdi said: 'When the five colors appear at the bright hall, allowing one to observe the qi of the five viscera, do their positions — left and right, high and low — each have their own distinct form?' Qibo said: 'The viscera and bowels, as they are arranged within the body, each occupy their own place in sequence; their positions left and right, above and below, on the face each correspond to their proper measure.'

灵枢经.title_en: Compliant and Contrary Flow in the Fat and the Thin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I have received instruction from you, Master, in the way of the needle, and its many aspects have all been made clear to me. Yet the way you apply it seems as effortless as if something were simply released, and there appears to be nothing fixed or rigid about it. Is this mastery something you gained through long study and practice, or does it arise in your mind through careful observation of things?" Qibo said: "Sages, in practicing the Way, accord above with Heaven, accord below with Earth, and accord in the middle with human affairs. There must always be a clear method by which measures and numbers are established, and models and standards by which things are checked and verified, before the teaching can be transmitted to others. Thus a craftsman cannot set aside the foot-rule and simply guess at length, nor cast off the ink-line and expect to strike a level. Nor can an artisan dispense with the compass and still draw a circle, or discard the square and still form a right angle. To know how to apply these instruments is simply to make use of the natural properties of things — it is a teaching easily put into practice, the constant principle of compliant and contrary flow."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear what is meant by according with the natural properties of things." Qibo said: "If one stands over a deep channel and breaches it, the water will drain away of itself without the need for great effort. If one follows an already-cut, obstructed watercourse and breaches its blockage, the channel can be made to flow through freely. This is a way of speaking about the smoothness or roughness of qi, and the clarity or turbidity of blood and fluids, and about the compliant or contrary directions in which they travel."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear whether people who are fair or dark, fat or thin, short or tall, each have their own proper measure for needling." Qibo said: "When a person is in the prime of life, robust and large, with blood and qi abundant and full, and skin and flesh firm and solid, if evil qi then invades, one should needle deeply and retain the needle — such is the fat person. Consider one with broad shoulders and neck, thin flesh but thick skin, and a dark complexion, with heavy, drooping lips — such a person's blood is dark and turbid, and the qi is rough and slow-moving. In temperament, such a person tends to be greedy in taking and giving. In needling this type, one should needle deeply, retain the needle, and considerably increase the count."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle a thin person?" Qibo said: "The thin person has thin skin and a pale complexion, with lean, spare flesh, thin lips, and a light manner of speech. Such a person's blood is clear and the qi flows smoothly; qi is easily lost and blood is easily damaged. In needling this type, one should needle shallowly and withdraw the needle quickly."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle an ordinary person?" Qibo said: "One observes whether the complexion is fair or dark and regulates accordingly for each. In one who is upright and of generous, substantial build, the blood and qi are harmonious and well regulated; in needling this type, one need only follow the ordinary measure without deviation."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle a robust man of sturdy bone?" Qibo said: "In needling a robust man of sturdy bone, with firm flesh and supple joints, of imposing bearing — if such a person is of a heavy, ponderous constitution, the qi will be rough and the blood turbid; in needling this type, one should needle deeply and retain the needle, considerably increasing the count. If such a person is of a vigorous, forceful constitution, the qi will flow smoothly and the blood will be clear; in needling this type, one should needle shallowly and withdraw the needle quickly."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle an infant?" Qibo said: "In the infant, the flesh is tender and delicate, the blood scant and the qi weak. In needling this type, one should use the fine hair-needle, insert it shallowly, and withdraw it quickly; this may be done twice a day."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by standing over a deep channel and breaching it?" Qibo said: "When the blood is clear and the qi turbid, if one drains it rapidly, the qi will become exhausted." Huangdi said: "What is meant by following an obstructed watercourse and breaching its blockage?" Qibo said: "When the blood is turbid and the qi is rough, if one drains it rapidly, the channel can be made to flow through freely."

Huangdi said: "What is the compliant and contrary course along which the vessels travel?" Qibo said: "The three yin channels of the hand travel from the viscera to the hand; the three yang channels of the hand travel from the hand to the head; the three yang channels of the foot travel from the head to the foot; and the three yin channels of the foot travel from the foot to the abdomen."

Huangdi said: "Why is it that the Shaoyin vessel alone travels downward?" Qibo said: "It is not so. The Penetrating Vessel is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels, and the five viscera and six bowels all draw upon it. Its upper branch emerges at the pharyngeal cavity behind the nose, where it seeps into all the yang channels and irrigates all the essence. Its lower branch pours into the great network vessel of the Shaoyin, emerges at Qichong (the Qi Thoroughfare), travels along the inner border of the yin aspect of the thigh, enters the popliteal fossa, runs hidden along the inside of the shin bone, and descends to a point behind the inner ankle, where it attaches and then divides. One descending branch joins with the Shaoyin channel and seeps into the three yin channels; another branch running in front travels hidden and emerges to attach at the instep, descends along the instep, and enters between the great toe, seeping into all the network vessels and warming the flesh and muscles. Hence, when the branch network vessel becomes bound and obstructed, the instep will not move; when it does not move, this is reversal, and when there is reversal, there will be cold." Huangdi said: "How can this be verified?" Qibo said: "One guides the patient with words, palpates and tests accordingly; if the pulsation at that point is not present as it should be, then the compliant and contrary course of the flow may still be discerned." Huangdi said: "How profound indeed is the sages' practice of the Way! Bright as the sun and moon, discerning as fine as the tip of a hair — were it not for you, Master, who could expound it?"

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on the Blood Network Vessels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the strange evils that do not reside within the channels." Qibo said: "These are found in the blood network vessels."

Huangdi said: "When one needles a blood network vessel and the patient collapses, what does this mean? When the blood spurts out in a jet, what does this mean? When the blood is scant, dark, and turbid, what does this mean? When the blood that flows out is clear and half of it is like a watery fluid, what does this mean? When swelling occurs after the needle is withdrawn, what does this mean? When the blood that flows out is sometimes copious, sometimes scant, and the complexion turns pale and ashen, what does this mean? When the complexion does not change after the needle is withdrawn but the patient feels vexed and oppressed, what does this mean? When much blood is let yet the patient shows no distress at all, what does this mean? I wish to hear the reasons for these."
Qibo said: "When the vessel qi is exuberant but the blood is deficient, needling will cause the qi to collapse and be lost; when qi collapses, the patient falls."

When blood and qi are both exuberant and yin qi predominates, the blood flows smoothly, and needling causes it to spurt out. When yang qi has accumulated and stagnated for a long time without being drained, the blood is dark and turbid, and therefore cannot spurt out.

When one has just drunk fluids and they have seeped into the network vessels without yet blending with the blood, the blood that flows out will be accompanied by a separate watery fluid. In one who has not recently drunk fluids, if there is water lodged within the body, in time this will become swelling.
When yin qi has accumulated within yang and this qi has settled into the network vessels, then upon needling, before the blood emerges the qi moves out first, and so swelling results.

When the qi of yin and yang have only just come together and have not yet fully harmonized, if one then drains them, both yin and yang collapse together, exterior and interior become separated from each other, and so the complexion is drained of color and turns pale and ashen.

When needling causes much blood to flow out, yet the complexion does not change though the patient feels vexed and oppressed, this is because the network vessel has been needled while the channel itself has been left deficient; when the deficient channel belongs to yin, yin then collapses, and so the patient feels vexed and oppressed.

When yin and yang come together and combine to form impediment (bi), this is because pathogenic qi overflows the channels within and pours into the network vessels without. In such a case, both yin and yang have a surplus, and so even though much blood is let out, this cannot cause deficiency.

Huangdi said: "How does one assess this?" Qibo said: "The blood vessels to be needled are exuberant, firm, distended, and red, running crosswise without a fixed location above or below; the smaller ones are like a needle, the larger ones like a sinew. To needle and drain them according to this observation is entirely safe, and one will not fail to hit the proper measure. If one fails to observe the proper measure and acts contrary to it, the consequences will each correspond to their own degree of error."

Huangdi said: "Why does the flesh sometimes cling to the needle once it has been inserted?" Qibo said: "When heat qi gathers around the needle, the needle becomes hot; once hot, the flesh clings to the needle internally, and so it becomes firmly fixed."

灵枢经.title_en: Yin and Yang, the Clear and the Turbid
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard that the twelve channel vessels correspond to the twelve channel-waters of the land, each of which differs in its five colors and its clarity or turbidity. Yet in a person the blood and qi appear to be uniform — how, then, do they correspond to this diversity?" Qibo said: "If the blood and qi of a person were truly uniform throughout, then all under Heaven would be as one — how, then, could there be any disorder?" Huangdi said: "I am asking about a single person, not about the multitudes of all under Heaven." Qibo said: "Even within a single person there is disordered qi, just as within the pattern of all under Heaven there are disordered people — they correspond to one and the same principle."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the clear and turbid aspects of human qi." Qibo said: "That which receives grain is turbid; that which receives breath-qi is clear. The clear pours into yin, and the turbid pours into yang. Of the turbid, that portion which is clear rises to emerge at the throat; of the clear, that portion which is turbid descends and flows downward. When clear and turbid interfere with one another, this is called disordered qi."

Huangdi said: "Now, yin is clear and yang is turbid, yet within the turbid there is a clear portion, and within the clear there is a turbid portion. How does one distinguish the clear from the turbid?" Qibo said: "In the great division of qi, the clear rises to pour into the lung, and the turbid descends to flow into the stomach. The clear qi of the stomach rises to emerge at the mouth; the turbid qi of the lung descends to pour into the channels and accumulates internally in the sea of qi."

Huangdi said: "All the yang channels are turbid — which yang channel is most turbid?" Qibo said: "The Hand Taiyang Small Intestine channel alone receives the turbid portion of yang, while the Hand Taiyin Lung channel alone receives the clear portion of yin. The clear portion rises to travel to the body's open orifices, while the turbid portion descends to travel through the various channels. All the yin channels are clear, yet the Foot Taiyin Spleen channel alone receives a turbid portion."

Huangdi said: "How does one treat this?" Qibo said: "The qi of the clear is smooth-flowing, while the qi of the turbid is rough — this is the constant nature of qi. Thus, in needling yin, one needles deeply and retains the needle; in needling yang, one needles shallowly and withdraws quickly. Where clear and turbid interfere with one another, one regulates according to their proper measure."

灵枢经.title_en: Yin and Yang Correlated with the Sun and Moon
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard that Heaven is yang and Earth is yin, that the sun is yang and the moon is yin. How do these correspond to the human body?" Qibo said: "Above the waist corresponds to Heaven, and below the waist corresponds to Earth; thus Heaven is yang and Earth is yin. Accordingly, the twelve channel vessels of the feet correspond to the twelve months. The moon is generated from water, and so that which lies below is yin. The ten fingers of the hands correspond to the ten suns — the ten heavenly stems used to mark the days; the sun governs fire, and so that which lies above is yang."

Huangdi said: "How does this correspond to the vessels?" Qibo said: "Yin, the third earthly branch, is the first month, when yang is generated; it governs the Shaoyang of the left foot. Wei, the eighth branch, is the sixth month; it governs the Shaoyang of the right foot. Mao, the fourth branch, is the second month; it governs the Taiyang of the left foot. Wu, the seventh branch, is the fifth month; it governs the Taiyang of the right foot. Chen, the fifth branch, is the third month; it governs the Yangming of the left foot. Si, the sixth branch, is the fourth month; it governs the Yangming of the right foot. These two yang channels come together at the front, and so this is called Yangming, the confluence of yang. Shen, the ninth branch, is the seventh month, when yin is generated; it governs the Shaoyin of the right foot. Chou, the second branch, is the twelfth month; it governs the Shaoyin of the left foot. You, the tenth branch, is the eighth month; it governs the Taiyin of the right foot. Zi, the first branch, is the eleventh month; it governs the Taiyin of the left foot. Xu, the eleventh branch, is the ninth month; it governs the Jueyin of the right foot. Hai, the twelfth branch, is the tenth month; it governs the Jueyin of the left foot. These two yin channels meet and are exhausted together, and so this is called Jueyin."

Jia, the first heavenly stem, governs the Shaoyang of the left hand; Ji, the sixth stem, governs the Shaoyang of the right hand. Yi, the second stem, governs the Taiyang of the left hand; Wu, the fifth stem, governs the Taiyang of the right hand. Bing, the third stem, governs the Yangming of the left hand; Ding, the fourth stem, governs the Yangming of the right hand — these two fires unite together, and so this too is called Yangming. Geng, the seventh stem, governs the Shaoyin of the right hand; Gui, the tenth stem, governs the Shaoyin of the left hand; Xin, the eighth stem, governs the Taiyin of the right hand; Ren, the ninth stem, governs the Taiyin of the left hand.

Thus the yang channels of the foot are the lesser yang within yin; the yin channels of the foot are the greater yin within yin. The yang channels of the hand are the greater yang within yang; the yin channels of the hand are the lesser yin within yang. That which is above the waist is yang; that which is below the waist is yin.

With regard to the five viscera: the heart is the greater yang within yang; the lung is the lesser yin within yin; the liver is the lesser yang within yin; the spleen is the utmost yin within yin; and the kidney is the greater yin within yin.

Huangdi said: "How does one apply this in treatment?" Qibo said: "In the first, second, and third months, human qi resides on the left, so one should not needle the yang channels of the left foot. In the fourth, fifth, and sixth months, human qi resides on the right, so one should not needle the yang channels of the right foot. In the seventh, eighth, and ninth months, human qi resides on the right, so one should not needle the yin channels of the right foot. In the tenth, eleventh, and twelfth months, human qi resides on the left, so one should not needle the yin channels of the left foot."

Huangdi said: "Among the five phases, the east corresponds to Jia and Yi, which belong to wood and govern spring. Spring's color is green-blue, and it governs the liver; the liver corresponds to the Foot Jueyin. Yet now you say that Jia governs the Shaoyang of the left hand — this does not accord with the numerical sequence. Why is this?" Qibo said: "This concerns the yin and yang of Heaven and Earth, not the sequential progression of the four seasons and five phases. Moreover, yin and yang have names but no fixed form; hence they may be counted as ten, divided into a hundred, dispersed into a thousand, and extended into ten thousand — this is precisely what is meant."

Huangdi said: "I have received instruction in the nine needles from you, Master, and have also privately studied various other methods of treatment — daoyin exercises to guide the qi, qiaomo massage, moxibustion, hot compresses, puncture, cauterization, and the drinking of medicinal decoctions. Should one hold to a single one of these exclusively, or ought one to practice all of them together?" Qibo said: "These various methods are methods suited to different people; no single person can practice all of them at once."

Huangdi said: "This, then, is what is meant by holding to the one without losing it, whereby the myriad things are all accomplished. I have already heard of the essentials of yin and yang, the principles of deficiency and excess, the errors that come from their tipping and shifting out of balance, and the categories of what can be treated. I now wish to hear of the transformations of disease — of how it spreads and overwhelms until it becomes fatal and untreatable. May I hear of this?" Qibo said: "How essential a question this is! Bright, it is like waking from sleep into daylight; obscure, it is like being lost in the darkness of night. If one can take this in and put it into practice, the spirit will develop together with it; once one has fully absorbed it, the spirit will naturally attain it of itself. The principle by which the spirit is generated can be written down on bamboo and silk, but it cannot be truly handed down to one's own descendants without their own realization of it."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'waking into daylight'?" Qibo said: "To be clear about yin and yang is like having one's confusion resolved, like waking from drunkenness." Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'the darkness of night'?" Qibo said: "It is mute, without sound; vast and indistinct, without form. The hair and skin's texture are damaged, the upright qi is thrown into disorder, the invasive evil spreads and overflows, and is transmitted through the blood vessels. When the great evil qi enters the viscera, causing abdominal pain that spreads downward, this can bring about death and cannot bring about life."

Huangdi said: "What happens when the great qi enters the viscera?" Qibo said: "If disease first arises in the heart, in one day it moves to the lung, in three days it moves to the liver, in five days it moves to the spleen; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at midnight; in summer, at midday."

If disease first arises in the lung, in three days it moves to the liver, in one day it moves to the spleen, in five days it moves to the stomach; if after ten more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at sunset; in summer, at sunrise.

If disease first arises in the liver, in three days it moves to the spleen, in five days it moves to the stomach, in three days it moves to the kidney; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at sunset; in summer, at early mealtime.

If disease first arises in the spleen, in one day it moves to the stomach, in two days it moves to the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder; if after ten more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs when people have settled to rest; in summer, at late mealtime.

If disease first arises in the stomach, in five days it moves to the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder, in five days it rises to the heart; if after two more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at midnight; in summer, in the early afternoon.

If disease first arises in the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder, in three days it rises to the heart, in three days it moves to the small intestine; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs in the early morning; in summer, in the late afternoon.

If disease first arises in the bladder, in five days it moves to the kidney, in one day it moves to the small intestine, in one day it moves to the heart; if after two more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at the cock's crow; in summer, in the late afternoon.

When diseases are transmitted in this sequence, one after another, in every such case there is a fixed time of death, and needling cannot be applied. But when the disease skips over one viscus, or two, three, or four viscera in its transmission, then it can be treated with needling.

灵枢经.title_en: Invasive Evil Qi Giving Rise to Dreams
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I wish to hear how invasive evil qi spreads and overflows." Qibo said: "When upright-seeming evil qi invades from without to within, without yet having a fixed lodging place, it instead spreads through the viscera, unable to settle in a fixed location; it travels together with the nutritive and defensive qi, and flies about together with the hun and po souls, causing a person to be unable to rest peacefully in sleep and to dream frequently. When qi invades the bowels, there is a surplus in the exterior and a deficiency in the interior; when qi invades the viscera, there is a surplus in the interior and a deficiency in the exterior."

Huangdi said: "Do surplus and deficiency manifest in particular forms in dreams?" Qibo said: "When yin qi is exuberant, one dreams of wading through great waters and feels fear. When yang qi is exuberant, one dreams of great fires burning. When yin and yang are both exuberant, one dreams of mutual slaughter. When qi is exuberant in the upper body, one dreams of flying; when exuberant in the lower body, one dreams of falling. When extremely hungry, one dreams of taking things; when extremely full, one dreams of giving things away. When liver qi is exuberant, one dreams of anger. When lung qi is exuberant, one dreams of fear, weeping, and flying about. When heart qi is exuberant, one dreams of laughing heartily, or of fear and dread. When spleen qi is exuberant, one dreams of singing and music, and of the body feeling heavy and unable to move. When kidney qi is exuberant, one dreams of the lower back and spine coming apart and no longer connected. In all these twelve conditions of exuberance, one should drain the affected channel as soon as the qi arrives there, and the condition will be resolved at once."

When reversal qi lodges in the heart, one dreams of hills and mountains, smoke and fire. When it lodges in the lung, one dreams of flying about, and of seeing strange objects of metal and iron. When it lodges in the liver, one dreams of mountain forests and trees. When it lodges in the spleen, one dreams of hills and ridges, great marshes, and of ruined houses in wind and rain. When it lodges in the kidney, one dreams of standing at the edge of a deep abyss, or of being submerged and dwelling underwater. When it lodges in the bladder, one dreams of wandering about. When it lodges in the stomach, one dreams of food and drink. When it lodges in the large intestine, one dreams of open fields. When it lodges in the small intestine, one dreams of crowded settlements and busy crossroads. When it lodges in the gallbladder, one dreams of fighting, litigation, and self-disembowelment. When it lodges in the genitals, one dreams of sexual intercourse. When it lodges in the neck, one dreams of being beheaded. When it lodges in the shins, one dreams of walking yet being unable to go forward, and of dwelling in deep pits or in walled gardens underground. When it lodges in the thighs and arms, one dreams of ceremonial bowing and rising. When it lodges in the bladder and intestines, one dreams of urinating and defecating. In all these fifteen conditions of deficiency, one should supplement the affected channel as soon as the qi arrives there, and the condition will be resolved at once.

灵枢经.title_en: According with Qi: The Day Divided into Four Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "Now, the hundred diseases, in their origin, always arise from dryness, warmth, cold, summer-heat, wind, and rain, from yin and yang, from joy and anger, from food and drink, and from one's dwelling and manner of living; when qi combines with these factors a form results, and once it settles into a particular viscus it acquires a name — this much I understand. But why is it that in most of the hundred diseases, the patient is clear-headed at dawn, calm during the day, worsens toward evening, and is most severe at night?" Qibo said: "This is brought about by the qi of the four seasons."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the qi of the four seasons." Qibo said: "Spring brings forth life, summer brings growth, autumn brings gathering-in, and winter brings storage — this is the constant pattern of qi, and human beings correspond to it as well. If one divides a single day into the four seasons, dawn corresponds to spring, midday corresponds to summer, sunset corresponds to autumn, and midnight corresponds to winter. At dawn, human qi begins to be generated and the pathogenic qi of disease declines, and so the patient is clear-headed. At midday, human qi grows to its fullness, and in its fullness it overcomes the evil qi, and so the patient is calm. Toward evening, human qi begins to decline and evil qi begins to be generated, and so the illness worsens. At midnight, human qi withdraws into the viscera, and evil qi alone occupies the body, and so the illness is most severe."

Huangdi said: "Yet there are times when this pattern is reversed — why is this?" Qibo said: "This occurs when the disease does not correspond to the qi of the four seasons, but rather when a single viscus alone governs the disease; in such cases, the illness will necessarily worsen at the time when the viscus's own qi cannot overcome the prevailing seasonal qi, and will improve at the time when its qi does overcome it." Huangdi said: "How does one treat this?" Qibo said: "By following the timing of Heaven, one can anticipate the course of the disease. One who follows this principle is a skilled physician; one who goes against it is a crude one."

Huangdi said: "Excellent. I have heard that in needling there are five transformations, which govern the five transport points. I wish to hear their measure." Qibo said: "A person has five viscera, and the five viscera have five transformations. The five transformations correspond to five transport points, and so five times five gives twenty-five transport points, corresponding to the five seasonal periods."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of the five transformations." Qibo said: "The liver is a male viscus; its color is green-blue, its season is spring, its note is jue, its flavor is sour, and its days are Jia and Yi. The heart is a male viscus; its color is red, its season is summer, its days are Bing and Ding, its note is zhi, and its flavor is bitter. The spleen is a female viscus; its color is yellow, its season is late summer, its days are Wu and Ji, its note is gong, and its flavor is sweet. The lung is a female viscus; its color is white, its note is shang, its season is autumn, its days are Geng and Xin, and its flavor is pungent. The kidney is a female viscus; its color is black, its season is winter, its days are Ren and Gui, its note is yu, and its flavor is salty. These are the five transformations."

Huangdi said: "How do these govern the five transport points?" Qibo said: "The viscus governs winter, and in winter one needles the Well points. The color governs spring, and in spring one needles the Spring points. The season governs summer, and in summer one needles the Stream points. The note governs late summer, and in late summer one needles the River points. The flavor governs autumn, and in autumn one needles the Sea points. This is what is meant by the five transformations governing the five transport points."

Huangdi said: "But what of the Source points — how do they fit harmoniously among these, in relation to the five transport points?" Qibo said: "The Source points alone do not correspond to the five seasonal periods; they are joined together with the River points to correspond to their proper number, and so there are six times six, thirty-six transport points in total."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by saying that the viscus governs winter, the season governs summer, the note governs late summer, the flavor governs autumn, and the color governs spring? I wish to hear the reason for this." Qibo said: "When disease lies in the viscus itself, one selects the Well point. When disease manifests as a change in complexion, one selects the Spring point. When disease alternates between mild and severe at different times, one selects the Stream point. When disease manifests as a change in the voice, one selects the River point. When the channel is full and there is stagnant blood, the disease lies in the stomach; and when disease arises from irregular eating and drinking, one selects the Sea point — hence it is said that the flavor governs the Sea point. This is what is meant by the five transformations."

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on Inferring the Interior from the Exterior
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard the nine chapters on the nine needles; I have personally received their teaching and have grasped much of their meaning. Now, the nine needles begin with one and end with nine, yet I have not yet grasped their essential principle. The nine needles, in their smallness, admit of nothing smaller within; in their greatness, admit of nothing greater without; in their depth, cannot be surpassed from below; in their height, cannot be covered from above. They are vague and indistinct, without any fixed aperture, flowing and overflowing without limit. I know that they accord with the Way of Heaven, the affairs of man, and the transformations of the four seasons. Yet I wish to blend them together down to the finest hair, bundling them all into a single unity — can this be done?" Qibo said: "How penetrating a question this is! It is not the Way of the needle alone that is like this — the governance of a state is likewise so."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of the Way of the needle, not of affairs of state." Qibo said: "Now, the governance of a state depends solely upon the Way; without the Way, how could small and great, deep and shallow, all be blended together into a single unity?"

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear this through to the end." Qibo said: "Consider the sun and the moon, water and the mirror, the drum and its echo. The brightness of the sun and moon never fails to cast its reflection; the clarity of water and mirror never fails to capture form; the drum's echo in response never lags behind its sound. When something stirs and moves, the response follows in accord, and the true nature of the thing is thereby fully grasped."

Huangdi said: "How profound this is! A brightness so luminous cannot be obscured; and because it cannot be obscured, it never departs from yin and yang. By combining observation with palpation and verification, one perceives and grasps the truth, just as clear water and a bright mirror never fail to capture form. When the five notes are not manifest and the five colors are not clear, the five viscera are thrown into turbulence — when this is so, the internal and external correspond to one another just as the drum answers the drumstick, as the echo answers the sound, as the shadow resembles the form. Thus, for what is distant, one observes the exterior in order to infer the interior; for what is near, one observes the interior in order to infer the exterior. This is called the utmost expression of yin and yang, the canopy of Heaven and Earth. I ask that this be stored away in the Chamber of the Numinous Orchid, and that it not be allowed to leak out."

Huangdi asked Shaoyu, saying: 'I have heard that when the hundred diseases first begin, they must arise from wind, rain, cold, and heat, following the fine body hair and entering the interstices of the skin. Sometimes the pathogen turns back and departs; sometimes it lingers and lodges; sometimes it becomes wind-swelling with sweating; sometimes it becomes wasting-thirst; sometimes it becomes cold and heat; sometimes it becomes lingering impediment; sometimes it becomes accumulations and gatherings. Strange evils overflow in countless variety, and I wish to hear the reason for this. Now when people fall ill at the same time, some contract this disease and others that — is it perhaps that Heaven generates wind differently for different people? Why should there be such difference?' Shaoyu said: 'Now when Heaven generates wind, it is not out of partiality toward the common people. Its movement is fair, level, and upright: those who transgress against it are struck by it, and those who avoid it escape harm. It is not that Heaven seeks out a person, but that the person transgresses against it of their own accord.'

Huangdi said: 'When people meet with wind at one and the same time and fall ill together, why do their illnesses differ? I wish to hear the reason.' Shaoyu said: 'An excellent question! Let me explain it by comparison with a craftsman. When a craftsman sharpens his axe and hones his blade to cut and fell timber, the wood itself — being of yin and yang — still has its hard and its brittle portions. Where the wood is hard, the blade will not bite; where it is brittle, the bark gives way easily; and where the grain crosses at a knot, the axe itself may be nicked. Now within a single tree, the hard and the brittle portions differ: the hard is unyielding, the brittle is easily damaged — how much more so, then, when the timbers themselves differ, in the thickness of their bark and the abundance of their sap, so that each is distinct from the others! Consider trees that flower early and put forth leaves before others: if they meet with spring frost and fierce wind, their blossoms fall and their leaves wither. In prolonged exposure and great drought, those trees that are brittle-wooded and thin-barked have branches and twigs with little sap, and their leaves wither. In prolonged overcast and excessive rain, those that are thin-barked and rich in sap have their bark soaked through and shallowly rotted. When sudden violent wind arises, trees that are hard yet brittle have their branches snapped and broken. When autumn frost comes with swift wind, trees that are hard yet brittle have their roots shaken and their leaves shed. In each of these five cases, the harm done differs according to the tree — how much more must this be so for human beings!'

Huangdi said: 'How does this correspond in human beings, as with trees?' Shaoyu answered: 'Where a tree is harmed, it is always the branches that suffer the harm; a branch that is hard, brittle yet firm does not yet come to harm. In human beings, when a person has a recurring illness, it is likewise because of some place — in the bones and joints, the skin, or the interstices — that is not firm and solid; it is there that the pathogen lodges, and so illness recurs there constantly.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to wind reversal with dripping sweat, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the interior is not firm and the interstices are loose, one is prone to wind disease.' Huangdi said: 'How may one know that the flesh is not firm?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the flesh of the hollow of the knee is not firm and lacks distinct texture — that is, when its texture is coarse, and where the texture is coarse the skin is not close-knit — the interstices are loose. This describes the condition taken as a whole.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to wasting-thirst, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'Those in whom all five viscera are soft and weak are prone to wasting-thirst.' Huangdi said: 'How does one recognize that the five viscera are soft and weak?' Shaoyu answered: 'Now where there is softness and weakness, there must also be hardness and strength present together with it; hardness and strength incline toward frequent anger, while the soft is easily injured.' Huangdi said: 'How does one distinguish the soft and weak from the hard and strong?' Shaoyu answered: 'Such a person has thin skin, and eyes that are firm, fixed, and deep-set; the passage from throat to intestine is long and straight. Their heart is hard, and hardness inclines toward frequent anger; anger causes the qi to rebel upward, accumulating and gathering within the chest, so that blood and qi flow backward and lodge there. The flesh beneath the flank becomes engorged, the blood vessels fail to circulate, and this turns into heat; heat then consumes the flesh and skin, and so wasting-thirst results. This describes a person who is fierce and hard by temperament but whose flesh and muscles are weak.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to cold-heat disorders, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'Those with small bones and weak flesh are prone to cold-heat disorders.' Huangdi said: 'How does one determine whether the bones are large or small, the flesh hard or brittle, and the complexion uneven?' Shaoyu answered: 'The cheekbone is the root of the bony structure: when the cheekbone is large, the bones are large; when the cheekbone is small, the bones are small. When the skin is thin and the flesh lacks firm bulk, when the arms are soft and feeble, and when the complexion at the lower portion of the face appears dull and lifeless, not matching the color of the upper portion, but sullied and distinctly different — this is the sign. Moreover, in one whose arms are thin, the marrow is not full, and so such a person is prone to cold-heat disorders.'

Huangdi said: 'How may one recognize a person prone to impediment disorders?' Shaoyu answered: 'One whose skin texture is coarse and whose flesh is not firm is prone to impediment.' Huangdi said: 'Does impediment have fixed locations, whether high or low in the body?' Shaoyu answered: 'To know whether it will occur high or low, one need only observe the region affected in each case.'

Huangdi said: 'In people prone to accumulations and gatherings within the intestines, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the skin is thin and lacks luster, and the flesh is not firm but moist and soft, then the intestines and stomach are in poor condition. Being in poor condition, evil qi lingers and lodges there; accumulations and gatherings then injure the region between the spleen and stomach. When cold and warmth are not properly regulated, evil qi gradually arrives, accumulating and lingering until it lodges there, and a great gathering then arises.'

Huangdi said: 'I have now heard of the forms these illnesses take, and I understand them. I wish now to hear of their timing.' Shaoyu answered: 'One must first establish the person's age, so as to know the proper season. If the season is in its ascendant phase, the illness will arise; if the season is in its declining phase, the danger is greater still. Even where there is no marked decline, if in that year there is a pathway of clash and encounter, the illness will certainly arise. This is what is meant by disease arising according to the body's constitution — this is the guiding thread of the five variations.'

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on the Root Nature of the Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'The blood, qi, essence, and spirit of a person are what sustain life and circulate throughout one's nature and destiny. The channels are what move the blood and qi, nourish yin and yang, moisten the sinews and bones, and benefit the joints. The defensive qi is what warms the flesh between the muscles, fills out the skin, makes the interstices firm and full, and governs their opening and closing. The will and intent are what govern the essence and spirit, gather in the ethereal and corporeal souls, adapt to cold and warmth, and harmonize joy and anger. Therefore, when the blood is harmonious, the channels flow freely, nutritive qi restores yin and yang in proper balance, the sinews and bones are strong and vigorous, and the joints move clearly and smoothly. When the defensive qi is harmonious, the flesh between the muscles is loose and free, the skin is well-regulated and supple, and the interstices are close and dense. When the will and intent are harmonious, the essence and spirit are focused and upright, the ethereal and corporeal souls do not scatter, regret and anger do not arise, and the five viscera do not receive evil qi. When cold and warmth are harmonious, the six bowels transform the grain properly, wind impediment does not arise, the channels flow freely and smoothly, and the limbs and joints find peace. This is the ordinary state of balance in a person. The five viscera are what store the essence, spirit, blood, qi, and the ethereal and corporeal souls. The six bowels are what transform water and grain and move the body fluids along. These are the endowments that every person alike receives from Heaven, whether foolish or wise, worthy or unworthy, with no difference to favor one over another. And yet there are those who alone live out their full heavenly span, free of evil and deviant illness, undiminished after a hundred years, so that even when they encounter wind and rain, sudden cold, or extreme heat, none of these are able to harm them; while there are others who never leave the shelter and shade of their own rooms, free from any apprehension or fright, and yet still cannot escape illness. Why is this? I wish to hear the reason.'

Qibo replied: 'This is indeed a searching question. The five viscera are what participate with Heaven and Earth, correspond to yin and yang, set the four seasons in motion, and transform the five periods. The five viscera themselves inherently differ in being small or large, high or low in position, hard or brittle, upright or tilted; and the six bowels likewise differ in being small or large, long or short, thick or thin, coiled or straight, slack or tense. In all, these are twenty-five distinct variations, each different from the others — some favorable, some unfavorable, some auspicious, some inauspicious. Allow me to explain their particulars.'

When the heart is small, it is at ease, and evil qi cannot injure it, though it is easily injured by worry. When the heart is large, worry cannot injure it, but it is easily injured by evil qi. When the heart is positioned high, it presses full against the lung, causing oppression and forgetfulness, and such a person is difficult to move with words. When the heart is positioned low, the viscus lies exposed toward the exterior, easily injured by cold, and such a person is easily frightened by words. When the heart is firm, the viscus is secure and well-guarded. When the heart is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and heat in the middle. When the heart is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the heart is tilted to one side, one's conduct lacks consistency, with no proper self-governance.

When the lung is small, one drinks little and does not suffer panting and wheezing. When the lung is large, one drinks much, and is prone to chest impediment, throat impediment, and rebellious qi. When the lung is positioned high, qi rises upward, causing labored breathing with the shoulders heaving and coughing. When the lung is positioned low, it presses down against the diaphragm and crowds the lung itself, and one is prone to pain beneath the rib-side. When the lung is firm, one does not suffer illness — apart from cough with rising qi. When the lung is brittle, one suffers from wasting-thirst and impediment and is easily injured. When the lung is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the lung is tilted to one side, one suffers pain on one side of the chest.

When the liver is small, the viscus is secure, and there is no disease beneath the rib-side. When the liver is large, it presses upon the stomach and crowds against the throat; crowding the throat causes distress within the diaphragm, together with pain beneath the rib-side. When the liver is positioned high, it presses upward and cuts against the diaphragm, and the rib-side is drawn taut, producing 'panting mass.' When the liver is positioned low, it presses upon the stomach, leaving the region beneath the rib-side empty and hollow; when this region is hollow, it is easily invaded by evil qi. When the liver is firm, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the liver is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and impediment, and is easily injured. When the liver is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the liver is tilted to one side, one suffers pain beneath the rib-side.

When the spleen is small, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure with evil qi. When the spleen is large, one suffers pain from crowding at the soft flank, and cannot walk quickly. When the spleen is positioned high, the soft flank draws upon the free ribs and causes pain. When the spleen is positioned low, it descends and presses down toward the large intestine; pressing down upon the large intestine, the viscus readily receives evil qi. When the spleen is firm, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the spleen is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and impediment, and is easily injured. When the spleen is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the spleen is tilted to one side, one is prone to fullness and distension.

When the kidney is small, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the kidney is large, one is prone to lower back pain, unable to bend forward or backward, and easily injured by evil qi. When the kidney is positioned high, one suffers pain along the back and spine, unable to bend forward or backward. When the kidney is positioned low, one suffers pain in the lower back and sacrum, unable to bend forward or backward, and develops fox-mounting hernia. When the kidney is firm, one does not suffer pain in the lower back. When the kidney is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst, and is easily injured. When the kidney is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the kidney is tilted to one side, one suffers pain in the lower back and sacrum. In all, these twenty-five variations are the recurring afflictions that trouble people.

Huangdi said: 'How can one know this?' Qibo said: 'One whose complexion is red and whose skin texture is fine has a small heart; one whose texture is coarse has a large heart. One who lacks a xiphoid process has a heart positioned high; one whose xiphoid process is small, short, and raised has a heart positioned low. One whose xiphoid process is long has a firm heart; one whose xiphoid process is weak, small, and thin has a brittle heart. One whose xiphoid process extends straight down without being raised has an upright, centered heart; one whose xiphoid process leans to one side has a heart tilted to one side.'

One whose complexion is white and whose skin texture is fine has a small lung; one whose texture is coarse has a large lung. One with broad shoulders, a projecting chest, and a sunken throat has a lung positioned high; one whose armpits are drawn close and whose rib-sides are spread wide has a lung positioned low. One with well-formed shoulders and back, thick and full, has a firm lung; one whose shoulders and back are thin has a brittle lung. One whose back and chest are thick has an upright, centered lung; one whose rib-sides are uneven and loosely formed has a lung tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is green-blue and whose skin texture is fine has a small liver; one whose texture is coarse has a large liver. One with a broad chest and flaring lower ribs has a liver positioned high; one whose rib-sides are drawn close with narrow, rabbit-like lower ribs has a liver positioned low. One whose chest and rib-sides are well-formed has a firm liver; one whose rib bones are weak has a brittle liver. One whose chest and abdomen are well-proportioned and matched has an upright, centered liver; one whose rib bones are raised unevenly to one side has a liver tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is yellow and whose skin texture is fine has a small spleen; one whose texture is coarse has a large spleen. One whose lips are raised and everted has a spleen positioned high; one whose lower lip hangs loose has a spleen positioned low. One whose lips are firm has a firm spleen; one whose lips are large but not firm has a brittle spleen. One whose upper and lower lips are well-matched has an upright, centered spleen; one whose lips are raised unevenly to one side has a spleen tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is black and whose skin texture is fine has a small kidney; one whose texture is coarse has a large kidney.
