One with high-set ears has a kidney positioned high; one whose ears are sunken behind has a kidney positioned low. One whose ears are firm has a firm kidney; one whose ears are thin and not firm has a brittle kidney. One whose ears are well-formed and set forward near the jaw has an upright, centered kidney; one whose ears are set unevenly high has a kidney tilted to one side. In all these various conditions, when the corresponding feature is well-maintained, the person is at ease; when it is diminished or deficient, illness results.

Huangdi said: 'Excellent. But this is not what I originally asked. I wish to hear about those people who cannot be made ill — who live out their full heavenly span, and even when deeply disturbed or greatly frightened, their resolve cannot be diminished, and even severe cold or extreme heat cannot harm them — as well as about those who never leave the shelter of their own rooms and have no apprehension or fright, and yet still cannot escape illness. Why is this? I wish to hear the reason.' Qibo said: 'The five viscera and six bowels are the lodging places of evil qi — allow me to explain the reason. When all five viscera are small, one has little illness but suffers from an anxious, troubled heart and great worry and disturbance. When all five viscera are large, one is slow to attend to affairs and difficult to disturb or trouble. When all five viscera are positioned high, one is inclined to lofty ambition and grand undertakings. When all five viscera are positioned low, one is inclined to place oneself below others. When all five viscera are firm, one has no illness. When all five viscera are brittle, one is never free of illness. When all five viscera are upright and centered, one is harmonious and agreeable and wins the hearts of others. When all five viscera are tilted to one side, one harbors a deceitful heart and is inclined to thieving, cannot deal fairly with others, and is inconsistent and duplicitous in speech.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the correspondences of the six bowels.' Qibo answered: 'The lung is paired with the large intestine, and the large intestine corresponds to the skin. The heart is paired with the small intestine, and the small intestine corresponds to the vessels. The liver is paired with the gallbladder, and the gallbladder corresponds to the sinews. The spleen is paired with the stomach, and the stomach corresponds to the flesh. The kidney is paired with the triple burner and bladder, and the triple burner and bladder correspond to the interstices and fine body hair.'

Huangdi said: 'How does this correspondence work?' Qibo said: 'The lung corresponds to the skin. When the skin is thick, the large intestine is thick; when the skin is thin, the large intestine is thin. When the skin is loose and the abdomen is large within, the large intestine is large and long. When the skin is tight, the large intestine is tight and short. When the skin is smooth, the large intestine is straight. When the skin and flesh do not separate easily from each other, the large intestine is knotted.'

The heart corresponds to the vessels. When the skin is thick, the vessels are thick, and when the vessels are thick, the small intestine is thick. When the skin is thin, the vessels are thin, and when the vessels are thin, the small intestine is thin. When the skin is loose, the vessels are loose, and when the vessels are loose, the small intestine is large and long. When the skin is thin and the pulsing vessels are small, the small intestine is small and short. When the yang channel vessels are all markedly winding and tortuous, the small intestine is knotted.

The spleen corresponds to the flesh. When the muscular flesh is firm and full, the stomach is thick; when the muscular flesh is slight, the stomach is thin. When the muscular flesh is small and slight, the stomach is not firm. When the flesh does not match the body in proportion, the stomach is positioned low, and when the stomach is low, the lower duct is constricted and does not function smoothly. When the flesh is not firm, the stomach is slack. When the muscular flesh lacks small knotted masses within, the stomach is tight. When the muscular flesh has a moderate number of small knotted masses within, the stomach is knotted, and when the stomach is knotted, the upper duct is constricted and does not function smoothly.

The liver corresponds to the nails. When the nails are thick and yellow in color, the gallbladder is thick. When the nails are thin and red in color, the gallbladder is thin. When the nails are firm and green-blue in color, the gallbladder is tight. When the nails are soft and red in color, the gallbladder is slack. When the nails are straight and white in color without curvature, the gallbladder is straight. When the nails are poorly formed, black in color, with many ridges, the gallbladder is knotted.

The kidney corresponds to the bones. When the skin texture is dense and the skin is thick, the triple burner and bladder are thick; when the skin texture is coarse and the skin is thin, the triple burner and bladder are thin. When the interstices are loose, the triple burner and bladder are slack. When the skin is tight and lacks fine body hair, the triple burner and bladder are tight. When the fine body hair is fine yet full, the triple burner and bladder are straight. When the fine body hair is sparse, the triple burner and bladder are knotted.

Huangdi said: 'Thickness and thinness, fine form and poor form, all have their outward manifestations. I wish to hear of the illnesses associated with each.' Qibo answered: 'By observing the outward correspondence, one comes to know the condition of the inner viscus, and thus one knows what illness will occur.'

Leigong asked Huangdi, saying: 'This lowly disciple has received your instruction and gained understanding of the sixty chapters of the Nine Needles; morning and evening I have diligently applied myself to them, until the more recently bound volumes have had their binding cords worn through, and the older bamboo strips have grown soiled with handling. Yet still I recite and chant them without setting them aside, and there remain points whose meaning I have not fully grasped. In the chapter "External Estimation," it speaks of gathering and binding all things together into one — I do not know what this means. Now, that which is great has nothing outside it, and that which is small has nothing within it; the great and the small are without limit, the high and the low are without measure — how, then, can one bind them into one? Among scholars, natural talent and capacity vary, some deep and some shallow; my own understanding is narrow and superficial, unable to grasp what is broad, vast, and profound, though I strive as hard in study as this lowly disciple can. I fear that this teaching will scatter and be lost to later generations, cut off from my own descendants — may I dare ask how it can be bound into a compact and lasting form?' Huangdi said: 'An excellent question indeed. This is a teaching that my former masters restricted, transmitting it only in private, sealed with a covenant of cutting the arm and smearing the blood in oath. If you wish to receive it, why not first observe ritual fasting?'

Leigong bowed twice, rose, and said: 'I ask to receive your instruction on these terms.' He then fasted and kept vigil for three days, and requested, saying: 'May I ask that, this day being one of proper yang brightness, this lowly disciple be permitted to receive the covenant?' Huangdi then entered the fasting chamber together with him, cut his arm, and smeared the blood in oath. Huangdi himself pronounced the invocation, saying: 'On this day of proper yang brightness, we smear blood in oath to transmit these formulas. Should anyone dare to betray these words, may they instead receive calamity in return.' Leigong bowed twice and said: 'This lowly disciple receives it.' Huangdi then grasped his hand with his left hand, and with his right handed him the text, saying: 'Be cautious, be cautious. I shall now explain it to you. In all the principles of needling, the channel vessels are the starting point. One must trace the course along which they flow, and know their proper measures and dimensions. Within, one needles the five viscera; without, one needles the six bowels. One must carefully examine the defensive qi, for it is the mother of the hundred diseases. One regulates deficiency and excess, and only when deficiency and excess are corrected does one stop; one drains the blood in the network vessels, and when the blood is exhausted, there is no danger.'

Leigong said: 'All of this is what this lowly disciple already understands; what I do not yet know is how to bind it into a compact form.' Huangdi said: 'Now to bind a formula compactly is like binding shut a sack: if the sack is full and not bound shut, its contents will leak and spill out; if a formula is complete but not bound into compact form, the spirit will not accompany it.' Leigong said: 'I wish, for the sake of those of lesser talent, that you would bind it compactly before it becomes overfull.' Huangdi said: 'One who knows how to bind it compactly before it becomes overfull may become a skilled practitioner, but cannot thereby become a teacher to all under Heaven.'

Leigong said: 'I wish to hear how one becomes a skilled practitioner.' Huangdi said: 'The cunkou pulse governs the interior, and the renying pulse governs the exterior. The two correspond to each other, moving together and arriving together, like a stretched cord equal in thickness throughout its length. In spring and summer, the renying pulse is slightly larger; in autumn and winter, the cunkou pulse is slightly larger. When this is so, the person is called balanced and healthy.'

When the renying pulse is one time larger than the cunkou pulse, the disease is in the Foot Shaoyang channel; if it is one time larger and also agitated, it is in the Hand Shaoyang channel. When the renying pulse is two times larger, the disease is in the Foot Taiyang channel; if two times larger and agitated, the disease is in the Hand Taiyang channel. When the renying pulse is three times larger, the disease is in the Foot Yangming channel; if three times larger and agitated, the disease is in the Hand Yangming channel. When the pulse is replete, this indicates heat; when deficient, it indicates cold; when taut, it indicates painful impediment; when intermittent, the condition alternates between severe and eased. When replete, one drains it; when deficient, one supplements it; when taut, one needles the flesh between the muscles; when intermittent, one drains the network vessels of blood and also administers medicinal decoctions; when the pulse is sunken and depressed, one applies moxibustion; when neither replete nor deficient, one selects points along the affected channel itself — this is called channel puncture. When the renying pulse is four times larger, both large and rapid, this is called yang overflow; yang overflow becomes exterior blockage, and is fatal, beyond treatment. One must carefully press and examine the root and branch of the pulse, observe whether it is cold or hot, so as to verify the disease of the viscera and bowels.'

When the cunkou pulse is one time larger than the renying pulse, the disease is in the Foot Jueyin channel; if one time larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Xin Zhu (Pericardium) channel. When the cunkou pulse is two times larger, the disease is in the Foot Shaoyin channel; if two times larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Shaoyin channel. When the cunkou pulse is three times larger, the disease is in the Foot Taiyin channel; if three times larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Taiyin channel. When the pulse is replete, this indicates distension and fullness, cold in the middle, and undigested food; when deficient, it indicates heat in the middle, discharge of sloughing stool, shortness of qi, and altered color of the urine; when taut, it indicates painful impediment; when intermittent, the pain alternates between occurring and ceasing. When replete, one drains it; when deficient, one supplements it; when taut, one needles first and then applies moxibustion afterward; when intermittent, one drains the network vessels of blood and afterward regulates the condition; when sunken and depressed, one applies moxibustion alone — for when the pulse is sunken and depressed, the blood within the vessel has become knotted and bound, with stagnant blood lodged within, and the blood is cold, so moxibustion is fitting. When neither replete nor deficient, one selects points along the affected channel itself. When the cunkou pulse is four times larger, this is called inward blockage; inward blockage, being both large and rapid, is fatal, beyond treatment. One must carefully examine whether the root and branch of the pulse are cold or warm, so as to verify the disease of the viscera and bowels.'

Only when one has thoroughly grasped the nutritive qi and the transport points can one go on to transmit the great essentials. The great essentials state: when replete, simply drain it; when deficient, simply supplement it; when taut, apply moxibustion and needling together with medicinal decoctions; when sunken and depressed, simply apply moxibustion; when neither replete nor deficient, select points along the affected channel itself. What is called treating by the channel means administering medicinal decoctions as well as moxibustion and needling. When the pulse is taut and tense, one should guide and stretch the affected area; when the pulse is large yet weak, one should seek calm and quietude, exerting no strenuous effort or labor.'

Leigong asked Huangdi, saying: 'Is it solely by the Bright Hall that the five colors are judged? I do not yet understand what this means.' Huangdi said: 'The Bright Hall (mingtang) is the nose; the Palace Gate (que) is the space between the eyebrows; the Court (ting) is the forehead; the Fan (fan) is the sides of the cheeks; the Curtain (bi) is the doorway of the ear. One wishes for the area between these features to be broad and full, so that even at a distance of ten paces they are all visible from without — a person such as this will be long-lived, certainly reaching a hundred years.'

Leigong said: 'How does one distinguish among the five regions spoken of?' Huangdi said: 'When the bones of the Bright Hall are high and raised, level and straight; when the five viscera take their proper places at the center, and the six bowels flank them on either side; when the head and face rise above at the Palace Gate and the Court; when the Royal Palace lies at the lower extremity between the eyes; when the five viscera rest securely within the chest — then the true color arrives and the color of disease does not appear; the Bright Hall is moist, lustrous, and clear. How, then, could the five official regions fail to be distinguished?'

Leigong said: 'May I hear about cases where they cannot be distinguished?' Huangdi said: 'When the five colors appear, each emerges in its own regional area. If the bone beneath that regional area is sunken, the person cannot escape illness. But if the colors of the regional areas overlie one another in proper sequence, then even though the illness is severe, the person will not die.'

Leigong said: 'How does one assign meaning to the five colors?' Huangdi said: 'Green-blue and black indicate pain; yellow and red indicate heat; white indicates cold — these are called the five offices.'

Leigong said: 'How can one tell whether a disease is growing more severe or is just beginning to wane?' Huangdi said: 'This can be discerned in both the exterior and interior pulses. When one palpates the Pulse Opening — the radial pulse — and finds it slippery, small, tight, and deep, the disease is growing more severe and lies within. When the Renying pulse at the neck is large, tight, and floating, the disease is growing more severe and lies without. If the Pulse Opening is floating and slippery, the disease advances day by day; if the Renying pulse is deep and slippery, the disease diminishes day by day. If the Pulse Opening is slippery and deep, the disease advances day by day and lies within; if the Renying pulse is slippery, exuberant, and floating, the disease advances day by day and lies without. When the floating and deep qualities of the pulse, and the size of the qi at Renying and the Inch Opening, are equal to one another, the disease is difficult to cure. When disease lies in the viscera, a deep and large pulse is easy to cure, while a small pulse indicates a contrary, unfavorable course. When disease lies in the bowels, a floating and large pulse is easy to cure. When the Renying pulse is exuberant and firm, the person has been injured by cold; when the qi at the Pulse Opening is exuberant and firm, the person has been injured by food.'

Leigong said: 'How does one use the colors to speak of the interval and severity of disease?' Huangdi said: 'When the color is coarse yet bright, this indicates the disease is mild; when it is sunken and lifeless, this indicates severity. When the color moves upward, the disease is growing more severe; when the color moves downward, dispersing like clouds clearing away, the disease is about to resolve. Each of the five colors has its own regional area corresponding to a viscus, and each has an outer portion and an inner portion. When a color moves from the outer portion toward the inner portion, the disease moves from the exterior toward the interior; when the color moves from the inner portion toward the outer portion, the disease moves from the interior toward the exterior. When disease arises in the interior, one should first treat the yin, and afterward treat the yang; to reverse this order will make the disease worse. When disease arises in the yang, one should first treat the exterior, and afterward treat the interior; to reverse this order will make the disease worse. When the pulse is slippery and large, intermittent yet long, the disease has come from without; the eyes perceive certain things and the mind feels aversion toward them — this is a case of yang qi massing together, and it can be altered and cured.'

Leigong said: 'I have heard that wind is the beginning of the hundred diseases, and that reversal-counterflow arises from cold and dampness. How does one distinguish between them?' Huangdi said: 'One regularly examines the area of the Palace Gate: a color that is thin and lustrous indicates wind; a color that is turbid and congested indicates impediment (bi). A color appearing at the earth region — the chin — indicates reversal. This is the general rule; in each case one speaks of the disease according to its color.'

Leigong said: 'When a person who was not ill suddenly dies, how can one know this in advance?' Huangdi said: 'When a massive evil qi enters the viscera and bowels, the person, without having been ill, will suddenly die.' Leigong said: 'When a person's illness has slightly improved and yet he suddenly dies, how can one know this?' Huangdi said: 'If a red color appears on both cheekbones, as large as a thumb, then even though the illness has slightly improved, the person will certainly die suddenly. If a black color appears on the Court, the forehead, as large as a thumb, the person, even without illness, will certainly die suddenly.'

Leigong bowed twice and said: 'Excellent! Is there a fixed time for such a death?' Huangdi said: 'One examines the color to determine the time.' Leigong said: 'Excellent! I wish to hear it in full.' Huangdi said: 'The Court is the region of the head and face; above the Palace Gate is the throat; within the Palace Gate is the lung; the lower extremity between the eyes is the heart; directly below that is the liver; to the left of the liver's region is the gallbladder; below that is the spleen; the region above and to the side is the stomach; the central region is the large intestine; flanking the large intestine's region is the kidney; opposite the kidney's region is the navel; above the tip of the nose is the small intestine, and below the tip of the nose is the bladder and the uterus; the cheekbones correspond to the shoulders; behind the cheekbones corresponds to the arms; below the arms' region corresponds to the hands; above the inner canthus of the eyes corresponds to the breasts; along and above the rope-like ridge of the cheek corresponds to the back; along the jaw and below corresponds to the thighs; the central point below the mouth corresponds to the knees; below the knees' region corresponds to the shins; opposite the shins' region corresponds to the feet; the Great Divide corresponds to the inner thigh; the Great Bend corresponds to the kneecap. These are the regional areas of the five viscera, six bowels, and limbs and joints — each has its own allotted place. Once one has these allotted places, one uses yin to harmonize yang, and yang to harmonize yin; one must clearly understand these regional areas, and then every application will be apt. To be able to distinguish left from right is called the great Way; male and female occupy different positions, hence it is said to accord with yin and yang. To examine carefully whether the color is moist or withered — this is called being a skilled physician.'

A sunken and turbid color indicates disease in the interior; a floating and lustrous color indicates disease in the exterior. Yellow and red indicate wind; green-blue and black indicate pain; white indicates cold; a yellow color that is oily and moist indicates pus; an intensely red color indicates painful blood stasis; when this pain is extreme, it causes spasm-contracture; when cold is extreme, it causes numbness of the skin. Each of the five colors appears in its own regional area; one examines whether it is floating or sunken to know whether the disease is shallow or deep; one examines whether it is moist or withered to observe whether the disease will resolve or worsen; one examines whether it is scattered or compact to know whether onset is near or distant; one observes whether the color is above or below to know the location of the disease; one concentrates the spirit in the heart-mind to know past and present. Thus, if one's reading of the qi is not subtle and attentive, one cannot know right from wrong; one must fix the attention and not let it wander, and only then can one distinguish the new from the old. When the color is bright and not coarse, but sunken and lifeless, the disease is severe; when the color is neither bright nor lustrous, the disease is not severe. When the color is scattered, flickering and indistinct, not yet gathered together, the disease too is scattered — the qi causes pain, but no accumulation has yet formed.
When the kidney overrides the heart, the heart falls ill first and the kidney responds in kind; the colors in every such case follow this same pattern.

In a man, when the color appears at the tip of the nose, this indicates pain in the lower abdomen; below that, it indicates pain in the testicles; when the color is round and straight, it indicates pain in the penis — the upper part corresponding to the root, the lower part to the glans — this is the category of fox-mounting hernia and swollen scrotum. In a woman, when the color appears at the tip of the nose, this indicates disease of the bladder and the uterus: if the color is scattered, this indicates pain; if it is compact, this indicates a mass. Whether the color is square or round, on the left or the right, each corresponds to the shape it takes. When it extends further downward, this indicates excessive discharge; when there is moistness like the sheen of grease, this indicates that the person has recently eaten unclean food to excess.

Left corresponds to left, and right corresponds to right. When the color harbors something pathological, it gathers and disperses irregularly, without proper order — this is what the color of the face indicates. As for the colors — green-blue, black, red, white, and yellow — each, when proper, is full and settled and has its own distinct region. When a color appears outside its proper region and is red, like the red color of elm-pod seeds in size, and it appears at the tip of the nose, death will come within a matter of days. When the tip of such a color points upward, its point directed toward the vertex, this indicates the disease is above; when its tip points downward, this indicates the disease is below — and this holds in the same way for left and right. The five viscera are named by the five colors: green-blue corresponds to the liver, red to the heart, white to the lung, yellow to the spleen, and black to the kidney. The liver is matched with the sinews, the heart with the vessels, the lung with the skin, the spleen with the flesh, and the kidney with the bones.

Huangdi asked Shaoyu, saying: 'Suppose there are people here walking together and standing together, equal in age, and equally dressed in clothing of the same thickness. If they suddenly encounter a fierce wind and violent rain, some fall ill and some do not; some fall ill altogether, and some remain entirely well. What is the reason for this?' Shaoyu said: 'Which aspect of this does Your Majesty wish to ask about most urgently?' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear it in full.' Shaoyu said: 'In spring there is the green-blue wind; in summer, the yang wind; in autumn, the cool wind; in winter, the cold wind. In each of these four seasons, the winds cause disease that takes a different form.'

Huangdi said: 'How do the winds of the four seasons cause people to fall ill?' Shaoyu said: 'A person with a yellow complexion, thin skin, and weak flesh cannot withstand the vacuous wind of spring; a person with a white complexion, thin skin, and weak flesh cannot withstand the vacuous wind of summer; a person with a green-blue complexion, thin skin, and weak flesh cannot withstand the vacuous wind of autumn; a person with a red complexion, thin skin, and weak flesh cannot withstand the vacuous wind of winter.' Huangdi said: 'Does a person with a black complexion never fall ill?' Shaoyu said: 'A person with a black complexion, thick skin, and firm flesh is indeed not injured by the winds of the four seasons. But if such a person's skin is thin and flesh is not firm, and the complexion is not uniform, then when the vacuous wind arrives at the height of late summer, illness results. If the person's skin is thick and muscles firm, then when the vacuous wind arrives at the height of late summer, no illness results. Yet even a person with thick skin and firm muscles, if doubly affected by cold — both from without and within — will then fall ill.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent.'

Huangdi said: 'Now, whether a person can endure pain or cannot endure pain is not simply a matter of courage or timidity. There are brave men who cannot endure pain: when they encounter danger they advance, but when they encounter pain they stop short. There are timid men who can endure pain: when they hear of danger they become afraid, yet when they meet with pain they do not flinch. There are brave men who can endure pain: when they encounter danger they show no fear, and when they meet with pain they do not flinch. And there are timid men who cannot endure pain: when they encounter danger and pain, their eyes dart about and their faces show distress, they are so frightened they cannot speak, they lose control of their qi, they are startled, their complexion changes, and they seem to hover between life and death. I have observed that this is so, but I do not know the reason for it — I wish to hear the explanation.' Shaoyu said: 'Whether a person can endure pain or cannot endure pain is determined by the thickness or thinness of the skin, the firmness or fragility of the flesh, and the slackness or tension of the tissues — it is not a matter of courage or timidity.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear how courage and timidity come about.' Shaoyu said: 'In the brave man, the eyes are deep-set and firm, with long, straight, upward-sweeping brows; the texture of the Triple Burner runs crosswise; his heart is upright and straight; his liver is large and firm; his gallbladder is full and set to the side. When angered, his qi becomes exuberant and his chest expands; the liver rises and the gallbladder turns crosswise; the canthi of his eyes split open and his gaze becomes fierce; his body hair stands on end and his face turns a greenish hue. This is how courage comes about in the brave man.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear how timidity comes about.' Shaoyu said: 'In the timid man, the eyes are large but lack the ability to hold their gaze steady; yin and yang lose their proper balance with each other; the texture of the Triple Burner runs lengthwise; the sternum is short and small; the ligament attaching the liver is slack; his gallbladder is not full and hangs loose; his intestines and stomach are elongated and straight; and there is emptiness beneath the rib-sides. Even when he is in the midst of great rage, his qi cannot fill his chest; though the liver and lung may rise, the qi soon weakens and sinks back down again — hence he cannot sustain his anger for long. This is how timidity comes about in the timid man.'

Huangdi said: 'When a timid man has drunk wine, his anger no longer defers even to a brave man — which viscus causes this?' Shaoyu said: 'Wine is the essence of water and grain, the fluid distilled from cooked grain; its qi is swift and fierce. When it enters the stomach, the stomach becomes distended, the qi rebels upward and fills the chest, the liver floats upward and the gallbladder turns crosswise. At such a moment, the timid man is indeed comparable to a brave man — but once the qi wanes, he regrets it. Though for the moment he resembles the brave man, he does not know how to restrain himself; this is called wine-derangement.'

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I wish to hear about the transport points of the five viscera that emerge on the back.' Qibo said: 'The great transport point of the back is located at the tip of the Zhu bone, the first thoracic vertebra. The Lung Transport point lies between the third vertebra; the Heart Transport point lies between the fifth vertebra; the Diaphragm Transport point lies between the seventh vertebra; the Liver Transport point lies between the ninth vertebra; the Spleen Transport point lies between the eleventh vertebra; and the Kidney Transport point lies between the fourteenth vertebra. All of these lie flanking the spine, about three cun to either side. To locate and verify them, one presses upon the spot, and if this produces a response within — the pain being relieved — this is indeed the transport point. These points may be treated with moxibustion, but not with needling. When the qi is exuberant, one drains it; when it is deficient, one supplements it. When supplementing with fire, one should not blow upon the flame, but let it die out on its own. When draining with fire, one should blow briskly upon the flame, causing the moxa to burn quickly, until the fire itself burns out.'

Huangdi said: 'The five viscera are that which store the essence, spirit, ethereal soul (hun), and corporeal soul (po); the six bowels are that which receive water and grain and carry out the transformation of substances. Their qi penetrates within to the five viscera, and connects without to the limbs and joints. The floating qi that does not follow the channels is defensive qi; the essential qi that travels through the channels is nutritive qi. Yin and yang follow one another, exterior and interior interlink, like a ring without end — so tranquil and so pure and undivided that who could ever steal it away? Yet in distinguishing yin and yang, each channel has its branch and root, and its places of separation between deficiency and excess. One who can distinguish the twelve channels of yin and yang can know whence disease arises. One who observes where deficiency and excess reside can determine whether the disease is located above or below. One who knows the qi-thoroughfares of the six bowels can know how to resolve knots and bind together the gateways of the body. One who knows whether a deficient or a replete condition is soft or hard can know where to supplement and where to drain. One who knows the branches and roots of the six channels can be free of confusion throughout the world.'

Qibo said: 'How broad is the sage sovereign's discourse! Allow your servant to express his understanding fully. The root of the Foot Taiyang channel lies within five cun above the heel; its branch lies at the two linking vessels of the Gate of Life. The Gate of Life is the eyes. The root of the Foot Shaoyang channel lies between Qiaoyin; its branch lies in front of the Window Cage. The Window Cage is the ear. The root of the Foot Shaoyin channel lies within three cun above the inner ankle; its branch lies at the back transport points and the two vessels beneath the tongue. The root of the Foot Jueyin channel lies about five cun above Xingjian; its branch lies at the back transport points. The root of the Foot Yangming channel lies at Lidui; its branch lies at Renying, at the cheeks flanking the pharynx. The root of the Foot Taiyin channel lies within four cun above and in front of Zhongfeng; its branch lies at the back transport points and the root of the tongue.'

The root of the Hand Taiyang channel lies behind the outer wrist bone; its branch lies one cun above the Gate of Life. The root of the Hand Shaoyang channel lies two cun above the point between the fourth and little fingers; its branch lies behind the ear, below the upper corner and the outer canthus. The root of the Hand Yangming channel lies within the elbow bone, extending up to Biyang; its branch lies below the forehead where it meets above the cheekbone. The root of the Hand Taiyin channel lies at the Inch Opening; its branch lies at the pulsating vessel within the armpit. The root of the Hand Shaoyin channel lies at the tip of the styloid bone at the wrist; its branch lies at the back transport points. The root of the Hand Jueyin, the Pericardium channel, lies two cun above the wrist, between the two sinews; its branch lies three cun below the armpit.

In examining these, when the lower root is deficient, reversal-cold occurs; when the lower is exuberant, heat occurs. When the upper branch is deficient, dizziness occurs; when the upper is exuberant, heat and pain occur. Thus, where there is hardness and stagnation, one should cut it off and halt it; where there is deficiency, one should draw the qi up and raise it.

Allow me to speak of the qi-thoroughfares: the chest has its qi-thoroughfare, the abdomen has its qi-thoroughfare, the head has its qi-thoroughfare, and the shin has its qi-thoroughfare. Thus, when qi is located in the head, one halts it at the brain; when qi is located in the chest, one halts it at the chest and the back transport points; when qi is located in the abdomen, one halts it at the back transport points and at the pulsating vessels of the Penetrating Vessel to the left and right of the navel; when qi is located in the shin, one halts it at Qijie, the qi-thoroughfare point, and at Chengshan and the points above the ankle. To needle these points, one uses the fine filiform needle; one must first press upon the point for a long while until a response is felt beneath the hand, and only then does one needle and administer treatment. What may be treated in this way includes headache, dizziness and collapse, abdominal pain with fullness and sudden distension, and newly formed accumulations. When the pain shifts from place to place, the condition is easy to cure; when there is an accumulation without pain, it is difficult to cure.

Huangdi asked Shaoyu, saying: 'The strength or weakness of the sinews and bones, the firmness or fragility of the muscles and flesh, the thickness or thinness of the skin, and the looseness or density of the interstitial texture all differ from person to person — how does this affect the pain of needling, stone therapy, and cauterization? The thickness, thinness, firmness, and fragility of the intestines and stomach also differ — how does this affect the response to toxic medicinals? I wish to hear it all.' Shaoyu said: 'A person whose bones are strong, sinews weak, flesh relaxed, and skin thick can endure pain; the same holds true for the pain of needling, stone therapy, and cauterization.'

Huangdi said: 'How can one know whether a person can endure cauterization?' Shaoyu answered: 'A person who, in addition, has a black complexion and well-formed bones can endure cauterization.' Huangdi said: 'How can one know whether a person cannot endure the pain of needling and stone therapy?' Shaoyu said: 'A person with firm flesh and thin skin cannot endure the pain of needling and stone therapy, and the same holds true for cauterization.'

Huangdi said: 'When people fall ill, some are injured at the same time, yet some recover easily while others recover with difficulty — what is the reason for this?' Shaoyu said: 'Among those injured at the same time, those whose bodies run more toward heat recover easily; those whose bodies run more toward cold recover with difficulty.'

Huangdi said: 'How can one know whether a person can tolerate toxic medicinals?' Shaoyu said: 'Those with a thick stomach, a black complexion, large bones, and a stout build can all tolerate toxic medicinals; thus those who are thin and have a thin stomach cannot tolerate toxic medicinals.'

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I wish to hear, concerning the beginning of human life, what qi constructs its foundation, and what stands as its shield; what is lost that brings death, and what is gained that brings life?' Qibo said: 'The mother serves as the foundation, and the father serves as the shield. One who loses the spirit dies; one who gains the spirit lives.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by spirit?' Qibo said: 'When the blood and qi have become harmonized, when nutritive and defensive qi have become connected, when the five viscera have become formed, when the spirit qi has taken up residence in the heart, and when the ethereal and corporeal souls are both fully present — then the person is complete.'

Huangdi said: 'People's lifespans differ, long or short — some die young, some die suddenly, and some suffer long illness. I wish to hear the principle behind this.' Qibo said: 'When the five viscera are firm and solid, the blood vessels harmonious and well-regulated, the muscles and flesh smoothly functioning, the skin tight and dense, the movement of nutritive and defensive qi never departing from its normal course, the breath gentle and unhurried, the qi moving according to its proper measure, the six bowels transforming grain properly, and the body fluids being spread and dispersed each in its accustomed way — then a person is able to live long.'

Huangdi said: 'What brings it about that a person lives to a hundred years before dying?' Qibo said: 'When the philtrum — the groove between the nose and mouth — is deep and long, when the foundation and walls of the face are high and squared, when the nutritive and defensive qi flow freely and in proper order, when the three sections of the face and the Sanli points are well-raised, and when the bones stand high and the flesh is full — then a person can live out a hundred years before reaching the end.'

Huangdi said: 'May I hear about the waxing and waning of a person's qi, leading up to death?' Qibo said: 'At ten years of age, a person's five viscera have just become settled, the blood and qi have already become connected, and the qi is concentrated in the lower body — hence the person loves to run. At twenty, the blood and qi begin to flourish and the muscles and flesh are just growing — hence the person loves to walk briskly. At thirty, the five viscera are fully settled, the muscles and flesh are firm and solid, and the blood vessels are full and abundant — hence the person loves to walk at a steady pace. At forty, the five viscera, six bowels, and twelve channels are all at their fullest and most settled; the interstitial texture begins to loosen, the luster of the complexion begins to fade, the hair begins to turn gray in patches, and the person, being at a settled and unshifting peak, loves to sit. At fifty, the liver qi begins to decline, the liver's lobes begin to thin, the bile begins to diminish, and the eyes begin to lose their clarity of vision. At sixty, the heart qi begins to decline, the person is prone to worry and sorrow, the blood and qi grow sluggish and idle — hence the person loves to lie down. At seventy, the spleen qi becomes deficient and the skin withers. At eighty, the lung qi declines, the corporeal soul departs, and hence the person's speech grows prone to error. At ninety, the kidney qi is scorched and the channels of the remaining four viscera become empty and hollow. At a hundred, the five viscera are all deficient, the spirit qi has entirely departed, and only the physical frame remains, until at last life comes to an end.'

Huangdi said: 'What of those who die before living out their full allotted years?' Qibo said: 'In such people, the five viscera are none of them firm and solid; the philtrum is not long, but hollow and flared outward; their breathing is labored and hurried. Moreover, their foundation and walls are low and thin, their vessels carry little blood, their flesh lacks the firmness of stone; they are repeatedly struck by wind and cold, their blood and qi are deficient, their vessels fail to flow freely; true qi and evil qi attack one another, thrown into disorder and drawing upon each other — hence such people die at a middling age, their years cut short.'

Huangdi asked Bogao, saying: 'I have heard that qi has its states of counterflow and compliance, that the pulse has its states of exuberance and decline, and that needling has its overarching principles — may I hear about these?' Bogao said: 'The counterflow and compliance of qi correspond to Heaven and Earth, yin and yang, the four seasons, and the five phases. The exuberance and decline of the pulse serve to indicate the deficiency, excess, surplus, or insufficiency of blood and qi. The overarching principle of needling is that one must clearly understand when a disease can be needled, when it cannot yet be needled, and when it can no longer be needled.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one observe this?' Bogao said: 'The art of war says: do not meet head-on an army advancing with banners flying in full array; do not strike an enemy formation arrayed in imposing order. Similarly, the art of needling says: do not needle a blazing, scorching fever; do not needle a profusely dripping sweat; do not needle a pulse that is turbulent and chaotic; do not needle when the disease and the pulse are in mutual contradiction.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one determine when a disease may be needled?' Bogao said: 'The superior physician needles a disease before it has arisen; the next best physician needles it before it has become exuberant; the next needles it after it has already begun to decline. The inferior physician needles a disease just as it is striking; needles when the body's form is at its most exuberant; and needles when the disease and the pulse are in mutual contradiction. Hence it is said: while a disease is at its height, one must not dare to attack it; needle it only once it has begun to decline, and the outcome will surely be excellent. Hence it is said: the superior physician treats illness that has not yet arisen, not illness that has already arisen — this is precisely what is meant.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear how the five flavors of grain qi are distributed as they enter the five viscera.' Bogao said: 'The stomach is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; water and grain all enter the stomach, and the five viscera and six bowels all draw their qi from the stomach. Each of the five flavors travels to the viscus it favors: the sour flavor of grain travels first to the liver; the bitter flavor of grain travels first to the heart; the sweet flavor of grain travels first to the spleen; the pungent flavor of grain travels first to the lung; the salty flavor of grain travels first to the kidney. Once the qi and fluids of grain have circulated, and the nutritive and defensive qi have become freely connected, the dregs are then transformed and passed down in due order.'

Huangdi said: 'How do the nutritive and defensive qi circulate?' Bogao said: 'When grain first enters the stomach, its essential and subtle portion first emerges from the two burners, the upper and middle burners, of the stomach, to irrigate the five viscera; it separates and travels along two distinct pathways, the paths of nutritive and defensive qi. The massed great qi that gathers together and does not circulate accumulates within the chest; this is called the Sea of Qi. It emerges from the lung and follows the throat, so that upon exhalation it is expelled, and upon inhalation it is drawn in. Of the essential qi of Heaven and Earth, the general rule is that three parts are expelled for every one part drawn in. Thus, if grain does not enter the body, after half a day the qi declines, and after a full day the qi grows scant.'

Huangdi said: 'May I hear of the five flavors of the grains?' Bogao said: 'Allow me to describe them fully. Among the five grains: unhulled rice is sweet, hemp seed is sour, soybeans are salty, wheat is bitter, and yellow millet is pungent. Among the five fruits: jujubes are sweet, plums are sour, chestnuts are salty, apricots are bitter, and peaches are pungent. Among the five domestic animals: beef is sweet, dog meat is sour, pork is salty, mutton is bitter, and chicken is pungent. Among the five vegetables: mallow is sweet, chives are sour, bean leaves are salty, scallions are bitter, and spring onions are pungent.'

As for the five colors: yellow is suited to the sweet flavor, green-blue to the sour flavor, black to the salty flavor, red to the bitter flavor, and white to the pungent flavor. In each of these five correspondences, each color has the flavor suited to it. Speaking of the five suitable correspondences in terms of the five colors and the viscera they represent: for a person with disease of the spleen, it is suitable to eat rice, beef, jujubes, and mallow; for a person with disease of the heart, it is suitable to eat wheat, mutton, apricots, and scallions; for a person with disease of the kidney, it is suitable to eat soybeans, sprouted soybeans, pork, chestnuts, and bean leaves; for a person with disease of the liver, it is suitable to eat hemp seed, dog meat, plums, and chives; for a person with disease of the lung, it is suitable to eat yellow millet, chicken, peaches, and spring onions.

The five prohibitions: in disease of the liver, avoid the pungent flavor; in disease of the heart, avoid the salty flavor; in disease of the spleen, avoid the sour flavor; in disease of the kidney, avoid the sweet flavor; in disease of the lung, avoid the bitter flavor.

The liver's color is green-blue, and it is suited to the sweet flavor; rice, beef, jujubes, and mallow are all sweet. The heart's color is red, and it is suited to the sour flavor; dog meat, hemp seed, plums, and chives are all sour. The spleen's color is yellow, and it is suited to the salty flavor; soybeans, pork, chestnuts, and bean leaves are all salty. The lung's color is white, and it is suited to the bitter flavor; wheat, mutton, apricots, and scallions are all bitter. The kidney's color is black, and it is suited to the pungent flavor; yellow millet, chicken, peaches, and spring onions are all pungent.

Huangdi asked Qibo: "How does one distinguish water swelling (shuizhang) from skin swelling (fuzhang), drum distension (guzhang), intestinal accumulation (changtan), stone conglomeration (shijia), and stone water (shishui)?" Qibo replied: "When water swelling first begins, there is slight swelling above the eye sockets, as if one had just risen from sleep; the neck vessel throbs, there is occasional coughing, cold is felt between the inner thighs, the feet and shins swell, and then the abdomen becomes large — at this point the water has fully formed. If one presses the abdomen with the hand, it rises back up as the hand is withdrawn, as though water were wrapped within — this is its sign."

Huangdi said: "How does one recognize skin swelling?" Qibo said: "In skin swelling, cold qi lodges between the skin and flesh, producing a hollow drumming sound without firmness; the abdomen is large, the entire body is swollen, and the skin is thickened. When one presses the abdomen, it stays depressed and does not rise back up, and the color of the abdomen does not change — this is its sign."

"What of drum distension?" Qibo said: "In drum distension, the abdomen is distended and the whole body is enlarged, its size equal to that of skin swelling, but the complexion is greenish-yellow and the abdominal veins stand out — this is its sign."

"What of intestinal accumulation?" Qibo said: "Cold qi lodges outside the intestines and contends with the defensive qi; the qi cannot flow to nourish that place, and so becomes fixed and attached there, forming an accumulation lodged within. Malign qi then arises, and a fleshy excrescence is generated. At its onset it is as large as a hen's egg; gradually it grows larger, until, once fully formed, it resembles pregnancy. In a case of long standing it may persist for more than a year. When pressed it is firm, when pushed it moves, and the woman's monthly courses still come at their proper time — this is its sign."

"What of stone conglomeration?" Qibo said: "Stone conglomeration arises within the uterus. Cold qi lodges in the gate of the womb, so that the gate of the womb becomes closed and blocked, and the qi cannot pass through. Malign blood that should be discharged is not discharged; it collects and stagnates, remaining fixed in place, and grows larger day by day, taking on the appearance of pregnancy. The monthly courses fail to come at their proper time. This condition arises only in women, and can be treated by guiding the stagnant blood downward and out."

Huangdi said: "Can skin swelling and drum distension be treated by needling?" Qibo said: "First drain the blood in the network vessels of the distended area, and afterward regulate the channels — needle and remove the stagnant blood from the blood network vessels."

Huangdi said: "You have said that thief winds and evil qi injure people and cause illness. But now there are those who never leave their shelter, who never go out from within their chambers, and yet suddenly fall ill — this is not because they have failed to avoid thief winds and evil qi. What is the reason for this?" Qibo said: "These are all cases in which the person had previously been injured by damp qi, which became hidden within the blood vessels and between the muscle divisions, lingering there for a long time without leaving. Or perhaps there had been a fall or injury, so that malign blood remained within and was not dispelled. Then, suddenly, joy or anger became unregulated, eating and drinking became improper, or cold and warmth were not seasonable, causing the interstitial texture to close and become impassable. If the interstices then open and one encounters wind and cold, the qi and blood congeal and bind together, and this joins with the old lingering evil to become cold impediment. If instead there is heat, sweating occurs, and sweating exposes one to wind — thus even without directly encountering thief wind and evil qi, illness will inevitably erupt because of these compounding causes."

Huangdi said: "What you have described are all cases the patient can recall for himself. But what of one who has encountered no evil qi and has had no fright or apprehension in his mind, yet suddenly falls ill — what is the reason for this? Is it perhaps due to the doings of ghosts and spirits?" Qibo said: "This too is a case where an old evil has lingered without yet erupting. Then, because the mind comes to harbor aversion toward something, or attachment and longing for something, the qi and blood become disordered within, and the two qi contend with one another. Since the origin of this is subtle, it cannot be seen by looking nor heard by listening, and so it seems to resemble the doings of ghosts and spirits."

Huangdi said: "What is the reason that such illness can be cured by incantation alone?" Qibo said: "The shamans of old, because they understood what could overcome the hundred diseases, and knew in advance the origin from which each illness arose, were able to cure it by incantation alone."

Huangdi said: "When defensive qi lingers within the abdomen, contracting into an accumulation that fails to move, stagnating and pent up without a constant place, causing a person to feel fullness in the rib-sides and stomach, panting and gasping with rebellious breath — how can this be dispelled?" Bogao said: "When the qi accumulates in the chest, treat it from above; when it accumulates in the abdomen, treat it from below; when both above and below are full, treat it from the sides."

Huangdi said: "How is this treated?" Bogao replied: "When the accumulation is above, drain Renying (ST-9), Tiantu (CV-22), and the points within the throat. When it is below, drain Zusanli (ST-36) and Qichong (ST-30). When both above and below are full, treat both above and below together, along with the point one cun below the free rib. In severe cases, use the chicken-foot needling technique (three oblique insertions radiating out like a fowl's foot). But if on examination the pulse is large, wiry, and taut, or intermittent and failing to arrive, or if the skin of the abdomen is extremely tense, one must not needle." Huangdi said: "Good."

Huangdi asked Bogao: "How does one know whether an illness is located in the skin, the flesh, the qi and blood, the sinews, or the bones?" Bogao said: "When a thin, glossy color appears above the two eyebrows, the disease is in the skin. When the lips show a green-blue, yellow, red, white, or black color, the disease is in the muscles and flesh. When the nutritive qi appears moist and glistening, the disease is in the blood and qi. When the eyes show a green-blue, yellow, red, white, or black color, the disease is in the sinews. When the ears appear scorched and withered, coated with dust and grime, the disease is in the bones."

Huangdi said: "What are the forms these diseases take, and how are they treated?" Bogao said: "The transformations of the hundred diseases are too numerous to count in full; yet the skin has its regions, the flesh has its pillars, the qi and blood have their transport points, and the bones have their attachments." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear the reasons for this." Bogao said: "The regions of the skin are conveyed to the four extremities. The pillars of the flesh are found between the muscle divisions of the yang channels of the arms and legs, and in the muscle divisions along the Foot Shaoyin channel. The transport points of the qi and blood are conveyed to the various network vessels; where qi and blood linger and collect, there they become abundant and rise up prominently. The regions of the sinews have no fixed yin or yang, no fixed left or right, but wherever they are found, one may observe the location of the disease there. The attachments of the bones are the bone-hollows through which nourishment is received and by which the brain and marrow are nourished."

Huangdi said: "How, then, is treatment applied?" Bogao said: "The transformations of disease — floating or sunken, deep or shallow — cannot be exhaustively investigated, yet each has its proper location. When the illness is mild, needle shallowly; when severe, needle deeply. When mild, use few needles; when severe, use many. One regulates the qi according to these transformations — this is what is called a superior physician."

Huangdi asked Bogao: "People differ in being fat or thin, large or small, warm or cold in constitution, and in being old, robust, young, or small — how does one distinguish these categories?" Bogao replied: "A person past fifty years of age is called old; past twenty, robust; past eighteen, young; and past six, a child."

Huangdi said: "How does one measure and know whether a person is fat or thin?" Bogao said: "Among people there are those who are fei (sturdy-fat), those who are gao (soft-fat), and those who are rou (fleshy)." Huangdi said: "How does one distinguish these?" Bogao said: "One whose flesh at the back of the knee is firm and whose skin is full is fei. One whose flesh at the back of the knee is not firm and whose skin is loose is gao. One whose skin and flesh do not separate from each other is rou."

Huangdi said: "What of the warmth or coldness of the body?" Bogao said: "In the gao type, the flesh is soft and moist; if the texture is coarse, the body is cold, and if the texture is fine, the body is hot. In the zhi (firm-fat) type, the flesh is firm; if the texture is fine, the body is hot, and if the texture is coarse, the body is cold."

Huangdi said: "What of their fatness, thinness, largeness, and smallness?" Bogao said: "In the gao type, there is abundant qi and the skin is loose and slack, so the abdomen hangs loosely and the flanks droop. In the rou type, the body and frame are ample and large. In the zhi type, the body is compact and small."

Huangdi said: "What of the relative amounts of qi and blood in these three types?" Bogao said: "The gao type has abundant qi; abundant qi produces heat, and heat allows one to withstand cold. The rou type has abundant blood, which fills out the form, and a filled-out form is well balanced. The zhi type has clear, thin blood and scant, slippery qi, and so cannot grow large. These are what distinguish such people from the ordinary run of people."

Huangdi said: "What of the ordinary person?" Bogao said: "In the ordinary person, the skin, flesh, zhi, and gao do not exceed one another, and the blood and qi are not excessively abundant in either; hence the form is neither too small nor too large, but each part is proportionate to the body as a whole — such a person is called an ordinary person."

Huangdi said: "Good. How, then, should treatment be applied?" Bogao said: "One must first distinguish the three body types, the relative abundance of blood, and the clarity or turbidity of qi, and only afterward regulate accordingly, so that treatment does not depart from its constant norms. Thus the gao type has a loosely hanging abdomen and drooping flanks; the rou type is ample and large both above and below; and the zhi type, although fatty, cannot grow large."

Huangdi said: "I had thought the small needle to be a trifling thing, yet you speak of it as being above united with Heaven, below united with Earth, and in the middle united with man — I feel this goes beyond the true meaning of the needle. I wish to hear your reasoning." Qibo said: "What thing is greater than Heaven? Yet greater in significance than the needle are only the five weapons, and the five weapons are instruments prepared for death, not implements for life. Now, man is that which stabilizes Heaven and Earth — must this not be taken into account? In governing the people, one also relies solely on the needle. Between the needle and the five weapons, which, then, is truly the lesser?"

Huangdi said: "When disease arises, there is sometimes unpredictable joy and anger, immoderate eating and drinking; the yin qi becomes insufficient, the yang qi becomes excessive, and the nutritive qi fails to circulate, and thus welling-abscess and flat-abscess erupt. When yin and yang fail to communicate, the two heats contend with one another and transform into pus — can the small needle remove this?" Qibo said: "Even the sage cannot make what has already transformed undo itself; once evil has taken hold, it cannot simply be left to remain. Consider: when two armies stand opposed, their banners facing one another, bare blades arrayed across the open field — this is not the outcome of a single day's planning. To make one's soldiers obey commands, and to keep the rank and file free of the calamity of bared blades, is not the achievement of a single day's instruction, nor something gained in a mere instant. So too, to allow the body to reach the point of suffering welling-abscess and flat-abscess, with pus and blood gathering within — is this not to have strayed far from the Way? The generation of welling-abscess and flat-abscess, the formation of pus and blood, does not descend from Heaven nor rise up from Earth — it is generated from the accumulation of subtle beginnings. Thus the sage treats the condition before it has taken form, while the fool encounters it only after it has already formed."

Huangdi said: "But suppose one does not encounter it before it has taken form, and does not see it before the pus has already formed — what is to be done then?" Qibo said: "Once pus has already formed, of ten cases nine die and only one survives. This is why the sage does not allow it to reach completion, but clearly sets forth excellent prescriptions, inscribes them on bamboo and silk, and has capable persons carry them forward and transmit them to later generations without end — precisely because one may fail to intervene in time."

Huangdi said: "But if one does encounter it only after pus and blood have already formed, can it not be guided out and treated with the small needle?" Qibo said: "To treat a small matter with a small instrument yields only small results; to treat a great matter with a great instrument brings much harm. Thus, once pus and blood have already formed, only the stone lancet and the pointed blade-needle can remove it."

Huangdi said: "Since much harm may result, can such cases never be brought to a full recovery?" Qibo said: "This depends on whether the signs are contrary or favorable." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of the contrary and favorable signs." Qibo said: "In one already so injured, if the white of the eye turns bluish-green and the black of the eye grows small, this is the first contrary sign. If, upon taking medicine, the patient vomits, this is the second contrary sign. If there is abdominal pain with severe thirst, this is the third contrary sign. If the shoulders and neck become unable to move freely, this is the fourth contrary sign. If the voice becomes hoarse and the complexion drains of color, this is the fifth contrary sign. Where these five signs are absent, the case is favorable."

Huangdi said: "All diseases have their contrary and favorable signs — may I hear of these as well?" Qibo said: "Abdominal distension, fever, and a large pulse — this is the first contrary sign. Rumbling and fullness in the abdomen, cold limbs with diarrhea, and a large pulse — this is the second contrary sign. Unremitting nosebleed with a large pulse — this is the third contrary sign. Coughing with blood in the urine and wasting of the body, with a small, taut pulse — this is the fourth contrary sign. Coughing with wasting of the body, fever, and a small, rapid pulse — this is called the fifth contrary sign. In cases such as these, death occurs within no more than fifteen days."

Great abdominal distension, cold in the four extremities, wasting of the body, and severe diarrhea — this is the first contrary sign. Abdominal distension with blood in the stool, and a large, intermittently interrupted pulse — this is the second contrary sign. Coughing with blood in the urine, wasting of the flesh and body, and a beating, forceful pulse — this is the third contrary sign. Vomiting blood, fullness in the chest drawing into the back, with a small, rapid pulse — this is the fourth contrary sign. Coughing and vomiting, abdominal distension together with diarrhea of undigested food, and an interrupted pulse — this is the fifth contrary sign. In cases such as these, death occurs within less than a single two-hour period. A physician who fails to discern these signs and needles the patient regardless is said to be treating contrary to the proper course.

Huangdi said: "Your account of the needle is truly magnificent — matched to Heaven and Earth, reckoning the patterns of the heavens above and measuring the order of the earth below, distinguishing the five viscera within, arranging the six bowels without, encompassing the twenty-eight meeting-points of the channel vessels, complete with all their cyclical order. Yet it can kill a living person, and cannot revive the dead — can you reverse this?" Qibo said: "It can kill a living person; it cannot revive the dead." Huangdi said: "Hearing this, I would consider it unkind, and yet I wish to hear this teaching, though I will not put it into practice on others." Qibo said: "This is a manifest truth, and it must necessarily be so — like a blade or sword that can kill a person, or like wine that can make a person drunk: even without examining the patient, this much can already be known."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear this fully." Qibo said: "That from which a person receives qi is grain. That into which grain pours is the stomach. The stomach is the sea of water, grain, qi, and blood. That through which the sea sends forth cloud-like qi is all under Heaven. That through which the stomach sends forth qi and blood is the channel passageways. These passageways are the great network vessels of the five viscera and six bowels — one need only intercept and drain them there."

Huangdi said: "Is there a fixed count for above and below?" Qibo said: "If one intercepts qi at Wuli (LI-13), it should be stopped after the middle passage; five arrivals and it is done — for after five passes, the qi of the viscera there is exhausted. Thus five times five is twenty-five, and the transport point's qi is drained to its limit. This is what is called robbing the patient of his heavenly-endowed qi; it does not sever his life or curtail his allotted years." Huangdi said: "I wish to hear this fully." Qibo said: "One who peers in at the gate and then needles there causes death within the household; one who has already entered the gate and then needles there causes death in the hall itself." Huangdi said: "Excellent is this method, and luminous is this Way. I ask that it be inscribed upon a jade tablet, to be treasured as a weighty treasure and transmitted to later generations as a prohibition upon needling, so that the people will not dare transgress it."

Huangdi asked Qibo: "I have heard that in needling there are five prohibitions — what is meant by the five prohibitions?" Qibo said: "These are prohibitions against needling when it must not be done." Huangdi said: "I have heard that in needling there are five depletions." Qibo said: "One must not drain that which cannot afford to be taken from." Huangdi said: "I have heard that in needling there are five excesses." Qibo said: "In supplementing and draining, one must not exceed the proper degree." Huangdi said: "I have heard that in needling there are five contrary signs." Qibo said: "When the disease and the pulse are at odds with one another, this is called the five contrary signs." Huangdi said: "I have heard that in needling there are nine appropriatenesses." Qibo said: "To have a clear understanding of the theory of the nine needles — this is called the nine appropriatenesses."

Huangdi said: "What are the five prohibitions? I wish to hear the times when needling must not be performed." Qibo said: "On jia and yi days — when wood qi compounds upon itself — one must not needle to drain excess, nor use the Fameng ('Rousing from Befuddlement') technique within the ear. On bing and ding days — when fire qi compounds upon itself — one must not use the Zhen'ai ('Shaking Off Dust') technique at the shoulder, throat, or Lianquan (CV-23). On wu and ji days — when earth qi compounds upon itself, governing the four intervening seasons — one must not needle the abdomen, nor pare the nails to drain water excess. On geng and xin days — when metal qi compounds upon itself — one must not needle the joints of the thigh and knee. On ren and gui days — when water qi compounds upon itself — one must not needle the foot and shin. These are called the five prohibitions."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by the five depletions?" Qibo said: "When the body and flesh are already depleted, this is the first depletion. After a great loss of blood, this is the second depletion. After profuse sweating, this is the third depletion. After severe diarrhea, this is the fourth depletion. After recent childbirth, or after a great loss of blood, this is the fifth depletion. In none of these cases may one drain."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by the five contrary signs?" Qibo said: "In heat disease, when the pulse is calm after sweating has occurred, but then becomes full and agitated — this is the first contrary sign. In diarrheal disease, when the pulse is surging and large — this is the second contrary sign. In fixed impediment where the affliction does not shift and the muscular flesh breaks down, with fever and a pulse that is interrupted on one side — this is the third contrary sign. In excessive wasting of the body with fever, when the complexion turns a lifeless white, and afterward blood appears in the stool or as nosebleed, with the bleeding severe and unrelenting — this is called the fourth contrary sign. In cold-heat disease with wasting of the body and a pulse that is hard and forcefully beating — this is called the fifth contrary sign."

Huangdi said: "Among the twelve channels, only the Hand Taiyin, Foot Shaoyin, and Foot Yangming pulse ceaselessly — why is this?" Qibo said: "This is properly explained by the vessel of the stomach. The stomach is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; its clear qi rises and pours into the lung, and the lung qi then travels by way of the Taiyin. Its movement proceeds together with the breath, coming and going: thus with each exhalation the vessel pulses twice, and with each inhalation the vessel likewise pulses twice. As the breathing never ceases, so the pulsing never stops."

Huangdi said: "When the qi passes through the Cunkou (the pulse-taking point at the wrist), it rises for ten breaths and then sinks and hides for eight breaths — by what pathway does it circle back? I do not understand where this cycle ends."

Qibo said: "When qi departs from the viscera, it does so suddenly, like an arrow loosed from a crossbow, like water rushing down from a bank. It rises to the thenar eminence and there begins to wane; its remaining qi, waning and dispersing, moves contrarily upward, and so its movement there becomes faint."

Huangdi said: "What is the reason the Foot Yangming pulses?" Qibo said: "The stomach qi rises and pours into the lung; its fierce, swift portion surges upward to the head, following the throat, ascending through the body's open orifices, following the eye connector, entering to net the brain, emerging at the cheek, descending to Kezhuren (GB-3), following the jaw, joining with the Yangming, and together descending to Renying (ST-9) — this is the branch of stomach qi that runs separately along the Yangming. Thus yin and yang, above and below, pulse as though they were one. Hence, if a yang disease occurs and the yang pulse is small, this is contrary; if a yin disease occurs and the yin pulse is large, this is contrary. Thus when yin and yang are both calm or both agitated together, as though pulled taut by a single cord tilting to one side, this indicates disease."

Huangdi said: "What is the reason the Foot Shaoyin pulses?" Qibo said: "The Penetrating Vessel is the sea of the twelve channels. Together with the great network vessel of the Shaoyin, it arises below the kidney, emerges at Qichong (ST-30), follows the inner border of the thigh, slants into the popliteal fossa, follows the inner border of the shinbone, runs alongside the Shaoyin channel, and descends to enter behind the inner ankle. It then enters the sole of the foot. Its branch slants into the ankle, emerges to connect with the instep, enters between the great toe, and pours into the various network vessels, so as to warm the foot and shin — this is the vessel whose pulsing is constant."

Huangdi said: "The movement of nutritive and defensive qi passes through above and below, joined together like a ring without end. Now, if one suddenly encounters evil wind, or meets with severe cold, so that the hands and feet become slack and weak, and along the pathway of yin and yang vessels, at the junctures where they convey to one another, their movement becomes disrupted — by what route does the qi return?" Qibo said: "The meeting-places of yin and yang at the four extremities are the most concentrated network vessels of the qi; the four thoroughfares (qi jie) are the direct pathways of the qi. Thus when a network vessel is cut off, the direct pathway remains open; when the four extremities become slack, the qi still follows through to meet, conveying to one another as in a ring." Huangdi said: "Excellent. This is precisely what is meant by 'like a ring without end, no one knowing its beginning, ending only to begin again.'"

Huangdi asked Shaoyu: "When the five flavors enter the mouth, each travels to its own destination and each gives rise to its own disorder. Sour travels to the sinews, and eaten to excess it causes urinary blockage (long). Salty travels to the blood, and eaten to excess it causes thirst. Pungent travels to the qi, and eaten to excess it causes a hollow, gnawing sensation in the heart. Bitter travels to the bones, and eaten to excess it causes retching. Sweet travels to the flesh, and eaten to excess it causes oppression in the heart. I know that this is so, but I do not know the reason why — I wish to hear the explanation." Shaoyu replied: "Sour enters the stomach; its qi is astringent and constricting. Rising to the two upper burners, it cannot pass freely in or out; failing to be dispersed, it lingers within the stomach. When the stomach is warm and harmonious, it pours downward into the bladder, but the bladder's thin covering is delicate and yielding, and upon receiving the sour qi it contracts and shrivels, becoming constricted and impassable, so that the waterways fail to function — hence urinary blockage results. The genital region is where the accumulated sinews converge and terminate; hence sour, upon entering, travels to the sinews."

Huangdi said: "Salty travels to the blood, and eaten to excess it causes thirst — why is this?" Shaoyu said: "Salty enters the stomach; its qi travels upward to the middle burner and pours into the vessels, whereupon the blood and qi travel with it. When blood and salt combine, coagulation occurs; once coagulation occurs, the fluids of the stomach pour in to compensate, and once they pour in, the stomach's fluids become exhausted; once exhausted, the pathway of the throat becomes parched, so that the root of the tongue dries and the person is prone to thirst. The blood vessels are the pathway of the middle burner; hence salty, upon entering, travels to the blood."

Huangdi said: "Pungent travels to the qi, and eaten to excess it causes a hollow, gnawing sensation in the heart — why is this?" Shaoyu said: "Pungent enters the stomach, and its qi travels to the upper burner. The upper burner is that which receives qi and nourishes the various yang regions. When the fumes of ginger and chives suffuse it, the nutritive and defensive qi cannot receive this in due measure, and it lingers long beneath the heart — hence the hollow, gnawing sensation. Pungent travels together with the qi; hence pungent, upon entering, is expelled together with the sweat."

Huangdi said: "Bitter travels to the bones, and eaten to excess it causes retching — why is this?" Shaoyu said: "Bitter enters the stomach, and the qi of the five grains cannot overcome it. As the bitter enters the lower stomach duct, the pathways of the three burners all close and become impassable — hence retching occurs. The teeth are where the bones converge and terminate; hence bitter, upon entering, travels to the bones, and, having entered, comes back out again — this is how one knows that it travels to the bones."

Huangdi said: "Sweet travels to the flesh, and eaten to excess it causes oppression in the heart — why is this?" Shaoyu said: "Sweet enters the stomach; its qi is weak and slight, and cannot rise all the way to the upper burner. Remaining together with the grain within the stomach, it makes the stomach soft and moist; when the stomach is soft, it becomes slack, and when slack, the parasitic worms are stirred to movement, and when the worms stir, this causes oppression in the heart. Its qi communicates outwardly with the flesh; hence sweet travels to the flesh."

灵枢经.title_en: The Twenty-Five Types of Persons Classified by Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I have heard of the yin-yang types of persons — what are they?' Bogao replied: 'Within Heaven and Earth, within the six directions, nothing departs from the five phases, and human beings correspond to them as well. Hence the five phases, each divided fivefold, give rise to the twenty-five types of persons — but the yin-yang types of persons are not among them. Among these twenty-five, there are also five whose bearing does not conform to the ordinary pattern, and these I already understand. I wish to hear of the bodily forms of the twenty-five types, of how they arise from blood and qi, so that, distinguishing them, one may use them as signs — knowing the interior from the exterior. How is this done?' Qibo said: 'How thorough a question this is! This is a secret handed down by my former teachers, one which even Bogao himself is not able to fully explain.' Huangdi rose from his mat, stepped back respectfully, and said: 'I have heard that to find the right person and not teach him is called a grave loss; yet to obtain such knowledge and disclose it carelessly, Heaven itself would despise the act. I wish only to obtain it and understand it clearly, to store it away in a casket of gold, and not dare to make it known abroad.' Qibo said: 'One must first establish the five bodily forms — metal, wood, water, fire, and earth — distinguish their five colors, and differentiate the persons of these five forms; then the twenty-five types will all be accounted for.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo said: 'This must be handled with the utmost care — allow your servant to speak of it.'

The wood-type person corresponds to shangjiao (upper jiao), jiao being the note of the pentatonic scale associated with the wood phase, and resembles the Blue Sovereign, one of the five legendary rulers of antiquity correlated with the five phases. Such a person is blue-green in complexion, small-headed, long-faced, broad-shouldered and straight-backed, with a small trunk and shapely hands and feet. He is talented, taxes his mind but has little physical strength, and is much given to worry; he labors hard at his affairs. He thrives in spring and summer but cannot tolerate autumn and winter — when affected by these seasons, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Jueyin channel, and carries himself with a settled, dignified bearing. The da-jiao (great jiao) type corresponds to the left Foot Shaoyang channel, and above the Shaoyang moves with an easy, yielding gait.
