The zuo-jiao (left jiao) type corresponds to the right Foot Shaoyang channel, and below the Shaoyang moves with a following, compliant bearing. The dai-jiao (metal-tipped jiao) type corresponds to the right Foot Shaoyang channel, and above the Shaoyang moves with a pushing, forward-leaning bearing. The pan-jiao (split jiao) type corresponds to the left Foot Shaoyang channel, and below the Shaoyang carries a withered, gaunt bearing.

The fire-type person corresponds to shangzhi (upper zhi), the note associated with the fire phase, and resembles the Red Sovereign. Such a person is red in complexion, broad in the ridges of the back, narrow in the face, small-headed, with fine shoulders and back, a small pelvis and abdomen, and small hands and feet. He walks with his feet planted firmly yet his heart urgent, and as he walks his shoulders sway and his back is well fleshed. He is spirited but careless with money, has little constancy, and is much given to anxious deliberation; he sees matters clearly and has a fine countenance, but being impetuous of heart he does not live long and may die suddenly. He thrives in spring and summer but cannot tolerate autumn and winter — in autumn and winter, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Hand Shaoyin channel, and carries himself with a firm, compact bearing. The zhi-zhi (substantive zhi) type corresponds to the left Hand Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang has a full-fleshed bearing. The shao-zhi (lesser zhi) type corresponds to the right Hand Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang moves with a leisurely, unhurried bearing. The you-zhi (right zhi) type corresponds to the right Hand Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a sharp, angular bearing. The zhi-pan (substantive-split zhi) type corresponds to the left Hand Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang carries a full-cheeked, ample-jawed bearing.

The earth-type person corresponds to shanggong (upper gong), the note associated with the earth phase, and resembles the Yellow Sovereign of high antiquity. Such a person is yellow in complexion, round-faced, large-headed, with fine shoulders and back, a large abdomen, shapely thighs and shins, small hands and feet, and an ample build; the upper and lower body are well proportioned, he walks with his feet planted firmly, yet lifts his feet lightly as he steps. He is calm at heart, delights in benefiting others, has no fondness for power or position, and readily attaches himself to others. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Taiyin channel, and carries himself with a solid, substantial bearing. The da-gong (great gong) type corresponds to the left Foot Yangming channel, and above the Yangming moves with a graceful, yielding bearing. The jia-gong (added gong) type corresponds to the left Foot Yangming channel, and below the Yangming moves with a halting, uneven bearing. The shao-gong (lesser gong) type corresponds to the right Foot Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries a pivoting, well-hinged bearing. The zuo-gong (left gong) type corresponds to the right Foot Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a lofty, towering bearing.

The metal-type person corresponds to shangshang (upper shang), the note associated with the metal phase, and resembles the White Sovereign. Such a person has a square face, white complexion, a small head, small shoulders and back, a small abdomen, small hands and feet; his bones seem to protrude at the heels, and his skeletal frame is light. He is upright and incorruptible in character, urgent of heart yet composed and resolute, and makes a fine official. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Hand Taiyin channel, and carries himself with a solid, substantial bearing. The dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) type corresponds to the left Hand Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries an incisive, well-defined bearing. The you-shang (right shang) type corresponds to the left Hand Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a loose, unrestrained bearing. The zuo-shang (left shang) type corresponds to the right Hand Yangming channel, and above the Yangming carries a watchful, scrutinizing bearing. The shao-shang (lesser shang) type corresponds to the right Hand Yangming channel, and below the Yangming carries a stern, severe bearing.

The water-type person corresponds to shangyu (upper yu), the note associated with the water phase, and resembles the Black Sovereign. Such a person is dark in complexion, with an uneven face, a large head, a narrow jaw, small shoulders, a large abdomen, and restless hands and feet; when he walks his body sways, his lower back and buttocks are long, and his back stretches out in a lengthy, drawn-out line. He shows little reverence or awe, is prone to deceiving and slighting others, and often meets a violent death. He thrives in autumn and winter but cannot tolerate spring and summer — in spring and summer, when affected, illness arises. He belongs to the Foot Shaoyin channel, and carries himself with a moist, yielding bearing. The da-yu (great yu) type corresponds to the right Foot Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a full-cheeked bearing. The shao-yu (lesser yu) type corresponds to the left Foot Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang carries a clean, spare bearing. The zhong-yu (multitude yu) type corresponds to the right Foot Taiyang channel, and below the Taiyang likewise carries a clean, spare bearing. The zhi-yu (fettered yu) type corresponds to the left Foot Taiyang channel, and above the Taiyang carries a settled, composed bearing. Thus the twenty-five variations among the five bodily-type persons are precisely what ordinary people confuse with one another.

Huangdi said: 'What of one who has the bodily form but not the corresponding color?' Qibo said: 'When the form overcomes the color, or the color overcomes the form, then, when the year arrives at which one phase overcomes the other, if the person is affected by pathogenic influence at that time, illness will follow; if he fails to take precautions, he will have cause for concern. When form and color correspond properly to one another, the person will enjoy wealth, honor, and great happiness.' Huangdi said: 'Can one know in advance the years at which form and color come to overcome one another?' Qibo said: 'In general, the years of taboo for all persons, above and below, follow a great rule: they recur every seven years — at sixteen, twenty-five, thirty-four, forty-three, fifty-two, and sixty-one years of age, these are all great taboo-years for a person, and one must take care to keep oneself at ease during them. If affected at such a time, illness will follow; if precautions are neglected, there will be cause for concern. At such times, one should refrain from reckless and unwholesome conduct — this is what is called the yearly taboo.'

Huangdi said: 'You have spoken of the upper and lower portions of the vessels, and of how the signs of blood and qi seem to reveal the state of one's bodily form — how does this work?' Qibo said: 'When blood and qi are abundant in the upper portion of the Foot Yangming channel, the beard grows fine and long; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the beard is short; when qi is scant and blood abundant, the beard is sparse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no beard at all, and the corners of the mouth show many fine lines. When blood and qi are abundant in the lower portion of the Foot Yangming channel, the pubic hair grows fine and reaches up to the chest; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the pubic hair is fine but shorter, reaching only to the navel, and in walking such a person tends to lift his feet high, with little flesh on the toes and feet that are prone to cold; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the feet are fleshy but prone to chilblains; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no hair at all, or if present it is sparse and withered, and such a person is prone to wilting weakness, reversal, and impediment of the feet.'

In the upper portion of the Foot Shaoyang channel, when qi and blood are abundant, the connecting whiskers linking the beard and sideburns grow fine and long; when blood is abundant and qi scant, they are fine but short; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the beard is sparse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no beard at all, and such a person, when affected by cold and dampness, is prone to impediment, with pain in the bones and withered nails. In the lower portion of the Foot Shaoyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the leg hair grows fine and long, and the outer ankle is full; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the leg hair is fine but short, and the skin of the outer ankle is firm and thick; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the leg hair is sparse, and the skin of the outer ankle is thin and soft; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no hair at all, and the outer ankle is thin and fleshless.

In the upper portion of the Foot Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the eyebrows are fine, with long fine hairs among them; when blood is abundant and qi scant, the eyebrows are coarse, and the face has fine, sparse lines; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the face is well fleshed; and when blood and qi are harmonious, the complexion is fine. In the lower portion of the Foot Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the heel is well fleshed and firm; when qi is scant and blood abundant, the heel is thin and hollow; and when both blood and qi are scant, such a person is prone to cramping of the sinews and pain beneath the heel.

In the upper portion of the Hand Yangming channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the mustache is fine; when blood is scant and qi abundant, the mustache is coarse; and when both blood and qi are scant, there is no mustache at all. In the lower portion of the Hand Yangming channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the underarm hair is fine, and the fleshy mound at the base of the thumb is warm; and when both qi and blood are scant, the hand is thin and cold.

In the upper portion of the Hand Shaoyin channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the eyebrows are fine and long, and the color of the ears is fine; when both blood and qi are scant, the ears are scorched-looking and of poor color. In the lower portion of the Hand Shaoyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the hand is well-rounded, fleshy, and warm; when both blood and qi are scant, the hand is cold and thin; and when qi is scant and blood abundant, the hand is thin with prominent vessels.

In the upper portion of the Hand Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the beard is thick, and the face is well fleshed and even; when both blood and qi are scant, the face is thin and of poor color. In the lower portion of the Hand Taiyang channel, when blood and qi are abundant, the palm is full and fleshy; when both blood and qi are scant, the palm is thin and cold.

Huangdi said: 'For these twenty-five types of persons, is there a general rule for needling them?' Qibo said: 'One with fine eyebrows has abundant qi and blood in the Foot Taiyang channel; one with coarse eyebrows has scant qi and blood there. One who is stout and lustrous has a surplus of both qi and blood; one who is stout but without luster has a surplus of qi but a deficiency of blood; one who is thin and without luster has both qi and blood deficient. By carefully examining whether the bodily form and qi are in surplus or deficiency, and regulating accordingly, one can know whether the condition runs contrary to or in accord with what is proper.'

Huangdi said: 'How should one needle the various yin and yang channels?' Qibo said: 'Press the Cunkou and Renying pulses to gauge the balance of yin and yang, and palpate along the channels and network vessels for places of congelation and roughness, of binding and obstruction — wherever this occurs in the body, it manifests as pain and impediment, and in severe cases the flow ceases altogether; hence congelation and roughness arise. Where there is congelation and roughness, one should bring qi to the area to warm it, and only stop once the blood flows harmoniously. Where the network vessels are bound, the vessel is knotted and the blood disharmonious; one must open it before the flow can resume. Thus it is said: where qi has a surplus above, guide it downward; where qi is deficient above, press upon the area and let it rest; where qi lingers and fails to arrive, draw it forth by meeting it; one must be thoroughly versed in the pathways of the channels before one can properly control this. Where cold and heat contend with one another, guide the qi so that it flows freely. Where stagnant, old blood is present but not yet bound into a knot, press it out and disperse it. One must first thoroughly understand, for each of the twenty-five types of persons, where blood and qi are located, whether to the left or right, above or below — only then is the essential method of needling complete.'

灵枢经.title_en: The Five Notes and the Five Flavors
paragraphs_en: You-zhi (right zhi) and shao-zhi (lesser zhi): regulate the upper portion of the right Hand Taiyang channel. Zuo-shang (left shang) and zuo-zhi (left zhi): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. Shao-zhi (lesser zhi) and da-gong (great gong): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. You-jiao (right jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao): regulate the lower portion of the right Hand Shaoyang channel. Da-zhi (great zhi) and shao-zhi (lesser zhi): regulate the upper portion of the left Hand Taiyang channel. Zhong-yu (multitude yu) and shao-yu (lesser yu): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Shao-shang (lesser shang) and you-shang (right shang): regulate the lower portion of the right Hand Taiyang channel. Zhi-yu (fettered yu) and zhong-yu (multitude yu): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Shao-gong (lesser gong) and da-gong (great gong): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Pan-jiao (split jiao) and shao-jiao (lesser jiao): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Shaoyang channel. Dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) and shang-shang (upper shang): regulate the lower portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Dai-shang (metal-tipped shang) and shang-jiao (upper jiao): regulate the lower portion of the left Foot Taiyang channel.

Shang-zhi (upper zhi) and you-zhi (right zhi) share the same grain, wheat; the same domestic animal, the sheep; and the same fruit, the apricot. They belong to the Hand Shaoyin channel and store in the heart; their color is red, their flavor bitter, and their season summer. Shang-yu (upper yu) and da-yu (great yu) share the same grain, the large bean; the same domestic animal, the pig; and the same fruit, the chestnut. They belong to the Foot Shaoyin channel and store in the kidney; their color is black, their flavor salty, and their season winter. Shang-gong (upper gong) and da-gong (great gong) share the same grain, panicled millet; the same domestic animal, the ox; and the same fruit, the jujube. They belong to the Foot Taiyin channel and store in the spleen; their color is yellow, their flavor sweet, and their season late summer. Shang-shang (upper shang) and you-shang (right shang) share the same grain, glutinous millet; the same domestic animal, the fowl; and the same fruit, the peach. They belong to the Hand Taiyin channel and store in the lung; their color is white, their flavor pungent, and their season autumn. Shang-jiao (upper jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the same grain, hemp; the same domestic animal, the dog; and the same fruit, the plum. They belong to the Foot Jueyin channel and store in the liver; their color is green-blue, their flavor sour, and their season spring.

Da-gong (great gong) and shang-jiao (upper jiao) share the same location, the upper portion of the right Foot Yangming channel. Zuo-jiao (left jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the upper portion of the left Foot Yangming channel. Shao-yu (lesser yu) and da-yu (great yu) share the lower portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Zuo-shang (left shang) and you-shang (right shang) share the upper portion of the left Hand Yangming channel. Jia-gong (added gong) and da-gong (great gong) share the upper portion of the left Foot Shaoyang channel. Zhi-pan (substantive-split zhi) and da-gong (great gong) share the lower portion of the left Hand Taiyang channel. Pan-jiao (split jiao) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the lower portion of the left Foot Shaoyang channel. Da-yu (great yu) and da-jiao (great jiao) share the upper portion of the right Foot Taiyang channel. Da-jiao (great jiao) and da-gong (great gong) share the upper portion of the right Foot Shaoyang channel. The subtypes of zhi are: you-zhi, shao-zhi, zhi-zhi, shang-zhi, and pan-zhi. The subtypes of jiao are: you-jiao, dai-jiao, shang-jiao, da-jiao, and pan-jiao. The subtypes of shang are: you-shang, shao-shang, dai-shang, shang-shang, and zuo-shang. The subtypes of gong are: shao-gong, shang-gong, da-gong, jia-gong, and zuo-jiaogong. The subtypes of yu are: zhong-yu, zhi-yu, shang-yu, da-yu, and shao-yu.

Huangdi said: 'Women have no beard — is this because they lack blood and qi?' Qibo said: 'The Penetrating Vessel (Chongmai) and the Conception Vessel (Renmai) both arise within the uterus, ascend along the interior of the back, and form the sea of the channels and network vessels. Their branch that floats to the exterior travels up along the abdomen, converges at the throat, and then divides to network around the lips and mouth. When blood and qi are abundant, they fill the skin and warm the flesh; when blood alone is abundant, it seeps out and moistens the skin, giving rise to fine hair. Now, in the constitution of women there is a surplus of qi but a deficiency of blood, because they repeatedly lose blood through menstruation; hence the vessels of the Penetrating and Conception channels cannot nourish the lips and mouth, and so no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'When a man is injured in his yin — when his yin qi is cut off so that he cannot become erect, and his yin organ is rendered useless — his beard nonetheless does not fall away. Why is this? And why is it that eunuchs alone lose their beard? I wish to hear the reason.' Qibo said: 'In the eunuch, the ancestral sinew, the genital organ, has been removed, injuring the Penetrating Vessel; the blood that is drained away cannot be restored, the skin binds up internally, and the lips and mouth are no longer nourished — hence no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'There are also natural eunuchs, who have never been injured and have never lost blood, and yet their beard does not grow. Why is this?' Qibo said: 'This is a congenital insufficiency bestowed by Heaven. In such persons the Conception and Penetrating vessels are not abundant, and the ancestral sinew fails to develop fully; they have qi but no blood, and so the lips and mouth are not nourished, and no beard grows.'

Huangdi said: 'Excellent indeed! The sage's comprehension of the myriad things is like the light and shadow cast by the sun and moon, like the resonance struck from a drum by sound — one hears the tone and knows the shape. Were it not for you, Master, who could clarify the essence of the myriad things?' Thus the sage, observing a person's complexion, knows that one who is yellow or red has abundant heat qi; one who is green-blue or white has scant heat qi; one who is dark has abundant blood but scant qi. One with fine eyebrows has abundant blood in the Taiyang; one with connecting whiskers and a full beard has abundant blood in the Shaoyang; and one with a fine beard has abundant blood in the Yangming — this is simply how it naturally is.

Now, the constant measure for human beings is this: the Taiyang habitually has abundant blood and scant qi; the Shaoyang habitually has abundant qi and scant blood; the Yangming habitually has abundant blood and abundant qi; the Jueyin habitually has abundant qi and scant blood; the Shaoyin habitually has abundant blood and scant qi; and the Taiyin habitually has abundant blood and scant qi. This is the constant measure bestowed by Heaven.

灵枢经.title_en: The Origin of the Hundred Diseases
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'Now, the hundred diseases, in their beginning, all arise from wind and rain, cold and summer-heat, clear coolness and dampness, and joy and anger. When joy and anger are not moderated, they injure the viscera; when wind and rain assail the body, they injure the upper regions; when clear coolness and dampness assail the body, they injure the lower regions. The three regions are each injured by different categories of cause — I wish to hear how they converge.' Qibo said: 'The qi of the three regions each differ: some diseases arise from yin, others from yang. Allow me to explain the pattern. When joy and anger are not moderated, they injure the viscera, and when the viscera are injured, disease arises from yin. When clear coolness and dampness take advantage of a deficiency, disease arises from the lower region. When wind and rain take advantage of a deficiency, disease arises from the upper region. These are what is called the three regions. As for the excessive overflow of these pathogenic influences once they take hold, their manifestations cannot be exhausted by counting.'

Huangdi said: 'Indeed, I cannot count them all — this is precisely why I ask my former teacher; I wish to hear the whole of this Way.' Qibo said: 'Wind and rain, cold and heat, cannot by themselves harm a person unless there is a deficiency present; evil qi alone cannot injure the body. Thus, when a person suddenly encounters a fierce wind or violent rain and yet does not fall ill, it is because there is no deficiency present, and so the evil qi cannot injure him by itself. This must always depend upon a vacuous, thieving wind meeting with a body that is itself deficient — when the two deficiencies meet one another, the evil is then able to lodge in the body. Where two fullnesses meet one another, the flesh of most people remains firm and resistant. Whether the body is struck by vacuous, thieving evil depends on the season of Heaven together with the condition of the body itself; weighing deficiency against fullness together, serious disease is thereby formed. The qi of disease has a fixed lodging place, and it is named according to where it settles — above, below, in the center, or on the exterior — and thus is divided into three regions.'

Thus, when vacuous, thieving evil strikes a person, it begins in the skin. When the skin is lax, the interstices open; once open, the evil enters through the body hair, and once entered, it presses inward and deepens; as it deepens, the body hair stands erect, and when the hair stands erect there is a chilled, shivering sensation, and so the skin becomes painful. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the network vessels; while in the network vessels, there is pain in the flesh and muscles, so that the pain comes and goes intermittently. If it is not expelled, the great channels then take over as its lodging place; if it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the channels; while in the channels, one shivers with cold and is easily startled. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges at the transport points; while at the transport points, the six channels fail to communicate with the four limbs, so the joints of the limbs ache and the lower back and spine become stiff. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the Hidden-Chong Vessel; while in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, the body feels heavy and aches all over. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges in the intestines and stomach; while in the intestines and stomach, there is rumbling and distension of the abdomen. Where cold predominates, there is intestinal rumbling with diarrhea of undigested food and failure to digest food; where heat predominates, there is sloppy, mushy stool. If it lingers and does not depart, it is transmitted and lodges outside the intestines and stomach, in the space of the membrane-source, the fatty membranes, where it settles into the vessels; if it remains there without departing, it accumulates over time and forms a mass. It may settle in the grandchild vessels, or in the network vessels, or in the channel vessels, or at the transport points, or in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, or in the sinews alongside the spine, or in the membrane-source between the intestines and stomach, extending upward to connect with the slack sinews. The excessive overflow of evil qi in this way cannot be exhaustively described.

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear fully how each of these comes about.' Qibo said: 'When the evil settles in the grandchild vessels and forms a mass, the mass moves back and forth, upward and downward, for the grandchild vessels are slight and shallow, floating and lax, unable to grip and hold the mass in place; hence it travels back and forth between the intestines and stomach. Water gathers and seeps in, pouring and irrigating, producing a sloshing sound; where cold is present, there is bloated fullness with rumbling that radiates through the abdomen, and so there is intermittent, sharp pain. When it settles in the Yangming channel, it lodges beside the navel; when the person eats to fullness, it grows larger, and when hungry, it grows smaller. When it settles in the slack sinews, it resembles a mass of the Yangming, so that eating to fullness brings pain and hunger brings ease. When it settles in the membrane-source between the intestines and stomach, there is pain that extends outward to connect with the slack sinews; eating to fullness brings ease, and hunger brings pain. When it settles in the Hidden-Chong Vessel, palpation reveals it pulsing beneath the hand, and when the hand is released, hot qi descends into both thighs as though hot water were being poured over them. When it settles in the sinews alongside the spine, behind the intestines, the mass becomes apparent when hungry and disappears when full, so that it cannot be found on palpation. When it settles at the transport points, there is blockage and obstruction so that fluids fail to descend, and the body's orifices become dry and congested. This is how evil qi passes from the exterior into the interior, and travels from above to below.'

Huangdi said: 'From the very beginning of a mass's formation to its full development, how does this occur?' Qibo said: 'A mass, at its beginning, arises from exposure to cold; through reversal of qi it becomes a fully formed mass.' Huangdi said: 'How does it become a mass?' Qibo said: 'Reversal qi first produces a distressed feeling in the feet; this distress produces coldness in the shins; coldness in the shins causes the blood vessels to congeal and become rough; when the blood vessels congeal and become rough, cold qi ascends and enters the intestines and stomach; once it enters the intestines and stomach, there is bloated distension; and this bloated distension causes the fluid and froth outside the intestines to be forced together and unable to disperse, so that over time a mass is formed. If a person suddenly eats and drinks to excess, the intestines become full; if rising and resting are not regulated, and if one exerts oneself with excessive force, the network vessels become injured. When the yang network vessels are injured, blood overflows to the exterior, and overflowing blood causes nosebleed; when the yin network vessels are injured, blood overflows internally, and this causes bleeding from the stool. When the network vessels of the intestines and stomach are injured, blood overflows outside the intestines; if there is cold outside the intestines, the fluid and froth contend with the blood there, and the two combine and congeal together, unable to disperse, and a mass is thereby formed. If a person is suddenly struck by cold from without, or is internally injured by worry and anger, the qi rebels upward; when qi rebels upward, the six transport points fail to communicate, warming qi cannot circulate, congealed blood accumulates within and cannot disperse, and bodily fluids become rough and seep out, settling in place without departing — and thus a mass is fully formed in every case.'

Huangdi said: 'What of disease that arises from yin?' Qibo said: 'Worry and excessive contemplation injure the heart; doubled exposure to cold injures the lung; rage and anger injure the liver; entering the bedchamber while intoxicated, and sweating while exposed to wind, injures the spleen; excessive physical exertion, or entering the bedchamber and sweating profusely thereafter, injures the kidney. These are the diseases produced within the three regions, both internal and external.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one skillfully treat these conditions?' Qibo answered: 'Examine the location of the pain in order to know its correspondence, and determine whether there is surplus or deficiency. Where supplementation is called for, supplement; where drainage is called for, drain; and never act contrary to the proper timing of Heaven — this is what is called the perfection of treatment.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have learned of the nine needles from you, Master, and have put them into practice among the common people. Yet the blood and qi of the common people take differing forms: in some, the spirit stirs and the qi moves ahead of the needle itself; in some, the qi and the needle arrive together; in some, once the needle is withdrawn, the qi continues to move on its own; in some, only after repeated needling is the response felt; in some, upon inserting the needle the qi rebels; and in some, repeated needling only worsens the disease. These six patterns each take a different form — I wish to hear the explanation for them.'

Qibo said: 'In a person of doubled yang, the spirit is easily stirred and the qi easily moves forth.' Huangdi said: 'What is meant by a person of doubled yang?' Qibo said: 'A person of doubled yang is fiery and expansive in manner, quick and animated in speech, and lifts his feet high when walking; the qi of his heart and lung viscera is in surplus, and his yang qi is smooth, abundant, and outwardly manifest — hence his spirit stirs and his qi moves ahead of the needle.'

Huangdi said: 'Why is it that in some persons of doubled yang, the spirit does not move ahead of the needle?' Qibo said: 'Such a person still has a considerable measure of yin within him.' Huangdi said: 'How can one know that he has a considerable measure of yin?' Qibo said: 'One with abundant yang is much given to joy; one with abundant yin is much given to anger, though such frequent anger is also easily dispelled — hence it is said that he has a considerable measure of yin. Since the separation and union of his yin and yang are difficult to resolve, his spirit is unable to move ahead of the needle.'

Huangdi said: 'What of the case where the qi and the needle arrive together?' Qibo said: 'In such a person, yin and yang are harmonious and well regulated, and blood and qi are moist and smoothly flowing; hence when the needle enters, the qi issues forth at once, and the two meet swiftly together.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the needle, once withdrawn, to be followed by the qi moving on its own?' Qibo said: 'In such a person, yin qi is abundant and yang qi scant; the yin qi lies deep while the yang qi, though floating, remains stored within — hence, once the needle is withdrawn, the qi follows after it, moving on its own.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the response to be felt only after repeated needling?' Qibo said: 'Such a person has abundant yin and scant yang; his qi lies deep and moves forth only with difficulty, hence the response is felt only after repeated needling.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes the qi to rebel when the needle enters?' Qibo said: 'Where the qi rebels, and where repeated needling only worsens the disease, this is not due to the disposition of yin and yang qi, nor to whether they lie deep or float near the surface — in every such case, the fault lies with the unskilled practitioner and the error of poor technique; the patient's own bodily form and qi are not at fault.'

Huangdi said: 'When qi causes upper-diaphragm obstruction, food and drink, once swallowed, are returned and vomited back up — this I already understand. But when parasites cause lower-diaphragm obstruction, the food is retained for a time and only later expelled — I have not yet grasped the meaning of this, and I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo said: 'When joy and anger are not properly regulated, when eating and drinking are not moderated, and when cold and warmth are not kept in their proper season, cold fluid flows into the intestines. Once it flows into the intestines, the parasites there become chilled; once chilled, they gather and accumulate, taking up residence at the lower portion of the stomach duct. This causes the intestines and stomach to become distended and full, the defensive qi fails to circulate properly there, and evil qi comes to lodge in that place. When a person eats, the parasites rise up to feed on the food; when the parasites rise up to feed, the lower stomach duct becomes empty, and when it is empty, evil qi overcomes it there, accumulating and lingering. Once it lingers, an abscess forms, and once the abscess forms, the lower stomach duct becomes constricted. Where the abscess lies within the duct itself, the pain, though close at hand, lies deep; where the abscess lies outside the duct, it protrudes outward and the pain lies near the surface, with the skin over the abscess feeling hot.'

Huangdi said: 'How should one needle this condition?' Qibo said: 'Press gently upon the abscess and observe the direction in which the qi moves. First needle shallowly at its periphery, then gradually insert the needle deeper, pursuing the abscess with successive needlings, but not exceeding three rounds of insertion. Examine whether the qi lies deep or near the surface, and let this determine the depth of needling. After needling, one must always apply a warming compress, so as to let the heat penetrate within; by causing the heat to enter day after day, the evil qi will gradually decline, and the great abscess will eventually break open and drain. Combine this with the appropriate dietary prohibitions in order to clear the interior; keep the patient tranquil and free of exertion, so that the qi may be made to circulate properly. Afterward, use salty and bitter flavors to help transform the grain so that it may descend properly.'

灵枢经.title_en: On Grief and Rage Causing Loss of Voice
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Shaoshi: 'When a person is suddenly overcome by grief and rage and thereafter speaks without sound, what pathway has become obstructed, and what qi is escaping outward, so that the voice fails to be expressed? I should like to hear the explanation.' Shaoshi replied: 'The throat is the pathway of water and grain. The windpipe is that by which qi ascends and descends. The epiglottis is the door of the voice. The lips are the shutters of the voice. The tongue is the trigger of the voice. The uvula is the pass that gates the voice. The nasopharynx is where the divided qi is vented. The hyoid bone is what the spirit qi employs to govern the movement of the tongue. Thus, when a person's nose runs with clear discharge that cannot be checked, it is because the nasopharynx has failed to open and the divided qi has lost its outlet. Therefore, when the epiglottis is small and moves quickly and thinly, the qi is issued swiftly, its opening and closing are smooth, and its qi passes out with ease; but when the epiglottis is large and thick, its opening and closing are difficult, its qi is expelled slowly, and so the person's speech becomes heavy and halting. When a person suddenly loses the voice altogether, it is because cold qi has lodged in the epiglottis, so that the epiglottis can no longer rise, and having risen cannot come back down; its opening and closing fail to be accomplished, and hence there is no voice.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one needle this?' Qibo said: 'The Foot Shaoyin Kidney channel ascends to bind with the tongue, connects with the hyoid bone, and terminates at the epiglottis. Drain its blood vessels on both sides, and the turbid qi will thereby be dispelled. The vessel of the epiglottis ascends to connect with the Conception Vessel; select the point Tiantu (Celestial Chimney), and the epiglottis will then be restored to function.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'Cold-heat scrofula that appears at the neck and armpit — by what qi does it in every case arise?' Qibo said: 'These are all due to the toxic qi of mouse-fistula cold-heat disorder, which lodges in the vessels and fails to depart.'

Huangdi said: 'How is it removed?' Qibo said: 'The root of mouse-fistula always lies in the viscera, while its branch emerges above, between the neck and the armpit. When it floats within the vessels and has not yet lodged internally within the flesh, but externally forms pus and blood, it is easily removed.'

Huangdi said: 'How is it removed?' Qibo said: 'One should proceed from the root and draw out the branch, so that it may be made to wane and depart, and its cold and heat cut off. Carefully press along its pathway before administering the needle; move slowly in and slowly out to remove it. When it is as small as a grain of wheat, a single puncture brings a response, and three punctures complete the cure.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one determine life or death in this condition?' Qibo said: 'Turn back the patient's eyelids and examine the eyes. If there are red vessels within that run up and down through the pupil: if one such vessel is seen, the patient will die within one year; if one and a half vessels are seen, within a year and a half; if two vessels are seen, within two years; if two and a half vessels are seen, within two and a half years; if three vessels are seen, the patient will die within three years. If a red vessel is seen but does not run down through the pupil, the condition can be treated.'

Huangdi asked Bogao: 'Now when evil qi lodges within a person, it may cause the eyes to be unable to close, so that the person cannot lie down and sleep — what qi brings this about?' Bogao replied: 'When the five grains enter the stomach, their dregs, fluids, and ancestral qi (zongqi, the qi that gathers in the chest) are divided into three separate passages. The ancestral qi accumulates in the chest, emerges through the windpipe, threads through the heart vessel, and carries out respiration. The nutritive qi secretes the fluids, pours them into the vessels, transforms them into blood, and thereby nourishes the four extremities; internally it pours into the five viscera and six bowels, in accordance with the water-clock's measured intervals. The defensive qi issues forth its fierce and swift qi, which travels first through the four extremities, between the muscles and the skin, without ever resting. By day it travels through the yang, and by night through the yin, ordinarily proceeding from the dividing space of the Foot Shaoyin channel to travel through the five viscera and six bowels. Now, when reversal qi lodges in the five viscera and six bowels, the defensive qi alone guards the exterior, traveling through the yang and unable to enter the yin. When it travels through the yang, the yang qi becomes abundant; when the yang qi is abundant, the yang heel vessel (yangqiao) becomes engorged, and the qi is unable to enter the yin; the yin then becomes deficient, and hence the eyes cannot close.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. How is this treated?' Bogao said: 'Supplement what is deficient, drain what is in excess, regulate the deficiency and excess, so as to open the pathway and remove the evil. Give the patient a single dose of Pinellia Decoction (banxia tang); once yin and yang have been made to communicate, sleep will come at once.' Huangdi said: 'Good. This is what is called opening a blocked channel, so that the channels and network vessels are made to communicate fully and yin and yang attain harmony. I should like to hear the formula.' Bogao said: 'For this decoction, take eight sheng of water drawn from a stream that has flowed a thousand li, stir it ten thousand times, and skim off five sheng of its clear portion. Boil this over a fire of reed kindling; once it boils, add one sheng of polished glutinous millet and five ge of prepared pinellia. Simmer slowly until the liquid is reduced to one and a half sheng, then remove the dregs. Drink one small cup of the broth, gradually increasing to three times a day, using the patient's response as the measure. Thus, in a case of recent onset, as soon as the cup is set down the patient will fall asleep, and once sweating occurs the illness is resolved. In a chronic case, three doses will complete the cure.'

Huangdi asked Bogao: 'I should like to hear how the joints and limbs of a person correspond to Heaven and Earth.' Bogao replied: 'Heaven is round and Earth is square; the human head is round and the feet are square, in correspondence with this. Heaven has the sun and moon; the person has two eyes. Earth has nine provinces; the person has nine orifices. Heaven has wind and rain; the person has joy and anger. Heaven has thunder and lightning; the person has voice and sound. Heaven has the four seasons; the person has four limbs. Heaven has the five notes; the person has the five viscera. Heaven has the six pitch-pipes; the person has the six bowels. Heaven has winter and summer; the person has cold and heat. Heaven has the ten days of its cycle; the person has ten fingers on the hands. The twelve double-hours correspond to the twelve toes of the feet, together with the penis and testicles, by which a person's form is completed — though a woman lacks these two members, and thereby holds the human form within her. Heaven has yin and yang; the person has husband and wife. The year has three hundred sixty-five days; the person has three hundred sixty joints. Earth has high mountains; the person has shoulders and knees. Earth has deep valleys; the person has armpits and the hollows behind the knees. Earth has the twelve channel-rivers; the person has the twelve channel vessels. Earth has springs and veins of water; the person has defensive qi. Earth has grasses and duckweed; the person has fine body hair. Heaven has day and night; the person has waking and sleeping. Heaven has arrayed stars; the person has teeth. Earth has small hills; the person has small joints. Earth has crags and rocks; the person has prominent bones. Earth has forests and groves; the person has binding sinews. Earth has gathered towns; the person has mounds of flesh. The year has twelve months; the person has twelve joints. Earth has seasons in which the grass does not grow; the person has those who bear no children. These are the ways in which the person corresponds to Heaven and Earth.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I wish to hear the numbers and techniques of holding the needle, the principles of inserting the needle, the meaning of advancing and withholding it, and how one presses the skin and opens the interstices. As for the bends and turns of the vessels, the places where they emerge and enter — where do they emerge, where do they stop, where do they move slowly, where do they move swiftly, where do they enter, and how the six bowels are transported throughout the body — I wish to hear all of this in full, together with a brief account of the places where the channels diverge and separate: where they diverge to enter the yin, and separate to enter the yang. By what pathway do they proceed thus? I wish to hear the whole of this account.' Qibo said: 'What Your Majesty asks encompasses the entirety of the Way of needling.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear it in full.' Qibo said: 'The vessel of the Hand Taiyin Lung channel emerges at the tip of the thumb, bends inward, follows the border between the red and white flesh, and reaches Taiyuan (Great Abyss) behind the base joint, where it lingers and pools. It then bends outward, ascends below the base joint, bends inward again, and meets the various yin network vessels at Yuji (Fish Border); several vessels converge and pour in there, and its qi flows smoothly and freely. It travels hidden beneath the styloid bone, bends outward, emerges at the Cunkou (inch-opening) and proceeds upward to the inner border of the elbow, enters beneath the great sinew, bends inward, travels upward along the yin aspect of the upper arm, enters below the armpit, bends inward again, and runs to the lung. These are the bends and turns of its course, traced forward in its natural flow and backward in enumeration. The vessel of the Heart Ruler channel (the Pericardium) emerges at the tip of the middle finger, bends inward, follows the inner border of the middle finger upward, and lingers in the center of the palm. It travels hidden between the two bones, bends outward, emerges between the two sinews at the juncture of bone and flesh, where its qi flows smoothly and freely. It ascends two cun, bends outward, emerges to travel between the two sinews, ascends to the inner border of the elbow, enters beneath the small sinew, lingers at the junction of the two bones, ascends into the chest, and internally connects with the vessel of the heart.'

Huangdi said: 'The Hand Shaoyin Heart channel alone has no transport point at the wrist — why is this?' Qibo said: 'The Shaoyin is the vessel of the heart. The heart is the great sovereign of the five viscera and six bowels, the dwelling place of essence and spirit. Its viscus is firm and solid, and evil qi cannot be admitted into it. If it were admitted, the heart would be injured; if the heart is injured, the spirit departs; and if the spirit departs, the person dies. Thus all evil that would settle in the heart instead settles in the heart's enclosing network, the pericardium. The enclosing network is the vessel of the Heart Ruler; this is why it alone has no transport point.'

Huangdi said: 'If the Shaoyin alone has no transport point, does it then never fall ill?' Qibo said: 'Its outer channel may fall ill even while the viscus itself does not; hence one selects only its channel point at the tip of the protruding bone behind the palm. As for the emerging, entering, bending, and turning of its remaining vessels, and the slowness or swiftness of their travel, all proceed just as does the vessel of the Hand Shaoyin, the Heart Ruler. Thus, as for the root transport points, in every case one selects them according to whether the qi is deficient or excess, slow or swift: this is called draining when the qi surges and supplementing when it wanes. When this is done, evil qi is made to depart and the true qi becomes firm and secure — this is what is meant by following the sequence ordained by Heaven.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one hold the needle in advancing and withholding it?' Qibo said: 'One must first clearly understand the roots and branches of the twelve channel vessels, the cold and heat of the skin, and the abundance, decline, smoothness, or roughness of the pulse. When the pulse is smooth and abundant, the disease is advancing day by day. When it is deficient and fine, the disease has long persisted and must be sustained in treatment. When it is large and rough, this indicates painful impediment (bi). When yin and yang appear as one and the same, the disease is difficult to treat. If the root and branch of the channel are still hot, the disease is still present; if that heat has waned, the disease has likewise departed. Palpate the patient's forearm, and examine the firmness or fragility of the flesh, its size, its smoothness or roughness, and its cold, warmth, dryness, or dampness. Then observe the five colors of the eyes, in order to know the state of the five viscera and determine life or death. Observe the blood vessels and examine their color, in order to know the cold, heat, pain, or impediment present.'

Huangdi said: 'As for holding the needle in advancing and withholding it, I have not yet grasped its meaning.' Qibo said: 'The Way of holding the needle requires that the hand be upright and correct, calm and still. First know whether the condition is deficient or excess, and proceed accordingly with swiftness or slowness. The left hand grips the bone, while the right hand follows along it; do not let the needle become entangled with the flesh. In draining, the needle should be held upright and correct; in supplementing, one must close the skin afterward. Assist the needle in guiding the qi, so that evil qi is made to disperse and scatter, and the true qi is able to settle and abide.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one press the skin and open the interstices?' Qibo said: 'One follows along the muscle divisions; with the left hand separate the skin, insert the needle slightly and slowly guide it upright, so that the spirit is not caused to scatter, and the evil qi is thereby made to depart.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'A person has eight hollows — what does each of them indicate?' Qibo replied: 'They serve to indicate the condition of the five viscera.' Huangdi said: 'How does one read them?' Qibo said: 'If the lung or heart harbors evil qi, that qi lodges in the two elbows. If the liver harbors evil qi, that qi flows to the two armpits. If the spleen harbors evil qi, that qi lodges in the two hip-thighs. If the kidney harbors evil qi, that qi lodges in the two hollows behind the knees. In every case, these eight hollows are chambers of the body's pivots and joints, places through which the true qi passes and the blood network vessels travel. Evil qi and malign blood are ordinarily not permitted to lodge and remain there. If they do lodge and remain, they injure the sinews, network vessels, bones, and joints, so that the pivots and joints can no longer bend and extend — hence contracture and spasm result.'

Huangdi asked Shaoshi: 'I have heard it said that people have yin and yang natures — what is meant by a yin person, and what by a yang person?' Shaoshi said: 'Within Heaven and Earth, within the six directions, nothing departs from the five phases, and the person corresponds to this as well; it is not simply a matter of one yin and one yang. But this can only be spoken of in outline, for the mouth cannot fully expound it.' Huangdi said: 'I should like to hear its outline. Are there worthies and sages whose hearts are able to comprehend it fully and put it into practice?' Shaoshi said: 'There are the Taiyin person, the Shaoyin person, the Taiyang person, the Shaoyang person, and the person of harmonious and balanced yin and yang. Among these five types of people, their bearing differs, and their sinews, bones, qi, and blood are each unequal.'

Huangdi said: 'May I hear of these inequalities?' Shaoshi said: 'The Taiyin person is greedy and lacking in benevolence; outwardly composed and deeply reserved, they are fond of taking in but averse to giving out; their heart is calm and does not readily reveal itself; they do not attend to the moment, and only act after others have already moved. This is the Taiyin person.'

The Shaoyin person is mildly greedy and harbors a thievish heart; on seeing another suffer loss, they behave as though they themselves had gained something; they are fond of injuring and harming others, and on seeing another prosper, they instead become resentful and angry; their heart is malicious and without kindness. This is the Shaoyin person.

The Taiyang person dwells in a manner that is self-satisfied and complacent; they are fond of speaking of great affairs, though lacking real ability, given to empty boasting; their ambitions extend to the four wilds, and their conduct pays no heed to right or wrong; in undertaking affairs they habitually rely only on their own judgment, and though their undertakings may fail, they are ordinarily without regret. This is the Taiyang person.

The Shaoyang person is careful and scrupulous, fond of examining themselves; on obtaining even the smallest office, they consider themselves greatly deserving; they are fond of forming external associations, but do not attach themselves inwardly. This is the Shaoyang person.

The person of harmonious and balanced yin and yang dwells in peace and quiet, neither fearful nor overjoyed; they accommodate themselves gently to things, neither contending nor withholding, and change together with the times; when honored, they remain modest and yielding; they govern by seeming not to govern at all — this is called the utmost governance.

Those of antiquity who were skilled in the use of the needle and moxa observed which of the five types of bearing a person belonged to, and only then treated them: what was excessive they drained, and what was deficient they supplemented.

Huangdi said: 'How does one treat a person according to these five types?' Shaoshi said: 'The Taiyin person has much yin and little yang; their yin blood is turbid and their defensive qi moves roughly; yin and yang are not in harmony; their sinews are lax and their skin is thick. If one does not drain them swiftly, the condition cannot be shifted.'

The Shaoyin person has much yin and little yang; their stomach is small and their intestines are large; the six bowels are not well coordinated; their Yangming vessel is small while their Taiyang vessel is large. One must carefully and precisely regulate them, for their blood is easily lost and their qi is easily thrown into disorder.

The Taiyang person has much yang and little yin. One must regulate them with great care, taking care not to deplete their yin while draining their yang. If their yang is severely depleted, they easily become manic; if both yin and yang are depleted together, sudden death occurs, with loss of consciousness.

The Shaoyang person has much yang and little yin; their channels are small while their network vessels are large; their blood lies within while their qi lies without. One should fortify their yin while treating their deficient yang. If one drains only their network vessels, the strong qi will be lost precipitously, the central qi will become insufficient, and the illness will not be overcome.

In the person of harmonious and balanced yin and yang, the yin and yang qi are in harmony and the blood vessels are well regulated. One should carefully diagnose their yin and yang, observe whether evil or upright qi prevails, note their bearing and demeanor, and determine whether there is excess or deficiency: what is excessive, drain; what is deficient, supplement; where there is neither excess nor deficiency, select points along the channel itself. This is the means by which one regulates yin and yang and distinguishes among the five types of people.

Huangdi said: 'Now, when among these five types of people there has been no prior acquaintance, and one has just newly met a stranger whose conduct is not yet known, how does one distinguish which type they belong to?' Shaoshi answered: 'Among the generality of people, apart from those who belong to the five types just described, there are five times five, twenty-five types of people, and the five types under discussion are not counted among them; the five types are in fact those who stand apart from and do not conform to the ordinary run of people.' Huangdi said: 'How, then, does one distinguish among the five types?' Shaoshi said: 'The Taiyin person has the appearance of a dark, murky black complexion; they seem humble and self-effacing in bearing, yet are imposingly tall and large in stature, and though somewhat stooped at the knees, are not truly bent. This is the Taiyin person.'

The Shaoyin person has the appearance of being clear yet furtive; they are inherently sly and calculating; standing, they appear restless and treacherous, and walking, they seem to slink as though in hiding. This is the Shaoyin person.
The Taiyang person has the appearance of holding themselves high and self-important, standing with the body thrown back and the knees bent backward. This is the Taiyang person.

The Shaoyang person, when standing, is fond of tilting the head back, and when walking, is fond of swaying the body; their two arms and elbows are habitually held out behind the back. This is the Shaoyang person.

The person of harmonious and balanced yin and yang has the appearance of being composed and graceful, accommodating and easy-going, dignified and respectful, cheerful and content, mild-eyed and gentle in gaze, measured and steady in bearing; the crowd all call such a person a gentleman (junzi) of true refinement. This is the person of harmonious and balanced yin and yang.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard from you, Master, of the nine needles — their applications are so numerous that they cannot all be counted. I have deduced from them and set out a summary account. Let me recite it aloud, and you attend to whether the principles are correct; where they are not, tell me, so that I may correct the Way and ensure that it may be transmitted for a long time to later generations without mishap — to be transmitted only to the right person, and never spoken of to one unfit for it.' Qibo bowed his head to the ground and made obeisance twice, saying: 'I beg to hear the Way of the sage-king.'

Huangdi said: 'The principle of using the needle is this: one must know where the body's form and qi reside — left and right, above and below, yin and yang, exterior and interior, the relative amount of blood and qi, the direction of their flow whether contrary or compliant, and the confluence of their emerging and entering — and plan one's attack upon the excess accordingly. One must know how to release knotted binding, how to supplement deficiency and drain excess, and the qi-gates above and below, so as to be clearly conversant with the four seas of the body (the seas of qi, blood, marrow, and nourishment). Examine where the disorder resides, whether cold or heat, whether draining or exposed, so as to select the appropriate points for treatment; be thorough in regulating the qi, well versed in the channels and their conduits, and in the network vessels of the left and right limbs, knowing fully where they converge. When cold and heat contend with one another, one must be able to unite and regulate them; when deficiency and excess lie adjacent, one must know how to resolve and open a way through them; when the left and right sides are not in balance, one must take hold of the imbalance and correct it. Being clear about what is contrary and what is compliant, one then knows what can be treated; when yin and yang are not at variance, one thereby knows the proper time for the disease to resolve. Examine the root and branch of the disorder, observe its cold or heat, and once the location of the evil is grasped, ten thousand needlings will proceed without mishap. To know how to employ the nine needles is to have mastered the entirety of the Way of needling.'

Be clear about the location of the five transport points and where slowness and swiftness apply, for the bending, stretching, emerging, and entering of the needle all follow an orderly sequence. Speak of yin together with yang, in accordance with the five phases; the five viscera and six bowels each have their own store; the four seasons and eight winds all partake of yin and yang. Each has its proper position, corresponding to the Bright Hall (mingtang, the nose, used in facial diagnosis); examine each region and its complexion in relation to the five viscera and six bowels. Observe where the pain lies, whether left or right, above or below, and know whether it is cold or warm, and in which channel it resides. Examine whether the skin is cold or warm, smooth or rough, so as to know the nature of the patient's distress; the diaphragm has an upper and lower aspect, so know where the qi resides in relation to it. First grasp the proper approach: where the qi is sparse, needle sparingly; go somewhat deeper and retain the needle, so that it may be advanced slowly. When great heat lies in the upper body, push the needle and draw the heat downward; when the disorder proceeds from above to below, draw it out and remove it; when a prior instance of pain is observed, always treat that point first. When great cold lies on the exterior, retain the needle and supplement; when the cold has entered the interior, drain it by way of the confluent (he) points. Where the needle cannot serve, moxibustion is appropriate. When the qi above is insufficient, push and raise it up; when the qi below is insufficient, accumulate it and let it follow its course; when both yin and yang are deficient, moxa fire alone should be applied. In reversal-cold with severe chill, when the bone borders sink and cold has spread past the knee, select Zusanli (also called Xialing). Where the yin network vessels pass, when the disorder is found, retain the needle to halt it there; when cold has entered the interior, push the needle to move it along; where a channel has sunk and collapsed, moxa fire should be applied; where the network vessels are knotted and taut, moxa fire is what treats it. When the location of distress cannot be determined, seek it below the two heel vessels (qiaomai) — yin for a man and yang for a woman; this is what the accomplished practitioner must be cautious of. Herewith the discourse on needling is complete.

In the practice of needling, there must always be method and rule. Above, observe the brightness of Heaven; below, attend to the eight upright directions, so as to ward off strange evils and to examine the common people; be thorough in distinguishing deficiency from excess, and do not offend against the prevailing evil qi. This is to obtain the numinous power of Heaven. If one encounters a year of deficient qi and attempts to rescue a patient without being able to overcome the evil, one will instead suffer calamity oneself. Hence it is said that one must know the prohibitions of Heaven before one may speak of the true intent of needling.

Take one's model from ages past, and verify it in times to come; observe what is hidden and obscure, and penetrate what is inexhaustible. What the unskilled practitioner cannot perceive is precisely what the accomplished practitioner prizes; though its form cannot be known, it seems almost as though touched by the spirit.

When evil qi strikes a person, it produces a shivering that disturbs the body's form. When a mild pathogenic influence strikes a person, it first appears faintly in the complexion, without the person being aware of it in the body itself — as though present, as though absent; as though gone, as though still there; having form yet seeming formless — none can know its true state. This is why the superior practitioner, in taking hold of the qi, rescues the disorder while it is still in its budding stage, whereas the inferior practitioner waits until it has already taken shape, and thereby allows the patient's form to be ruined.

This is why the practitioner, in employing the needle, must know where the qi resides and guard its gateway, being clear in the regulation of qi, in the proper location for supplementing or draining, in the meaning of slowness and swiftness, and in the place to be selected. In draining, one must employ the round technique: press the needle and turn it, so that the qi is set moving; withdraw it swiftly after entering slowly, so that the evil qi is thereby expelled; extend the hand to meet the qi as it comes, and enlarge the point opening somewhat, so that the qi comes out swiftly. In supplementing, one must employ the square technique: draw the skin outward on the exterior, so as to align it with the point's opening; on the left, draw the pivot of the needle; on the right, press the skin; rotate the needle slightly and advance it slowly; the hand must be upright and correct, calm and still; hold the mind firm without wavering, insert the needle gently and let it linger; once the qi descends, withdraw the needle swiftly, press the skin, and seal the outer gate, so that the true qi is preserved within. The essential point in using the needle is never to forget the spirit.

Lei Gong asked Huangdi: 'The discourse on needling states: transmit it only to the right person, and never speak of it to one unfit for it — how does one know whether a person is fit to receive its transmission?' Huangdi said: 'One assigns each person according to what suits them, entrusting them with tasks according to their particular ability, and so each is able to master their own charge.' Lei Gong said: 'I should like to hear how offices are assigned according to ability.' Huangdi said: 'One with clear eyesight may be employed to observe complexion. One with keen hearing may be employed to listen to sounds. One quick and agile in speech may be employed to transmit and expound the teachings. One whose speech is measured and calm, whose hands are skillful and whose mind is careful and discerning, may be employed to perform needling and moxibustion, to regulate the blood and qi and harmonize their various states of compliance and rebellion, and to discern yin and yang together with the various treatment methods. One whose joints are supple and sinews pliant, whose mind is harmonious and even-tempered, may be employed to guide the patient in stretching exercises and the circulation of qi (daoyin). One whose speech is sharp and cutting, and who makes light of others, may be employed to spit upon abscesses and to incant against disease. One whose fingernails are cutting and whose hands are forceful, given to injuring things in the course of their work, may be employed to press upon accumulations and suppress impediment disorders. When each is assigned according to their particular ability, the treatment method can then be properly carried out, and the practitioner's reputation will accordingly become manifest. If the wrong person is chosen, the work will not succeed, and the teacher will gain no reputation. Hence it is said: speak of it only to the right person; do not transmit it to one unfit for it — this is what is meant. As for one whose hand is forceful, one may test this by having them press upon a live turtle: place the turtle beneath a vessel, and press upon it from above; if the turtle dies within fifty days, the hand is forceful. If instead the turtle revives and lives on as before, the hand is gentle.'

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on Diagnosing Disease by the Cubit Skin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I wish to diagnose illness without observing complexion or holding the pulse, relying solely on examining the cubit skin (chi fu — the skin of the forearm between wrist and elbow), so as to know what lies within the body from what appears without. How is this done?" Qibo replied: "By examining whether the cubit skin is slack or tense, small or large, smooth or rough, and whether the flesh beneath it is firm or frail, the pattern of the disease can be determined."

When one observes a person whose eye sockets are slightly swollen, as though he had just risen from sleep, whose neck vessel throbs visibly, who coughs from time to time, and when the hands and feet are pressed the depression left behind does not spring back — this is wind-water skin distension.

If the cubit skin is smooth and moist, this is wind. If the flesh of the cubit is weak and frail, this is jieyi — a languor disorder marked by generalized weakness, lassitude, and wasting; if the patient lies quietly at ease and yet the flesh wastes away, this is a cold-heat disorder, and it cannot be treated. If the cubit skin is smooth and glossy as though oiled, this is wind. If the cubit skin is rough, this is wind impediment. If the cubit skin is coarse like the scales of a withered fish, this is water-overflow with fluid retention. If the cubit skin is very hot and the pulse is full and restless, this is warm disease; if the pulse is full and also slippery, the disease is about to break out. If the cubit skin is cold and the pulse is small, this is diarrhea with depleted qi. If the cubit skin burns like a torch, hot at first and turning cold afterward, this is a cold-heat disorder; if the cubit skin is cold at first, and after being held for a long while grows warm, this too is a cold-heat disorder.

If heat is localized to the elbow alone, there is heat above the lower back; if heat is localized to the hand alone, there is heat below the lower back. If heat is localized in front of the elbow, there is heat in the chest; if heat is localized behind the elbow, there is heat in the shoulder and back. If heat is localized in the mid-forearm, there is heat in the lower back and abdomen; if heat is felt three or four inches below the coarse skin behind the elbow, there are parasites in the intestines. If the palm is hot, there is heat in the abdomen; if the palm is cold, there is cold in the abdomen. If, on the white flesh above the thenar eminence (the "fish" region at the base of the thumb), greenish blood vessels appear, there is cold in the stomach.

If the cubit skin burns like a torch and the Renying pulse is large, there ought to be blood loss; if the cubit skin is hard and large while the pulse is very small, the qi depleted, and the oppressive distress increases, the patient will die at once.

If the eyes show a red color, the disease is in the heart; white, in the lung; green-blue, in the liver; yellow, in the spleen; black, in the kidney. If the yellow color cannot be clearly named, the disease is in the chest.

In diagnosing eye pain: if a red vessel runs from above downward, this is Taiyang disease; if it runs from below upward, this is Yangming disease; if it runs from the outer canthus toward the inner, this is Shaoyang disease.

In diagnosing cold-heat disorders, when a red vessel extends up and down as far as the pupil: if one such vessel is seen, the patient will die within a year; if one and a half vessels are seen, within a year and a half; if two vessels, within two years; if two and a half vessels, within two years and a half; if three vessels, within three years.

In diagnosing toothache from caries, press along the course of the yang channels as they arrive: wherever there is excess, heat is localized there alone — if on the left, the left is hot; if on the right, the right is hot; if above, the upper part is hot; if below, the lower part is hot.

In diagnosing the blood vessels: much redness indicates much heat; much green-blue indicates much pain; much black indicates a longstanding impediment disorder; if redness, black, and green-blue all appear together, this is a cold-heat disorder.

When the body aches and the complexion is faintly yellow, the teeth are coated with a yellow film, and the nails are yellow, this is jaundice. When the patient lies quietly at ease, the urine is dark yellow-red, and the pulse is small and rough, the patient has no appetite.
When a person is ill, if the pulse at the Cunkou is equal in size to the pulse at Renying, and equal also in depth, whether floating or sunken, the disease will be difficult to resolve.
In a woman, if the pulse of the Hand Shaoyin channel beats vigorously, she is pregnant.

When an infant is ill, if all the hair on the head stands up reversed, the infant will certainly die. If a green-blue vessel rises up between the ears, this indicates pulling pain. If the stool contains red flecks together with diarrhea of undigested food, and the pulse is small while the hands and feet are cold, this is difficult to resolve; if there is diarrhea of undigested food with a small pulse but the hands and feet are warm, the diarrhea is easily resolved.

In the transformations of the four seasons and the ascendancy of cold and heat, doubled yin must turn into yang, and doubled yang must turn into yin. Thus yin governs cold and yang governs heat; hence when cold reaches its extreme it turns to heat, and when heat reaches its extreme it turns to cold. Thus it is said: cold gives rise to heat, and heat gives rise to cold — this is the transformation of yin and yang.

Thus it is said: if one is injured by cold in winter, in spring one will develop warm disease; if injured by wind in spring, in summer one will develop diarrhea with undigested food and dysenteric disorders; if injured by summer-heat in summer, in autumn one will develop malarial fevers; if injured by dampness in autumn, in winter one will develop cough. This is called the sequence of the four seasons.

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on the Categories of Needling, and on True and Evil Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I have heard that there are five guarding regimens of needling — what are they?" Qibo replied: "Indeed there are five guarding regimens: the first is called 'Shaking Off the Dust' (zhen'ai); the second, 'Dispelling the Blindness' (fameng); the third, 'Removing the Nails' (quzhao); the fourth, 'Stripping Off the Robe' (chexi); and the fifth, 'Resolving Confusion' (jiehuo)." Huangdi said: "You speak of these five guarding regimens, Master, but I do not yet understand their meaning." Qibo said: "'Shaking Off the Dust' means needling the outer channels to remove yang disease; 'Dispelling the Blindness' means needling the transport points of the bowels to remove disease of the bowels; 'Removing the Nails' means needling the joints and the limb network vessels; 'Stripping Off the Robe' means needling all of the unusual transport points of the yang channels; 'Resolving Confusion' means fully understanding how to regulate yin and yang, to supplement and drain what is in excess or deficient, and to shift the balance between them."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Shaking Off the Dust,' yet you explain it as needling the outer channels to remove yang disease — I do not understand what this means. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "In 'Shaking Off the Dust,' the yang qi surges greatly in reversal, filling and swelling the chest; the patient heaves his shoulders in labored breathing, the great qi rebels upward, he pants and gasps, sitting slumped forward, and finds even the smell of dust or smoke intolerable; he cannot eat without becoming breathless. Let me speak of 'Shaking Off the Dust' — its effect is swifter even than the shaking off of dust." Huangdi said: "Good. How is it applied?" Qibo said: "It is applied at Tianrong." Huangdi said: "And for one who coughs, whose qi rebels upward, who is exhausted and clumsy in movement, and who suffers chest pain, how is it applied?" Qibo said: "It is applied at Lianquan." Huangdi said: "Is there a measure to observe in applying it?" Qibo said: "In needling Tianrong, one should not exceed one li in depth; in needling Lianquan, one stops once the blood changes in color." Huangdi said: "Excellent."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Dispelling the Blindness,' but I still do not grasp its meaning. Now 'dispelling the blindness' concerns one whose ears can hear nothing and whose eyes can see nothing — yet you explain it as needling the transport points of the bowels to remove disease of the bowels. Through which transport point is this accomplished? I wish to hear the reason." Qibo said: "How subtle a question this is! This is the great essence of needling, the utmost of the needling art, belonging to the realm of spiritual brilliance — something that spoken word or written text still cannot fully convey. Let me speak of 'Dispelling the Blindness' — its effect is swifter even than the dispelling of blindness." Huangdi said: "Good. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "In needling for this, one must do so at midday, needling Tinggong, aiming so as to reach the pupil, so that a sound is heard within the ear — this is the transport point." Huangdi said: "Good. What is meant by 'a sound heard within the ear'?" Qibo said: "In needling this pattern, one presses firmly with the hand upon both nostrils, and then has the patient abruptly hold his breath and cry out — the response will surely be felt through the needle." Huangdi said: "Good. This is what is called 'acting upon what cannot be seen' — needling without visual observation, yet grasping the disease as though it were seen, so that spirit and brightness meet one another."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Removing the Nails,' yet you explain it as needling the joints and the limb network vessels. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "The lower back and spine are the great joints of the body; the limbs and shins are the channels by which a person walks and moves freely; the penis and scrotum are the pivot within the body, the sign of yin essence, and the pathway of the fluids. Thus when eating and drinking are not moderated, and joy and anger arise at improper times, the fluids overflow within and descend to lodge in the testicles; the pathway of blood becomes obstructed, and the swelling grows larger day by day without ceasing, so that bending and stretching become difficult and walking becomes impossible. In this disease, water gathers glistening within, neither rising nor descending; it is treated with the pi-needle or the stone lancet. Its form cannot be hidden, and it is always exposed and cannot be concealed — hence it is called 'Removing the Nails.'" Huangdi said: "Good."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Stripping Off the Robe,' yet you explain it as needling all of the unusual transport points of the yang channels, which have no fixed location. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "This concerns a case in which the yang qi is in excess while the yin qi is insufficient. When yin qi is insufficient, heat arises within; when yang qi is in excess, heat arises without. When internal and external heat contend with one another, the patient feels hotter than if embracing burning charcoal; outwardly he dreads the touch even of silk or cotton cloth, unable to bear it near his body, nor even near his sleeping mat. The pores become blocked and closed, so that sweat cannot be released; the tongue becomes scorched and the lips wither, drying out further day by day, and the patient no longer distinguishes good food from bad in eating and drinking." Huangdi said: "Good. How is this treated?" Qibo said: "One needles Tianfu and Dazhu, three punctures each, and further needles Zhonglü, in order to remove the heat; one supplements the Foot and Hand Taiyin channels, in order to remove the sweating. Once the heat is removed and the sweating diminishes, the effect is swifter than the stripping off of a robe." Huangdi said: "Good."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Resolving Confusion,' yet you explain it as fully understanding how to regulate yin and yang, to supplement and drain what is in excess or deficient, and to shift the balance between them. How, then, is confusion actually resolved?" Qibo said: "When great wind evil lodges in the body, the blood vessels become unevenly deficient on one side — where deficient, there is insufficiency, and where replete, there is excess.
