The body loses its balance between light and heavy, tilting and slumping to one side; the patient no longer knows east from west, nor north from south; he is now up, now down, now turned over, now turned back, tumbling about without constancy, worse even than ordinary confusion." Huangdi said: "Good. How is this treated?" Qibo said: "One drains what is in excess and supplements what is deficient, so that yin and yang are restored to balance. When the needle is used in this way, its effect is swifter than the resolving of confusion." Huangdi said: "Good. Let this be stored in the chamber of the Numinous Orchid, and I dare not let it be recklessly disclosed."

Huangdi said: "I have heard that there are five evils treated by needling — what are the five evils?" Qibo said: "There is the disease that carries welling-abscess, the disease of excessive fullness, the disease of narrowness and smallness, the disease of heat, and the disease of cold — these are called the five evils." Huangdi said: "How does one needle the five evils?" Qibo said: "In needling the five evils, there are no more than five methods: intense heat is made to dissolve and vanish; swelling and accumulation are made to disperse and disappear; cold impediment is warmed further; smallness is supplemented with yang; and largeness must be removed. Let me set forth these methods."

In needling welling-abscess evil in general, do not confront it head-on while it is at its height; alter its habitual course and shift its nature. If pus has not yet formed, the fragile pathway can still be redirected; drive the evil from its lodging place, so that, finding no place to settle, it disperses and vanishes. Wherever excess occurs along the yin or yang channels as a welling-abscess, needle its transport point and drain it.

In needling great evil in general, make it smaller day by day: drain and dislodge its excess, and thereby increase its deficiency. Strike swiftly at its open pathway, needling the evil itself; observe closely the flesh and muscle, taking care not to go against what is true and proper. Needle between the flesh and muscle of the yang channels.

In needling small evil in general, make it larger day by day: supplement its deficiency, and thereby there will be no harm. Observe where it lies, and meet it at its boundary, so that, whether near or far, the qi fully arrives; when the evil can no longer invade from without, it exhausts itself of its own accord. Needle between the flesh and muscle.

In needling heat evil in general, disperse it outward until it pales and fades away, letting it wander off and never return, and thereby there will be no more disease. Open up a passage, throw open the gate, so that the evil may find its way out — then the disease will be cured.

In needling cold evil in general, make it warm day by day: insert and withdraw the needle slowly, so as to summon the spirit qi. Once the gate is closed, the qi does not scatter; deficiency and excess become regulated, and the qi is thereby preserved.

Huangdi said: "What needles are properly used for these?" Qibo said: "In needling welling-abscess, use the pi-needle, the sword-shaped needle; in needling the great excess, use the sharp-pointed needle; in needling the small deficiency, use the round-sharp needle; in needling heat, use the caizhen, a slender tapering needle; in needling cold, use the haozhen, the fine, hair-like needle."

Let me now speak of an explanatory discourse: the human body corresponds to Heaven and Earth, and accords with the four seasons; the human being takes part with Heaven and Earth, and so this correspondence can be explained. Where the ground below is damp and marshy, reeds and cattails grow above it — this is how one may know the relative abundance of a person's physical form and qi. Yin and yang correspond to cold and heat: when it is hot, moistening rains fall from above, and the roots and stalks of plants hold little sap; in the human being, the qi lies at the exterior, the skin is relaxed, the pores are open, the blood and qi thin out at the surface, sweat is greatly released, and the skin is moist and glossy. When it is cold, the earth freezes and the water turns to ice; in the human being, the qi lies within, the skin becomes dense, the pores are closed, sweat does not come out, the blood and qi are strong and concentrated within, and the flesh is firm and rough. At such a time, even one skilled at conducting water cannot make it flow through ice; even one skilled at digging through earth cannot break through the frozen ground; and even one skilled in using the needle cannot immediately relieve reversal disorder of the four limbs when the blood vessels are congealed and bound, firm and pressed together, no longer flowing back and forth — this too cannot at once be softened. Thus one who would conduct water must wait for the weather to turn warm, so that the ice melts and the frozen ground thaws, before the water can flow and the earth can be dug. The human pulse and vessels are just the same. In treating reversal disorders, one must first apply warming compresses to harmonize the channels — the palms and armpits, the elbows and legs, the nape and spine — in order to bring them into balance; once the fire qi has penetrated through, the blood and vessels then begin to flow. Only afterward does one examine the disease: if the pulse is moist and glossy, one needles it to bring it to balance; if it is hard and tight, one breaks through it to disperse it, stopping only once the qi has descended — this is what is called 'releasing the knot.'

The general method of using the needle lies in regulating the qi. Qi accumulates in the stomach, from which nutritive qi and defensive qi are made to flow freely, each along its own pathway. Ancestral qi (zongqi — the qi formed from the union of respired air and the qi of grain, which gathers in the chest) collects in the Sea of Qi; that which descends flows into the Qi Thoroughfare (qijie), and that which ascends travels along the pathway of respiration. Thus when reversal cold occurs in the feet, it is because the ancestral qi fails to descend, and the blood within the vessels congeals and stagnates; if one does not use warming to regulate it, one cannot resolve the condition.

One who uses the needle must first examine whether the channels and network vessels are excess or deficient, palpating along their course, pressing and plucking at them, observing where they respond and move — only then does one select the point and needle it. When the six channels are all in balance, this is called 'without disease'; even if disease is present, it is called one that resolves of itself. If, in a single channel, the upper part is excess and the lower part deficient so that the flow is obstructed, this must be because a transverse network vessel, engorged and full, presses upon the great channel, causing the obstruction; one observes this and drains it — this too is what is called 'releasing the knot.'

When there is cold above and heat below, first needle the Taiyang points at the nape, retaining the needle for a long while; once needling is complete, apply warming compresses to the nape and shoulder blades, allowing the heat to descend and merge with the lower region, stopping only then — this is what is called 'pushing it upward.' When there is heat above and cold below, observe the deficient vessel that has sunk into the channels and network vessels, and needle it, stopping only once the qi has descended — this is what is called 'drawing it downward.'

When great heat pervades the whole body, and the patient is manic, seeing, hearing, and speaking of things that are not real, one examines the Foot Yangming channel and its great network vessel and needles them; if deficient, one supplements it, and where there is stagnant blood with excess, one drains it. One has the patient lie face-up, and, standing at his head, presses the pulsating vessels of the neck firmly with the four fingers of both hands, holding them for a long while, kneading and pinching along them, pushing downward as far as the supraclavicular fossa, and then, stopping and beginning again as before, continues until the heat is dispelled, only then stopping — this is what is called 'pushing it out to disperse it.'

Huangdi said: "There are cases where a single vessel gives rise to dozens of different diseases — sometimes pain, sometimes welling-abscess, sometimes heat, sometimes cold, sometimes itching, sometimes impediment, sometimes numbness — the transformations are endless. What is the reason for this?" Qibo said: "All of these are produced by evil qi." Huangdi said: "I have heard that there is true qi, upright qi, and evil qi. What is meant by true qi?" Qibo said: "True qi is that which is received from Heaven; it combines with the qi of grain to fill and nourish the body. Upright qi (zhengqi) is upright wind — wind that comes from a single direction, being neither a replete wind nor a vacuous, thief-like wind. Evil qi is the thieving harm done to a person by vacuous, thief-like wind; when it strikes a person, it penetrates deeply and cannot leave of its own accord. Upright wind, when it strikes a person, penetrates only shallowly; it combines with the body's own qi and departs of its own accord, for its qi arrives weak and soft, unable to overcome the true qi, and so it departs by itself."

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind strikes a person, it makes him shiver and his body tremble, raising the fine body hair and opening the pores. If it penetrates deeply and, within, strikes against the bone, it becomes bone impediment; if it strikes against the sinews, it becomes sinew contracture; if it strikes within the vessels, it becomes blood stasis, and where blood fails to flow freely, it becomes welling-abscess. If it strikes the flesh and contends with the defensive qi, then, where yang prevails, it becomes heat, and where yin prevails, it becomes cold. When it becomes cold, the true qi departs; once departed, there is deficiency, and in deficiency, cold lodges between the skin and flesh. When this qi is expelled outward, the pores open, the fine body hair stirs, and the qi moves back and forth — this produces itching. If it lingers and does not depart, it becomes impediment. If the defensive qi fails to circulate, it becomes numbness.

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind lodges unevenly in one half of the body, penetrating deeply and settling within among the nutritive and defensive qi, then, as the nutritive and defensive qi gradually decline, the true qi departs, and the evil qi alone remains, giving rise to hemiplegia. Where the evil qi lies more shallowly, there is pain confined to one side of the vessel.

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind penetrates deeply into the body, cold and heat contend with one another, lingering long and becoming fixed within. Where cold prevails over heat, the bones ache and the flesh withers; where heat prevails over cold, the flesh rots and the muscle decays into pus, and if this injures the bone within, the injury to the bone within becomes bone-corrosion. Where there is a preexisting affliction of the sinews in front, so that the sinews are bent and cannot be extended, and the evil qi lodges between them without dispersing, this gives rise to sinew-tumor (jinliu). Where there is a binding-up, and qi gathers there, and defensive qi lodges there without returning, so that fluids remain long in that place and combine with the stagnant qi, this becomes intestinal-tumor (changliu) — a condition that, if longstanding, takes several years to form, and which feels soft when pressed with the hand. Where there is already a binding-up, and qi gathers there, and fluids are retained there, and evil qi strikes into it, the congealing binds tighter day by day, connecting and accumulating in one place, this becomes a xiliu, a hardened, longstanding nodular swelling, which feels firm when pressed with the hand. Where there is a binding-up that penetrates deeply into the bone, so that qi becomes bound up with the bone, and bone and qi merge together, growing larger day by day, this becomes bone-welling (guju, a bone abscess). Where there is a binding-up that strikes into the flesh, and the ancestral qi gathers there, and the evil qi lingers without departing — if there is heat, it transforms into pus; if there is no heat, it becomes a flesh-welling (rouju, a flesh abscess). Now, all these several conditions of qi arise in no fixed place in the body, yet each has its own fixed name.

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on the Circulation of Defensive Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I wish to hear how the defensive qi circulates, and how its emergence and entry correspond to the body — how does this work?" Qibo replied: "The year has twelve months, and the day has twelve double-hours; zi and wu form the warp, and mao and you form the weft. Heaven's circuit comprises twenty-eight lunar lodges, seven stars to each of the four quarters, four sevens making twenty-eight stars in all. Fang and Mao form the weft, while Xu and Zhang form the warp. Thus, from the lodge Fang to the lodge Bi is yang, and from the lodge Mao to the lodge Xin is yin; yang governs the daytime, and yin governs the night. Therefore the circulation of the defensive qi, over the course of one day and one night, makes fifty circuits of the body: during the daytime it travels through the yang for twenty-five circuits, and during the night it travels through the yin for twenty-five circuits, circulating through the five viscera."

Thus, at dawn, when yin is exhausted, the yang qi emerges from the eyes; when the eyes open, the qi ascends to the head, follows the nape, and descends along the Foot Taiyang channel, following the back down to the tip of the little toe. A branch of it separates at the outer canthus of the eye and descends along the Hand Taiyang channel, down to the outer side, between the little finger and the hand. Another branch separates at the outer canthus and descends along the Foot Shaoyang channel, pouring into the space between the fourth and little toes. It then ascends and follows the lateral aspect of the Hand Shaoyang channel, descending to the space between the fingers. A separate branch ascends to a point in front of the ear, joins with the mandibular vessel, and pours into the Foot Yangming channel, descending to the instep and entering the space between the toes. A branch of it descends from below the ear along the Hand Yangming channel, entering the space between the thumb and index finger, and into the palm. When the qi reaches the foot, it enters the sole, emerges at the medial malleolus, travels downward through the yin portion of the leg, and once again joins with the eyes — thus completing one circuit.

Thus, when the sun travels one lodge, the human qi makes one circuit of the body plus eight-tenths of a circuit; when the sun travels two lodges, the human qi makes three circuits of the body plus six-tenths; when the sun travels three lodges, the human qi makes five circuits of the body plus four-tenths; when the sun travels four lodges, the human qi makes seven circuits of the body plus two-tenths; when the sun travels five lodges, the human qi makes nine circuits of the body; when the sun travels six lodges, the human qi makes ten circuits of the body plus eight-tenths; when the sun travels seven lodges, the human qi makes twelve circuits of the body plus six-tenths; when the sun travels fourteen lodges, the human qi makes twenty-five circuits of the body with a remaining fraction of two-tenths — at this point yang is exhausted and passes into yin, and yin then receives the qi. When the qi first enters yin, it always proceeds from the Foot Shaoyin channel and pours into the kidney; the kidney pours into the heart, the heart pours into the lung, the lung pours into the liver, the liver pours into the spleen, and the spleen once again pours into the kidney, completing one circuit. Thus, in the course of the night, for each lodge the sun would travel, the human qi makes one circuit through the yin viscera plus eight-tenths of a circuit, just as in its daytime passage through yang it makes twenty-five circuits, and then once again joins with the eyes. Combining the yang and yin circuits of a single day and night, there remains a fraction of four-tenths of a bodily circuit and two-tenths of a visceral circuit; this is the reason that the time at which people fall asleep and awaken varies, being sometimes early and sometimes late — because this remaining fraction never divides evenly.

Huangdi said: "Since the defensive qi, as it moves through the body, travels up and down without a fixed schedule, how does one wait for the proper moment of qi in order to needle it?" Bogao replied: "The divisions of day and night have their greater and lesser portions, and the days have their longer and shorter lengths; in spring, autumn, winter, and summer, each has its own proportional pattern. Nevertheless, one should always take dawn as the starting point of reckoning, and the end of night as the beginning. Thus, over the course of one day and one night, the water clock drips down a hundred markings (ke); twenty-five markings constitute the measure of half a day. This pattern holds constantly without exception, ending when the sun sets; one reckons according to the varying length of the day and needles accordingly. If one carefully observes the proper timing, the disease can be anticipated and treated in step with it; but if one misses the timing and goes against the proper observation, the hundred diseases cannot be cured. Hence it is said: in needling excess, one needles as the qi is arriving; in needling deficiency, one needles as the qi is departing. This refers to the timing of the qi's presence or absence, by which one observes deficiency and excess and needles accordingly. Thus one must carefully observe where the qi is located and needle it there — this is called 'meeting the proper time.' When the disease is in the three yang channels, one must wait until the qi is in the yang before needling; when the disease is in the three yin channels, one must wait until the qi is in the yin portion before needling."

When the water clock has dripped down one marking, the human qi is in Taiyang; at two markings, the human qi is in Shaoyang; at three markings, the human qi is in Yangming; at four markings, the human qi is in the yin portion. At five markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at six markings, in Shaoyang; at seven markings, in Yangming; at eight markings, in the yin portion. At nine markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at ten markings, in Shaoyang; at eleven markings, in Yangming; at twelve markings, in the yin portion. At thirteen markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at fourteen markings, in Shaoyang; at fifteen markings, in Yangming; at sixteen markings, in the yin portion. At seventeen markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at eighteen markings, in Shaoyang; at nineteen markings, in Yangming; at twenty markings, in the yin portion. At twenty-one markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at twenty-two markings, in Shaoyang; at twenty-three markings, in Yangming; at twenty-four markings, in the yin portion. At twenty-five markings, the human qi is in Taiyang — this is the measure of half a day. From the lodge Fang to the lodge Bi is fourteen lodges, corresponding to fifty markings of the water clock, during which the sun travels half of its circuit, returning through one lodge for every three markings and four-sevenths of a marking. The essential rule states: whenever the sun arrives at a given lodge, the human qi is in Taiyang; thus, as the sun travels one lodge, the human qi travels through the three yang channels and the yin portion. This pattern continues constantly without ever ceasing; Heaven and Earth share the same reckoning, moving on in endless succession, ending only to begin again — over the course of one day and one night, the water clock drips down a hundred markings, and the cycle is complete.

Taiyi (the Grand One — the cosmic pivot whose movements through nine mystical palaces were tracked for divination, akin to a wandering pole star) always begins, on the day of the winter solstice, by dwelling in the Palace of Yezhe (Leaf-Fall and Hibernation) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Tianliu (Celestial Retention) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Cangmen (Granary Gate) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Yinluo for forty-five days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Tiangong (Celestial Palace) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Xuanwei (Mysterious Repose) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Cangguo (Granary Fruit) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Xinluo (New Luo) for forty-five days. The next day it returns once more to dwell in the Palace of Yezhe — and this marks the return of the winter solstice.

Taiyi wanders daily. On the day of the winter solstice it takes up residence in the Palace of Yezhe; one counts the days spent in each location, proceeding from the first palace through to the ninth, and then returning again to the first. It is always thus, without cease, ending only to begin anew.

On the day Taiyi shifts its residence, Heaven invariably responds with wind and rain. If the wind and rain arrive precisely on that day, it is auspicious — the year will be bountiful, the people at peace, and illness scarce. If the wind and rain come before that day, there will be excessive rain; if they come after, there will be drought. Should Taiyi undergo change on the day of the winter solstice, the omen concerns the ruler; should it undergo change on the day of the spring equinox, the omen concerns the ministers; should it undergo change on the day it resides in the central palace, the omen concerns the officials; should it undergo change on the day of the autumn equinox, the omen concerns the generals; should it undergo change on the day of the summer solstice, the omen concerns the common people. What is meant by 'undergoing change' is this: on the days when Taiyi dwells in these five palaces, if disease-bearing winds snap trees and scatter sand and stones, then, according to what each palace governs, one may divine matters of noble and base rank. One divines according to the direction from which the wind arises: wind arising from the direction in which Taiyi currently dwells is called a replete wind; it governs life, and nourishes and rears the myriad things. Wind arising from the direction opposite Taiyi's dwelling is called a vacuous wind; it injures people, governing death and harm. One must carefully watch for vacuous winds and avoid them. Hence the sage's daily practice of avoiding vacuous evil follows this same principle — avoiding it as one would avoid arrows and stones, so that evil cannot cause harm. This is what is meant.

Accordingly, when Taiyi enters and takes its stand in the central palace, it holds court over the eight winds, and by this one divines fortune and misfortune. When the wind comes from the south, it is called the Great Weak Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the heart and appears without in the vessels, and its qi governs heat. When the wind comes from the southwest, it is called the Scheming Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the spleen and appears without in the flesh, and its qi governs weakness. When the wind comes from the west, it is called the Rigid Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the lung and appears without in the skin, and its qi governs dryness. When the wind comes from the northwest, it is called the Breaking Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the small intestine and appears without in the Hand Taiyang vessel; when the vessel is severed there is overflow, and when the vessel is blocked there is binding and non-communication, so that sudden death readily follows. When the wind comes from the north, it is called the Great Rigid Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the kidney and appears without in the bones and in the sinews of the shoulders and back, and its qi governs cold. When the wind comes from the northeast, it is called the Baleful Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the large intestine and appears without at the two flanks, beneath the armpit bones, and at the joints of the limbs. When the wind comes from the east, it is called the Ying-wu Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the liver and appears without at the sinew-knots, and its qi governs bodily dampness. When the wind comes from the southeast, it is called the Weak Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the stomach and appears without in the flesh, and its qi governs heaviness of the body. These eight winds all come from the direction of vacuity, and it is only then that they can cause illness in a person. When three vacuities strike together, this brings about sudden, fulminant disease and abrupt death. When two are replete and one vacuous, illness manifests as painful stranguria and exposure to cold and heat. If one is exposed to a place doubly damp, this brings about wilting weakness. Hence the sage avoids wind as one avoids arrows and stones. When a person has three vacuities and is struck squarely by evil wind, this results in collapse and hemiplegia.

Huangdi said: 'I have heard from you, Master, of the nine needles — a subject vast and profound indeed — yet I still cannot fully grasp it. May I ask: from what did the nine needles arise, and for what reason were they so named?' Qibo said: 'The nine needles embody the great numbers of Heaven and Earth; they begin at one and end at nine. Thus it is said: the first takes Heaven as its model; the second takes Earth as its model; the third takes Man as its model; the fourth takes the seasons as its model; the fifth takes the musical notes as its model; the sixth takes the pitch-pipes as its model; the seventh takes the stars as its model; the eighth takes the winds as its model; and the ninth takes the fields of the earth as its model.'

Huangdi said: 'How do the needles correspond to the number nine?' Qibo said: 'When the sages established the numbers of Heaven and Earth, they took one and multiplied it ninefold, and thereby established the nine fields. Nine multiplied ninefold gives nine nines, eighty-one, from which the pitch of the Yellow Bell was derived — and it is to this number that the needles correspond.'

The first corresponds to Heaven. Heaven is yang. Among the five viscera, the one that corresponds to Heaven is the lung, for the lung is the canopy over the five viscera and six bowels; the skin, being the lung's outward correlate, is the yang of the body. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its head large and its tip sharp, so that it cannot penetrate deeply, and so that the yang qi may be released.

The second corresponds to Earth. That in the human body which corresponds to soil is the flesh. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft tubular and its tip round, so that it cannot injure the partitions of the flesh — for if injured, the qi would become exhausted.

The third corresponds to Man. That by which a person comes to life is the blood and vessels. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft thick and its tip round, so that it can press upon the vessel without sinking into it, in order to summon forth the qi and allow the evil qi alone to be expelled.

The fourth corresponds to the seasons. The seasons are when the qi of the four seasons and eight winds lodges within the channels and network vessels, giving rise to tumor-like afflictions. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft tubular and its tip bladed, so that it can drain heat and let blood, thereby exhausting stubborn and chronic disease.

The fifth corresponds to the musical notes. The notes mark the division between winter and summer, divided at the hours of zi and wu, where yin and yang separate and cold and heat contend; when these two qi clash, they combine to form welling-abscess and pus. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its tip like the blade of a sword, so that it can drain great accumulations of pus.

The sixth corresponds to the pitch-pipes. The pitch-pipes regulate yin and yang and the four seasons, and correspond to the twelve channels; this is where vacuous evil lodges in the channels and network vessels, producing sudden impediment disorders. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip must be as fine as a hair yet rounded at its point, with the shaft slightly thickened at the middle, so as to draw out sudden and fulminant qi.

The seventh corresponds to the stars. The stars correspond to the seven orifices of the person, where evil lodges in the channels and becomes painful impediment, joined with the channels and network vessels. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip should be like the proboscis of a mosquito or gnat; it should be inserted quietly and slowly, retained gently for a long while, so that the upright qi may follow it and the true and the evil qi both depart together; the needle is then withdrawn, allowing the patient to be nourished and restored.

The eighth corresponds to the winds. The winds correspond to the eight joints of a person's thighs and arms. The vacuous winds of the eight solar terms, the eight winds, injure a person by lodging within, between the bone joints, the lower back and spine, and the interstitial spaces, producing deep impediment. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft long and its tip bladed, so that it can reach deep evil and distant impediment.

The ninth corresponds to the fields. The fields correspond to the spaces between a person's joints and skin. This is where excessive evil overflows through the body, resembling the swelling of wind-water, and lingers, unable to pass through the pivoting mechanisms of the great joints. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip should be like a rod, its point slightly rounded, so as to draw out great qi that cannot pass through the joints.

Huangdi said: 'Do the needles have fixed measures of length?' Qibo said: 'The first is called the chan needle — a broad-headed needle modeled after the sewing needle; it tapers abruptly to a point one and a half cun from its tip, and measures one cun and six fen in length; it governs heat located in the head and body. The second is called the round needle; it is modeled after the felting needle, its shaft tubular and its tip egg-shaped, one cun and six fen in length; it governs disorders of qi between the flesh partitions. The third is called the blunt needle; it is modeled after the sharp point of a millet grain, three and a half cun in length; it governs pressing upon the vessel to summon the qi and expel evil. The fourth is called the lancet needle; it is modeled after the felting needle, its shaft tubular and its tip bladed, one cun and six fen in length; it governs draining heat from welling-abscess and letting blood. The fifth is called the sword-tip needle; it is modeled after the edge of a sword, two and a half fen in width and four cun in length; it governs great welling-abscess and pus, where two heats contend with one another. The sixth is called the round-sharp needle; it is modeled after a fine, hair-thin needle, its tip made slightly thicker while its shaft is made slightly thinner, so that it may penetrate deeply; it is one cun and six fen in length, and governs the treatment of welling-abscess and impediment. The seventh is called the filiform needle; it takes its form from a fine hair, is one cun and six fen in length, and governs cold and heat, pain and impediment located in the network vessels. The eighth is called the long needle; it is modeled after a long, slender probe, seven cun in length, and governs the extraction of deep evil and distant impediment. The ninth is called the great needle; it is modeled after the lancet needle, its tip made slightly rounded, four cun in length, and governs the extraction of great qi that cannot pass through the joints. Thus the forms of the needles are complete — these are the rules governing the size and length of the nine needles.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear how the body's form corresponds to the nine fields.' Qibo said: 'Allow me to explain how the body's form corresponds to the nine fields. The left foot corresponds to the Establishment of Spring, its days being wuyin and jichou. The left rib-side corresponds to the Spring Equinox, its day being yimao. The left hand corresponds to the Establishment of Summer, its days being wuchen and jisi. The chest, throat, and head correspond to the Summer Solstice, its day being bingwu. The right hand corresponds to the Establishment of Autumn, its days being wushen and jiwei. The right rib-side corresponds to the Autumn Equinox, its day being xinyou. The right foot corresponds to the Establishment of Winter, its days being wuxu and jihai. The lower back, sacrum, and lower orifices correspond to the Winter Solstice, its day being renzi. The six bowels and, below the diaphragm, the three viscera correspond to the central region, and its great prohibition falls on the days on which Taiyi resides there, as well as on all wu and ji days. In all nine of these cases, one does well to watch for the times when the eight solar terms fall. If welling-abscess or swelling appears on the left, right, upper, or lower part of the body governed by these regions, and one wishes to treat it, one must not lance and treat it on the very day corresponding to that region — these are what are called the heaven-forbidden days.'

When the body is at ease but the mind distressed, disease arises in the vessels, and it is treated with moxibustion and needling. When the body is distressed but the mind at ease, disease arises in the sinews, and it is treated with hot compress and stretching exercises. When both body and mind are at ease, disease arises in the flesh, and it is treated with needle and stone. When both body and mind are distressed, disease arises in the throat, and it is treated with sweet-flavored medicinals. When a person is repeatedly startled and frightened, so that the sinews and vessels fail to communicate, disease arises as numbness and insensitivity, and it is treated with massage and medicinal wine. This is what is meant by the body's condition.
As for the qi of the five viscera: the heart governs belching; the lung governs coughing; the liver governs excessive talking; the spleen governs swallowing; and the kidney governs yawning.

As for the qi of the six bowels: the gallbladder governs anger; the stomach governs rebellious qi and foul emissions; the large and small intestines govern diarrhea; when the bladder fails to control itself, this governs enuresis; and when the lower burner overflows, this governs water swelling.

The five flavors: sour enters the liver, pungent enters the lung, bitter enters the heart, sweet enters the spleen, salty enters the kidney, and bland enters the stomach — these are called the five flavors.

The five concentrations: when essential qi concentrates in the liver, this causes sorrow; when it concentrates in the heart, this causes joy; when it concentrates in the lung, this causes grief; when it concentrates in the kidney, this causes fear; when it concentrates in the spleen, this causes apprehension — these are called the concentrations of the five essential qi within the viscera.

The five aversions: the liver detests wind; the heart detests heat; the lung detests cold; the kidney detests dryness; and the spleen detests dampness — these are what the qi of the five viscera detest.

The five fluids: the heart governs sweat; the liver governs tears; the lung governs nasal mucus; the kidney governs spittle; and the spleen governs saliva — these are the sources from which the five fluids emerge.

The five forms of taxation: prolonged looking injures the blood; prolonged lying injures the qi; prolonged sitting injures the flesh; prolonged standing injures the bones; and prolonged walking injures the sinews — these are the illnesses caused by the five prolonged exertions.

The five destinations: sour flavor travels to the sinews; pungent flavor travels to the qi; bitter flavor travels to the blood; salty flavor travels to the bones; and sweet flavor travels to the flesh — these are called the five destinations.

The five restrictions: when disease is in the sinews, one should not eat sour flavors; when disease is in the qi, one should not eat pungent flavors; when disease is in the bones, one should not eat salty flavors; when disease is in the blood, one should not eat bitter flavors; when disease is in the flesh, one should not eat sweet flavors. Even if the mouth craves and desires such flavors, one must not indulge in excess — one must restrain oneself. This is called the five restrictions.
The five arisings: yin disease arises in the bones; yang disease arises in the blood; yin disease arises in the flesh; yang disease arises in winter; yin disease arises in summer.

The five evils: when evil enters yang, it causes mania; when evil enters yin, it causes blood impediment; when evil enters yang and shifts, it causes withdrawal epilepsy; when evil enters yin and shifts, it causes muteness; when yang evil enters into yin, the illness is marked by quietude; when yin evil emerges into yang, the illness is marked by irritability and anger.
The five viscera: the heart stores the spirit; the lung stores the corporeal soul (po); the liver stores the ethereal soul (hun); the spleen stores intent; and the kidney stores essence and will.
The five governances: the heart governs the vessels; the lung governs the skin; the liver governs the sinews; the spleen governs the muscles; and the kidney governs the bones.

Yangming is abundant in both blood and qi; Taiyang is abundant in blood but scant in qi; Shaoyang is abundant in qi but scant in blood; Taiyin is abundant in blood but scant in qi; Jueyin is abundant in blood but scant in qi; and Shaoyin is abundant in qi but scant in blood. Hence it is said: when needling Yangming, let out both blood and qi; when needling Taiyang, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; when needling Shaoyang, let out qi but avoid depleting the blood; when needling Taiyin, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; when needling Jueyin, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; and when needling Shaoyin, let out qi but avoid depleting the blood.

The Foot Yangming and Taiyin channels stand in interior-exterior relation; the Foot Shaoyang and Jueyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Foot Taiyang and Shaoyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation — these are the yin and yang pairings of the foot channels. The Hand Yangming and Taiyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Hand Shaoyang and Pericardium (Heart Ruler) channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Hand Taiyang and Shaoyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation — these are the yin and yang pairings of the hand channels.

灵枢经.title_en: Discourse on the Dew of the Year
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The canonical texts say that being injured by summer-heat in summer causes malarial disease in autumn, and that the onset of malarial disease follows a fixed schedule — what is the reason for this?' Qibo replied: 'The evil lodges in Fengfu (the Wind Mansion), and the disease then travels downward along the spine. The defensive qi, over the course of a day and night, always converges fully at Fengfu; on the following day it descends one vertebral segment, and so the onset of the attack each day comes progressively later — this is because the evil first lodged in the spine and back. Thus, each time the defensive qi reaches Fengfu, the interstices open; when the interstices open, the evil qi enters; when the evil qi enters, the disease erupts — this is why the onset comes progressively later each day. As the defensive qi travels down from Fengfu, descending one segment per day, by the twenty-first day it reaches down to the tailbone, and on the twenty-second day it enters the interior of the spine and pours into the Fuchong (Hidden Penetrating) Vessel; traveling for nine days, it emerges within Quepen, the supraclavicular fossa, and its qi then moves upward — hence the disease gradually intensifies as time goes on. When the evil strikes internally against the five viscera and connects laterally with the membrane-source, its pathway is long and its qi lies deep, so its movement is slow and it cannot erupt daily; hence it accumulates over the following day before erupting.'

Huangdi said: 'When the defensive qi reaches Fengfu each time, the interstices open, and once opened, the evil enters. Yet if the defensive qi descends one segment each day, then it will no longer coincide with Fengfu — how is this?' Qibo said: 'Fengfu has no fixed location. Wherever the defensive qi responds, it must open the interstices there; the joint-segment where the qi lodges becomes, at that moment, its mansion.'

Huangdi said: 'Excellent. Now, wind disorders and malarial disease belong to the same category, yet wind persists constantly while malarial disease alone rests according to a fixed schedule — why is this?' Qibo said: 'Wind qi lingers in its location, whereas malarial qi follows the channels and network vessels, sinking down to strike internally; hence it erupts only when the defensive qi responds to it there.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent.'

Huangdi asked Shaoshi: 'I have heard that when the qi of the four seasons and eight winds strikes a person, there is accordingly cold and heat: in cold, the skin becomes tight and the interstices close; in heat, the skin becomes relaxed and the interstices open. Is it through this that thieving wind and evil qi manage to enter? Or must they wait specifically for the vacuous evil of the eight solar terms before they are able to injure a person?' Shaoshi answered: 'It is not so. When thieving wind and evil qi strike a person, they do not do so according to any fixed season; but they must always take advantage of an opening. When they take advantage of an opening, they penetrate deeply, and the illness within reaches its extreme; such illness strikes the person suddenly and violently. When they take advantage of a closing, they penetrate only shallowly and merely linger; such illness develops slowly and gradually.'

Huangdi said: 'There are cases in which cold and warmth are perfectly balanced, and the interstices are not open, and yet sudden illness still occurs — what is the reason for this?' Shaoshi answered: 'Does Your Majesty not know how evil enters? Even in ordinary daily life, the opening and closing, slackening and tightening of the interstices, follow their own regular timing.' Huangdi said: 'May I hear of this?' Shaoshi said: 'Man corresponds to Heaven and Earth, and responds to the sun and moon. Thus, when the moon is full, the ocean waters swell in the west, a person's blood and qi accumulate, the flesh and muscles become full, the skin becomes dense, the body hair becomes firm, the interstices become closely knit, and soot and grime cling to the skin; at such a time, even if one encounters thieving wind, it penetrates only shallowly, not deeply. But when the moon's disk becomes empty, the ocean waters swell in the east, a person's qi and blood become deficient, the defensive qi departs, the body's form stands alone, the flesh and muscles diminish, the skin becomes slack, the interstices open, the body hair withers, the connective tissue thins, and soot and grime fall away; at such a time, if one encounters thieving wind, it penetrates deeply, and the illness it causes strikes the person suddenly and violently.'

Huangdi said: 'There are cases of sudden death and fulminant illness striking without warning — why is this?' Shaoshi answered: 'Those with the three vacuities die suddenly of fulminant disease; those who possess the three repletions cannot be harmed by evil.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the three vacuities.' Shaoshi said: 'Riding upon the decline of the year, encountering the emptiness of the moon, and losing the harmony of the season — and thereby being injured by thieving wind — these together are called the three vacuities. Hence, if in discussing disease one fails to recognize the three vacuities, even a practitioner is thereby rendered crude and unskilled.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the three repletions.' Shaoshi said: 'Encountering the abundance of the year, meeting the fullness of the moon, and obtaining the harmony of the season — then, even in the presence of thieving wind and evil qi, one cannot be endangered by it.' Huangdi said: 'How excellent this discourse! How clear this Way! Let it be stored in the golden casket, and let it be called the three repletions. And yet, this is but the account of a single person.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear why, in certain years, the whole population suffers the same illness together — for what reason is this so?' Shaoshi said: 'This is what is observed through the eight solar terms.' Huangdi said: 'How does one observe this?' Shaoshi said: 'To observe this, one always looks to the day of the winter solstice, when Taiyi takes its stand in the Palace of Yezhe. At its arrival, Heaven invariably responds with wind and rain. If the wind and rain come from the south, this is a vacuous wind, one that steals in and injures people. If it arrives at midnight, the myriad people are all asleep and are not exposed to it, and so in that year the people fall ill but little. If it arrives during the day, the myriad people are all relaxed and off guard, and all are struck by the vacuous wind, and so the people fall ill in great numbers. The vacuous evil enters and lodges in the bones without manifesting outwardly; when the Establishment of Spring arrives, the yang qi bursts forth greatly and the interstices open, and if, on the very day of the Establishment of Spring, wind comes from the west, the myriad people are again all struck by vacuous wind. When these two evils strike one another, the channel qi becomes bound and knotted, and the pulse becomes intermittent. Hence, whenever people encounter such wind and meet with such rain, this is called encountering the dew of the year. When the year is harmonious and thieving winds are few, the people fall ill but little and die but rarely. When the year has abundant thieving wind and evil qi, and cold and warmth are not in harmony, then the people fall ill in great numbers and many die.'

Huangdi said: 'As for the wind of vacuous evil, how does the harm it causes differ between the noble and the humble, and how does one observe it?' Shaoshi answered: 'On the first day of the first month, Taiyi resides in the Palace of Tianliu. If on that day the wind blows from the northwest without rain, many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dawn, the wind blows from the north, then in spring many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dawn, the north wind blows, the number of people who fall ill will be three in ten. If on the first day of the first month, at midday, the wind blows from the north, then in summer many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dusk, the wind blows from the north, then in autumn many people will die. If the north wind blows the whole day through, six in ten will fall gravely ill and die. If on the first day of the first month the wind comes from the south, this is called the Land of Drought. If it comes from the west, this is called White Bones, foretelling calamity for the state, with many people dying. If on the first day of the first month the wind comes from the east, it will tear roofs from houses and scatter sand and stones, and the state will suffer great disaster. If on the first day of the first month the wind blows from the southeast, there will be deaths in spring. If on the first day of the first month the weather is mild and warm without wind, grain prices will be low and the people will not fall ill; if the weather is cold and windy, grain prices will be high and the people will fall ill in great numbers. This is what is meant by observing the winds of the year that injure and harm people. If in the second month, on the chou day, there is no wind, the people will suffer greatly from disorders of the heart and abdomen. If in the third month, on the xu day, the weather is not warm, the people will suffer greatly from cold and heat disorders. If in the fourth month, on the si day, the weather is not hot, the people will suffer greatly from jaundice-heat disorders. If in the tenth month, on the shen day, the weather is not cold, the people will suffer greatly from sudden death. In all cases, what is called wind is that which tears roofs from houses, snaps trees, scatters sand and stones, raises the fine hairs of the body, and forces open the interstices.'

Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I once ascended the Terrace of Clear Coolness (Qingleng Tai), and midway up the stairway I turned to look back, then found myself creeping forward on hands and knees, overcome with confusion. I privately found this strange and secretly wondered at it within myself. I closed my eyes and looked again, calmed my heart and settled my qi, but for a long time the confusion did not resolve. My vision reeled and spun; I let my hair hang loose and knelt for a long while, bowing my head to look down, yet even after a long time the disorder did not stop. It came upon me suddenly as I climbed — what qi causes this to happen?' Qibo replied: 'The essence qi of the five viscera and six bowels all ascends to pour into the eyes and becomes their essence. The nest of this essence forms the eye itself; the essence of the bones forms the pupil; the essence of the sinews forms the black of the eye; the essence of the blood forms the network vessels binding the eye's socket; the essence of qi forms the white of the eye; and the essence of the flesh and muscles forms the surrounding enclosure. Together these wrap and gather the essence of sinew, bone, blood, and qi, and join with the vessels to form the eye-system (muxi). Above, this system attaches to the brain; behind, it emerges at the nape of the neck. Hence when evil qi strikes the nape and meets with a deficiency in the body, it penetrates deeply and follows the eye-system inward into the brain. Once it enters the brain, the brain is set spinning; when the brain spins, it pulls the eye-system taut; and when the eye-system is drawn taut, the eyes grow dizzy and everything seems to whirl. When evil strikes the essence itself, and the places struck no longer correspond evenly to one another, the essence scatters; and when the essence scatters, vision divides, so that one sees two objects where there is only one. The eyes are the essence of the five viscera and six bowels, the place where nutritive qi, defensive qi, and the hun and po souls constantly circulate, and where the spirit qi is generated. Hence when the spirit is overtaxed, the hun and po scatter and the will and intent fall into disorder. This is why the pupil and the black of the eye are patterned on yin, while the white of the eye and its red vessels are patterned on yang; only when yin and yang combine and are transmitted together does clear vision result. The eyes are the emissary of the heart, and the heart is the lodging place of the spirit. Hence when the spirit and essence fall into disorder and no longer turn properly, and one suddenly perceives things in unaccustomed places, the essence, spirit, hun, and po scatter and no longer cohere with one another — this is what is called confusion (huo).'

Huangdi said: 'I have suspected this might be so. Whenever I go to the East Garden, I am invariably overcome with confusion, and once I leave it, all returns to normal. Is it that the East Garden alone taxes my spirit? Why should it be so peculiar?' Qibo said: 'It is not so. When the heart delights in something while the spirit finds something distasteful, and the two suddenly clash, the essence qi falls into disorder and vision goes astray — hence confusion arises, and only when the spirit shifts does it return to normal. This is why a mild case is called disorientation (mi), while a severe case is called confusion (huo).'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to be forgetful?' Qibo said: 'When the qi of the upper body is insufficient while the qi of the lower body has a surplus, the intestines and stomach are replete while the heart and lung are deficient. When they are deficient, the nutritive and defensive qi linger below and, for a long time, fail to ascend at their proper time — hence the person becomes forgetful.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to be easily hungry yet have no appetite for food?' Qibo said: 'The essence qi has converged upon the spleen, while heat qi lingers in the stomach. When the stomach is heated it digests grain quickly, and because the grain is digested quickly the person becomes hungry easily. When the stomach qi rebels upward, the stomach duct turns cold, and hence the person has no appetite for food.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person, when ill, to be unable to sleep?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi is unable to enter yin and instead lingers constantly in yang. When it lingers in yang, the yang qi becomes full; when the yang qi is full, the Yang Springing Vessel (yangqiao) becomes excessive. Since the defensive qi cannot enter yin, the yin qi becomes deficient — hence the eyes will not close in sleep.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person's eyes to be afflicted so that they cannot see?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi lingers in yin and is unable to move into yang. When it lingers in yin, the yin qi becomes exuberant; when the yin qi is exuberant, the Yin Springing Vessel (yinqiao) becomes full. Since the defensive qi cannot enter yang, the yang qi becomes deficient — hence the eyes remain closed.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to sleep excessively?' Qibo said: 'In such a person the intestines and stomach are large, the skin is damp, and the interstitial flesh does not separate readily. When the intestines and stomach are large, the defensive qi lingers there for a long time; when the skin is damp and the interstitial flesh fails to separate readily, the qi's movement is slowed. Now, the defensive qi ordinarily travels through yang by day and through yin by night; hence when the yang qi is exhausted one sleeps, and when the yin qi is exhausted one wakes. Thus when the intestines and stomach are large, the defensive qi lingers long as it travels; when the skin is damp and the interstitial flesh fails to separate readily, its movement is slow. Having lingered long in yin, its qi grows turbid rather than clear, so the person longs to close the eyes — hence excessive sleep results. When, by contrast, the intestines and stomach are small, the skin is smooth and relaxed, and the interstitial flesh separates freely, the defensive qi lingers long in yang — hence the person sleeps little.'

Huangdi said: 'What of cases outside this ordinary pattern, where a person suddenly begins to sleep excessively — what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Evil qi lingers in the upper burner, and the upper burner becomes closed and impassable. After eating, or after drinking hot broth, the defensive qi lingers long in yin and fails to move — hence the person suddenly sleeps excessively.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. How should one treat all these various evils?' Qibo said: 'First address the viscera and bowels, eliminating their minor faults; afterward regulate the qi, draining what is excessive and supplementing what is deficient. One must first clearly ascertain whether the patient's body and will are at ease or in distress, and only once this is settled should one proceed to needle.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that when the intestines and stomach receive grain, the upper burner sends forth qi to warm the interstitial flesh, nourish the bones and joints, and open the interstices of the skin. The middle burner sends forth qi like dew, which ascends to pour into the valleys and ravines of the flesh and seeps into the grandchild vessels (sunmai); there the fluids and humors become harmonized, and are transformed, turning red to become blood. When the blood is harmonious, the grandchild vessels first become full to overflowing, and then pour into the network vessels; when these too are full, they pour into the channel vessels. Once yin and yang have thus become fully extended, the blood moves in step with the breath. Its movement follows a set order, its circuit follows a fixed course, corresponding with Heaven itself, and it never comes to rest. When one palpates the vessels in order to regulate them: proceeding from deficiency, one removes excess — yet if one drains what is already deficient, insufficiency only deepens; if this is done too hastily, the qi is diminished; and if the needle is retained, the effects unfold in their proper sequence, first one thing and then another. Proceeding from excess, one removes deficiency — yet if one supplements what is already in excess, a surplus results. Once the blood and qi have been regulated in this way, the body's form and qi are thereby sustained. I have already learned of the balance and imbalance of blood and qi, but I do not yet know from what welling-abscess (yong) and flat-abscess (ju) arise, the timing of their formation and resolution, nor the term allotted for death or survival, whether near or distant. How is this to be gauged? May I hear of it?'

Qibo said: 'The channel vessels flow without cease, in step with Heaven's own measure and in accord with Earth's own order. Thus when the lodges of Heaven lose their proper measure, the sun and moon become eclipsed and dimmed; when the pathways of Earth lose their proper order, the waterways overflow their banks, the grasses and weeds fail to mature, and the five grains fail to grow; when roads become impassable, the people cease to travel between one another, and those gathered in lanes and settled in towns become cut off and separated from one another. The blood and qi are just the same — allow me to explain the reason. Now the blood vessels, together with the nutritive and defensive qi, circulate ceaselessly, corresponding above to the stars and constellations, and below to the fixed measure of the channels. When cold evil lodges within the channels and network vessels, the blood congeals; when the blood congeals it fails to flow freely; and when it fails to flow freely, the defensive qi converges there and is unable to return along its course — hence welling-abscess and swelling result. The cold qi transforms into heat; when the heat prevails it putrefies the flesh, and when the flesh putrefies it becomes pus. If the pus is not drained away it will rot the sinews; when the sinews rot, the bone is injured; when the bone is injured, the marrow is consumed. If this does not open into a hollow of the bone through which it might drain, and cannot be released and discharged, the blood dries up and the area becomes hollow and empty, so that the sinews, bones, and flesh no longer nourish one another; the channel vessels decay and leak, and the noxious vapor steams upward into the five viscera — once the viscera are injured, death follows.'

Huangdi said: 'I should like to hear in full the various forms that welling-abscess and flat-abscess take, together with the names by which each is known and the dangers to be guarded against.' Qibo said: 'When an abscess breaks out within the throat, it is called mengju, the fierce ju. If fierce ju is not treated, it turns to pus; if the pus is not drained it will block the throat, and the patient dies within half a day. Once it has turned to pus, drain it, apply pig's fat, and have the patient eat cold food; within three days it will be resolved.'

When it breaks out on the neck, it is called yaoju, the fatal ju. The swelling is large and reddish-black; if not treated promptly, the heat qi will descend to Yuanye, the hollow of the armpit, injuring the Conception Vessel in front and steaming the liver and lung within. Once it steams the liver and lung, the patient dies within ten-odd days.

When yang heat lingers and erupts greatly, consuming the brain and lodging in the nape, it is called naoshuo, brain-scorching. Its color is dull and lusterless, and the nape aches as though pierced by a needle. If the patient becomes vexed at heart, death follows and the condition cannot be treated.

When it breaks out on the shoulder or upper arm, it is called ciyong, the blemish welling-abscess. Its appearance is reddish-black; treat it promptly. This will cause the patient to sweat down to the very feet, but it does not harm the five viscera. Once the abscess has been present for four or five days, proceed to cauterize it.

When it breaks out below the armpit, red and hard, it is called miju, the rice-grain ju. Treat it with the stone needle (bianshi); the stone should be fine and long, and the puncturing sparse. Apply pig's fat, and within six days it will heal; do not bind or wrap it. If the swelling is hard and does not rupture into pus, this is what is called saber-scrofula with goiter (madao xieying), a hard chain of swellings like beads along a saber hilt, accompanied by goiter, and it must be treated promptly.

When it breaks out on the chest, it is called jingju, the well ju. Its shape resembles a large bean, and it rises up over three or four days. If not treated early it will sink down into the abdomen; left untreated, the patient dies within seven days.

When it breaks out on the front of the chest, over the breastbone, it is called ganju, the sweet ju. Its color is greenish, and its shape resembles the fruit of a gourd. The patient constantly suffers from chills and fever; treat it promptly to eliminate the chills and fever. Left untreated, the patient dies within ten years, and pus emerges only after death.

When it breaks out on the rib-side, it is called baici, the rotting blemish. The rotting blemish is a disorder that afflicts women; moxibust it. As the disorder progresses it becomes a large welling-abscess full of pus; in treating it, once granulation flesh the size of a small red bean appears within, take one sheng each of zuoling root and qiaocao root, boil them in one dou six sheng of water, and reduce this down to three sheng. Have the patient drink it forcibly, then dress warmly and sit atop the cauldron to receive its warmth, so as to induce sweating down to the very feet; once this is achieved, the patient will recover.

When it breaks out on the thigh or shin, it is called gujingju, the thigh-shin ju. Its outward appearance shows little change, yet the abscess and its pus press against the bone. If not treated promptly, the patient dies within thirty days.
When it breaks out on the buttocks, it is called ruiju, the sharp ju. Its appearance is red, hard, and large; treat it promptly. Left untreated, the patient dies within thirty days.

When it breaks out on the inner thigh, it is called chishi, red-spreading. If not treated promptly, the patient dies within sixty days. Should it appear between the two thighs and go untreated, death should occur within ten days.

When it breaks out on the knee, it is called ciyong, the blemish welling-abscess. Its form is large, but the color of the swelling does not change; the patient suffers chills and fever, and the mass is hard as stone. Do not lance it with the stone needle — one who lances it while it is still hard will die. Only once it has softened should it be lanced; then the patient will live.

In all cases where welling-abscess or flat-abscess breaks out at a joint and its effects correspond straight through to the far side, it cannot be treated. If it breaks out on a yang aspect of the body, the patient dies within a hundred days; if on a yin aspect, within thirty days.
When it breaks out on the shin, it is called tunie, rabbit-bite. Its appearance is red down to the bone; treat it promptly. Left untreated, it will endanger the patient's life.

When it breaks out on the inner ankle, it is called zouhuan, sluggish walking. Its appearance is that of a welling-abscess, and its color does not change. Repeatedly lance its transport point with the stone needle to halt the chills and fever, and the patient will not die.
When it breaks out on the upper or lower foot, it is called siyin, the four excesses. Its form is a large welling-abscess; treat it promptly, for if untreated the patient dies within a hundred days.

When it breaks out at the side of the foot, it is called liyong, the dread welling-abscess. Its form is not large, at first no bigger than the little finger when it erupts; treat it promptly, removing whatever has turned black. If it fails to resolve and instead keeps enlarging, and is left untreated, the patient dies within a hundred days.

When it breaks out on the toe, it is called tuoyong, the sloughing welling-abscess. If its appearance is reddish-black it is fatal and cannot be treated; if it is not reddish-black, it is not fatal. If it fails to subside, amputate it promptly — otherwise the patient will die.

Huangdi said: 'Master, you have spoken of welling-abscess and flat-abscess — how are they to be distinguished from one another?' Qibo said: 'When the nutritive and defensive qi become detained within the channel vessels, the blood congeals and fails to move; when it fails to move, the defensive qi that follows it likewise becomes obstructed and impassable, and being blocked and dammed up, cannot flow — hence heat arises. When the great heat does not cease and the heat prevails, the flesh putrefies, and when the flesh putrefies it becomes pus. Yet in this case the disorder cannot sink in deeply: the bone and marrow are not scorched and withered, and the five viscera are not injured — hence this is termed a welling-abscess (yong).'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by a flat-abscess (ju)?' Qibo said: 'When the heat qi is thick and exuberant, it sinks down deeply into the flesh and skin; the sinews and marrow wither, and the disorder connects inwardly to the five viscera, exhausting the blood and qi. Beneath the site of the swelling, the sinews, bones, and healthy flesh are all consumed without remainder — hence this is termed a flat-abscess (ju). In a flat-abscess, the skin above is lusterless and hard, thick like the skin of an ox's neck. In a welling-abscess, the skin above is thin and glossy. These are the signs by which the two are distinguished.'
